topics.title: Origin of Biopotential and Its Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Origin of Biopotential and Its Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biopotential (Bioelectric Potential):** A biopotential is the **electrical potential difference (voltage)** generated across the membrane of a living cell (or tissue) due to the **unequal distribution of ions** (such as Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺) between the intracellular fluid (ICF) and the extracellular fluid (ECF), maintained by the **selective permeability of the cell membrane** and active transport mechanisms (e.g., the **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump**).

In the context of KTU 2024 scheme, three biopotentials are emphasized as syllabus-mandatory:
1. **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** — typically **-70 mV** (interior negative with respect to exterior).
2. **Action Potential (AP)** — transient reversal reaching **+30 mV to +40 mV**.
3. **Local / Graded Potential** — sub-threshold, decremental, variable-amplitude signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physiological Constants (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> - RMP of a typical neuron: **-70 mV**
> - Peak action potential: **+30 mV to +40 mV**
> - Body (core) temperature: **37 °C = 310 K**
> - Nernst temperature factor (RT/F) at 37 °C: **≈ 26.73 mV**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cell as a Tiny Battery"

Imagine a cell as a **miniature rechargeable battery** immersed in salt water:
- The **cell membrane** acts like a **semi-permeable wall** with tiny **one-way doors (ion channels)** that allow only specific ions (like K⁺) to leak out.
- The **Na⁺/K⁺ pump** is like a **janitor** constantly working 24×7, kicking **3 Na⁺ out** and pulling **2 K⁺ in** per ATP consumed — creating an ion imbalance.
- This imbalance is exactly what produces a **voltage across the membrane** — the **resting potential**.
- When a stimulus (a "tap on the shoulder") is strong enough to **open more Na⁺ doors**, positive charges rush in, the inside becomes positive → an **action potential (nerve impulse)** is generated and propagates like a **wave along a fuse wire**.

## 1.3 Cellular Electrophysiology — The Origin

Biopotentials originate because of four fundamental cellular phenomena:

| # | Phenomenon | Role in Biopotential Generation |
|---|-----------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Ionic concentration gradients** | K⁺ is **high inside**, Na⁺ is **high outside** |
| 2 | **Selective membrane permeability** | Membrane is ~**25× more permeable to K⁺** than Na⁺ at rest |
| 3 | **Active transport (Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase)** | Maintains gradients using **1 ATP → 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in** |
| 4 | **Voltage-gated ion channels** | Open/close in response to membrane voltage changes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The origin of biopotential is **NOT** a chemical reaction inside the cell — it is purely a **bio-physical electrochemical phenomenon** arising from the **Nernst-Planck diffusion principle** combined with the **active ionic pumping** by the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cell Membrane — The Bio-Electrical Interface

The **plasma membrane** is a **phospholipid bilayer (~7–10 nm thick)** with embedded proteins. Its electrical equivalent is a **parallel RC circuit**:
- **Capacitance (Cm):** ~ **1 μF/cm²** (due to lipid dielectric)
- **Resistance (Rm):** ~ **1000–10,000 Ω·cm²** (varies with ion channel state)

## 2.2 Resting Membrane Potential — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **K⁺ is the dominant intracellular cation** (~140 mM inside vs ~4 mM outside).
2. The membrane at rest is **highly permeable to K⁺** (leak channels).
3. **K⁺ diffuses outward** down its concentration gradient, leaving behind **negative proteins (anions)** that cannot follow.
4. This creates a **negative interior** → **RMP ≈ -70 mV**.
5. Equilibrium is reached when **electrical pull (negative interior attracts K⁺ back)** balances **chemical push (K⁺ diffusion outward)** → **Nernst equilibrium for K⁺**.

## 2.3 The Action Potential — Four Phases

| Phase | Mechanism | Voltage Change | Ion Channel Behavior |
|-------|-----------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Resting** | K⁺ leak dominant | **-70 mV** | K⁺ leak channels open; Na⁺ channels closed |
| **Depolarization** | Rapid Na⁺ influx | -70 mV → **+30 mV** | **Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open** (fast, positive feedback) |
| **Repolarization** | K⁺ efflux | +30 mV → -70 mV | Na⁺ channels inactivate; **voltage-gated K⁺ channels open** |
| **Hyperpolarization** | Excess K⁺ efflux | **~ -80 mV** | K⁺ channels slow to close → brief undershoot |
| **Return to Rest** | Na⁺/K⁺ pump restores | -80 mV → -70 mV | **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase** pumps ions back (uses ATP) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold potential ≈ -55 mV.** Once depolarization reaches this value, the **all-or-none law** applies — the action potential fires completely, regardless of stimulus strength.

## 2.4 Propagation of Action Potential

The action potential does not "jump" between adjacent membrane patches — it **regenerates sequentially** as local current flow depolarizes the neighboring patch to threshold.

### Two Modes of Propagation:

**A. Continuous Propagation (Unmyelinated Axons)**
- Speed: **0.5 – 2 m/s**
- Every patch of membrane depolarizes in sequence.
- Analogy: A wave of dominoes falling.

**B. Saltatory Propagation (Myelinated Axons)**
- Speed: **5 – 120 m/s** (e.g., up to **120 m/s** in large motor neurons)
- Action potential "leaps" between **Nodes of Ranvier** (unmyelinated gaps, ~1 μm apart).
- Myelin acts as a **high-resistance, low-capacitance insulator** (from Schwann cells / oligodendrocytes).
- Analogy: A frog hopping between lily pads.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 question paper on Module 1.**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Where Used |
|---|--------------|-------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Nernst Equation (single ion)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Equilibrium potential of one ion |
| 2 | **Nernst (log₁₀ form)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Practical calculation at 37 °C |
| 3 | **Nernst value at 37 °C (constant)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ mV | Quick numerical KTU problems |
| 4 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation** | $V_{m} = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\dfrac{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{in}}{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{out}}\right)$ | Resting membrane potential (multi-ion) |
| 5 | **Nernst-Planck Flux** | $J = -D\left(\dfrac{dC}{dx}\right) - \dfrac{zFDC}{RT}\left(\dfrac{dV}{dx}\right)$ | Total ionic flux = diffusion + drift |
| 6 | **Cable Equation (1-D)** | $\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = r_{m}\,c_{m}\,\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \dfrac{r_{m}}{r_{i}}\,V$ | Voltage spread in a passive cable |
| 7 | **Length Constant (λ)** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_{m}}{r_{i}}}$ | Distance over which V decays to $1/e$ |
| 8 | **Time Constant (τ)** | $\tau = r_{m}\,c_{m}$ | Time to reach $1 - 1/e$ of final V |
| 9 | **Conduction Velocity (Hursh factor)** | $v \propto \sqrt{\text{axon diameter}}$ (unmyelinated) | Velocity of propagation |
| 10 | **Conduction Velocity (myelinated)** | $v \propto \text{axon diameter}$ | Velocity in myelinated fibers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:**
> - $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
> - $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**
> - $z$ = Valency of ion
> - $P$ = Membrane permeability
> - $r_m$ = Membrane resistance per unit length
> - $r_i$ = Axial (intracellular) resistance per unit length
> - $c_m$ = Membrane capacitance per unit length

## 2.6 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Utility

Understanding biopotential origin is **foundational** for designing:
- **ECG machines** (cardiac biopotentials, 0.05 – 150 Hz)
- **EEG machines** (cortical biopotentials, 0.5 – 100 Hz)
- **EMG / NCS systems** (skeletal muscle / nerve biopotentials, 20 – 5000 Hz)
- **Pacemakers & defibrillators** (deliberate AP induction)
- **Neural prosthetics and brain-computer interfaces (BCIs)**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (for a Single Ion)

The Nernst equation describes the **equilibrium potential** at which the **chemical (diffusion) force** and the **electrical force** acting on an ion are **equal and opposite** — i.e., the **net flux is zero**.

### Starting Point — Free Energy Change

The molar free energy change when one mole of ion is transported from outside (out) to inside (in):

$$\Delta G = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$$

### Electrical Work Component

Moving an ion of charge $zF$ across a potential difference $V_m$ (inside minus outside) requires electrical work:

$$\Delta G_{elec} = zF \cdot V_m$$

### Equilibrium Condition

At electrochemical equilibrium, the **total free energy change is zero** (no net driving force):

$$\Delta G_{chem} + \Delta G_{elec} = 0$$

$$RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right) + zF \cdot V_m = 0$$

### Isolating the Equilibrium Potential $E_{ion}$

$$zF \cdot V_m = -RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$$

$$V_m = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$$

Using the property $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$:

$$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)}$$

### Conversion to Common (Base-10) Logarithm

Multiplying and dividing by 2.303:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303\,RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

### Substituting Values at 37 °C (310 K)

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = \frac{5934.0}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$

Therefore the **Nernst equation at body temperature** simplifies to the KTU-favorite:

$$\boxed{E_{ion}\,(\text{mV}) = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Nernst Potential for K⁺

**Given:**
- $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM
- $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM
- $z = +1$

**Step 1 — Plug into the 37 °C form:**

$$E_{K} = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{140}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) = \log_{10}(0.02857) = -1.5441$$

**Step 3 — Multiply:**

$$E_{K} = 61.5 \times (-1.5441) = -94.96 \text{ mV} \approx -95 \text{ mV}$$

**Interpretation:** If the membrane were permeable *only* to K⁺, the resting potential would be **-95 mV**. The actual RMP is **-70 mV** because a small Na⁺ permeability pulls it slightly positive.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Nernst Potential for Na⁺

**Given:**
- $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 12$ mM
- $z = +1$

$$E_{Na} = \frac{61.5}{1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{145}{12}\right)$$

$$\log_{10}(12.083) = 1.0822$$

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times 1.0822 = +66.55 \text{ mV} \approx +60 \text{ mV}$$

**Interpretation:** If the membrane were permeable *only* to Na⁺, the potential would be **+60 mV** — close to the peak action potential.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Cable Equation (Time + Space)

For a passive nerve fiber, the **axial current** (along the inside) equals the **transmembrane current** (across the membrane).

### Step 1 — Axial Current (Ohm's Law along x)

$$i_{i} = -\frac{1}{r_{i}}\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}$$

### Step 2 — Transmembrane Current (per unit length)

The membrane acts as a parallel RC element:

$$i_{m} = c_{m}\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \frac{V}{r_{m}}$$

### Step 3 — Kirchhoff's Current Law (Conservation)

The change in axial current along $x$ must equal the transmembrane current leaving the cable:

$$\frac{\partial i_{i}}{\partial x} = -i_{m}$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate and Substitute

$$-\frac{1}{r_{i}}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = -\left(c_{m}\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \frac{V}{r_{m}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Final Cable Equation

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{r_{i}}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = c_{m}\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \frac{V}{r_{m}}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = r_{m}c_{m}\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \frac{r_{m}}{r_{i}}V$$

## 3.5 Length Constant and Time Constant — Definitions

From the cable equation, assuming a **steady-state** (∂/∂t = 0) gives:

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_{m}}{r_{i}}}$$

- For a **typical unmyelinated axon:** $\lambda \approx 0.1$ to $1$ mm.
- For a **myelinated axon:** $\lambda \approx 5$ to $100$ mm (much longer due to high $r_m$).

The **time constant** $\tau = r_m c_m$ governs how fast the membrane voltage changes in response to current — typical values are **1 to 50 ms**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Propagation Modes

| Parameter | Continuous (Unmyelinated) | Saltatory (Myelinated) |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Speed | **0.5 – 2 m/s** | **5 – 120 m/s** |
| Energy per AP | Higher | ~**100× lower** |
| Membrane area involved | Entire axon | Only Nodes of Ranvier |
| Length constant λ | Short | Long (myelin insulates) |
| Equivalent circuit | Distributed RC | Discrete RC at nodes |
| Example fiber | Pain (C) fibers | Motor neurons, Aα fibers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Origin & Propagation of Biopotential

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STIMULUS arrives at neuron] --> B[Local depolarization of membrane]
    B --> C{Threshold reached -55 mV?}
    C -- No --> D[Sub-threshold local graded potential decays]
    C -- Yes --> E[Voltage-gated Na+ channels OPEN]
    E --> F[Rapid Na+ influx INTO cell]
    F --> G[Depolarization -70 mV to plus 30 mV]
    G --> H[Na+ channels INACTIVATE]
    H --> I[Voltage-gated K+ channels OPEN]
    I --> J[K+ efflux OUT of cell]
    J --> K[Repolarization plus 30 mV to -70 mV]
    K --> L[K+ channels slow to close - Hyperpolarization -80 mV]
    L --> M[Na+ and K+ ions redistributed by Na+ and K+ ATPase pump]
    M --> N[Return to resting potential -70 mV]
    N --> O[Action potential propagates along axon]
    O --> P{Myelinated?}
    P -- No --> Q[Continuous propagation -0.5 to 2 m per s]
    P -- Yes --> R[Action potential jumps between Nodes of Ranvier - Saltatory 5 to 120 m per s]
    Q --> S[AP reaches synapse - Neurotransmitter release]
    R --> S
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Bio-Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ION[IONIC GRADIENT] --> MEM[MEMBRANE PERMEABILITY]
    MEM --> NERN[NERNST EQUILIBRIUM]
    ATP[NA and K ATPase PUMP] --> MEM
    NERN --> RMP[RESTING MEMBRANE POTENTIAL -70 mV]
    STIM[STIMULUS] --> TH[THRESHOLD -55 mV]
    TH --> VGNA[VOLTAGE GATED NA CHANNELS]
    VGK[VOLTAGE GATED K CHANNELS] --> RMP
    VGNA --> AP[ACTION POTENTIAL plus 30 mV]
    AP --> PROP[PROPAGATION ALONG AXON]
    PROP --> REC[RECEIVING TISSUE]
    REC --> BIO[BIOELECTRODE]
    BIO --> AMP[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER]
    AMP --> DSP[DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING]
    DSP --> DISP[ECG / EEG / EMG DISPLAY]
```

## 4.3 Membrane Equivalent Circuit (KTU Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENA[(E_Na = plus 60 mV)] --- RNA{R_Na}
    EK[(E_K = -95 mV)] --- RK{R_K}
    ECL[(E_Cl = -65 mV)] --- RCL{R_Cl}
    RNA --- NODE((Node Vm))
    RK --- NODE
    RCL --- NODE
    NODE --- CM[Cm = 1 microFarad per cm squared]
    NODE --- RM[Leak Rm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Mapping:** The above is a **Hodgkin–Huxley style parallel-conductance model** of the membrane. Each ion species is modelled as a **battery (Nernst potential)** in series with a **variable resistor (channel conductance)**. All branches are in parallel with the **membrane capacitance (Cm)** and a **leak resistance (Rm)**. This is the canonical model used in nearly all KTU 2024 derivations of RMP.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define resting membrane potential. List the major ionic species responsible for it and state its typical value.**

**Model Answer:**
The **resting membrane potential (RMP)** is the steady-state potential difference across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell when it is not firing an action potential.
- **Typical value:** **-70 mV** (interior negative w.r.t. exterior).
- **Key ions responsible:** K⁺ (dominant — high intracellular, high permeability), Na⁺ (low intracellular, low permeability), Cl⁻ (passively distributed), and large intracellular **anionic proteins** (impermeant — net negative charge trapped inside).
- **Maintaining mechanisms:** (i) selective permeability (mainly to K⁺ at rest), (ii) Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplied]
**Differentiate between continuous and saltatory conduction of an action potential.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Continuous Conduction | Saltatory Conduction |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Fiber type** | Unmyelinated axons | Myelinated axons |
| **Speed** | 0.5 – 2 m/s | 5 – 120 m/s |
| **Mechanism** | AP regenerates at every patch of membrane | AP "leaps" between **Nodes of Ranvier** |
| **Energy cost** | High (every patch exchanges ions) | Low (only nodes depolarize) |
| **Examples** | Pain (C) fibers, autonomic post-ganglionic | Motor neurons, sensory Aα fibers |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation starting from electrochemical equilibrium. Hence calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37 °C given $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation** and discuss how it improves upon the Nernst equation for computing the resting membrane potential. Use typical values: $P_{Na} : P_{K} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 25 : 1$ and show that the RMP comes out close to **-70 mV**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation (Stepwise):**

**Step 1 — Chemical free energy** for moving 1 mole of ion from out → in:
$$\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Electrical work** for moving charge $zF$ across potential $V_m$:
$$\Delta G_{elec} = zF \cdot V_m$$

**Step 3 — At equilibrium**, net free energy change = 0:
$$RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right) + zF V_m = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_m$:**
$$V_m = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right) = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation at 37 °C** (T = 310 K):
$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.0267 \text{ V} = 26.7 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate $E_K$:**
$$E_K = 26.7 \ln\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) = 26.7 \times (-3.555) = -94.92 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -95 \text{ mV}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**GHK Equation:**
$$V_{m} = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{in}}{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{out}}\right)$$

**Improvements over Nernst:**
- Considers **multiple ions** simultaneously (K⁺, Na⁺, Cl⁻).
- Accounts for **relative permeabilities** ($P_{ion}$) of each ion.
- More realistic for biological membranes where several ions contribute.

**Numerical substitution (typical values):**

- $P_K = 25$, $P_{Na} = 1$, $P_{Cl} = 1$ (relative units)
- $[K^+]_{in} = 140$, $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ (mM)
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 12$, $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ (mM)
- $[Cl^-]_{in} = 4$, $[Cl^-]_{out} = 120$ (mM)

**Numerator:**
$$P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}$$
$$= 25(4) + 1(145) + 1(4) = 100 + 145 + 4 = 249$$

**Denominator:**
$$P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}$$
$$= 25(140) + 1(12) + 1(120) = 3500 + 12 + 120 = 3632$$

**Compute $V_m$:**
$$V_m = 26.7 \ln\left(\frac{249}{3632}\right) = 26.7 \times (-2.681) = -71.6 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{V_m \approx -71.6 \text{ mV} \approx -70 \text{ mV} \;\;\text{(matches RMP)}}$$

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, describe the **phases of an action potential** in a neuronal cell. Explain the role of the **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** in restoring the ionic gradients. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **cable equation** for a passive nerve fiber. Define and give the physical significance of the **length constant (λ)** and **time constant (τ)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Phases of Action Potential (with diagram description):**

1. **Resting state (Phase 4):** Membrane at -70 mV. Na⁺ channels closed; K⁺ leak channels open.
2. **Depolarization (Phase 0):** Stimulus raises Vm to threshold (-55 mV). Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels open** → rapid Na⁺ influx → Vm rises to **+30 mV**.
3. **Repolarization (Phase 3):** Na⁺ channels **inactivate**; voltage-gated **K⁺ channels open** → K⁺ efflux → Vm returns toward -70 mV.
4. **Hyperpolarization (Phase 4 dip):** K⁺ channels close slowly → Vm briefly undershoots to ~**-80 mV** (refractory tail).
5. **Return to rest:** **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase** restores gradients.

**Role of Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump:**
- **Mechanism:** Anti-port active transport using ATP.
- **Stoichiometry:** **3 Na⁺ pumped OUT** and **2 K⁺ pumped IN** per ATP hydrolyzed.
- **Net effect:** Direct contribution of **-4 mV** to RMP (electrogenic pump) + restoration of ionic gradients consumed during APs.
- **Critical for:** Maintaining the **excitability** of the cell — without it, gradients would collapse in seconds.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Cable Equation Derivation:**

(See Section 3.4 of these notes for the full stepwise derivation.)

**Final form:**
$$\frac{1}{r_{i}}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = c_{m}\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \frac{V}{r_{m}}$$

**Length Constant λ:**
$$\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_{m}}{r_{i}}}$$
- **Physical meaning:** The distance along the cable over which a steady-state voltage decays to **$1/e$ (~36.8%)** of its initial value.
- **Significance:** Larger λ ⇒ signal travels farther without decrement (critical for AP propagation in long axons). Myelination increases λ by **increasing $r_m$**.

**Time Constant τ:**
$$\tau = r_{m}\,c_{m}$$
- **Physical meaning:** Time required for the membrane voltage to reach **$1 - 1/e$ (~63.2%)** of its steady-state value in response to a step current.
- **Significance:** Smaller τ ⇒ faster response, faster AP firing. Typical values: **1 – 50 ms**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks in OEICT723 (Module 1):**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $z$** in the Nernst equation (anions like Cl⁻ need $z = -1$, not $+1$). Loss: ~1 mark.
> 2. **Using 25 mV instead of 26.7 mV** for $RT/F$ at body temperature (37 °C). The KTU syllabus explicitly uses **T = 310 K**. Loss: ~1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing the GHK numerator and denominator** — the rule is: *permeable ion's OUTside concentration in numerator, INside in denominator*. Loss: ~2 marks.
> 4. **Writing $E_K = -95$ mV and claiming that is the RMP** — Examiner expects the student to explain that -70 mV comes from the **GHK multi-ion equation**, not the single-ion Nernst. Loss: ~2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the units** in the final answer (e.g., writing -95 instead of -95 mV). Loss: ~0.5 mark.
> 6. **Omitting the derivation of the cable equation** in part (b) and jumping directly to λ and τ — KTU strictly demands the full derivation. Loss: ~3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Origin of Biopotential & Its Propagation**

- ✅ **Resting Membrane Potential = -70 mV** (interior negative); maintained by **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase** and **K⁺ leak channels**.
- ✅ **Threshold = -55 mV**; peak AP = **+30 mV to +40 mV**; hyperpolarization = **-80 mV**.
- ✅ **Nernst Equation (37 °C):** $E_{ion} = (61.5/z) \log_{10}([ion]_{out}/[ion]_{in})$ mV.
- ✅ **$E_K \approx -95$ mV**; **$E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV**; **$E_{Cl} \approx -65$ mV** (memorize these).
- ✅ **GHK equation** is the **multi-ion generalization** of the Nernst equation and gives the **actual RMP**.
- ✅ **Action potential phases:** Resting → Depolarization (Na⁺ in) → Repolarization (K⁺ out) → Hyperpolarization → Recovery (pump).
- ✅ **All-or-none law** — once threshold is crossed, AP amplitude is **independent of stimulus strength**.
- ✅ **Refractory periods** — **absolute** (no AP possible) and **relative** (AP possible only with stronger stimulus).
- ✅ **Continuous conduction** in unmyelinated axons (slow, 0.5–2 m/s); **saltatory conduction** in myelinated axons (fast, up to 120 m/s, jumps between **Nodes of Ranvier**).
- ✅ **Cable equation** governs passive spread; **λ (length constant)** and **τ (time constant)** define the spatial and temporal reach of signals.
- ✅ **Membrane equivalent circuit** is a parallel combination of (Nernst battery + variable channel resistance) for each ion, plus membrane capacitance $C_m$ and leak $R_m$.
- ✅ **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase** is **electrogenic** (3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in → contributes directly to RMP).
- ✅ **Typical concentrations** (mM): K⁺ — 140 in / 4 out; Na⁺ — 12 in / 145 out; Cl⁻ — 4 in / 120 out.
- ✅ **Permeability ratio at rest:** $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} \approx 25 : 1 : 1$.
- ✅ **Body temperature constant** for all KTU Nernst problems: $T = 310$ K → $RT/F = 26.73$ mV → $2.303\,RT/F = 61.5$ mV.
- ✅ **Engineering relevance:** ECG, EEG, EMG, NCS, pacemakers, BCIs — all depend on biopotential theory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nervous System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nervous System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Nervous System — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Nervous System** is a complex, highly organized network of specialized cells called **neurons** that coordinates, regulates, and integrates the body's physiological activities by transmitting electrochemical signals between the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral tissues. In the context of **Biomedical Instrumentation**, the nervous system is the foundational source of all bioelectrical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, ENG) that any medical instrument aims to measure, amplify, and analyze.

The nervous system is anatomically divided into two principal divisions:

| Division | Sub-Division | Primary Function | KTU 2024 Instrumentation Relevance |
|----------|--------------|------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Central Nervous System (CNS)** | Brain & Spinal Cord | Integration, processing, command generation | Source of **EEG** (Electroencephalography) signals |
| **Peripheral Nervous System (PNS)** | Somatic & Autonomic | Signal relay to/from CNS | Source of **EMG** (Electromyography) and **ENG** (Electroneurography) signals |
| **Autonomic NS (ANS)** | Sympathetic & Parasympathetic | Involuntary regulation | HRV (Heart Rate Variability) monitoring systems |

The **Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)** itself subdivides into:
- **Sympathetic Nervous System** — "Fight or Flight" response (increases heart rate, dilates pupils).
- **Parasympathetic Nervous System** — "Rest and Digest" response (decreases heart rate, stimulates digestion).
- **Enteric Nervous System** — Controls the gastrointestinal system independently.

## 1.2 The Neuron — The Fundamental Functional Unit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition — Neuron:**
> A **neuron** is a highly specialized, electrically excitable cell that processes and transmits information through electrochemical signaling. It is the **smallest functional unit** of the nervous system and is responsible for generating the **action potential** — the fundamental electrical signal in all neural bioinstrumentation.

A typical neuron consists of three main structural regions:

1. **Dendrites** — Branched, tree-like extensions that **receive** incoming signals from neighboring neurons.
2. **Soma (Cell Body)** — Contains the nucleus and organelles; **integrates** incoming signals.
3. **Axon** — A long, slender projection that **transmits** the action potential away from the soma to the axon terminals.
4. **Axon Terminals (Synaptic Endings)** — Release **neurotransmitters** to communicate with the next cell.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Electrical Wiring of the Body"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of the nervous system as the **electrical wiring of a house**, with neurons acting as **wires and switches**:
> - The **brain** is the **main control panel (CPU)**.
> - **Spinal cord** is the **main trunk cable** running throughout the house.
> - **Peripheral nerves** are the **branch wires** going to rooms (organs/muscles).
> - **Dendrites** are the **electrical outlets** that receive current.
> - The **axon** is the **copper wire** carrying current.
> - The **synapse** is the **junction box** where one wire connects to the next — and here, the signal "jumps" using a chemical messenger (neurotransmitter) rather than direct electrical contact.

This analogy makes it clear: when a biomedical instrument (like an EEG machine) "listens" to the brain, it is essentially **tapping into the electrical wiring** of millions of neurons firing simultaneously.

## 1.4 Key Physiological Constants and Standard Metrics

The following values are **standard physiological metrics** that KTU examiners expect students to memorize verbatim:

| Constant / Metric | Standard Value | Units | Significance |
|-------------------|----------------|-------|--------------|
| **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** | **−70 mV** (range: −60 to −90 mV) | millivolts (mV) | Voltage across neuronal membrane at rest |
| **Threshold Potential** | **−55 mV** (approximately) | millivolts (mV) | Critical voltage to trigger action potential |
| **Peak Action Potential** | **+30 to +40 mV** | millivolts (mV) | Maximum depolarization |
| **Nernst Equation Temperature** | **310 K** (37 °C / body temperature) | Kelvin | Body core temperature for ion calculations |
| **Neuron Conduction Velocity** | **0.5 to 120 m/s** | meters/second | Varies by axon diameter & myelination |
| **Synaptic Delay** | **0.5 to 1 millisecond** | ms | Time for chemical synaptic transmission |
| **Intracellular K⁺ Concentration** | **~140 mmol/L** | mmol/L | High inside the neuron |
| **Extracellular K⁺ Concentration** | **~4 to 5 mmol/L** | mmol/L | Low outside the neuron |
| **Intracellular Na⁺ Concentration** | **~10 to 15 mmol/L** | mmol/L | Low inside the neuron |
| **Extracellular Na⁺ Concentration** | **~145 mmol/L** | mmol/L | High outside the neuron |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The **−70 mV resting potential** and the **Nernst Equation** for ion equilibrium are the **two most frequently tested numerical facts** in Module 1. Memorize them precisely.

## 1.5 The Bioelectrical Foundation — Membrane Potential

The neuron's cell membrane maintains a **voltage difference** between the inside and outside of the cell. This voltage is called the **membrane potential (Vm)** and is defined as:

$$V_m = V_{inside} - V_{outside}$$

When the neuron is **not firing**, $V_m = -70$ mV, meaning the inside is **70 mV more negative** than the outside. This is the **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)**.

Two primary ion pumps and channels maintain this potential:
- **Sodium-Potassium Pump (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase)** — Actively pumps **3 Na⁺ out** and **2 K⁺ in** per ATP molecule consumed.
- **Ion Channels** — Passive pathways for K⁺ (leak channels, dominant at rest) and voltage-gated Na⁺/K⁺ channels (active during action potential).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Nernst Equation — Equilibrium Potential for a Single Ion

The **Nernst Equation** calculates the **equilibrium (reversal) potential** for a single ion species across a semipermeable membrane. At equilibrium, there is no net flow of that ion.

**Step-by-step logic** behind the Nernst Equation:

- **Step 1:** Consider a membrane separating two solutions with different concentrations of ion $X$.
- **Step 2:** Two opposing forces act on the ion:
  - **Chemical Gradient** (diffusion force) — drives ions from high to low concentration.
  - **Electrical Gradient** (Coulomb force) — drives ions based on membrane potential.
- **Step 3:** At **electrochemical equilibrium**, these two forces are exactly equal and opposite.
- **Step 4:** Equating the work done by the chemical force ($\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$) with the electrical work ($\Delta G_{elec} = zFE$) yields the **Nernst Equation**.

**The Nernst Equation (Full Form):**

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}}$$

At body temperature ($T = 310$ K), and converting natural log to base-10 log (multiply by 2.303):

$$E_{ion} = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}}$$

where $E_{ion}$ is in mV and $z$ is the valence of the ion.

## 2.2 The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation — Membrane Potential for Multiple Ions

Since the resting membrane is permeable to **multiple ions** (primarily K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻), the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation** is used to compute the actual membrane potential:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K [K^+]_{out} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K [K^+]_{in} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{out}}$$

where $P_{ion}$ is the **permeability** of the membrane to that specific ion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** At rest, $P_K \gg P_{Na} \gg P_{Cl}$, which means the **resting potential is dominated by K⁺ permeability** — this is why $V_m \approx E_K$ (Nernst potential for potassium).

## 2.3 The Action Potential — Step-by-Step Physiological Breakdown

The **action potential** is a rapid, transient, all-or-nothing depolarization and repolarization of the neuronal membrane. It consists of **five distinct phases**:

### Phase 1: Resting State
- $V_m = -70$ mV.
- Voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels are **closed**.
- K⁺ leak channels maintain the RMP.

### Phase 2: Depolarization (Rising Phase)
- Stimulus reaches **threshold (−55 mV)**.
- Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels open rapidly** (fast activation).
- Na⁺ rushes into the cell (down its electrochemical gradient).
- $V_m$ rises sharply toward $E_{Na}$ (≈ +60 mV).

### Phase 3: Peak / Overshoot
- $V_m$ reaches approximately **+30 to +40 mV**.
- Na⁺ channels **inactivate** (fast inactivation gate closes).
- Voltage-gated K⁺ channels open (delayed rectifier).

### Phase 4: Repolarization (Falling Phase)
- K⁺ flows **out** of the cell (down its electrochemical gradient).
- $V_m$ returns toward negative values.

### Phase 5: Hyperpolarization (Undershoot)
- K⁺ channels close slowly, causing excessive K⁺ efflux.
- $V_m$ briefly drops below −70 mV (to ~ −80 mV).
- This is the **refractory period** precursor.

### Phase 6: Return to Rest
- Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump restores ion gradients.
- $V_m$ stabilizes at −70 mV.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Following Table is the AUTHORITATIVE KTU 2024 Formula Reference for this Module. Memorize Every Entry.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | Typical KTU Use |
|---|--------------|----------|----------------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Nernst Equation (General)** | $E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ = temp in K, $z$ = ion valence, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol | Volts (V) | Single-ion equilibrium potential |
| 2 | **Nernst Equation (Body Temp, mV)** | $E = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | Same as above, output in **mV** | mV | Quick exam calculation |
| 3 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK)** | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K [K^+]_{out} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K [K^+]_{in} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{out}}$ | $P$ = permeability coefficient | Volts | Multi-ion resting potential |
| 4 | **Nernst-Planck Equation** | $J = -D \frac{dC}{dx} + \frac{zFDC}{RT} \frac{dV}{dx}$ | $J$ = flux, $D$ = diffusion coeff. | mol/(m²·s) | Ion flux across membrane |
| 5 | **Cable Equation (1D)** | $\frac{\partial^2 V_m}{\partial x^2} = r_m c_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} + r_m i_i$ | $r_m$ = membrane resistance, $c_m$ = capacitance | — | Signal propagation in axons |
| 6 | **Conduction Velocity (Myelinated)** | $v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot d \cdot f}{R_i \cdot C_m}}$ | $d$ = axon diameter, $f$ = frequency | m/s | Velocity in myelinated fibers |
| 7 | **Time Constant (τ)** | $\tau = R_m \cdot C_m$ | Membrane resistance × capacitance | seconds (s) | Membrane charging time |
| 8 | **Length Constant (λ)** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{R_m}{R_i}}$ | Membrane resistance / axial resistance | meters (m) | Signal decay length |
| 9 | **Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Ratio** | **3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in** per ATP | Stoichiometric ratio | — | Pump energetics |
| 10 | **Nernst Potential — K⁺** | $E_K = 61.5 \log_{10} \frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}} \approx -90$ mV | Using $[K^+]_{in} = 140$, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ | mV | Standard exam value |
| 11 | **Nernst Potential — Na⁺** | $E_{Na} = 61.5 \log_{10} \frac{[Na^+]_{out}}{[Na^+]_{in}} \approx +60$ mV | Using $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$, $[Na^+]_{in} = 15$ | mV | Standard exam value |
| 12 | **Threshold Relation** | $V_{th} \approx -55$ mV | Trigger voltage for AP | mV | Action potential initiation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Nernst equation and GHK equation are **not merely theoretical constructs** — they are foundational to:

- **Ion-Selective Electrodes (ISEs)** used in clinical blood-gas analyzers — measure K⁺, Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻ directly using Nernstian potential.
- **pH Meters** — pH electrode potential follows a Nernstian slope of **59.16 mV/pH unit at 25 °C**.
- **Neural Microelectrode Design** — Engineers designing EEG/ECG electrodes must understand the biopotential origin.
- **Drug Delivery Systems** — Iontophoretic transdermal drug delivery uses electric field-driven ion transport.
- **Neuroprosthetics and Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — Cochlear implants, deep brain stimulators, and retinal prosthetics all manipulate neuronal membrane potentials.
- **Defibrillators** — Apply a massive depolarizing current to reset cardiac (and in some designs, neuronal) membrane potentials.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Nernst Equation

The Nernst Equation is derived by equating the **electrochemical potential** of an ion on both sides of a permeable membrane at thermodynamic equilibrium.

### Step 1: Define the Chemical Potential (Gibbs Free Energy per mole)

The chemical potential of an ion in solution depends on its concentration. For an ideal solution, the chemical potential $\mu$ at concentration $[ion]$ is given by:

$$\mu = \mu^0 + RT \ln [ion]$$

**Explanation:** $\mu^0$ is the standard chemical potential (a constant reference). The $RT \ln [ion]$ term captures how the chemical potential changes with concentration.

### Step 2: Include the Electrical Potential Contribution

For a charged ion, the total **electrochemical potential** $\bar{\mu}$ adds the electrical work term $zF\phi$:

$$\bar{\mu} = \mu^0 + RT \ln [ion] + zF\phi$$

**Explanation:** $\phi$ is the local electric potential, $z$ is the ion valence, and $F$ is Faraday's constant. The $zF\phi$ term is the work done to move one mole of ion against the electric field.

### Step 3: Write the Electrochemical Potential on Both Sides

- **Outside the cell:** $\bar{\mu}_{out} = \mu^0 + RT \ln [ion]_{out} + zF\phi_{out}$
- **Inside the cell:** $\bar{\mu}_{in} = \mu^0 + RT \ln [ion]_{in} + zF\phi_{in}$

**Explanation:** Both sides share the same $\mu^0$ (same ion, same standard state).

### Step 4: Apply the Equilibrium Condition

At equilibrium, there is **no net flow** of the ion. This means the electrochemical potentials on both sides are **equal**:

$$\bar{\mu}_{out} = \bar{\mu}_{in}$$

$$\mu^0 + RT \ln [ion]_{out} + zF\phi_{out} = \mu^0 + RT \ln [ion]_{in} + zF\phi_{in}$$

**Explanation:** The $\mu^0$ terms cancel because they are identical on both sides.

### Step 5: Cancel and Rearrange

$$RT \ln [ion]_{out} + zF\phi_{out} = RT \ln [ion]_{in} + zF\phi_{in}$$

$$RT \ln [ion]_{out} - RT \ln [ion]_{in} = zF\phi_{in} - zF\phi_{out}$$

$$RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} = zF(\phi_{in} - \phi_{out})$$

**Explanation:** We use the logarithm identity $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$.

### Step 6: Define the Membrane Potential

The membrane potential is defined as the **inside potential minus the outside potential**:

$$E_{ion} = \phi_{in} - \phi_{out}$$

**Explanation:** This is the conventional sign convention in physiology — $V_m$ is negative at rest because the inside is more negative.

### Step 7: Final Form of the Nernst Equation

$$\phi_{in} - \phi_{out} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}}$$

**Explanation:** This is the **complete Nernst Equation** giving the equilibrium potential for any monovalent or polyvalent ion at any temperature.

### Step 8: Convert to Base-10 Logarithm and Body Temperature (T = 310 K)

Using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$

Substituting $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 310$ K, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{z \times 96485} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$

$$E_{ion} = \frac{5935.2}{z \times 96485} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$

$$E_{ion} = \frac{0.0615 \text{ V}}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}}$$

**Explanation:** The factor **61.5 mV** at body temperature is the **universal constant students must memorize** for KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Potassium Nernst Potential

**Problem:** Calculate the Nernst equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37 °C, given $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mmol/L and $[K^+]_{out} = 4.5$ mmol/L.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters. $z = +1$ (potassium is monovalent cation), $T = 310$ K.

**Step 2:** Apply the body-temperature Nernst equation:

$$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10} \frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values:

$$E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10} \frac{4.5}{140}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{4.5}{140} = 0.03214$$

**Step 5:** Take the base-10 logarithm:

$$\log_{10}(0.03214) = -1.4929$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by 61.5:

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.4929) = -91.81 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 7:** Round to standard physiological value:

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -92 \text{ mV}}$$

**Explanation:** This matches the experimentally observed K⁺ Nernst potential of **approximately −90 mV**, confirming that at rest, the neuronal membrane is **highly permeable to K⁺** — and the resting potential (−70 mV) is dominated by, but not equal to, $E_K$ because some Na⁺ permeability exists.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Sodium Nernst Potential

**Problem:** Calculate the Nernst equilibrium potential for Na⁺ at 37 °C, given $[Na^+]_{in} = 15$ mmol/L and $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mmol/L.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** $z = +1$ for Na⁺.

**Step 2:** Apply the equation:

$$E_{Na} = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10} \frac{[Na^+]_{out}}{[Na^+]_{in}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times \log_{10} \frac{145}{15}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{145}{15} = 9.667$$

**Step 5:** Take the logarithm:

$$\log_{10}(9.667) = 0.9853$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by 61.5:

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times 0.9853 = +60.6 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_{Na} \approx +60 \text{ mV}}$$

**Explanation:** This is the **theoretical maximum depolarization** a neuron can reach during an action potential. The actual peak (+30 to +40 mV) is less than $E_{Na}$ because Na⁺ channels begin to inactivate before full equilibrium is reached.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Nernst vs. GHK vs. Nernst-Planck

| Feature | Nernst Equation | GHK Equation | Nernst-Planck Equation |
|---------|-----------------|--------------|------------------------|
| **Number of Ions** | Single ion | Multiple ions (typically 3) | Single ion with full flux |
| **Membrane Permeability** | Not considered | Included (via $P$ coefficients) | Implicit in flux equation |
| **Steady State?** | Yes (full equilibrium) | Yes (steady-state resting) | No (dynamic flux) |
| **Output** | Reversal potential (mV) | Membrane potential (mV) | Ion flux (mol/m²/s) |
| **Assumes** | Pure diffusion equilibrium | Constant field across membrane | Nernst-Planck electrodiffusion |
| **KTU 2024 Use** | Single-ion problems | Multi-ion resting potential | Advanced transport problems |
| **Limitation** | Cannot predict $V_m$ with multiple ions | Constant field assumption | Hard to solve analytically |

## 3.5 Derivation of the Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Energy Requirement

**Step 1:** The pump moves **3 Na⁺ out** and **2 K⁺ in** per cycle. Net charge moved out per cycle = $3 - 2 = 1$ positive charge (net outward current).

**Step 2:** This means the pump is **electrogenic** — it contributes directly to the resting potential.

**Step 3:** Free energy required to move one mole of ion against both chemical and electrical gradients:

$$\Delta G = RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}} + zF(V_m)$$

**Step 4:** For 3 moles of Na⁺ moved out (against both gradients):

$$\Delta G_{Na} = 3 \left[ RT \ln \frac{[Na^+]_{in}}{[Na^+]_{out}} + F(V_{in} - V_{out}) \right]$$

**Step 5:** For 2 moles of K⁺ moved in (against electrical gradient, with chemical gradient):

$$\Delta G_K = 2 \left[ RT \ln \frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}} + F(V_{in} - V_{out}) \right]$$

**Step 6:** Total free energy per ATP hydrolyzed:

$$\Delta G_{total} = \Delta G_{Na} + \Delta G_K$$

**Step 7:** Numerical evaluation using standard concentrations and $V_m = -70$ mV:

$$\Delta G_{Na} = 3 \left[ (8.314)(310) \ln \frac{15}{145} + 96485(-0.070) \right]$$

$$\Delta G_{Na} = 3 \left[ -5714 - 6754 \right] = 3 \times (-12468) = -37{,}404 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 8:** Similarly for K⁺:

$$\Delta G_K = 2 \left[ (8.314)(310) \ln \frac{4.5}{140} + 96485(-0.070) \right]$$

$$\Delta G_K = 2 \left[ -3862 - 6754 \right] = 2 \times (-10616) = -21{,}232 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 9:** Sum (note: $\Delta G_{ATP} \approx -50{,}000$ J/mol, so the pump is thermodynamically feasible):

$$\Delta G_{total} = -37{,}404 + (-21{,}232) = -58{,}636 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Explanation:** The negative sign means the pump **releases** energy (since ions are moving down their gradients overall for the pump, but the work required to maintain the gradient is offset by ATP hydrolysis). The pump consumes ~**30% of the body's total ATP** at rest — a key fact for KTU viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Organization of the Nervous System (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[NERVOUS SYSTEM] --> B[CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM]
    A --> C[PERIPHERAL NERVOUS SYSTEM]
    B --> B1[BRAIN]
    B --> B2[SPINAL CORD]
    B1 --> B1A[CEREBRUM]
    B1 --> B1B[CEREBELLUM]
    B1 --> B1C[BRAIN STEM]
    C --> C1[SOMATIC NERVOUS SYSTEM]
    C --> C2[AUTONOMIC NERVOUS SYSTEM]
    C2 --> C2A[SYMPATHETIC]
    C2 --> C2B[PARASYMPATHETIC]
    C2 --> C2C[ENTERIC]
    C1 --> D1[VOLUNTARY MUSCLE CONTROL]
    C2A --> E1[FIGHT OR FLIGHT]
    C2B --> E2[REST AND DIGEST]
    C2C --> E3[GI TRACT CONTROL]
    B1A --> F1[EEG SIGNAL SOURCE]
    B2 --> F2[EVOKED POTENTIAL SOURCE]
    D1 --> F3[EMG SIGNAL SOURCE]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Neuron Structure and Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[DENDRITES] -->|Receive signals| B[SOMA Cell Body]
    B -->|Integrate EPSP IPSP| C[AXON HILLOCK]
    C -->|Threshold check| D[AXON]
    D -->|Action potential| E[AXON TERMINALS]
    E -->|Neurotransmitter release| F[SYNAPTIC CLEFT]
    F -->|Bind receptors| G[POSTSYNAPTIC NEURON]
    G --> A
    D --> D1[Myelin Sheath]
    D1 --> D2[Node of Ranvier]
    D2 -->|Saltatory conduction| D
    E --> H1[Voltage Gated Ca Channels]
    H1 --> H2[Vesicle Fusion]
    H2 --> E
```

## 4.3 Action Potential Phase Diagram — State Transition Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[PHASE 1: RESTING STATE] -->|Stimulus| P2[PHASE 2: DEPOLARIZATION]
    P2 -->|Vm reaches peak| P3[PHASE 3: PEAK OVERSHOOT]
    P3 -->|Na channels inactivate| P4[PHASE 4: REPOLARIZATION]
    P4 -->|K channels close slowly| P5[PHASE 5: HYPERPOLARIZATION]
    P5 -->|Na K pump restores| P6[PHASE 6: RETURN TO REST]
    P6 --> P1
    P1 -.->|Vm equals minus 70 mV| P1
    P2 -.->|Na channels OPEN| P2
    P3 -.->|K channels begin to OPEN| P3
    P4 -.->|Na channels INACTIVATED| P4
    P5 -.->|Absolute refractory period| P5
    P6 -.->|Relative refractory period| P6
```

## 4.4 Synaptic Transmission — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Action Potential Arrives at Axon Terminal] --> S2[Voltage Gated Ca Channels Open]
    S2 --> S3[Ca Influx into Terminal]
    S3 --> S4[Synaptic Vesicles Mobilize]
    S4 --> S5[Vesicles Fuse with Presynaptic Membrane]
    S5 --> S6[Neurotransmitter Released into Cleft]
    S6 --> S7[Neurotransmitter Diffuses Across Cleft]
    S7 --> S8[Binds Postsynaptic Receptors]
    S8 --> S9a[Excitatory Postsynaptic Potential EPSP]
    S8 --> S9b[Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potential IPSP]
    S9a --> S10[Depolarization of Postsynaptic Neuron]
    S9b --> S11[Hyperpolarization of Postsynaptic Neuron]
    S10 --> S12[May Trigger New Action Potential]
    S11 --> S13[Reduces Firing Probability]
    S6 --> S14[Reuptake or Enzymatic Degradation]
    S14 --> S15[Signal Termination]
```

## 4.5 Membrane Ion Channel State Machine (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Voltage Gated Na Channel] --> A2[CLOSED Activation Gate]
    A2 -->|Vm depolarization| A3[OPEN Fast Activation]
    A3 -->|Within ms| A4[INACTIVATED Fast Inactivation]
    A4 -->|Repolarization| A5[RESTING Closed Deactivation]
    A5 --> A2
    B1[Voltage Gated K Channel] --> B2[CLOSED]
    B2 -->|Depolarization delayed| B3[OPEN Delayed Rectifier]
    B3 -->|Repolarization| B4[CLOSED]
    B4 --> B2
    C1[Na K ATPase Pump] --> C2[3 Na OUT 2 K IN per ATP]
    C2 --> C3[Maintains Ion Gradients]
    C3 --> C1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Format Note:** Part A questions are **2-mark conceptual** definitions with **1 mark** for a precise, complete answer. Answer in **3–4 sentences maximum**. Use **bullet points** in the actual exam for clarity.

### Question 1: Define the Resting Membrane Potential. What is its typical value in a neuron?

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
- The **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** is the steady-state voltage difference across the neuronal cell membrane when the neuron is **not transmitting** a signal.
- It is defined as $V_m = V_{inside} - V_{outside}$, with the **inside being more negative** than the outside.
- The typical value is **−70 mV** (acceptable range: −60 mV to −90 mV).
- It is maintained primarily by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** and the **high permeability of the membrane to K⁺** at rest.

### Question 2: Differentiate between the Sympathetic and Parasympathetic Nervous Systems.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Sympathetic NS | Parasympathetic NS |
|---------|----------------|--------------------|
| Function | **Fight or Flight** | **Rest and Digest** |
| Heart Rate | **Increases** (via norepinephrine) | **Decreases** (via acetylcholine) |
| Pupils | **Dilates** | **Constricts** |
| Bronchi | **Dilates** | **Constricts** |
| GI Activity | **Inhibits** | **Stimulates** |
| Neurotransmitter | **Norepinephrine** | **Acetylcholine** |
| Origin | **Thoracolumbar** | **Craniosacral** |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Format Note:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** between two options (OR). Each option typically has **two sub-parts**: (a) worth 7 marks and (b) worth 7 marks. Marks are awarded for **diagrams, equations, and step-by-step derivation**.

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Nernst Equation starting from the electrochemical potential equilibrium condition. State all assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the Nernst equilibrium potential for a Calcium ion (Ca²⁺) at body temperature (37 °C), given $[Ca^{2+}]_{in} = 10^{-7}$ mol/L (intracellular) and $[Ca^{2+}]_{out} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L (extracellular). Comment on the physiological significance.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Nernst Equation

**Step 1: Define the Electrochemical Potential**
The electrochemical potential $\bar{\mu}$ of a charged ion in solution is the sum of its **chemical potential** and **electrical potential energy per mole**:

$$\bar{\mu} = \mu^0 + RT \ln [ion] + zF\phi$$

**Assumption 1:** The solution behaves ideally (activity = concentration).
**Assumption 2:** The membrane is permeable to **only one ion species**.
**Assumption 3:** The system is at **thermodynamic equilibrium**.

**Step 2: Apply Equilibrium Condition**
At equilibrium, the electrochemical potentials on both sides of the membrane are equal:

$$\mu^0 + RT \ln [ion]_{out} + zF\phi_{out} = \mu^0 + RT \ln [ion]_{in} + zF\phi_{in}$$

**Step 3: Simplify**
Cancel $\mu^0$ and rearrange:

$$RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} = zF(\phi_{in} - \phi_{out})$$

**Step 4: Final Form**

$$E_{ion} = \phi_{in} - \phi_{out} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$

**Step 5: At Body Temperature (T = 310 K)**

$$E_{ion} = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Calcium Nernst Potential Calculation

**Step 1: Identify Parameters**
- Valence: $z = +2$ (Ca²⁺ is a divalent cation)
- Temperature: $T = 310$ K
- $[Ca^{2+}]_{in} = 10^{-7}$ mol/L
- $[Ca^{2+}]_{out} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L

**Step 2: Apply the Nernst Equation**

$$E_{Ca} = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{+2} \log_{10} \frac{[Ca^{2+}]_{out}}{[Ca^{2+}]_{in}}$$

$$E_{Ca} = 30.75 \times \log_{10} \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-7}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Ratio**

$$\frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-7}} = 2 \times 10^{4} = 20{,}000$$

**Step 4: Take the Logarithm**

$$\log_{10}(20{,}000) = \log_{10}(2 \times 10^4) = \log_{10}(2) + 4 = 0.301 + 4 = 4.301$$

**Step 5: Multiply by 30.75**

$$E_{Ca} = 30.75 \times 4.301 = 132.26 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_{Ca} \approx +132 \text{ mV}}$$

**Step 6: Physiological Significance**
- This **large positive value** indicates that Ca²⁺ has an enormous driving force to enter the cell when channels open.
- Even a **small permeability increase** to Ca²⁺ produces a strong **depolarizing current**.
- Ca²⁺ influx triggers **neurotransmitter release** at the presynaptic terminal — a critical step in synaptic transmission.
- Intracellular Ca²⁺ concentration is kept **~20,000 times lower** than extracellular via pumps (PMCA, SERCA, Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of a typical neuron with a neat labelled diagram. Describe the function of each part in signal transmission.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the various phases of an action potential in a neuronal membrane. State the role of Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump in restoring ionic gradients.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B:**

#### Part (a) — Neuron Structure and Function

A neuron consists of the following parts:

1. **Dendrites:** Branched extensions that **receive** electrochemical signals from adjacent neurons and conduct them **toward the soma**.
2. **Soma (Cell Body):** Contains the **nucleus, Nissl bodies, and organelles**. It performs **signal integration** (sums EPSPs and IPSPs).
3. **Axon Hillock:** The **trigger zone** of the neuron. If the summed potential exceeds **−55 mV (threshold)**, an action potential is initiated here.
4. **Axon:** A long, tubular projection that **conducts** the action potential away from the soma at speeds of 0.5–120 m/s.
5. **Myelin Sheath:** Fatty insulating layer formed by **Schwann cells** (PNS) or **oligodendrocytes** (CNS). Enables **saltatory conduction**.
6. **Nodes of Ranvier:** Gaps in the myelin where **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** are concentrated. Action potential "jumps" from node to node.
7. **Axon Terminals:** Synaptic boutons that release **neurotransmitters** into the synaptic cleft upon Ca²⁺ influx.

#### Part (b) — Phases of Action Potential

**(See Section 4.3 Mermaid Diagram for visualization.)**

| Phase | Membrane Event | Ion Movement | Channel State |
|-------|----------------|--------------|---------------|
| **Resting** | $V_m = -70$ mV | K⁺ leak dominant | Na⁺/K⁺ channels closed |
| **Depolarization** | $V_m$ rises to +30 mV | Na⁺ rushes IN | Na⁺ channels OPEN |
| **Peak** | $V_m \approx +30$ to +40 mV | Na⁺ influx slows | Na⁺ channels INACTIVATE |
| **Repolarization** | $V_m$ returns to −70 mV | K⁺ flows OUT | K⁺ channels OPEN |
| **Hyperpolarization** | $V_m \approx -80$ mV | Excess K⁺ efflux | K⁺ channels slow to close |
| **Return to Rest** | $V_m = -70$ mV | Pump restores gradients | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase active |

**Role of Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase Pump:**
- An **electrogenic pump** that exports **3 Na⁺ out** and imports **2 K⁺ in** per ATP molecule hydrolyzed.
- Restores the **ionic concentration gradients** dissipated during each action potential.
- Contributes **−5 mV** directly to the resting potential (electrogenic effect).
- Accounts for **~30%** of the body's resting ATP consumption.
- Critical for **long-term neuronal excitability** — without it, neurons would cease firing after a few action potentials.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU 2024 Nervous System Questions:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Nernst Equation:** Students frequently write $\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}$ instead of the correct $\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ — this is a **1-mark error** in 3-mark questions and a **3-mark error** in 14-mark questions. **Always memorize: outside in numerator.**
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert to Body Temperature:** Using $T = 298$ K (25 °C) instead of **310 K (37 °C)** gives 58 mV instead of 61.5 mV — examiners explicitly check this.
> 3. **Mixing Up E_K and E_Na:** $E_K \approx -90$ mV, $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV. Confusing these yields a wrong sign — a fatal error.
> 4. **Omitting the Na⁺/K⁺ Pump in Action Potential Discussion:** The pump is required for **restoration**, not for the AP itself. Many students wrongly say the pump "causes" the action potential — this loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Drawing Neuron Without Labels:** In 7-mark diagram questions, **unlabelled diagrams** get **zero marks** even if the description is correct. Always label: dendrites, soma, axon hillock, axon, myelin sheath, Node of Ranvier, axon terminals.
> 6. **Wrong Unit for Valence z:** Ca²⁺ has $z = +2$, Na⁺ has $z = +1$, Cl⁻ has $z = -1$. Putting $z = 2$ for Na⁺ loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Ignoring Refractory Periods:** Always mention the **absolute refractory period** (no new AP possible) and **relative refractory period** (only strong stimulus can trigger AP) — at least 1 mark is reserved for this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nervous System (Module 1, OEICT723)**

### A. Anatomical Hierarchy
- **Nervous System** → **CNS** (Brain + Spinal Cord) + **PNS** (Somatic + Autonomic)
- **Autonomic NS** → Sympathetic (Fight/Flight) + Parasympathetic (Rest/Digest) + Enteric (GI tract)

### B. Neuron Anatomy
- **Dendrites** (input) → **Soma** (integration) → **Axon Hillock** (trigger) → **Axon** (conduction) → **Axon Terminals** (output)
- **Myelin Sheath** = insulator; **Node of Ranvier** = AP regeneration site (saltatory conduction)

### C. Membrane Potentials — MUST MEMORIZE
- **Resting Membrane Potential = −70 mV**
- **Threshold = −55 mV**
- **Peak AP = +30 to +40 mV**
- **$E_K \approx -90$ mV** (using $[K^+]_{in} = 140$, $[K^+]_{out} = 4.5$ mM)
- **$E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV** (using $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$, $[Na^+]_{in} = 15$ mM)

### D. Key Equations
- **Nernst:** $E = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ mV (at 37 °C)
- **GHK:** Includes permeability coefficients $P_K$, $P_{Na}$, $P_{Cl}$ for multi-ion case
- **Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Stoichiometry:** 3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in per ATP (electrogenic)
- **Cable Equation:** Governs passive signal spread in dendrites/axons (involves $R_m$, $C_m$, $\tau$, $\lambda$)

### E. Action Potential Phases (5-Phrase Mnemonic: **"Do Peek ReHy Re"**)
- **D**epolarization → **P**eak → **R**epolarization → **H**yperpolarization → **R**estoration
- **Na⁺ IN** during depolarization; **K⁺ OUT** during repolarization
- **Refractory periods:** Absolute (no AP) → Relative (needs strong stimulus)

### F. Synaptic Transmission Steps
- AP arrives → **Ca²⁺ influx** → **vesicle fusion** → **neurotransmitter release** → **receptor binding** → **EPSP/IPSP** in postsynaptic cell

### G. Engineering Connections
- **EEG** = brain electrical activity (CNS)
- **EMG** = muscle electrical activity (somatic NS)
- **ENG** = nerve conduction velocity (PNS)
- **ECG** = cardiac ANS activity
- **Nernst Equation** → pH meters, ion-selective electrodes, iontophoretic drug delivery

### H. Top 5 Numerical Values to Memorize
1. $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
2. $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
3. $T = 310$ K (body temperature)
4. **61.5 mV** (Nernst constant at body temperature)
5. **−70 mV** (resting membrane potential)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Muscular System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Muscular System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Muscular System** is a complex biological organ system composed of specialized contractile tissues (muscle fibers) that generate force, produce movement, and maintain posture. In the context of **Biomedical Instrumentation (OEICT723)**, the muscular system is studied as an **electrophysiological signal source** — muscles generate bioelectric potentials (Action Potentials / MUAPs) that can be measured, recorded, and analyzed using instruments such as the **Electromyograph (EMG)**.

From an engineering perspective, the muscle acts as a **biological transducer**, converting chemical energy (ATP hydrolysis) into mechanical work and electrical signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEICT723 – Module 1):**
> The muscular system is taught as a *signal source* for EMG instrumentation. Students must know the physiology (to understand origin of the signal) AND the electrical properties (to design the amplifier/filter chain).

## 1.2 Classification of Muscle Tissue

The human body contains **three distinct types of muscle tissue**, each with unique structural and functional characteristics:

| Type | Location | Striations | Control | Function |
|------|----------|------------|---------|----------|
| **Skeletal (Striated, Voluntary)** | Attached to bones | Yes (striated) | Voluntary (Somatic NS) | Body movement, posture |
| **Smooth (Non-striated, Visceral)** | Walls of hollow organs (GI tract, blood vessels, bladder) | No (smooth) | Involuntary (Autonomic NS) | Visceral movement, peristalsis |
| **Cardiac (Striated, Involuntary)** | Heart (Myocardium) | Yes (striated) | Involuntary (Autonomic NS) | Pumping blood |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biological Motor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box: The Muscle as a Microscopic Motor**
> Think of each muscle fiber as a tiny **electric motor** in a robot:
> - The **nerve impulse** is the *electrical control signal* sent to the motor.
> - **ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate)** is the *battery fuel* that powers contraction.
> - **Actin and Myosin filaments** are the *gears and shafts* that convert chemical energy into mechanical pulling force.
> - The **Action Potential** sweeping across the sarcolemma is the *back-EMF* that an engineer can tap to measure the motor's activity.
> - A **Motor Unit** is one motor + all the gears it drives — measure any one of them, and you know the motor's output.

## 1.4 Why Biomedical Engineers Study the Muscular System

The muscular system is the **primary source of EMG (Electromyography) signals**. Any disorder affecting muscles (e.g., *Myasthenia Gravis*, *Muscular Dystrophy*, *Peripheral Neuropathies*) alters the electrical signature of the muscle. Therefore:

1. **Diagnostic Value** — EMG helps neurologists detect neuromuscular disorders.
2. **Prosthetic Control** — Myoelectric signals from residual muscles control artificial limbs.
3. **Rehabilitation Engineering** — Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) uses electrical pulses to contract paralyzed muscles.
4. **Ergonomics & Sports Science** — Surface EMG (sEMG) measures muscle fatigue and activation patterns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:**
> - Skeletal muscle conduction velocity ≈ **2–6 m/s**
> - Normal EMG signal amplitude: **50 µV – 5 mV**
> - EMG signal frequency content: **20 Hz – 500 Hz** (dominant: 80–150 Hz)
> - Resting membrane potential of muscle fiber: **-90 mV** (interior negative)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Skeletal Muscle Fiber

A **skeletal muscle** is a hierarchical structure. From macroscopic to microscopic:

**Muscle → Fasciculus → Muscle Fiber (Cell) → Myofibril → Sarcomere → Myofilaments (Actin & Myosin)**

### 2.1.1 The Sarcomere — The Contractile Unit

The **sarcomere** is the functional contractile unit of muscle, bounded by two **Z-discs (Z-lines)**. It contains two key filaments:

- **Thick filaments** — composed of **Myosin** (with cross-bridges / heads).
- **Thin filaments** — composed of **Actin**, **Tropomyosin**, and **Troponin**.

The **Sliding Filament Theory (Huxley, 1954)** states that muscle contraction occurs when the thin filaments **slide past** the thick filaments, shortening the sarcomere without either filament changing length.

### 2.1.2 Sliding Filament Theory — Step-by-Step

1. **Resting State:** Tropomyosin blocks the myosin-binding sites on actin. Intracellular Ca²⁺ is low (~10⁻⁷ M).
2. **Neural Stimulation:** A motor neuron delivers an Action Potential at the **Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ)**, releasing **Acetylcholine (ACh)**.
3. **Excitation-Contraction Coupling:** ACh binds to nicotinic receptors on the motor end-plate, depolarizing the sarcolemma. The depolarization propagates down the **T-tubules** and triggers the **Sarcoplasmic Reticulum (SR)** to release Ca²⁺.
4. **Cross-Bridge Formation:** Ca²⁺ binds to **Troponin C**, causing a conformational change that moves Tropomyosin, exposing the myosin-binding sites on Actin. Myosin heads (already loaded with ADP + Pi) bind to Actin forming a **cross-bridge**.
5. **Power Stroke:** Pi is released, the myosin head pivots (~45°), pulling the actin filament toward the M-line (this is the **power stroke**). ADP is then released.
6. **Cross-Bridge Detachment:** A new ATP molecule binds to the myosin head, causing it to detach from actin.
7. **Re-cocking:** ATP is hydrolyzed to ADP + Pi, re-energizing the myosin head, ready for the next cycle.
8. **Relaxation:** Ca²⁺ is actively pumped back into the SR by the **SERCA pump**, tropomyosin re-covers the binding sites, and the muscle relaxes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note — Rigor Mortis:** After death, ATP production ceases. Myosin heads cannot detach from actin, causing permanent stiffness. This is why forensic pathologists use rigor to estimate time of death.

## 2.2 The Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ)

The NMJ is the **synapse** between a somatic motor neuron and a skeletal muscle fiber. It is the precise anatomical site where electrical-to-chemical-to-electrical signal conversion occurs.

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Motor Neuron Terminal** | Releases ACh into the synaptic cleft |
| **Synaptic Cleft** | ~20–40 nm gap filled with ACh-esterase enzyme |
| **Motor End-Plate** | Specialized region on the sarcolemma with junctional folds and nicotinic ACh receptors |
| **Acetylcholine (ACh)** | Excitatory neurotransmitter |
| **Acetylcholinesterase (AChE)** | Enzyme that rapidly hydrolyzes ACh to terminate the signal (~1 ms) |

**Sequence:** Action Potential → Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels open → Ca²⁺ influx → Synaptic vesicles fuse with presynaptic membrane → ACh released → Diffuses across cleft → Binds postsynaptic receptors → End-Plate Potential (EPP) → Triggers Muscle Action Potential.

## 2.3 The Motor Unit

The **Motor Unit (MU)** is the *fundamental functional unit* of the neuromuscular system and is the **single most important concept for EMG signal interpretation**.

A Motor Unit = **One Alpha Motor Neuron + All the muscle fibers it innervates**.

### 2.3.1 Motor Unit Types

| Type | Twitch Speed | Fatigue Resistance | Size (fibers/neuron) | EMG Signature |
|------|--------------|-------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Type I (Slow Oxidative / SO)** | Slow | High | Small (3–10) | Small, sustained amplitude |
| **Type IIa (Fast Oxidative-Glycolytic / FOG)** | Fast | Moderate | Medium | Moderate amplitude |
| **Type IIb/IIx (Fast Glycolytic / FG)** | Fast | Low | Large (100–1000) | Large, brief amplitude |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The *size principle* (Henneman) — motor units are recruited from smallest (Type I) to largest (Type II) as force demand increases. This is the physiological basis of the *graded EMG signal amplitude* observed during increasing voluntary contraction.

## 2.4 Electrical Activity of Muscle — The Electromyogram (EMG)

### 2.4.1 Origin of the EMG Signal

When a Motor Unit fires:
1. The motor neuron's action potential propagates down its axon to the NMJ.
2. All muscle fibers in that motor unit depolarize nearly simultaneously.
3. The summed extracellular potentials from these fibers produce the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**.

The **MUAP** is the fundamental waveform observed in clinical EMG, with typical duration of **3–15 ms** and amplitude of **100 µV – 2 mV** (needle electrode).

### 2.4.2 EMG Signal Classification

| Signal Type | Source | Amplitude | Use |
|-------------|--------|-----------|-----|
| **Single Fiber EMG (SFEMG)** | Single muscle fiber | ~1 mV | Neuromuscular junction disorders |
| **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** | One motor unit | 100 µV – 2 mV | Standard clinical EMG |
| **Interference Pattern** | Many active MUs | 2 – 10 mV | High-force voluntary contraction |
| **Surface EMG (sEMG)** | Multiple superficial MUs | 50 µV – 5 mV | Prosthetics, ergonomics |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FORMULA CHEAT SHEET — KTU Module 1: Muscular System**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Nernst Equation** (Ion equilibrium potential) | $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}$$ | Volts; $R$=8.314 J/mol·K, $F$=96485 C/mol |
| 2 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage** | $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$ | Resting Vm of muscle ≈ -90 mV |
| 3 | **Cable Equation** (Signal propagation in fiber) | $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = r_m \cdot c_m \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + r_m \cdot i_i$$ | Foundation of EMG volume conduction |
| 4 | **Conduction Velocity** | $$v = \frac{d}{\Delta t}$$ | Skeletal muscle: 2–6 m/s |
| 5 | **EMG RMS Amplitude** (force estimation) | $$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$$ | Correlates with muscle force |
| 6 | **EMG-Frequency Median** (fatigue index) | $$f_{med} : \int_0^{f_{med}} PSD(f)\,df = \int_{f_{med}}^{\infty} PSD(f)\,df$$ | Shifts to lower frequencies during fatigue |
| 7 | **Energy Liberation (Hill's Equation)** | $$E = E_0 + a\frac{(v+b)(V_{max}-v)}{V_{max}}$$ | v = shortening velocity |
| 8 | **Acetylcholine Equilibrium Potential** | $$E_{ACh} = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{[Na^+]_o + 0.018[K^+]_o}{[Na^+]_i + 0.018[K^+]_i}$$ | ≈ 0 mV (depolarizing) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in KTU exams:** The Nernst and GHK equations are frequently asked to derive the resting membrane potential — a 7-mark favorite. Cable theory appears in short-answer questions on signal propagation.

## 2.6 Resting Membrane Potential of Skeletal Muscle (Derivation-Style Explanation)

The resting potential of **-90 mV** in skeletal muscle arises from:
- High intracellular K⁺ (~150 mM) vs. low extracellular K⁺ (~4 mM)
- High extracellular Na⁺ (~145 mM) vs. low intracellular Na⁺ (~12 mM)
- Membrane permeability: P_K ≫ P_Na ≫ P_Cl at rest

The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (active transport) maintains these gradients by pumping 3 Na⁺ out for every 2 K⁺ in (electrogenic — contributes ~-4 mV).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | How Muscular System Knowledge is Used |
|-------------|---------------------------------------|
| **EMG-based Prosthetic Limbs** | sEMG signals from residual limb muscles decode intent (e.g., hand open/close) using machine learning classifiers |
| **Rehabilitation Robotics** | FES devices apply controlled electrical pulses to paralyzed muscles to restore function (e.g., foot drop in stroke patients) |
| **Athletic Performance Monitoring** | Wireless sEMG patches monitor muscle activation patterns and fatigue in real-time |
| **Ergonomic Design** | sEMG guides the design of chairs, keyboards, and tools to minimize muscle strain |
| **Diagnosis of Neuropathies** | Nerve Conduction Studies (NCS) combined with EMG localize lesions in the motor pathway |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resting Membrane Potential via the Nernst Equation (Goldman Approximation)

The **Nernst equation** gives the equilibrium potential of a single ion across a membrane. At equilibrium, the electrical force balances the chemical diffusion force.

### Step 1: Define the Electrochemical Driving Force

Consider a single ionic species, say K⁺, at concentrations $[K^+]_i$ inside and $[K^+]_o$ outside the membrane. The Gibbs free energy change for moving one mole of ions from outside to inside has two components:

- **Chemical work** (concentration gradient): $\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln \frac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o}$
- **Electrical work** (potential difference): $\Delta G_{elec} = zFV_m$ where $z$ is the ion valence, $F$ is Faraday's constant, and $V_m$ is the membrane potential (inside relative to outside).

### Step 2: Apply the Equilibrium Condition

At electrochemical equilibrium, $\Delta G_{total} = 0$:

$$RT \ln \frac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o} + zFV_m = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for $V_m$

Rearranging:

$$V_m = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}$$

> **Conversion logic:** We inverted the ratio inside the logarithm and flipped the sign outside, since $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$.

### Step 4: Numerical Evaluation for K⁺ at 37°C (310 K)

Using the constant $\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} \approx 0.0267$ V:

$$E_K = 0.0267 \times \ln \frac{4}{150} = 0.0267 \times (-3.614) \approx -0.0965 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -96.5 \text{ mV}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** $\ln(4/150) = \ln(0.02667) \approx -3.614$. Multiply by 0.0267 V to get -0.0965 V, which is close to the measured resting potential of -90 mV (the small difference is because other ions contribute, as GHK accounts for).

### Step 5: Calculate $E_{Na}$ Similarly

$$E_{Na} = 0.0267 \times \ln \frac{145}{12} = 0.0267 \times (2.495) \approx +0.0666 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{Na} \approx +66.6 \text{ mV}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** This positive value reflects that Na⁺ is far from equilibrium at rest — this driving force is what causes rapid depolarization during an action potential.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

The Nernst equation assumes only one ion is permeable. Real membranes are permeable to multiple ions. The **GHK equation** computes a weighted-average resting potential:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$

### Step 1: Identify the Permeability Ratios

For skeletal muscle at rest: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.01 : 0.3$ (relative).

### Step 2: Substitute Concentrations (mM)

- $[K^+]_o = 4$, $[K^+]_i = 150$
- $[Na^+]_o = 145$, $[Na^+]_i = 12$
- $[Cl^-]_i = 4$, $[Cl^-]_o = 123$

### Step 3: Compute the Numerator (P·[ion]_out for cations, P·[ion]_in for Cl⁻)

Using the rule: cations (K⁺, Na⁺) use OUTSIDE concentrations; anions (Cl⁻) use INSIDE concentration.

$$\text{Num} = (1)(4) + (0.01)(145) + (0.3)(4)$$
$$\text{Num} = 4 + 1.45 + 1.2 = 6.65$$

### Step 4: Compute the Denominator (P·[ion]_in for cations, P·[ion]_out for Cl⁻)

$$\text{Den} = (1)(150) + (0.01)(12) + (0.3)(123)$$
$$\text{Den} = 150 + 0.12 + 36.9 = 187.02$$

### Step 5: Apply the Logarithm and Multiply by $RT/F$

$$V_m = 0.0267 \times \ln \frac{6.65}{187.02} = 0.0267 \times \ln(0.03555)$$

$$V_m = 0.0267 \times (-3.338) \approx -0.0891 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_m \approx -89.1 \text{ mV}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** This matches the experimentally measured resting potential of -90 mV almost exactly. The high K⁺ permeability dominates the equation, pulling the resting potential close to $E_K = -96$ mV.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: EMG Conduction Velocity from Recorded Signals

A common KTU numerical problem provides two EMG traces recorded by electrodes spaced a known distance apart and asks for **muscle fiber conduction velocity**.

### Step 1: Identify the Time Delay

From the recorded signals, measure the time difference ($\Delta t$) between the same characteristic peak (e.g., the main positive deflection) on the two channels.

### Step 2: Apply the Conduction Velocity Formula

$$v = \frac{d}{\Delta t}$$

where $d$ = inter-electrode distance (in meters), $\Delta t$ = time delay (in seconds).

### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Two surface EMG electrodes are placed **10 cm** apart along a muscle fiber. The recorded MUAP peaks appear at $t_1 = 3.5$ ms and $t_2 = 5.7$ ms. Calculate the conduction velocity.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta t = 5.7 - 3.5 = 2.2 \text{ ms} = 2.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

$$d = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$$

$$v = \frac{0.1 \text{ m}}{2.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 45.45 \text{ m/s}}$$
