## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Differential Amplifier Output and CMRR for Bipolar Configuration

**Given:**
- Two active electrodes with potentials $V_1(t)$ and $V_2(t)$.
- Each potential contains a bioelectric component plus a common-mode noise:

$$V_1 = S_1(t) + N_{cm}$$
$$V_2 = S_2(t) + N_{cm}$$

- The instrumentation amplifier has differential gain $A_d$ and common-mode gain $A_c$ (ideally $A_c = 0$).

**Derivation Steps:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_o &= A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_c \left( \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \right) &&\text{(Standard difference-amp equation)} \\[6pt]
V_o &= A_d \big[(S_1 + N_{cm}) - (S_2 + N_{cm})\big] + A_c \left[ \frac{(S_1 + N_{cm}) + (S_2 + N_{cm})}{2} \right] &&\text{(Substitute } V_1, V_2)\\[6pt]
V_o &= A_d (S_1 - S_2) + A_d (N_{cm} - N_{cm}) + A_c \left( \frac{S_1 + S_2}{2} + N_{cm} \right) &&\text{(Expand brackets)} \\[6pt]
V_o &= A_d (S_1 - S_2) + 0 + A_c \left( \frac{S_1 + S_2}{2} + N_{cm} \right) &&\text{(Common-mode cancels in first term)} \\[6pt]
V_o &= A_d (S_1 - S_2) + \frac{A_c}{2}(S_1 + S_2) + A_c N_{cm} &&\text{(Re-distribute)} \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}$$

**Analysis of the three terms:**
1. **$A_d (S_1 - S_2)$** — the *desired* bioelectric signal, amplified by the differential gain.
2. **$\frac{A_c}{2}(S_1 + S_2)$** — leakage of the common-mode *bioelectric* component (e.g., far-field heart activity in EEG). Ideally this should be zero, which requires $A_c \to 0$.
3. **$A_c N_{cm}$** — the residual mains interference. This is the term that *must* be minimized.

**Final Design Criterion:**

$$\text{For } N_{cm} \text{ to be negligible: } A_c N_{cm} \ll A_d (S_1 - S_2)$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{A_d}{A_c} \gg \frac{N_{cm}}{S_1 - S_2}$$

This ratio $A_d / A_c$ is defined as the **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**. In decibels:

$$\boxed{\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Benchmark for KTU:** A clinical-quality ECG amplifier has **CMRR ≥ 100 dB**, meaning $A_d / A_c \geq 10^5$. A 1 V 50 Hz interference would appear as only **10 µV** at the output — safely below the ECG's intrinsic amplitude.

### 3.2 Derivation: Motion-Artifact Voltage from Interface Impedance Variation

**Given:** A patient moves, causing the skin-electrode impedance to change by $\Delta Z$ in a short time $\Delta t$. The amplifier's bias current $I_b$ flows continuously through this interface.

**Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{artifact}}(t) &= I_b \cdot Z(t) &&\text{(Ohm's law at the electrode-skin node)} \\[6pt]
\Delta V_{\text{artifact}} &= I_b \cdot \Delta Z &&\text{(Change in voltage due to change in impedance)} \\[6pt]
\Delta V_{\text{artifact}} &= I_b \cdot \frac{\partial Z}{\partial t} \cdot \Delta t &&\text{(Express in differential form)} \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting the parallel-RC skin model $Z = \dfrac{R_s}{1 + j 2 \pi f R_s C_s}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial t} &= \frac{\partial Z}{\partial R_s} \cdot \frac{\partial R_s}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial Z}{\partial C_s} \cdot \frac{\partial C_s}{\partial t} &&\text{(Total time-derivative)} \\[6pt]
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial R_s} &= \frac{1}{(1 + j 2 \pi f R_s C_s)^2} &&\text{(Chain rule on R_s)} \\[6pt]
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial C_s} &= \frac{-j 2 \pi f R_s^{\,2}}{(1 + j 2 \pi f R_s C_s)^2} &&\text{(Chain rule on C_s)} \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{\text{artifact}} = I_b \cdot \frac{ \dfrac{\partial R_s}{\partial t} - j 2 \pi f R_s^{\,2} \dfrac{\partial C_s}{\partial t} }{ (1 + j 2 \pi f R_s C_s)^2 } \cdot \Delta t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Take-Away:** The artifact grows when (a) the bias current $I_b$ is large, (b) the skin resistance is high (poor prep), or (c) the motion is fast (high $\partial R_s / \partial t$). The KTU-design lesson: **use FET-input amplifiers (low $I_b$) and abrade the skin (lower $R_s$) before electrode placement**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Common-Mode Rejection in a Monopolar Configuration

A true monopolar system uses a single active site and a single reference site. The reference is *not* on the body, or is on a "quiet" site. The CMRR degrades because the source impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ are unequal.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out,monopolar}} &= A_d \big[ S_1 - S_2 \big] + A_c \left[ \frac{S_1 + S_2}{2} \right] + I_b (Z_1 - Z_2) &&\text{(Including electrode impedance mismatch)} \\[6pt]
\text{Effective CMRR}_{\text{monopolar}} &= \frac{A_d}{A_c + I_b (Z_1 - Z_2) / N_{cm}} &&\text{(Reduced by source-impedance imbalance)} \\[6pt]
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Result:** If $Z_1 \neq Z_2$ (which is *always* true in practice), even a perfect differential amplifier will let some common-mode noise through. The bipolar configuration *minimises* this because the two active electrodes are close together and have nearly identical impedances.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Sources of Motion Artifacts in a Bioelectric Recording Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeSrc1[HEART OR BRAIN OR MUSCLE BIOELECTRIC SOURCE] --> nodeAmp1[BIOELECTRIC SIGNAL 1 MV TO 100 UV]
    nodeSkin[SKIN ELECTRODE INTERFACE ZS RC PARALLEL] --> nodeAmp1
    nodeMove[PATIENT MOVEMENT ELECTRODE SLIP CABLE SWAY] --> nodeSkin
    nodeMove --> nodeCable
    nodeCable[LEAD WIRE CABLE TRIBoeLECTRIC EFFECT] --> nodeAmp2
    nodePersp[PERSPIRATION ELECTROLYTE CHANGE] --> nodeSkin
    nodeAmp1 --> nodeAmp2[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER HIGH CMRR]
    nodeAmp2 --> nodeAmp3[FILTER AND ADC STAGE]
    nodeAmp3 --> nodeOut[RECORDED SIGNAL CONTAMINATED]
    nodePSU[POWER LINE 50 HZ 60 HZ] -.-> nodeAmp2
    nodePSU -.-> nodeCable
```

**Figure 4.1 — Functional Block Diagram of Motion-Artifact Generation and Propagation.**
*Each dashed red line represents an injection path of an unwanted signal. The student should note that motion acts at the skin-electrode interface, not at the amplifier; therefore the fix must be largely at the front-end.*

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Monopolar vs Bipolar Electrode Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph nodeMonoBlock[MONOPOLAR UNIPOLAR CONFIGURATION]
        nodeM1[ACTIVE ELECTRODE OVER SOURCE] --> nodeM2[IN AMP POSITIVE INPUT]
        nodeM3[REFERENCE ELECTRODE ON QUIET SITE] --> nodeM4[IN AMP NEGATIVE INPUT]
        nodeM5[GROUND ELECTRODE] --> nodeM6[AMPLIFIER CHASSIS GROUND]
    end
    
    subgraph nodeBiBlock[BIPOLAR DIFFERENTIAL CONFIGURATION]
        nodeB1[ACTIVE ELECTRODE 1 OVER SITE A] --> nodeB2[IN AMP POSITIVE INPUT]
        nodeB3[ACTIVE ELECTRODE 2 OVER SITE B] --> nodeB4[IN AMP NEGATIVE INPUT]
        nodeB5[GROUND OR DRIVEN RIGHT LEG] --> nodeB6[AMPLIFIER CHASSIS GROUND]
    end
```

**Figure 4.2 — Side-by-Side Functional Comparison.**
*In the monopolar block, only one active electrode carries the bioelectric information; in the bipolar block, both electrodes are equally active and the difference between them is amplified. The ground electrode is mandatory in both for safety and bias-current return.*

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Full Bioelectric Recording Chain with Motion-Artifact Mitigation Points

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodePat[PATIENT BIOELECTRIC SOURCE] --> nodeSkin1[ELECTRODE GEL SKIN INTERFACE]
    nodeSkin1 --> nodeCable1[SHIELDED LEAD WIRE FIXED WITH CLIPS]
    nodeCable1 --> nodePreamp[PREAMP HIGH Z FET INPUT]
    nodePreamp --> nodeInsAmp[INSTRUMENTATION AMP CMRR 100 DB]
    nodeInsAmp --> nodeFilter[BANDPASS FILTER 0.5 HZ TO 40 HZ FOR ECG]
    nodeFilter --> nodeADC[ADC AND DISPLAY]
    nodeDRL[DRIVEN RIGHT LEG FEEDBACK] -.-> nodePat
    
    nodeMit1[SKIN PREP ABRASION ALCOHOL] -.-> nodeSkin1
    nodeMit2[ADHESIVE ELECTRODE HOOK AND LOOP STRAP] -.-> nodeSkin1
    nodeMit3[SHORT TWISTED SHIELDED CABLE] -.-> nodeCable1
    nodeMit4[ADAPTIVE 50 HZ NOTCH FILTER] -.-> nodeInsAmp
```

**Figure 4.3 — Mitigation Strategy Map.**
*Each blue dashed box represents an engineering intervention that reduces motion artifacts at that specific node. KTU students are expected to map mitigations to sources in any 7-mark question.*

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define motion artifact. List any four sources of motion artifacts in bioelectric signal recording.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Motion artifact is a low-frequency spurious voltage (0.1 Hz – 10 Hz) generated by mechanical disturbance at the electrode-skin interface, lead wire, or patient body.
- **Any four sources (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
  1. Electrode slip or loose attachment on the skin.
  2. Patient body movement (respiration, limb motion).
  3. Cable sway causing triboelectric charge redistribution.
  4. Skin stretching under the electrode changing skin impedance.
  5. Perspiration altering the electrolyte concentration and half-cell potential.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between monopolar and bipolar electrode configurations. (Any 4 points)*

**Model Answer (4 × 0.75 ≈ 3 marks):**

| Sl. | Monopolar | Bipolar |
|-----|-----------|---------|
| 1 | One active + one reference electrode. | Two active electrodes + one ground. |
| 2 | Picks up broader bioelectric activity. | Picks up the *difference* between two sites. |
| 3 | Lower inherent CMRR (more noise). | Higher CMRR (better noise rejection). |
| 4 | Used in EEG with linked-ear reference. | Used in ECG limb leads, EMG, clinical EEG montages. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the various recording problems encountered during bioelectric signal recording. Classify them with typical frequency ranges and examples.**

**Model Solution:**

Bioelectric signal recording is affected by the following categories of problems:

**1. Inherent Electronic Noise (1.5 marks)**
- *Thermal (Johnson) noise* in the electrode and amplifier input resistance.
  $$V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$$
  with $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T$ in K, $R$ in Ω, $\Delta f$ in Hz.
- *Shot noise* in semiconductor junctions due to discrete charge carrier flow.
- *Flicker (1/f) noise* dominant below ~10 Hz — overlaps with the EEG / ECG band.

**2. Environmental Interference (1.5 marks)**
- *Power-line interference* at **50 Hz / 60 Hz** (mains hum).
- *Radio-frequency interference* from mobile phones, diathermy, fluorescent lamps.
- *Capacitive and inductive pickup* from nearby cables and transformers.

**3. Motion Artifacts (1.5 marks)**
- Frequency range **0.1 Hz – 10 Hz**, amplitude up to several mV.
- Sources: electrode slip, cable sway, respiration, perspiration.
- Mitigation: skin abrasion, adhesive electrodes, cable fixation, adaptive filters.

**4. Electrode-Skin Interface Issues (1.5 marks)**
- Half-cell potential drift.
- Polarization at the gel interface.
- Skin-electrode impedance $Z_s$ in series with the source.

**5. Physiological Crosstalk and Baseline Wander (1 mark)**
- EMG contamination of ECG, eye-blink artifact in EEG.
- Respiration-induced baseline wander (typically < 0.5 Hz).

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the phenomenon of motion artifacts in detail. Derive the expression for motion-induced voltage and discuss at least four mitigation techniques.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Motion artifact is the unwanted voltage change at the amplifier input caused by mechanical disturbance of the electrode-skin interface, the lead wire, or the patient.

**Derivation of Motion-Induced Voltage (3 marks):**

A change in the skin-electrode impedance $\Delta Z$ combined with the amplifier's input bias current $I_b$ produces an artifact voltage:

$$\Delta V_{\text{artifact}} = I_b \cdot \Delta Z_s$$

For a parallel-RC skin model $Z_s = R_s / (1 + j 2\pi f R_s C_s)$, the change in $Z_s$ due to motion is:

$$\Delta Z_s = \frac{ \dfrac{\partial R_s}{\partial t} - j 2\pi f R_s^2 \dfrac{\partial C_s}{\partial t} }{ (1 + j 2\pi f R_s C_s)^2 } \Delta t$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{\text{artifact}} = I_b \cdot \frac{ \dfrac{\partial R_s}{\partial t} - j 2\pi f R_s^2 \dfrac{\partial C_s}{\partial t} }{ (1 + j 2\pi f R_s C_s)^2 } \cdot \Delta t}$$

**Mitigation Techniques (3 marks — any four):**

1. **Skin preparation:** clean with alcohol, mild abrasion to lower $R_s$ and reduce $\partial R_s / \partial t$.
2. **Aggressive adhesion:** use solid-gel adhesive electrodes, athletic tape, or elastic straps to minimise slip.
3. **Use of active electrodes:** preamplifier placed *at* the electrode site eliminates cable-triboelectric pickup and lowers $I_b$ contribution.
4. **Cable management:** short, twisted, shielded leads, mechanically clamped to the patient.
5. **Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit:** actively drives the body to amplifier common-mode potential, reducing the common-mode voltage that motion modulates.
6. **Adaptive filtering:** real-time LMS or RLS algorithm tracks the artifact's low-frequency drift and subtracts it digitally.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks] With the help of neat block diagrams, explain monopolar and bipolar electrode configurations used in bioelectric recording. Compare their merits and demerits.**

**Model Solution:**

**Monopolar (Unipolar) Configuration (3 marks):**
A single *active* electrode is placed on the source region (e.g., a scalp location for EEG). A *reference* electrode is placed on an electrically neutral site (linked ears, mastoid, or bony prominence). The amplifier measures the potential of the active site with respect to this reference.

*Block flow:*
`Active Electrode → IN-AMP (+) input`
`Reference Electrode → IN-AMP (–) input`
`Ground Electrode → Chassis ground`

**Bipolar Configuration (3 marks):**
Two *active* electrodes are placed at two points of interest. A third *ground* electrode provides a safety return path and a reference for the driven-right-leg circuit. The IN-AMP measures $V_1 - V_2$.

*Block flow:*
`Active 1 → IN-AMP (+) input`
`Active 2 → IN-AMP (–) input`
`Ground → Driven Right Leg Driver → Body`

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Monopolar | Bipolar |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| Common-mode rejection | Lower | **Higher** (CMRR > 100 dB achievable) |
| Spatial resolution | Broader | Localized |
| Susceptibility to motion | Higher | Lower |
| Circuit complexity | Simpler | Slightly more complex |
| Typical use | EEG referential, EOG | ECG limb leads, EMG, clinical EEG montages |

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the output of a differential amplifier used in a bipolar bioelectric recording. Define CMRR and state its typical KTU-required value for a clinical ECG amplifier.**

**Model Solution:**

**Output Equation (4 marks):**
For a differential amplifier with differential gain $A_d$ and common-mode gain $A_c$:

$$V_o = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_c \left( \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \right)$$

With $V_1 = S_1 + N_{cm}$ and $V_2 = S_2 + N_{cm}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_o &= A_d \big[(S_1 + N_{cm}) - (S_2 + N_{cm})\big] + A_c \left( \frac{S_1 + S_2 + 2 N_{cm}}{2} \right) \\[4pt]
&= A_d (S_1 - S_2) + A_c \left( \frac{S_1 + S_2}{2} + N_{cm} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Since the two electrodes see the same $N_{cm}$, the first term cancels the common-mode noise. The residual interference is governed by $A_c$.

**CMRR Definition (2 marks):**

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_c} \quad \text{(linear ratio)}$$

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right)$$

**KTU Required Value (1 mark):** A clinical ECG amplifier must have **CMRR ≥ 100 dB** (i.e., $A_d/A_c \geq 10^5$) at 50 Hz to ensure that a 1 V mains interference appears as ≤ 10 µV at the output.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "monopolar" with "unipolar"** — both terms are accepted, but mixing "monopole" (a physics term) will lose marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the ground electrode in bipolar diagrams** — every block diagram in the exam must show the third (ground/DRL) electrode, or 1 mark is deducted.
> 3. **Writing $CMRR = 20 \log(A_d)$ instead of $20 \log(A_d / A_c)$** — this is the single most-common formula error in KTU papers. Always include $A_c$ in the denominator.
> 4. **Stating the motion-artifact frequency range as "DC to 50 Hz"** — be precise. The accepted KTU answer is **0.1 Hz to 10 Hz**.
> 5. **Mixing units in the artifact equation** — $I_b$ is in amperes and $Z_s$ in ohms; the artifact voltage is in volts. Many students forget the unit and lose 0.5 marks.
> 6. **Omitting the role of the Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit** — in any 14-mark question on noise rejection, a 2-mark sub-part specifically asks about the third electrode. Always mention DRL.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read this just before entering the exam hall:**

- **Recording problems** = noise + interference + motion artifacts + electrode interface issues + physiological crosstalk.
- **Motion artifact frequency band:** **0.1 Hz – 10 Hz** (low frequency, hard to filter digitally without distorting the baseline).
- **Motion artifact amplitude:** up to several mV — can swamp ECG (1 mV) and EEG (10–100 µV).
- **Primary sources:** electrode slip, cable sway (triboelectric), skin stretching, perspiration, respiration.
- **Mitigation acronym — "SCAN DA":** **S**kin prep, **C**able fixation, **A**dhesive electrodes, **N**otch filter, **D**riven Right Leg, **A**daptive LMS filter.
- **Monopolar config:** 1 active + 1 reference + 1 ground → broad pickup, less noise rejection.
- **Bipolar config:** 2 active + 1 ground → localized pickup, **high CMRR**.
- **Differential amplifier output:** $V_o = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_c \left( \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \right)$.
- **CMRR definition:** $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} (A_d / A_c)$.
- **Clinical ECG CMRR requirement:** **≥ 100 dB** at 50 Hz.
- **Electrode-skin interface model:** parallel RC with $R_s = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ to $500 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_s = 10 \text{ nF}$ to $1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$.
- **Impedance matching rule:** $Z_{\text{input,amp}} \gg Z_{\text{source,electrode}}$ (use FET-input IN-AMP with $Z_{\text{in}} \geq 10^{10} \ \Omega$).
- **Artifact voltage equation:** $\Delta V_{\text{artifact}} = I_b \cdot \Delta Z_s$ — minimised by lowering $I_b$ and $\Delta Z_s$.
- **Half-cell potential of Ag/AgCl:** ≈ +0.22 V (DC, cancelled by the differential amplifier).
- **Nernst equation:** $E_{hc} = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln(a_{\text{ion}})$ — explains why electrolyte concentration changes cause drift.
- **Always draw a third electrode (ground/DRL)** in KTU circuit diagrams, or marks are deducted.
- **Triboelectric noise** in cables → use **shielded, twisted, short** leads; fix mechanically with clips/tape.
- **Driven Right Leg (DRL)** actively drives the body to a low common-mode voltage, drastically reducing 50 Hz interference.
- **For KTU 14-mark answers:** always combine *block diagram + derivation + comparison table + mitigation list* for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Bio-Amplifier - Differential Bio-Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Need for Bio-Amplifier - Differential Bio-Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Bio-Amplifier?

A **Bio-Amplifier** (also called a biopotential amplifier) is a specialized electronic amplifier designed to extract, amplify, and process the very weak bioelectric signals generated by the human body (such as ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG) while rejecting the much larger noise and interference signals that accompany them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A bio-amplifier is a low-noise, high-gain, high-input-impedance differential amplifier used to condition biopotential signals for further processing, analysis, recording, or display, with built-in safety isolation between the patient and the measurement system.

### 1.2 Why Do We Need a Bio-Amplifier? — The Core Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Biopotential signals are characterized by **extremely small amplitudes (typically 0.1 mV to 5 mV)**, **very high source impedances (kΩ to MΩ range)**, and a **highly noisy electromagnetic environment (50/60 Hz power-line interference, RF noise, motion artefacts)**. A general-purpose amplifier cannot faithfully recover them. Hence, a dedicated bio-amplifier is mandatory.

The major reasons for needing a bio-amplifier are summarized below:

| # | Reason | Explanation |
|---|--------|-------------|
| 1 | **Signal Magnitude** | Bio-signals are in microvolts to millivolts. They must be raised to volts (0–5 V) to drive A/D converters, displays, or recorders. |
| 2 | **Source Impedance Matching** | The body (electrode-tissue interface) presents a high and variable source impedance. A high-input-impedance amplifier avoids signal attenuation. |
| 3 | **Noise Rejection (CMRR)** | Power-line interference (50/60 Hz) is a *common-mode* signal on both electrode leads. A differential amplifier with high CMRR rejects it. |
| 4 | **Patient Safety (Isolation)** | A bio-amplifier incorporates **isolation barriers** (optical or transformer) to protect the patient from microshock hazards. |
| 5 | **Bandwidth Limitation** | Each biosignal occupies a known frequency band (e.g., ECG: 0.05–150 Hz). The bio-amplifier provides appropriate band-pass filtering. |
| 6 | **Impedance Balancing** | Active ground/reference electrode drives the body at common-mode potential, improving CMRR in practice. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering Conversation in a Crowded Stadium"

Imagine two friends trying to have a conversation in a packed, noisy stadium. Their voices (the differential bio-signal) are barely audible at **microvolt levels**, while the crowd roar (power-line interference, 50/60 Hz noise) is **thousands of times louder** and equally affects both of them.

A **bio-amplifier** is like a pair of high-tech, noise-cancelling, directional microphones that:
- **Boost the whisper** (amplification, high gain),
- **Subtract the common roar** heard by both mics (differential action, high CMRR),
- **Ignore the stadium echo distortions** (bandwidth filtering),
- **Have a built-in safety glass booth** (galvanic isolation) protecting the friends from any electrical hazard.

This analogy makes it immediately clear *why* a standard amplifier is insufficient and *why* a specialized bio-amplifier is essential.

### 1.4 Differential Bio-Amplifier — Definition

A **Differential Bio-Amplifier** is a bio-amplifier that amplifies only the **difference** between two input electrode potentials while rejecting any signal that is **common to both inputs** (common-mode voltage). It is the foundational building block of every practical bio-amplifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common-Mode Voltage ($V_{cm}$):** A signal that appears identically and in phase on both inputs, e.g., 50 Hz mains pickup picked up equally by both skin electrodes.
> 
> **Differential-Mode Voltage ($V_{d}$):** The actual bioelectric signal of interest that appears as a difference between the two inputs.

### 1.5 Standard Amplitude Ranges of Common Biopotentials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table (KTU syllabus must-know) shows why amplification is critical:

| Biosignal | Typical Amplitude | Frequency Band | Source Impedance |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|------------------|
| ECG (Electrocardiogram) | **0.5 – 5 mV** | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 1 – 100 kΩ |
| EEG (Electroencephalogram) | **5 – 300 µV** | 0.1 – 100 Hz | 1 – 50 kΩ |
| EMG (Electromyogram) | **50 µV – 5 mV** | 20 – 2000 Hz | 1 – 10 kΩ |
| EOG (Electro-oculogram) | **0.1 – 1 mV** | DC – 10 Hz | 1 – 50 kΩ |
| ERG (Electroretinogram) | **10 µV – 1 mV** | 0.1 – 200 Hz | High |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Characteristics / Requirements of an Ideal Bio-Amplifier

A bio-amplifier is not a "normal" amplifier. The following requirements must be satisfied simultaneously:

1. **High Input Impedance ($Z_{in} \geq 10^{8}\ \Omega$):** Required to avoid loading the high-impedance electrode-tissue interface.
2. **High Differential Gain ($A_{d} \geq 1000$):** To amplify microvolt-level signals to volts.
3. **High Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR $\geq$ 80 dB, ideally $\geq$ 100 dB):** To reject 50/60 Hz interference and other common-mode noise.
4. **Low Noise Figure:** Input-referred noise must be below a few µV (typically < 1 µV RMS).
5. **Adjustable Gain:** Gain is selectable per biosignal (e.g., ×1000 for EEG, ×1000–×10000 for ECG).
6. **Patient Isolation:** Galvanic isolation barrier (≥ 1500 V) between patient and mains-powered circuitry.
7. **Patient Safety / Current Limiting:** Leakage current must remain < **10 µA** (Type CF — cardiac floating) per IEC 60601.
8. **Bandwidth Selection:** Bandwidth must be matched to the biopotential (HPF + LPF per biosignal).

## 2.2 The Basic Difference Amplifier (1-Op-Amp Configuration)

The simplest differential bio-amplifier uses a single op-amp with four matched resistors:

### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology

- Two inputs: $V_1$ (inverting input side) and $V_2$ (non-inverting input side).
- The output is proportional to the difference $(V_2 - V_1)$ **only if** all four resistors are perfectly matched:
  
  $$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$$

### 2.2.2 Transfer Function

The output voltage is given by:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_1}\,(V_2 - V_1) \quad \text{(when } R_1 = R_3 \text{ and } R_2 = R_4\text{)}$$

Equivalently, in terms of common-mode and differential components:

$$V_{out} = A_{d}\,V_{d} + A_{cm}\,V_{cm}$$

### 2.2.3 Limitations of the 1-Op-Amp Difference Amplifier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single-op-amp difference amplifier is **rarely used directly** as a bio-amplifier because:
> 1. The **input impedances at $V_1$ and $V_2$ are unequal and not very high** (they are not symmetric to ground).
> 2. **Gain cannot be changed without disturbing CMRR** (resistor matching is destroyed).
> 3. **Trimming 4 resistors to perfectly match** is impractical in production.
> 4. The **common-mode input range is limited by the op-amp supply rails**.
> 5. It is **sensitive to source-impedance imbalance** at the two electrode sites — destroys CMRR.

## 2.3 The Instrumentation Amplifier (3-Op-Amp IA) — The Workhorse of Bio-Amplification

To overcome the limitations of the 1-op-amp difference amplifier, the **3-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** is universally used in all professional bio-amplifier designs.

### 2.3.1 Topology

The IA consists of:
- **Stage 1 (Buffer / Pre-amp):** Two non-inverting amplifiers (Op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$) sharing a single gain-setting resistor $R_{gain}$ between their inverting inputs.
- **Stage 2 (Difference Amplifier):** A standard 1-op-amp difference amplifier with unity gain (or fixed gain) that subtracts the two buffered outputs, removing the common-mode component.

### 2.3.2 Transfer Function

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}\,(V_2 - V_1)$$

Defining the **first-stage gain** $A_1 = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$ and the **second-stage gain** $A_2 = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$:

$$V_{out} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot (V_2 - V_1) = A_{total} \cdot V_{d}$$

### 2.3.3 Why the IA Solves Bio-Amplifier Problems

| Problem of 1-Op-Amp Difference Amp | How the 3-Op-Amp IA Solves It |
|------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Unequal / moderate input impedance | Both $V_1$, $V_2$ feed directly into op-amp non-inverting inputs → $Z_{in}$ is **extremely high** (≥ 10¹² Ω) and **symmetric** |
| Gain change disturbs CMRR | Gain is set by **a single resistor $R_{gain}$**; the difference amplifier (Stage 2) still has perfectly matched resistors and preserves high CMRR |
| Sensitive to source-impedance imbalance | The buffered inputs isolate the source impedances, so mismatched electrode impedances hardly degrade CMRR |
| Limited common-mode range | Internal nodes carry shifted voltages; CM range is extended |

## 2.4 Key Performance Parameters of a Bio-Amplifier

### 2.4.1 Differential Gain ($A_d$)

$$A_d = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{d}} \quad \text{when } V_{cm} = 0$$

### 2.4.2 Common-Mode Gain ($A_{cm}$)

$$A_{cm} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{cm}} \quad \text{when } V_{d} = 0$$

Ideally $A_{cm} = 0$. In practice, it is small but non-zero due to resistor mismatch and op-amp non-idealities.

### 2.4.3 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

$$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert \quad \text{(dimensionless ratio)}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CMRR is the single most important spec of a bio-amplifier:** 50/60 Hz mains interference is a common-mode signal of **several volts** (e.g., 5 V), while ECG is a **few mV**. If CMRR is low, the 50 Hz noise swamps the ECG. With CMRR = 80 dB, the noise is reduced by a factor of **10,000**.

### 2.4.4 Noise Analysis

Total input-referred noise is the quadrature sum of all noise sources:

$$V_{n,total} = \sqrt{(V_{n,amp})^2 + (V_{n,thermal})^2 + (V_{n,flicker})^2 + (V_{n,electrode})^2}$$

**Thermal noise (Johnson noise) of the source resistance** $R_s$:

$$V_{n,thermal} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R_s \,\Delta f}$$

where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T$ is absolute temperature (K), and $\Delta f$ is the noise bandwidth (Hz).

### 2.4.5 Input Impedance

For a bio-amplifier:

$$Z_{in,d} \geq 10^{8}\ \Omega$$

This is required because the electrode-tissue impedance (typically 1 kΩ to 100 kΩ) forms a voltage divider with $Z_{in}$; if $Z_{in}$ is low, the signal is attenuated.

### 2.4.6 Slew Rate and Bandwidth

The gain-bandwidth product (GBW) of the IA must be high enough that the closed-loop bandwidth covers the biosignal range (e.g., GBW ≥ 1 MHz for ECG at gain 1000).

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{\text{GBW}}{A_{closed-loop}}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use \vert or \mid in formulas. NEVER use bare \vert inside markdown tables (use \vert or \mid to avoid breaking table syntax).**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Differential Output | $V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_d + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}$ | V | General expression |
| 2 | 1-Op-Amp Diff. Amp Gain | $A_d = R_3 / R_1$ | dimensionless | Requires $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_4$ |
| 3 | 3-Op-Amp IA Gain | $A_{total} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | dimensionless | Universal IA formula |
| 4 | CMRR (ratio) | $\text{CMRR} = \vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert$ | dimensionless | Ideal $\rightarrow \infty$ |
| 5 | CMRR (in dB) | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert\right)$ | dB | $\geq$ 80 dB desired |
| 6 | Thermal Noise | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R_s \,\Delta f}$ | V | At $T = 310$ K (body) |
| 7 | Closed-loop Bandwidth | $f_{3dB} = \text{GBW} / A_{cl}$ | Hz | Limits high-frequency response |
| 8 | Common-Mode Input Range | $V_{cm,max} \approx V_{supply} - 1.5$ V | V | Depends on op-amp rails |
| 9 | Input Referred Noise | $V_{n,IRN} = V_{n,out} / A_d$ | V | Use the smallest value |
| 10 | Patient Leakage Limit (Type CF) | $I_{leak} < 10\ \mu A$ | A | IEC 60601 safety standard |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

The differential bio-amplifier / instrumentation amplifier is the **front-end of virtually every modern patient monitor, ECG/EEG/EMG machine, defibrillator, Holter monitor, and implantable telemetry device**. Industry-standard IA chips like the **INA128, INA333, AD620, AD8421, and AD8221** (Analog Devices / Texas Instruments) implement exactly the 3-op-amp architecture. The CMRR, low noise, and gain-selectable via a single resistor make them indispensable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 1-Op-Amp Difference Amplifier Transfer Function

### 3.1.1 Circuit Setup

A standard difference amplifier has:
- $V_1$ connected via $R_1$ to the inverting input (node A).
- $V_2$ connected via $R_2$ to the non-inverting input.
- Feedback resistor $R_3$ from output to node A.
- $R_4$ from the non-inverting input to ground.
- Op-amp output is $V_{out}$.

### 3.1.2 Assumptions (Ideal Op-Amp)

1. Infinite open-loop gain: $A_{OL} \rightarrow \infty$.
2. Infinite input impedance: no current flows into the op-amp input terminals.
3. Zero output impedance.
4. Virtual short: $V_+ = V_-$.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Voltage at the non-inverting input ($V_+$):**

The non-inverting input forms a voltage divider between $V_2$ and ground through $R_2$ and $R_4$. No current enters the op-amp, so:

$$V_+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the virtual short condition:**

$$V_- = V_+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4}$$

**Step 3 — KCL at the inverting input node (node A):**

Current entering node A from $V_1$ through $R_1$ + current entering from $V_{out}$ through $R_3$ = 0 (no current into op-amp):

$$\frac{V_1 - V_-}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V_-}{R_3} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V_{out} - V_-}{R_3} = \frac{V_- - V_1}{R_1}$$

$$V_{out} - V_- = \frac{R_3}{R_1}\,(V_- - V_1)$$

$$V_{out} = V_- \left(1 + \frac{R_3}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_3}{R_1}\,V_1$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V_-$ from Step 1:**

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{R_3}{R_1}\right) \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4}\,V_2 \;-\; \frac{R_3}{R_1}\,V_1$$

**Step 6 — Apply the matching condition $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$:**

With matching, $\left(1 + \dfrac{R_3}{R_1}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = 1$, and $R_3/R_1 = 1$:

$$V_{out} = V_2 - V_1 = V_d$$

**Step 7 — General matched case ($R_1 = R_3$, $R_2 = R_4$):**

The general transfer function simplifies to:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_1}\,(V_2 - V_1) = A_d \cdot V_d$$

where $A_d = R_3 / R_1$ is the differential gain.

> **Verification check:** If $V_1 = V_2 = V_{cm}$, then $V_{out} = 0$. ✔ (Perfect common-mode rejection when perfectly matched.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) Transfer Function

### 3.2.1 Circuit Description

- **Stage 1:** Op-amps $A_1$ (driven by $V_1$) and $A_2$ (driven by $V_2$), with output voltages $V_{o1}$ and $V_{o2}$.
- A single gain-setting resistor $R_{gain}$ connects the two inverting inputs.
- Feedback resistors $R_1$ connect each inverting input to the corresponding op-amp output.
- **Stage 2:** A unity-gain (or fixed-gain) difference amplifier with matched resistors $R_2$ and $R_3$, producing $V_{out}$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Currents through $R_{gain}$:**

The op-amp inputs draw no current. The voltage across $R_{gain}$ is $(V_2 - V_1) = V_d$ (virtual short: $V_{-1} = V_1$ and $V_{-2} = V_2$). Hence the current through $R_{gain}$ is:

$$I_{R_{gain}} = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_{gain}}$$

**Step 2 — This same current flows through both feedback resistors $R_1$:**

(Since no current enters op-amp inputs, the same current must pass through both $R_1$ resistors.)

**Step 3 — Output of $A_1$:**

The voltage at the inverting input of $A_1$ is $V_1$ (virtual short). The current $I_{R_{gain}}$ flows from this node through $R_1$ toward $V_{o1}$:

$$I_{R_{gain}} = \frac{V_{o1} - V_1}{R_1}$$

Setting the two expressions equal:

$$\frac{V_{o1} - V_1}{R_1} = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_{gain}}$$

$$V_{o1} - V_1 = \frac{R_1}{R_{gain}}\,(V_2 - V_1)$$

$$V_{o1} = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_{gain}}\,(V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 4 — Output of $A_2$:**

By symmetry (with $V_2$ at the non-inverting input of $A_2$):

$$V_{o2} = V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_{gain}}\,(V_2 - V_1) = V_2 + \frac{R_1}{R_{gain}}\,(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the differential output of Stage 1:**

$$V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1) - \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\,(V_2 - V_1)$$

$$V_{o2} - V_{o1} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$$

Define the first-stage gain:

$$A_1 = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$$

**Step 6 — Stage 2 (Difference Amplifier) with matched resistors ($R_2 = R_3$):**

The difference amplifier subtracts $V_{o1}$ from $V_{o2}$ and applies gain $A_2 = R_3/R_2$:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\,(V_{o2} - V_{o1})$$

**Step 7 — Combine both stages:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\,\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = A_{total} \cdot V_d, \quad \text{where } A_{total} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)}$$

> **Verification check:** If $R_{gain} \rightarrow \infty$ (open), $A_1 = 1$ (just buffering). If $R_{gain} \rightarrow 0$ (short), $A_1 \rightarrow \infty$ (saturated). ✔

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** Design a 3-op-amp IA for an ECG amplifier with the following specifications:
- Differential gain $A_d = 1000$
- First-stage gain $A_1 = 100$ (to maximize CMRR — most gain in Stage 1)
- Second-stage gain $A_2 = 10$
- Use $R_1 = 50$ kΩ in Stage 1 and $R_2 = R_3 = 10$ kΩ in Stage 2.

**Find:** The required value of $R_{gain}$.

**Solution:**

$$A_1 = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$$

$$100 = 1 + \frac{2 \times 50{,}000}{R_{gain}}$$

$$\frac{2 \times 50{,}000}{R_{gain}} = 99$$

$$R_{gain} = \frac{100{,}000}{99} \approx 1010.1\ \Omega \approx 1\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Verify total gain:**

$$A_{total} = A_1 \times A_2 = 100 \times 10 = 1000\ \checkmark$$

**For an ECG input of 1 mV, the output would be:**

$$V_{out} = 1000 \times 1\ \text{mV} = 1\ \text{V}$$

This 1 V output is ideal for driving a 0–5 V ADC input.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: CMRR of the IA

The overall CMRR of the 3-op-amp IA depends on the **op-amp's intrinsic CMRR** ($\text{CMRR}_{opamp}$) and the **resistor matching tolerance** ($\delta$) in the second-stage difference amplifier.

**Approximate formula for resistor-mismatch-limited CMRR:**

$$\text{CMRR}_{total} \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{1}{\text{CMRR}_{opamp}}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\delta}{1+\delta}\right)^2}}$$

**Example numerical estimate:** If $\text{CMRR}_{opamp} = 100$ dB (10⁵) and $\delta = 0.1\%$ (0.001):

$$\text{CMRR}_{total} \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-10} + 10^{-6}}} \approx \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000 \approx 60\ \text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **critical KTU insight**: With 0.1% resistors, the overall CMRR is **dominated by resistor matching** (≈ 60 dB), not by the op-amp itself. To achieve >80 dB, you need **0.01% resistors or laser-trimmed IA modules** (e.g., INA128 has internal trimmed resistors).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Differential Bio-Amplifier Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef elec fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,color:#000
    classDef bio fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d3557,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000
    classDef safe fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#9d4edd,color:#000

    elec1[Electrode 1 On Skin]:::elec
    elec2[Electrode 2 On Skin]:::elec
    refElec[Reference Electrode Right Leg Driver]:::elec

    sigBio[Bioelectric Signal Microvolts to Millivolts]:::bio
    cmNoise[Common Mode Noise 50 60 Hz Power Line]:::bio

    elec1 --> sigBio
    elec2 --> sigBio
    elec1 --> cmNoise
    elec2 --> cmNoise

    sigBio --> stage1IA[Stage 1 Instrumentation Amplifier Buffers and High Common Mode Rejection]:::amp
    cmNoise --> stage1IA

    stage1IA --> isoBarrier[Galvanic Isolation Barrier Optical or Transformer]:::safe

    isoBarrier --> stage2Filter[Stage 2 Gain Adjustment and Bandpass Filtering 0.05 to 150 Hz for ECG]:::amp

    stage2Filter --> levelShift[Level Shifter and Output Driver 0 to 5 V]:::amp

    refElec --> cmNoise

    levelShift --> adcOut[Output to ADC Display Recorder DSP]:::amp
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef node fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#bc4749,color:#000

    V1[Input V1 to Opamp A1]:::node
    V2[Input V2 to Opamp A2]:::node
    Rgain[Gain Setting Resistor Rgain]:::node
    R1a[Feedback Resistor R1 on A1]:::node
    R1b[Feedback Resistor R1 on A2]:::node
    Vo1[Output of A1 Vo1]:::out
    Vo2[Output of A2 Vo2]:::out
    A3[Difference Amplifier Opamp A3 with Matched R2 R3]:::node
    Vout[Final Output Vout]:::out

    V1 --> A1Op[Opamp A1 Non Inverting Buffer]:::node
    V2 --> A2Op[Opamp A2 Non Inverting Buffer]:::node

    A1Op --- R1a
    A2Op --- R1b

    R1a --- Rgain
    R1b --- Rgain

    A1Op --> Vo1
    A2Op --> Vo2

    Vo1 --> A3
    Vo2 --> A3

    A3 --> Vout
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow Explanation of the Block Diagram

The block diagram models the **complete patient-to-display signal path** in a clinical bio-amplifier:

1. **Electrodes (red blocks):** Two measurement electrodes on the body pick up both the desired bioelectric signal (blue block) and a much larger common-mode noise (e.g., 50 Hz mains). A third "right-leg drive" electrode actively drives the patient's body to minimize the common-mode voltage.

2. **Stage 1 — Instrumentation Amplifier (green block):** Buffers the high-impedance electrode signals, amplifies the differential component by a factor $A_1 = 1 + 2R_1/R_{gain}$, and rejects the common-mode component. Both inputs see very high impedance (≥ 10¹² Ω), eliminating loading.

3. **Galvanic Isolation Barrier (yellow block):** An optocoupler or isolation transformer provides **>1500 V isolation** between the patient side (which may have leakage paths) and the mains-powered or digital side, ensuring patient safety (IEC 60601).

4. **Stage 2 — Gain and Filter (green block):** Additional gain $A_2 = R_3/R_2$ is applied, along with a bandpass filter (e.g., 0.05–150 Hz for ECG) to remove DC offset and high-frequency noise.

5. **Level Shifter & Output Driver (green block):** Shifts the bipolar bio-signal to a unipolar 0–5 V range suitable for ADC input.

6. **Output (green block):** Drives an ADC, digital display, recorder, or DSP module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive Block Diagram Note:** Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 directive, since a true schematic of a 3-op-amp IA (with 7 resistors, 3 op-amps, power rails, offset-null pins) is not naturally expressible in Mermaid, the diagram above renders the **functional architecture and signal-flow interactions** — the pedagogical equivalent used in standard biomedical instrumentation textbooks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

### Q1. Define a bio-amplifier. List any four essential requirements that a bio-amplifier must satisfy. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

A bio-amplifier is a low-noise, high-gain, high-input-impedance differential amplifier used to extract, amplify, and condition the weak bioelectric signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, etc.) generated by the body, while rejecting environmental noise and ensuring patient safety.

**Four essential requirements:**
1. **High input impedance** (≥ 10⁸ Ω) to avoid loading the high-impedance electrode-tissue interface.
2. **High CMRR** (≥ 80 dB) to reject 50/60 Hz common-mode power-line interference.
3. **High differential gain** (selectable, typically 100 to 10,000) to amplify µV–mV signals to volt level.
4. **Galvanic isolation** between patient and measurement circuitry to protect against microshock (leakage current < 10 µA per IEC 60601).

> *Examiner tip: 1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for the four points.*

---

### Q2. Define CMRR. Why is high CMRR critical for a bio-amplifier? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) is the ratio of the differential-mode gain $A_d$ to the common-mode gain $A_{cm}$ of an amplifier, expressed in dB:

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert\right)$$

**Why high CMRR is critical for a bio-amplifier:**
Biopotential signals (ECG ≈ 1 mV) are **masked by power-line interference** (50/60 Hz mains pickup ≈ several volts) that appears as a common-mode signal on both electrode leads. A high CMRR ensures that this huge noise is rejected, allowing the small bio-signal to be amplified cleanly. For example, a CMRR of 80 dB reduces 5 V of 50 Hz noise to 0.5 mV — comparable to the ECG amplitude — and 100 dB reduces it to 50 µV, well below the ECG.

> *Examiner tip: 1 mark formula, 2 marks explanation.*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of the **3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** used as a bio-amplifier. Derive its overall voltage gain. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An instrumentation amplifier has $R_1 = 25$ kΩ, $R_2 = R_3 = 10$ kΩ, and $R_{gain} = 1.1$ kΩ. The input biosignal is an ECG of 1.5 mV (peak) riding on a common-mode 50 Hz interference of 3 V. If the op-amp used has a CMRR of 90 dB, calculate:
1. The total voltage gain of the IA.
2. The differential output voltage.
3. The output noise voltage due to the common-mode interference.
4. Comment on whether the bio-signal can be recovered cleanly. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Working and Derivation of the 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier:**

**Circuit Diagram Description:** The IA consists of two non-inverting buffer amplifiers ($A_1$, $A_2$) in the first stage sharing a single gain-setting resistor $R_{gain}$, followed by a unity/fixed-gain difference amplifier ($A_3$) in the second stage.

**Working Principle:**
- The two bioelectric electrode signals $V_1$ and $V_2$ are applied to the non-inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$ respectively, providing very high (ideally infinite) input impedance — essential for high-impedance electrode sources.
- The op-amp virtual-short property forces $V_{-1} = V_1$ and $V_{-2} = V_2$. Hence the voltage across $R_{gain}$ is $(V_2 - V_1) = V_d$.
- The same current flows through both feedback resistors $R_1$ and through $R_{gain}$.
- The output voltages of $A_1$ and $A_2$ are:

$$V_{o1} = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_{gain}}(V_2 - V_1), \quad V_{o2} = V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_{gain}}(V_2 - V_1)$$

- The differential output of Stage 1 is:

$$V_{o2} - V_{o1} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$$

- The second stage ($A_3$) subtracts $V_{o1}$ from $V_{o2}$ and applies a gain of $R_3/R_2$, while perfectly rejecting the common-mode component (which appears equally at $V_{o1}$ and $V_{o2}$).

**Derivation of Overall Gain:**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)(V_2 - V_1) = A_{total} \cdot V_d}$$

$$A_{total} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)$$

> *(See Section 3.2 for the full step-by-step derivation.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $R_1 = 25$ kΩ, $R_2 = R_3 = 10$ kΩ, $R_{gain} = 1.1$ kΩ
- $V_d = 1.5$ mV (peak)
- $V_{cm} = 3$ V
- $\text{CMRR} = 90$ dB

**Step 1 — Total Voltage Gain:**

$$A_1 = 1 + \frac{2 \times 25{,}000}{1{,}100} = 1 + \frac{50{,}000}{1{,}100} = 1 + 45.45 = 46.45$$

$$A_2 = \frac{R_3}{R_2} = \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000} = 1$$

$$A_{total} = A_1 \times A_2 = 46.45 \times 1 = 46.45$$

**Step 2 — Differential Output Voltage:**

$$V_{out,d} = A_{total} \times V_d = 46.45 \times 1.5\ \text{mV} = 69.68\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Output Noise Voltage due to Common-Mode Interference:**

First, convert CMRR from dB to ratio:

$$\text{CMRR} = 10^{90/20} = 10^{4.5} \approx 31{,}623$$

Common-mode gain:

$$A_{cm} = \frac{A_{total}}{\text{CMRR}} = \frac{46.45}{31{,}623} \approx 1.469 \times 10^{-3}$$

Output noise:

$$V_{out,cm} = A_{cm} \times V_{cm} = 1.469 \times 10^{-3} \times 3\ \text{V} = 4.41\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Recovery:**

- Differential ECG output: **69.68 mV**
- 50 Hz interference at output: **4.41 mV**
- Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) at output:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{V_{out,d}}{V_{out,cm}} = \frac{69.68\ \text{mV}}{4.41\ \text{mV}} \approx 15.8 \;\;(\approx 24\ \text{dB})$$

Since the ECG output (69.68 mV) is **roughly 15.8 times larger** than the residual 50 Hz noise (4.41 mV), the bio-signal **can be recovered cleanly** with simple post-amplification and 50 Hz notch filtering. The IA has done its job of rejecting the 3 V interference down to 4.41 mV — a 680-fold attenuation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - July 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **need for a bio-amplifier** in biomedical instrumentation. Discuss the various **characteristics** an ideal bio-amplifier must possess, with emphasis on input impedance and CMRR. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Design a **3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** for an EEG acquisition system with the following specifications:
- Differential gain required: $A_{total} = 5000$
- First-stage gain: $A_1 = 50$
- Second-stage gain: $A_2 = 100$
- Choose $R_1 = 49.4$ kΩ (standard E-series).

Calculate:
1. The value of $R_{gain}$.
2. The value of $R_2$ and $R_3$ (assuming $R_2 = R_3$).
3. If the input EEG signal is 100 µV, what is the output voltage?
4. If the op-amp CMRR is 100 dB, what is the residual common-mode output if a 5 V mains interference is present? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Need for a Bio-Amplifier — Discussion:**

The need for a bio-amplifier arises from the **inherent challenges in measuring bioelectric signals** directly. These signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG) are characterized by:
1. **Extremely small amplitudes** (µV to mV range) — too small to drive recorders, displays, or ADCs directly.
2. **High source impedances** (electrode-tissue interface, 1 kΩ to 100 kΩ).
3. **Heavy electromagnetic interference** from 50/60 Hz power lines, RF, fluorescent lamps, etc.
4. **Patient safety concerns** (microshock from leakage currents).
5. **Limited bandwidth** and the need to remove DC offset and high-frequency noise.

A bio-amplifier solves all these issues simultaneously. Its **essential characteristics** are:

| Characteristic | Typical Value | Reason |
|----------------|---------------|--------|
| High input impedance | $\geq 10^8\ \Omega$ | Avoids loading of high-impedance source |
| High CMRR | $\geq 80$ dB (ideally $\geq 100$ dB) | Rejects 50/60 Hz mains interference |
| High gain | Selectable, 100 to 10,000 | Amplifies µV–mV signals to volts |
| Low noise | Input-referred noise < 1 µV RMS | Preserves the small signal buried in noise |
| Patient isolation | $\geq 1500$ V galvanic isolation | Patient safety |
| Bandwidth control | Switchable per biosignal | E.g., 0.05–150 Hz for ECG |

**Emphasis on Input Impedance and CMRR:**
- The **input impedance** must be at least **10× higher** than the source impedance (which can be 100 kΩ for dry EEG electrodes). A low $Z_{in}$ forms a voltage divider with the source and attenuates the signal. With $Z_{in} = 10^{10}$ Ω (typical for a JFET-input op-amp IA), the loading error is negligible.
- The **CMRR** is crucial because 50/60 Hz interference is a common-mode signal of several volts, much larger than the bio-signal. High CMRR ensures this noise is rejected. At 80 dB CMRR, the noise is reduced by a factor of 10,000; at 100 dB, by 100,000.

---

**(b) Numerical Design Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A_{total} = 5000$, $A_1 = 50$, $A_2 = 100$
- $R_1 = 49.4$ kΩ, $R_2 = R_3$ (unknown)

**Step 1 — Value of $R_{gain}$:**

$$A_1 = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$$

$$50 = 1 + \frac{2 \times 49{,}400}{R_{gain}}$$

$$\frac{98{,}800}{R_{gain}} = 49$$

$$R_{gain} = \frac{98{,}800}{49} = 2{,}016.33\ \Omega \approx 2\ \text{k}\Omega\ \text{(standard)}$$

**Step 2 — Value of $R_2$ and $R_3$:**

Since $A_2 = R_3/R_2$ and $R_2 = R_3$:

$$A_2 = \frac{R_3}{R_2} = 1 \quad \text{???}$$

This is a contradiction because $A_2$ is specified as 100 but $R_2 = R_3$ was assumed. Let us re-interpret: the design says $A_2 = 100$, so $R_2$ and $R_3$ need not be equal. Choose $R_2 = 1$ kΩ (standard). Then:

$$R_3 = A_2 \cdot R_2 = 100 \times 1\ \text{k}\Omega = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$$

(Equivalently, choose $R_2 = 100$ Ω and $R_3 = 10$ kΩ; the ratio is what matters.)

**Verify total gain:**

$$A_{total} = A_1 \times A_2 = 50 \times 100 = 5000\ \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Output Voltage for 100 µV EEG input:**

$$V_{out} = A_{total} \times V_d = 5000 \times 100\ \mu V = 0.5\ \text{V}$$

This is a clean, ADC-friendly voltage level.

**Step 4 — Residual Common-Mode Output for 5 V Mains Interference:**

Convert CMRR from dB:

$$\text{CMRR} = 10^{100/20} = 10^{5} = 100{,}000$$

Common-mode gain:

$$A_{cm} = \frac{A_{total}}{\text{CMRR}} = \frac{5000}{100{,}000} = 0.05$$

Residual common-mode output:

$$V_{out,cm} = A_{cm} \times V_{cm} = 0.05 \times 5\ \text{V} = 0.25\ \text{V} = 250\ \text{mV}$$

**Comment:** The differential EEG output is 0.5 V, while the residual 50 Hz noise is 250 mV — the noise is comparable to half the signal. This is **not acceptable** in practice. Solutions: (i) Use a higher-CMRR op-amp (e.g., >120 dB), (ii) Add a 50 Hz notch filter after Stage 2, (iii) Use a driven-right-leg circuit to reduce the common-mode voltage at the body.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert CMRR from dB to ratio** before using it. CMRR in dB must be converted via $10^{\text{CMRR}_{dB}/20}$ before dividing $A_d$.
> 2. **Using the wrong CMRR formula** (some students write $A_{cm} = A_d \cdot \text{CMRR}$ instead of $A_{cm} = A_d / \text{CMRR}$).
> 3. **Sign convention error:** Many students write $V_{out} = (V_1 - V_2)$ instead of $(V_2 - V_1)$; the IA's transfer function is $(V_2 - V_1)$, not $(V_1 - V_2)$. This can produce a wrong sign on the output.
> 4. **Mixing up $A_{cm}$ and $A_{cm,out}$:** Some students forget to multiply $A_{cm}$ by $V_{cm}$ to get the noise output.
> 5. **Units of $\mu$V vs mV vs V** in final answers — always state units clearly.
> 6. **Resistor matching condition** — students often forget to state "$R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$" when writing the 1-op-amp difference amplifier transfer function. This is required for the simple form $V_{out} = (R_3/R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$ to hold.
> 7. **Safety / isolation** is often skipped in answers about bio-amplifiers. Examiners **expect** a mention of galvanic isolation, IEC 60601, leakage current limits (10 µA for Type CF), and the driven-right-leg circuit.
> 8. **CMRR and frequency:** CMRR decreases with frequency. At 50/60 Hz, the **effective** CMRR may be 20–40 dB lower than the DC CMRR. This is rarely mentioned by students and is a high-value point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ✅ A **bio-amplifier** is a **low-noise, high-CMRR, high-input-impedance differential amplifier** with patient isolation, used to amplify µV–mV bioelectric signals.
- ✅ **Biopotential amplitudes:** ECG 0.5–5 mV, EEG 5–300 µV, EMG 50 µV–5 mV, EOG 0.1–1 mV.
- ✅ **Need for a bio-amplifier:** Small signal amplitude, high source impedance, common-mode noise, patient safety, bandwidth limiting.
- ✅ **The 1-op-amp difference amplifier** has the transfer function $V_{out} = (R_3/R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$ **only if** $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_4$. It suffers from unequal input impedance, low CMRR sensitivity to resistor matching, and difficulty in varying gain.
- ✅ **The 3-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** uses a two-stage topology: a buffered pre-amp with single-resistor gain control, followed by a difference amplifier. Its transfer function is:
  $$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$$
- ✅ **CMRR** is the **single most important specification** of a bio-amplifier. $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert$. Required: $\geq$ 80 dB, ideally $\geq$ 100 dB.
- ✅ **Input impedance** of the IA is **extremely high** (≥ 10¹² Ω) and **symmetric** — this is the key advantage over the 1-op-amp difference amplifier.
- ✅ **Thermal (Johnson) noise** of a source resistance $R_s$ over bandwidth $\Delta f$ is $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R_s \,\Delta f}$. At body temperature $T = 310$ K, this is the dominant noise source for high-impedance electrodes.
- ✅ **Patient safety:** Galvanic isolation $\geq$ 1500 V; leakage current < 10 µA (IEC 60601 Type CF).
- ✅ **Driven Right-Leg (DRL) circuit:** actively drives the body to reduce common-mode voltage and improve effective CMRR.
- ✅ **Industry-standard IA chips:** INA128, INA333, AD620, AD8421, AD8221 — all implement the 3-op-amp architecture with internal laser-trimmed resistors for high CMRR.
- ✅ **Practical CMRR limit** is set by **resistor matching**, not by op-amp CMRR: 0.1% resistors → ~60 dB; 0.01% or laser-trimmed → >100 dB.
- ✅ **CMRR degrades with frequency** — check CMRR at the specific interference frequency (50/60 Hz), not just DC.
- ✅ **Gain-bandwidth product (GBW)** must be chosen such that the closed-loop bandwidth covers the biosignal: $f_{3dB} = \text{GBW}/A_{cl}$.
- ✅ **KTU exam-favourite line:** "The 3-op-amp IA is preferred over the 1-op-amp difference amplifier in bio-signal applications because of its high and symmetric input impedance, single-resistor gain control, and superior CMRR."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Bio-Amplifiers for the Measurement of Physiological Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Bio-Amplifiers for the Measurement of Physiological Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Bio-Amplifier** (also termed a **Biomedical Amplifier** or **Physiological Amplifier**) is a specialized electronic amplifier explicitly engineered to faithfully detect, condition, and amplify low-amplitude bioelectric signals generated by the human body, while simultaneously rejecting large common-mode interference, suppressing 50/60 Hz power-line noise, and ensuring complete electrical isolation between the patient and the measurement/monitoring equipment to guarantee patient safety.

In the KTU 2024 OEICT723 framework, a bio-amplifier is characterized as the **front-end signal-conditioning subsystem** of any biomedical instrument, placed immediately after the bioelectrode transducer stage and before any display, recording, or A/D conversion stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** The design of bio-amplifiers focuses on the unique requirements imposed by physiological variables, which include **(a) low signal amplitude (µV to mV range)**, **(b) low frequency content (sub-Hz to a few kHz)**, **(c) high source impedance (electrode-tissue interface)**, and **(d) hostile electrical environment (mains interference, ESD, defibrillation pulses)**.

### 1.2 Physiological Variables Typically Measured

| Physiological Variable | Typical Amplitude | Frequency Band | Source/Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** | **0.5 – 5 mV** | 0.05 – 150 Hz | Cardiac muscle depolarization |
| **Electroencephalogram (EEG)** | **10 – 100 µV** | 0.5 – 100 Hz | Cortical neural activity |
| **Electromyogram (EMG)** | **50 µV – 5 mV** | 20 – 5000 Hz | Skeletal muscle fibers |
| **Electrooculogram (EOG)** | **0.05 – 3.5 mV** | DC – 100 Hz | Corneo-retinal potential |
| **Electrogastrogram (EGG)** | **0.1 – 1 mV** | 0.01 – 0.5 Hz | Stomach smooth muscle |
| **Blood Pressure (BP)** | 0 – 300 mmHg (transduced) | DC – 60 Hz | Arterial wall pulsation |
| **Body Temperature** | 32 – 42 °C (transduced) | DC | Core/skin thermoreceptors |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "The Whisper Listener in a Stadium"

Imagine trying to hear a friend whispering from the far end of a packed football stadium during a roaring match. The whisper is the **biological signal** (microvolts to millivolts). The crowd roar is the **environmental noise** (50 Hz mains hum, EMG artifacts, RF interference). Your ears are the **electrodes** (imperfect transducers). The amplifier you would need is essentially a **super-smart hearing aid** that:
1. **Boosts the whisper** (high differential gain ≈ 1000–10,000).
2. **Cancels the crowd noise** that hits *both* your ears equally (high **CMRR ≥ 80–100 dB**).
3. **Ignores low-frequency rumbles** like the wind (high-pass filter, 0.05 Hz).
4. **Ignores high-frequency hisses** like a passing jet (low-pass filter, 150 Hz for ECG).

That "smart hearing aid" is precisely what a **bio-amplifier** is. It is not just a simple voltage amplifier; it is an *intelligent, safe, and noise-immune signal-conditioning engine*.

### 1.4 Why a Standard Op-Amp Will NOT Work Directly

A bare operational amplifier (e.g., µA741) connected to skin electrodes fails because:
- The **electrode-skin impedance** is unbalanced and can reach **MΩ**, causing differential distortion.
- A standard op-amp has a **CMRR of only 70–90 dB**; in a clinical environment, 50 Hz pickup can saturate the output.
- Patient **safety** demands galvanic isolation (> 4000 V isolation voltage) to prevent microshock macroshock.

Hence, a **multi-stage architecture** is mandatory: pre-amp (instrumentation amp) → isolation (optical/transformer) → driver amp → filters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Objectives (Recall for 14-mark KTU questions):**
> 1. **High Input Impedance (Zin ≥ 10 MΩ)** to avoid loading the electrode source.
> 2. **High CMRR (≥ 100 dB)** for 50/60 Hz rejection.
> 3. **Low Input-Referred Noise** (sub-µV rms in ECG band).
> 4. **Adjustable Gain** (typically 100 – 10,000) via a single resistor.
> 5. **Patient Isolation / Protection** (defibrillation-proof, leakage < 10 µA).
> 6. **Bandwidth Limiting** (HPF + LPF with selectable cut-offs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Building Block: Differential Amplifier

A bio-amplifier must, at its core, perform a **differential measurement**: it amplifies the voltage difference between two electrode sites and rejects any voltage that is common to both. The simplest realization is the classic **single-op-amp difference amplifier**.

For a symmetric difference amplifier with **R1 = R3** and **R2 = R4**:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} \left( V_2 - V_1 \right)$$

The **Differential Gain** $A_d$ and **Common-Mode Gain** $A_{cm}$ are:

$$A_d = \frac{R_2}{R_1}, \qquad A_{cm} = \frac{R_2 \cdot \delta}{R_1 + R_2}, \quad \delta = \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

When the four resistors are perfectly matched, $A_{cm} \to 0$ and the **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** becomes theoretically infinite. In practice, resistor tolerances of 0.1 % give CMRR ≈ 60 dB, which is **insufficient** for biomedical applications.

### 2.2 The Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) — The Heart of Every Bio-Amplifier

The **3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier** is the canonical front-end for all KTU-level bio-amplifier design questions. It solves the three classic problems of the basic diff amp:
1. **Impedance imbalance** (input buffer stage provides infinite Zin).
2. **Gain adjustment without disturbing CMRR** (single resistor Rg sets gain).
3. **High CMRR** (matched feedback network in the second stage).

#### 2.2.1 Two-Stage Architecture Explanation

**Stage 1 — Buffering / Pre-Gain Stage (Op-Amps A1 and A2):**
Two op-amps are configured as non-inverting amplifiers. The signal input sees the gates of the op-amp inputs, giving **Zin ≈ 10^12 Ω**. The differential gain of this stage is set by an external resistor Rg:

$$A_{d1} = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The brilliance of this topology: the **common-mode voltage at the inputs of A1 and A2 is transmitted unchanged** to the outputs of A1 and A2 (since the gain is applied to the *difference* only). This means the second stage sees an already-amplified differential signal riding on a clean common-mode voltage, which the second stage can reject easily.

**Stage 2 — Difference / Subtraction Stage (Op-Amp A3):**
The classic difference amplifier with R2 = R3 and R4 = R5 (matched) provides a unity differential gain and complete common-mode rejection. If a gain $G_2$ is desired in stage 2, it is built-in:

$$A_{d2} = \frac{R_4}{R_2} = G_2$$

**Overall Transfer Function:**

$$V_{out} = \left( 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g} \right) \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_2} \cdot (V_2 - V_1)$$

### 2.3 Right-Leg Drive (RLD) — Active Grounding for ECG/EEG

In ECG/EEG, the third electrode (reference/ground) is not simply connected to the chassis. Instead, it is fed back through an auxiliary op-amp that drives the patient body to cancel the common-mode voltage actively. This is called **Driven Right Leg (DRL)** in ECG terminology. The effective common-mode voltage is reduced by a factor of $(1 + A_{RLD})$, dramatically improving CMRR in real life.

### 2.4 Isolation Amplifier — Patient Safety Subsystem

To meet the **IEC 60601-1** standard (leakage current < 10 µA under normal conditions, < 50 µA under single-fault), the bio-amplifier must include a **galvanic barrier**. Three common techniques:
1. **Optical isolation** (LED + photodiode, e.g., ISO124).
2. **Transformer isolation** (carrier modulation + transformer coupling).
3. **Capacitive isolation** (digital isolators like ADuM3154).

### 2.5 Filtering Stage

A practical bio-amplifier (e.g., for ECG) includes:
- **High-Pass Filter (HPF):** $f_c \approx 0.05$ Hz (removes DC electrode offset, motion artifacts).
- **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** $f_c \approx 150$ Hz (anti-aliasing, noise limiting).
- **Notch Filter:** $f_0 = 50/60$ Hz, Q ≈ 5–10 (mains line rejection).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE/IA. Memorize the boundary conditions in **bold**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IA Differential Gain | $A_d = \left( 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g} \right) \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_2}$ | Dimensionless (V/V) |
| 2 | IA Pre-Stage Gain | $A_{d1} = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}$ | Set by **single resistor Rg** |
| 3 | IA Subtractor Gain | $A_{d2} = R_4 / R_2$ | Must be unity (R4 = R2) for CMRR max |
| 4 | CMRR Definition | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert \right)$ | **Always in dB** |
| 5 | CMRR via Resistor Mismatch | $\text{CMRR}^{-1} \approx \frac{\Delta R}{R} + \frac{1}{2}\left( 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g} \right) \frac{\Delta R'}{R'}$ | Mismatched IA |
| 6 | Input Referred Noise (thermal) | $e_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \cdot \Delta f}$ | **kB = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**, T in K |
| 7 | 1st-Order LPF Cut-off | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz |
| 8 | 1st-Order HPF Cut-off | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz |
| 9 | Notch Quality Factor | $Q = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f_{-3dB}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | ECG Bandwidth | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** | Standard clinical |
| 11 | EEG Bandwidth | **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz** | Standard clinical |
| 12 | EMG Bandwidth | **20 Hz – 5 kHz** | Standard clinical |
| 13 | Patient Leakage Limit | **< 10 µA** (NC), **< 50 µA** (SFC) | IEC 60601-1 |
| 14 | Right-Leg Drive Improvement | $\text{CMRR}_\text{eff} \approx \text{CMRR}_\text{IA} \cdot (1 + A_\text{RLD})$ | Active grounding |
| 15 | Input Impedance of IA | $Z_{in} \approx 2 \cdot Z_{in,\text{op-amp}} \cdot \beta A$ | **Effectively > 10 GΩ** |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 3-op-amp IA is the universal front-end of **every** modern patient monitor (Philips IntelliVue, GE CARESCAPE, Mindray ePM). It is also embedded in:
- **Defibrillators** (with defibrillation-protection resistors + gas-discharge tubes at input).
- **Holter monitors** (low-power CMOS versions, e.g., AD8232, ADS129x).
- **Brain-computer interfaces** (high-density EEG, 64–256 channels).
- **Implantable devices** (pacemakers, neural recorders) using chopper-stabilized IAs.
- **Polygraphs / lie detectors** (simultaneous ECG + GSR + respiration).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Voltage of the 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

**Given:** Standard 3-op-amp IA with R1 (between A1 output and its inverting input), R1 (symmetric), Rg (between the two inverting inputs of A1 and A2), and a unity-gain difference amplifier at the output (R2, R4 = matched, R3, R5 = matched).

**Step 1 — Apply virtual-short at A1 and A2 inputs:**
Because both op-amps have negative feedback, $V_- = V_+$ at each input.

$$V_{1}^{+} = V_1 \quad \text{(input voltage at non-inv. of A1)}$$
$$V_{2}^{+} = V_2 \quad \text{(input voltage at non-inv. of A2)}$$

**Step 2 — Express voltages at the inverting inputs:**
By the virtual short:

$$V_{A1}^{-} = V_1, \qquad V_{A2}^{-} = V_2$$

**Step 3 — The current through Rg:**
Since no current flows into the ideal op-amp inputs, the current through Rg equals the current through both R1 resistors:

$$i = \frac{V_{A1}^{-} - V_{A2}^{-}}{R_g} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage at the outputs of A1 and A2:**
The voltage drop across each R1 is $i \cdot R_1$, and this drop is added to the inverting-input voltage to yield the op-amp output:

$$V_{o1} = V_1 + i R_1 = V_1 + \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{R_g} R_1$$

$$V_{o2} = V_2 - i R_1 = V_2 - \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{R_g} R_1$$

**Step 5 — Take the difference:**

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} (V_1 - V_2) = \left( 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} \right) (V_1 - V_2)$$

This proves the **differential gain of stage 1**:

$$A_{d1} = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the unity-gain subtractor (stage 2):**
With $R_2 = R_3$ and $R_4 = R_5$, the output is:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_4}{R_2} (V_{o2} - V_{o1})$$

**Step 7 — Combine both stages:**

$$V_{out} = \left( 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} \right) \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_2} \cdot (V_2 - V_1)$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = A_d \,(V_2 - V_1), \quad A_d = \left( 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} \right) \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_2}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: CMRR of the 3-Op-Amp IA with Resistor Mismatch

**Given:** R4 ≠ R5 and R2 ≠ R3, i.e., the subtractor has resistor mismatches. Let $R_2 = R$, $R_3 = R(1+\epsilon_1)$, $R_4 = R$, $R_5 = R(1+\epsilon_2)$.

**Step 1 — General expression for subtractor output:**

$$V_{out} = V_{o2} \left( \frac{R_5}{R_3 + R_5} \right) \left( 1 + \frac{R_4}{R_2} \right) - V_{o1} \left( \frac{R_4}{R_2} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute matched values and separate differential/common-mode:**

For a differential input $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ and common-mode $V_{cm} = (V_1 + V_2)/2$:

$$V_{o1} = V_{cm} + \frac{V_d}{2} A_{d1}, \qquad V_{o2} = V_{cm} - \frac{V_d}{2} A_{d1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and expand to first order in mismatches:**

After algebra (omitted for brevity, but expanded in KTU textbooks):

$$A_d = \frac{R_4}{R_2} \left( 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2}{2}}$$

$$A_{cm} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \epsilon_2 - \epsilon_1 \right) \left( 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} \right) \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Form the CMRR ratio:**

$$\text{CMRR}^{-1} = \frac{A_{cm}}{A_d} \approx \frac{\vert \epsilon_2 - \epsilon_1 \vert}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB:**

$$\boxed{\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{2}{\vert \epsilon_2 - \epsilon_1 \vert} \right)}$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** If resistors are 0.1 % matched, $\vert \epsilon_2 - \epsilon_1 \vert \leq 0.002$:

$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(2 / 0.002) = 20 \log_{10}(1000) = 60 \text{ dB}$$

This is **not** enough. With 0.01 % resistors: $\text{CMRR} = 86$ dB — acceptable for many bio apps. The best IAs (e.g., AD620, INA128) use **laser-trimmed** thin-film resistors to achieve $\text{CMRR} \geq 120$ dB at DC.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Input-Referred Noise of the IA

**Step 1 — Each op-amp has voltage noise density $e_n$ (V/√Hz) and current noise $i_n$ (A/√Hz).**

**Step 2 — Total input-referred voltage noise of the 3-op-amp IA:**

$$e_{n,\text{in}}^2 = 2 e_{n,A1}^2 + 2 e_{n,A2}^2 + \left( \frac{e_{n,A3}}{A_{d1}} \right)^2 + (i_{n,A1} R_s)^2 + (i_{n,A2} R_s)^2$$

**Step 3 — For ECG (bandwidth $\Delta f = 150$ Hz) with $e_n = 10$ nV/√Hz:**

$$e_{n,\text{rms}} = e_n \sqrt{\Delta f} = 10 \times 10^{-9} \times \sqrt{150} \approx 122 \text{ nV}_\text{rms}$$

This is **100× smaller** than a typical QRS complex (~1 mV) — so noise is acceptable.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Right-Leg Drive Common-Mode Improvement

**Given:** Common-mode voltage $V_{cm}$ on patient, RLD op-amp gain $A_\text{RLD}$, feedback resistor $R_f$ and input resistor $R_\text{in}$.

**Step 1 — Drive current from the RLD op-amp to the patient:**

$$I_{RL} = \frac{V_{cm} \cdot A_\text{RLD}}{R_f + R_\text{body}/2 + R_\text{in}}$$

**Step 2 — The RLD cancels the common-mode voltage by injecting an equal-and-opposite current:**

$$V_{cm,\text{eff}} = \frac{V_{cm}}{1 + A_\text{RLD} \cdot \beta}, \quad \beta = \frac{R_\text{body}/2}{R_f + R_\text{body}/2 + R_\text{in}}$$

**Step 3 — Effective CMRR improvement:**

$$\boxed{\text{CMRR}_\text{eff, dB} = \text{CMRR}_\text{IA, dB} + 20 \log_{10} \vert 1 + A_\text{RLD} \beta \vert}$$

If $A_\text{RLD} = 100$ and $\beta = 0.5$, the RLD adds $\approx 34$ dB to the IA's CMRR. This is why **every clinical-grade ECG machine** uses RLD.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Rg for a Given Gain

**Problem:** Design an ECG amplifier for an IA chip (e.g., AD620) with $R_1 = 24.7$ kΩ internal. The differential gain must be **1000**.

**Step 1 — Apply the gain equation:**

$$A_d = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} = 1000 \implies \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} = 999$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Rg:**

$$R_g = \frac{2 R_1}{999} = \frac{2 \times 24700}{999} = 49.45 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3 — Choose standard E96 value: $R_g = 49.9$ Ω.**

**Step 4 — Verify gain:**

$$A_d = 1 + \frac{2 \times 24700}{49.9} = 1 + 990 = 991 \approx 1000 \quad \text{✓}$$

**Step 5 — Compute CMRR (with 0.01 % resistors):** $\text{CMRR} \approx 86$ dB; with RLD = +34 dB ⇒ **total ≈ 120 dB**.

### 3.6 Numerical Example — Noise Calculation

**Step 1 — Compute thermal noise of a 10 kΩ source in 150 Hz bandwidth:**

$$e_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f} = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 10^4 \times 150}$$
$$= \sqrt{2.484 \times 10^{-15}} = 4.98 \times 10^{-8} \text{ V} \approx 50 \text{ nV}_\text{rms}$$

**Step 2 — This is negligible vs. the 122 nV op-amp noise (computed earlier).**

**Step 3 — Total input-referred noise (RSS):**

$$e_{n,\text{total}} = \sqrt{50^2 + 122^2} = 132 \text{ nV}_\text{rms} \approx 0.13 \text{ µV}_\text{rms}$$

**Step 4 — SNR with 1 mV ECG input:** $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}(1000 / 0.13) = 20 \log_{10}(7692) \approx 77.7$ dB — **excellent**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any three specifications of an ideal bio-amplifier used for ECG measurement. State why each specification is important.*

**Model Answer:**
An ideal ECG bio-amplifier must satisfy:
1. **High Input Impedance (≥ 10 MΩ):** The electrode-skin impedance is typically 5–50 kΩ and is mismatched between electrodes. A high-Zin prevents signal attenuation and differential distortion caused by the source-impedance imbalance.
2. **High CMRR (≥ 100 dB):** In a clinical environment, 50 Hz mains interference appears as a large common-mode signal (~1–10 V) compared to the small ECG signal (~1 mV). A CMRR of 100 dB attenuates this by 100,000×, leaving a tolerable residue.
3. **Patient Isolation (leakage < 10 µA):** To prevent microshock (which can cause ventricular fibrillation at currents as low as 10 µA directly into the heart), the amplifier must provide galvanic isolation between the patient and the mains-powered equipment, per IEC 60601-1.

*(Examiner awards 1 mark per correct point with justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a differential amplifier. How is it related to differential and common-mode gains?*

**Model Answer:**
CMRR is the ability of a differential amplifier to amplify differential signals while rejecting common-mode signals. It is defined as the ratio of differential gain $A_d$ to common-mode gain $A_{cm}$:

$$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$$

Expressed in decibels:

$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$$

A high CMRR indicates excellent rejection of interference (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum) that appears equally on both inputs.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the working of a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. Derive its output voltage expression. Why is this topology preferred over a simple difference amplifier for bio-signal acquisition?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ECG bio-amplifier uses an IA with $R_1 = 25$ kΩ and $R_g = 50$ Ω. The subtractor stage is unity-gain. (i) Calculate the differential gain. (ii) If the ECG input is 1.2 mV (peak), what is the IA output? (iii) If the IA has a CMRR of 90 dB and a 50 Hz common-mode interference of 5 V is present, what is the output noise voltage from the CM interference?

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the topology.

**Working Explanation:**
- The first stage uses two op-amps (A1, A2) configured as non-inverting buffers with a shared gain-setting resistor Rg. The two input signals $V_1$ and $V_2$ see the high-impedance non-inverting inputs of A1 and A2, providing a very high input impedance ($Z_{in} > 10$ GΩ).
- The voltage across Rg is $(V_1 - V_2)$, and the same current flows through both R1 resistors. This current produces a voltage drop that adds to the input voltages, yielding a differential output from stage 1: $V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (1 + 2R_1/R_g)(V_1 - V_2)$.
- The second stage (A3) is a unity-gain difference amplifier that subtracts the two outputs and provides further common-mode rejection.

**Derivation (brief):** Apply virtual short at A1 and A2 inputs. The current through Rg is $(V_1 - V_2)/R_g$. Voltage at outputs:

$$V_{o1} = V_1 + R_1 \cdot \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}, \qquad V_{o2} = V_2 - R_1 \cdot \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}$$

Subtracting:

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left( 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} \right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

After the unity-gain subtractor:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \left( 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} \right)(V_2 - V_1)}$$

**Why preferred over simple diff amp:**
1. **High Zin** — both inputs see the op-amp non-inverting terminals, eliminating loading of the high-impedance electrode source.
2. **Single-resistor gain control** — gain can be varied by changing only Rg without disturbing the carefully matched resistors of the subtractor (so CMRR is preserved).
3. **High CMRR** — the subtractor stage's resistor matching is decoupled from the gain-setting.
4. **Low noise** — both op-amps of the input stage contribute equally to the differential signal but their common-mode noise is rejected by stage 2.

*(7 marks: 2 diagram, 2 working, 1 derivation, 2 comparison)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Differential gain:**

$$A_d = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} = 1 + \frac{2 \times 25{,}000}{50} = 1 + 1000 = 1001 \approx 1000$$

**(ii) Output for 1.2 mV input:**

$$V_{out} = A_d \times V_{in} = 1001 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 1.2012 \text{ V} \approx 1.2 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Output noise from common-mode interference:**
CMRR in linear scale: $A_{cm} = A_d / 10^{\text{CMRR}/20} = 1001 / 10^{90/20} = 1001 / 10^{4.5} = 1001 / 31{,}623 = 0.0317$

Output CM noise:
