$$V_{out,cm} = A_{cm} \times V_{cm} = 0.0317 \times 5 = 0.158 \text{ V} \approx 158 \text{ mV}$$

This is significant and shows why a notch filter and RLD are essential in practice.

*(7 marks: 2 + 1.5 + 3.5)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the design of an ECG bio-amplifier with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of (i) instrumentation amplifier, (ii) Right-Leg Drive (RLD), and (iii) isolation amplifier in the design.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1st-order RC high-pass filter is used in an ECG amplifier to remove the DC electrode offset. The electrode offset is ±300 mV and the ECG bandwidth starts at 0.05 Hz. (i) If R = 100 kΩ, calculate the required C. (ii) Calculate the attenuation of a 50 Hz mains interference by this HPF. (iii) Why is a separate notch filter still needed at 50 Hz?

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Roles:**

(i) **Instrumentation Amplifier (Front-end):**
- Provides high input impedance to avoid loading the electrode-tissue interface.
- Provides high CMRR (> 100 dB) to reject 50 Hz interference.
- Provides programmable gain (typically 10–100) using a single resistor.
- Buffers the small bioelectric signal (mV/µV) for further processing.

(ii) **Right-Leg Drive (Active Grounding):**
- The third electrode is connected to the output of an auxiliary op-amp (RLD) instead of directly to chassis ground.
- The RLD op-amp senses the common-mode voltage on the patient and drives an equal-and-opposite current back into the body.
- This actively cancels the common-mode voltage, improving the effective CMRR by 20–40 dB.
- Also enhances patient safety by limiting the current that can flow through the patient.

(iii) **Isolation Amplifier (Patient Safety):**
- Provides galvanic isolation (> 4000 V) between the patient-connected circuitry and the mains-powered recording/display equipment.
- Uses optical (LED + photodiode) or transformer coupling to break the electrical path.
- Limits leakage current to < 10 µA (NC) and < 50 µA (SFC) as per IEC 60601-1.
- Prevents macroshock/microshock hazards in case of equipment fault.

*(7 marks: 2 diagram + 5 explanation split across the 3 subsystems)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Required capacitance for $f_c = 0.05$ Hz with R = 100 kΩ:**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C} \implies C = \frac{1}{2 \pi R f_c} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^5 \times 0.05}$$

$$C = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 5000} = \frac{1}{31{,}416} = 31.83 \text{ µF}$$

**Choose standard value: C = 33 µF (electrolytic, but for precision use tantalum).**

**(ii) Attenuation of 50 Hz by 1st-order HPF:**
Transfer function magnitude:

$$\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{f/f_c}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$

For $f = 50$ Hz, $f_c = 0.05$ Hz, $f/f_c = 1000$:

$$\vert H(50) \vert = \frac{1000}{\sqrt{1 + 10^6}} = \frac{1000}{1000.0005} \approx 0.9995$$

In dB: $20 \log_{10}(0.9995) \approx -0.0043$ dB — **essentially no attenuation** at 50 Hz.

**(iii) Why a separate notch filter is still needed:**
The 1st-order HPF is designed to pass the entire ECG bandwidth (≥ 0.05 Hz) up to its LPF cut-off (150 Hz). It is intentionally wide-band, so it cannot reject the 50 Hz mains line interference (which lies in the middle of the pass-band). A **50 Hz notch filter** (band-stop, Q = 5–10) provides 30–50 dB of attenuation precisely at 50 Hz without affecting the rest of the ECG spectrum, which is essential because mains pickup is the dominant noise source in clinical ECG recording.

*(7 marks: 2 + 2.5 + 2.5)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing differential and common-mode gain** — Students often write $A_d = R_2/R_1$ for the 3-op-amp IA, forgetting the $1 + 2R_1/R_g$ pre-stage term. **Always use the full IA gain expression.**
> 2. **CMRR unit error** — CMRR is **always in dB** in the final answer; the linear ratio is intermediate. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for unit mismatch.
> 3. **Bandwidth confusion** — ECG is 0.05–150 Hz, EEG is 0.5–100 Hz, EMG is 20 Hz–5 kHz. Mixing these up costs 1 mark per occurrence.
> 4. **Forgetting RLD** — When asked to "design an ECG amplifier," many students omit the Right-Leg Drive, which is a **mandatory sub-system** for clinical-grade ECG. Deduct 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Patient isolation confusion** — Isolation is for **patient safety**, not just for noise. Citing it as "for better noise rejection" is incomplete.
> 6. **Sign convention** — The output $V_{out} = A_d (V_2 - V_1)$ is the convention; if you define it as $V_1 - V_2$, you get a sign flip — KTU allows either if consistent, but flag it.
> 7. **R1, R2 vs. Rg confusion** — In AD620, $R_1 = 24.7$ kΩ is internal, and gain is set only by Rg. Using the wrong $R_1$ value will yield wrong gain.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ **Bio-amplifier** = front-end signal conditioner for biological signals; sits between electrodes and ADC/display.
- ✅ **Block chain:** Electrodes → Protection → IA → HPF → LPF → Notch → Isolation → Output.
- ✅ **3-Op-Amp IA gain:** $A_d = (1 + 2R_1/R_g)(R_4/R_2)$. Single Rg adjusts gain; subtractor is normally unity gain.
- ✅ **CMRR definition:** $20 \log_{10} \vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert$ in dB. Required ≥ 100 dB for ECG.
- ✅ **Input impedance** of IA is essentially the op-amp's input impedance (10⁹–10¹² Ω) — that's why IA is preferred over diff amp.
- ✅ **ECG band:** 0.05–150 Hz; **EEG band:** 0.5–100 Hz; **EMG band:** 20 Hz–5 kHz.
- ✅ **Typical ECG amplitude:** ~1 mV. **EEG:** ~50 µV. **EMG:** ~100 µV–1 mV.
- ✅ **Right-Leg Drive (RLD/DRL)** is mandatory for clinical ECG; adds 20–40 dB to effective CMRR.
- ✅ **Isolation amplifier** uses optical / transformer / capacitive barrier; limits leakage current < 10 µA.
- ✅ **Patient safety standard:** IEC 60601-1.
- ✅ **High-pass filter** removes DC electrode offset (cut-off ≈ 0.05 Hz for ECG).
- ✅ **Low-pass filter** provides anti-aliasing (cut-off ≈ 150 Hz for ECG).
- ✅ **Notch filter** at 50/60 Hz with Q ≈ 5–10 rejects mains interference.
- ✅ **Input-referred noise** is dominated by op-amp voltage noise $e_n \sqrt{\Delta f}$; must be << signal amplitude.
- ✅ **Common off-the-shelf IA chips:** AD620, AD8221, INA128, INA333, ADS129x (integrated).
- ✅ **Resistor matching** is the primary determinant of CMRR: 0.01 % → ~86 dB; 0.001 % → ~106 dB.
- ✅ **Thermal (Johnson) noise:** $e_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ with $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- ✅ **Gain-bandwidth product (GBW)** of the op-amp must be ≥ $A_d \times f_{max}$ of the signal.
- ✅ **Defibrillation protection** requires external resistors + gas-discharge tubes / MOVs at the input.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Line Interference
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Power Line Interference

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Line Interference

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Line Interference (PLI)**, also termed **Mains Hum** or **AC Line Noise**, is defined as the unwanted electrical disturbance superimposed on a bioelectric signal (e.g., ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG) originating from the alternating current (AC) power distribution network. In India and most of Asia, Europe, Africa, and Australia, the standard mains frequency is **50 Hz**, while in North America it is **60 Hz**. In India, as per the Central Electricity Authority, the nominal supply frequency is **50 Hz ± 0.5 Hz** with a standard voltage of **230 V (single phase)** or **415 V (three phase)**.

The interference manifests as a sinusoidal component at the fundamental mains frequency (and its harmonics) riding on top of the low-amplitude biological signal (typically in the range of **0.5 mV to 5 mV** for ECG, and **5 μV to 200 μV** for EEG), often completely masking the diagnostic waveform if left unfiltered.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Trying to Hear a Whisper in a Crowded Market"**
>
> Imagine you are trying to listen to a friend whispering a secret to you in a bustling marketplace. Your friend's whisper is your **biological signal** (very weak), and the constant hum of generators, neon signs, and air conditioners is the **power line interference**.
>
> The marketplace hum isn't coming directly from your friend; it bleeds into your conversation because:
> 1. The air (the human body) acts as an antenna, picking up the hum from the surroundings (**capacitive coupling**).
> 2. Your friend is touching an electrically charged railing (a grounded wire that has leakage current) (**resistive coupling / ground loops**).
>
> To actually hear the whisper, you need a **noise-canceling headphone** (analogous to a **Notch Filter**), and the microphone (electrodes) must be designed to reject surrounding chatter (**Common Mode Rejection Ratio - CMRR**).

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| Indian Mains Frequency | **50 Hz** | Fundamental interference frequency |
| US Mains Frequency | 60 Hz | For global biomedical equipment design |
| Standard Indian AC Voltage | **230 V RMS** | Single-phase domestic supply |
| ECG Signal Amplitude | **0.5 mV to 5 mV** | Easily corrupted by PLI |
| EEG Signal Amplitude | **5 μV to 200 μV** | Highly vulnerable to PLI |
| PLI Amplitude on Body | Up to several mV | Without shielding, can be 1000× larger than signal |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Sources of Power Line Interference

Power line interference enters a bioelectric measurement system through two fundamental physical mechanisms:

#### A. Capacitive Coupling (Displacement Current Coupling)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The human body and the power line act as the two plates of a **distributed capacitor**. The 230 V AC line creates an alternating electric field, which induces a displacement current into the body.

The induced current is given by:
$$i_{c} = C_{b} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$

Where $C_{b}$ is the body-to-line capacitance (typically **2 to 6 pF**), and $\frac{dV}{dt}$ is the time-derivative of the 50 Hz sinusoidal line voltage.

#### B. Resistive (Galvanic) Coupling / Ground Loops

This occurs due to:
- **Leakage currents** from defective line-powered equipment touching the patient.
- **Ground loops** formed when the patient is connected to multiple instruments, each with a slightly different "ground" potential.
- **Electrode-skin impedance imbalance** between the positive and negative electrodes, which converts common-mode noise into differential-mode noise.

### Mathematical Representation of PLI

The measured signal at the amplifier input is:
$$V_{measured}(t) = V_{bio}(t) + V_{PLI}(t) + V_{noise}(t)$$

Where the power line interference component is:
$$V_{PLI}(t) = A_{50}\sin(2\pi f_{m} t + \phi_{1}) + A_{100}\sin(2\pi \cdot 2f_{m} t + \phi_{2}) + \ldots$$

With $f_{m} = 50$ Hz (fundamental) and $2f_{m} = 100$ Hz (second harmonic, often from rectifier non-linearities).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations**

| Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---------|-------------|------------------------------|
| $V_{PLI} = A \sin(2\pi f_{m} t)$ | Instantaneous PLI voltage at 50 Hz | $f_{m} = 50$ Hz (India) |
| $i_{c} = C_{b} \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | Capacitively coupled displacement current | $C_{b} \approx 2\text{–}6$ pF |
| $CMRR = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common Mode Rejection Ratio | Desired: $\geq 80$ dB (medical grade: $\geq 100$ dB) |
| $A_{dm} = \dfrac{A_{d}}{CMRR} \cdot A_{cm}$ | Differential noise from finite CMRR | $CMRR$ in linear ratio |
| $f_{n} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_{1}R_{2}C_{1}C_{2}}}$ | Notch filter center (notch) frequency | Tuned to $f_{m} = 50$ Hz |
| $Q = \dfrac{f_{n}}{BW}$ | Quality factor of notch filter | Higher $Q$ → narrower notch |
| $Z_{e} = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{e}}$ | Electrode-skin impedance | $\omega = 2\pi \times 50$ rad/s |
| $V_{n,out} = \Delta Z_{e} \cdot I_{cm}$ | Common-mode → differential-mode conversion voltage | $\Delta Z_{e}$ = electrode impedance mismatch |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Power line interference suppression is critical in:
- **ECG Machines** (electrocardiographs) used in hospitals.
- **EEG Systems** for neurological diagnostics.
- **Patient Monitoring Systems** in ICUs.
- **Holter Recorders** for ambulatory cardiac monitoring.
- **Defibrillators and Pacemakers** (EMI immunity testing per IEC 60601-1-2).
- **Medical Telemetry** and wireless biomedical devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Capacitively Coupled Interference Current

We start with the assumption that the human body forms a capacitor with the surrounding power line carrying voltage $V(t) = V_{0}\sin(\omega t)$, where $V_{0} = 230\sqrt{2}$ V (peak) and $\omega = 2\pi f_{m} = 2\pi \times 50$ rad/s.

**Step 1:** Write the expression for the time-varying line voltage.

The instantaneous mains voltage is:
$$V(t) = V_{0}\sin(\omega t) = 325.27 \sin(100\pi t)$$

where $V_{0} = 230\sqrt{2} \approx 325.27$ V is the peak voltage of the 230 V RMS supply.

**Step 2:** Compute the time derivative of the voltage.

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = V_{0} \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

This derivative represents the rate of change of the electric field, which drives the displacement current through the body-to-line capacitance.

**Step 3:** Apply the fundamental capacitor current equation.

For a capacitor, current is the product of capacitance and the rate of change of voltage:
$$i_{c}(t) = C_{b} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the derivative.

$$i_{c}(t) = C_{b} \cdot V_{0} \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 5:** Express in terms of RMS quantities.

Since $V_{0}\omega = 230\sqrt{2} \times 2\pi \times 50$, the peak interference current is:
$$i_{c,peak} = C_{b} \times 230\sqrt{2} \times 100\pi$$

For a typical $C_{b} = 4$ pF:
$$i_{c,peak} = 4 \times 10^{-12} \times 230\sqrt{2} \times 100\pi$$
$$i_{c,peak} \approx 4.085 \times 10^{-7} \text{ A} = 0.4085 \text{ μA}$$

This small current, when flowing through the body-to-ground impedance, develops a measurable interference voltage of several millivolts — easily comparable in magnitude to the ECG signal itself.

### Derivation 2: Common-Mode to Differential-Mode Conversion

When the two electrodes (say, LA and RA in an ECG) have unequal contact impedances, the common-mode voltage $V_{cm}$ is converted into a differential-mode interference voltage.

**Step 1:** Define the electrode impedances.

Let $Z_{e1}$ and $Z_{e2}$ be the impedances of the two electrodes, and $Z_{in}$ be the input impedance of the differential amplifier.

**Step 2:** Express the voltage at the non-inverting input (+) of the amplifier.

Using the voltage divider between the electrode and the common ground:
$$V_{+} = V_{cm} \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_{e1} + Z_{in}}$$

**Step 3:** Express the voltage at the inverting input (−) of the amplifier.

$$V_{-} = V_{cm} \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_{e2} + Z_{in}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the differential input voltage (the interference seen as a signal).

$$V_{dm} = V_{+} - V_{-} = V_{cm} \cdot Z_{in} \left( \frac{1}{Z_{e1} + Z_{in}} - \frac{1}{Z_{e2} + Z_{in}} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Simplify assuming $Z_{in} \gg Z_{e1}, Z_{e2}$.

When the amplifier input impedance is much larger than the electrode impedance, this reduces to:
$$V_{dm} \approx V_{cm} \cdot \frac{\Delta Z_{e}}{Z_{in}}$$

where $\Delta Z_{e} = Z_{e1} - Z_{e2}$ is the imbalance.

**Step 6:** Numerical Example.

For $V_{cm} = 5$ mV, $Z_{in} = 10$ MΩ, and $\Delta Z_{e} = 5$ kΩ (a realistic mismatch):
$$V_{dm} = 5 \text{ mV} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{3}}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 2.5 \text{ μV}$$

This shows that even small mismatches in electrode impedance produce measurable interference.

### Derivation 3: CMRR and Interference Rejection

The Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) is the amplifier's ability to reject signals common to both inputs.

**Step 1:** Define CMRR in linear and dB scales.

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

where $A_{d}$ is the differential gain and $A_{cm}$ is the common-mode gain.

**Step 2:** Express the differential interference at the output.

A finite CMRR converts common-mode voltage into a differential-like output interference:
$$V_{out,interference} = \frac{V_{cm}}{CMRR_{linear}} \times A_{d}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate residual interference for a typical medical amplifier.

For $V_{cm} = 1$ V (large common-mode), $CMRR = 100$ dB (linear ratio = $10^{5}$), and $A_{d} = 1000$:
$$V_{out,interference} = \frac{1 \text{ V}}{10^{5}} \times 1000 = 10 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4:** Compare to desired signal level.

An ECG QRS complex is approximately 1 mV. Even after a 100 dB CMRR, residual interference is 10 mV, which is unacceptable — illustrating why **shielding**, **right-leg drive**, and **notch filters** are all needed in combination.

### Derivation 4: Twin-T Notch Filter Transfer Function

The classic **Twin-T (or Parallel-T) Notch Filter** is widely used for 50 Hz rejection in biomedical amplifiers.

**Step 1:** Define the Twin-T topology.

It consists of two T-networks in parallel:
- One T-network: Two series resistors $R$ and a shunt capacitor $2C$ to ground.
- The other T-network: Two series capacitors $C$ and a shunt resistor $R/2$ to ground.

**Step 2:** Write the transfer function.

For matched components (R, C identical in both paths), the transfer function is:
$$H(s) = \frac{s^{2} + \omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 4\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$

where $\omega_{n} = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ is the notch (resonant) frequency.

**Step 3:** Determine the notch frequency.

Setting $s = j\omega_{n}$ makes the numerator zero, giving infinite attenuation at the notch frequency:
$$f_{n} = \frac{\omega_{n}}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 4:** Tune to 50 Hz.

Solving for $RC$:
$$RC = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50} = 3.183 \times 10^{-3} \text{ seconds}$$

Choose $C = 100$ nF, then $R = 31.83$ kΩ. The components for the second T-network are $2C = 200$ nF and $R/2 = 15.92$ kΩ.

**Step 5:** Quality factor.

For the symmetric Twin-T, $Q \approx 0.25$ without feedback. To sharpen the notch, a positive feedback amplifier is added, increasing $Q$ to typically 5–10.

### Analytical Matrix: PLI Reduction Techniques

| Technique | Typical Attenuation | Cost | Best Used For |
|-----------|---------------------|------|---------------|
| Shielded Cables | 20–40 dB | Moderate | All bioelectric measurements |
| Driven Right-Leg (DRL) | 20–40 dB | Low | ECG, EEG patient safety systems |
| High CMRR Amplifier | 60–100 dB | Moderate | All differential measurements |
| Notch Filter (Twin-T) | 30–50 dB | Low | Fixed-frequency 50 Hz removal |
| Adaptive Notch (ANF) | 40–60 dB | High (DSP) | Frequency-varying PLI |
| Optical Isolation | 80–100 dB | High | Patient-connected equipment |
| Battery Operation | Eliminates conduction | High | Ambulatory/Holter recorders |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Coupling Mechanisms of Power Line Interference

This block diagram shows the two primary paths by which 50 Hz interference enters a biomedical measurement system.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["AC Power Line 230V 50Hz"] --> nodeB["Capacitive Coupling"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Inductive Coupling"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Resistive Ground Loop"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Human Body Acts as Antenna"]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF["Leakage Current Path"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Electrode Skin Interface"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Differential Bio Amplifier Input"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Corrupted Bioelectric Signal Output"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ECG EEG EMG Display"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Diagnostic Decision"]
```

### Diagram 2: ECG Signal Path with Power Line Interference Suppression

This block diagram maps the complete signal-conditioning chain for ECG measurement, showing where each PLI suppression technique is applied.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Patient Body 0.5 to 5 mV ECG"] --> nodeB["Electrodes RA LA LL"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Shielded Lead Wires"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Input Protection and Buffering"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Instrumentation Amplifier High CMRR"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Driven Right Leg Feedback Loop"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Bandpass Filter 0.05 to 150 Hz"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["50 Hz Notch Filter"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Main Amplifier Gain 1000"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ADC and Display"]
    nodeK["Common Mode Voltage"] -.-> nodeF
    nodeL["Right Leg Electrode"] -.-> nodeF
```

### Diagram 3: Twin-T Notch Filter Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Input Signal V in ECG plus 50Hz"] --> nodeB["Twin T Network R 2C"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Twin T Network C R by 2"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Summing Node"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["Op Amp Buffer"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Positive Feedback Path for Q Enhancement"]
    nodeF --> nodeD
    nodeE --> nodeG["Output V out ECG only"]
```

### Diagram 4: Driven Right-Leg Circuit (DRL) Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Common Mode Voltage on Body"] --> nodeB["Summing Amplifier Detects V cm"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Inverting Amplifier with Gain 100"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Current Limiting Resistor 1 Mohm"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Right Leg Electrode"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Feedback Cancels Common Mode"]
    nodeF -.-> nodeB
    nodeG["Patient Safety Clamp"] -.-> nodeE
```

### Diagram 5: Complete PLI Reduction Strategy (Layered Defense)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Layer 1 Shielding and Grounding"] --> nodeB["Layer 2 Balanced Electrodes"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Layer 3 High CMRR Instrumentation Amplifier"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Layer 4 Driven Right Leg Circuit"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Layer 5 Analog Notch Filter 50 Hz"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Layer 6 Digital Adaptive Filtering"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Clean Bioelectric Signal Output"]
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

> Define power line interference. Why is it a critical problem in biomedical signal acquisition?

**Model Answer:**

Power line interference (PLI) is the unwanted 50 Hz (in India) or 60 Hz (in US) sinusoidal noise superimposed on bioelectric signals such as ECG, EEG, and EMG, originating from the AC mains distribution network.

It is critical because:
1. The biological signal amplitudes are very small (μV to low mV), while PLI can be several mV — comparable to or larger than the signal.
2. The 50 Hz frequency falls within the bandwidth of most bioelectric signals (ECG: 0.05–150 Hz, EEG: 0.5–100 Hz), making frequency-domain separation difficult without sophisticated filters.
3. PLI masks the diagnostic features of the waveform (e.g., the P, QRS, T waves of ECG), potentially leading to misdiagnosis.
4. PLI is coupled both capacitively and resistively, requiring multi-pronged suppression strategies.

**[Marks Distribution: Definition 1M, Three reasons 1.5M, Conclusion 0.5M = 3M]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

> List any three sources of power line interference in a typical ECG measurement setup.

**Model Answer:**

The three main sources of PLI in ECG measurement are:

1. **Capacitive coupling** between the patient's body and nearby 230 V AC power lines. The body acts as one plate of a distributed capacitor, picking up displacement currents of order 0.4 μA.

2. **Ground loops** formed when the patient is connected to multiple line-powered instruments (e.g., ECG, defibrillator, infusion pump), each having a slightly different ground potential. The potential difference drives a circulating 50 Hz current through the patient.

3. **Electrode-skin impedance imbalance** between the positive and negative ECG electrodes. Even small mismatches in the order of a few kΩ convert common-mode interference into differential-mode noise, which is amplified along with the ECG signal.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **(a)** Explain the various sources and coupling mechanisms of power line interference in biomedical instrumentation. Discuss capacitive and resistive coupling with relevant equations. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of the **Driven Right-Leg (DRL) circuit** used in modern ECG monitors. Derive an expression for its effectiveness in reducing common-mode interference. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Power line interference originates from the AC mains (230 V, 50 Hz in India) and enters the biomedical measurement system through two main coupling mechanisms:

**1. Capacitive (Displacement Current) Coupling:**

The human body, being a conductive mass, forms a distributed capacitance with the surrounding power line. This body-to-line capacitance $C_{b}$ is typically 2–6 pF. When the line voltage $V(t) = V_{0}\sin(\omega t)$ changes, a displacement current flows into the body:

$$i_{c}(t) = C_{b} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} = C_{b} \cdot V_{0} \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

This current then flows to ground through the patient, developing a 50 Hz voltage across the body impedance (typically a few kΩ), producing an interference of several mV.

**2. Resistive (Galvanic) Coupling:**

This occurs due to leakage currents (typically 10 μA allowed per medical safety standards, IEC 60601-1) from defective line-powered equipment that may come into contact with the patient. It also arises from ground loops when multiple instruments share a patient but have different ground potentials.

**3. Inductive Coupling:**

The 50 Hz current in nearby power cables generates a 50 Hz magnetic field, which induces an EMF in the patient lead wires if they are not twisted and shielded.

The measured signal becomes:
$$V_{measured} = V_{bio} + V_{cm} + \Delta Z_{e} \cdot I_{cm} / Z_{in}$$

where the last term is the differential-mode interference due to electrode imbalance.

**Model Solution (b):**

The Driven Right-Leg (DRL) circuit, originally developed by Dr. Thomas Winter and John Webster, is an active feedback system that actively cancels the common-mode voltage on the patient's body.

**Operation:**

1. The common-mode voltage $V_{cm}$ is detected by the summing junction of the instrumentation amplifier.
2. This $V_{cm}$ is fed to an inverting amplifier with high gain (typically ×100 to ×200).
3. The amplified and inverted common-mode signal is fed back to the patient through the right-leg electrode.
4. This negative feedback drives the patient's body potential toward the amplifier's reference (ground), effectively canceling $V_{cm}$.

**Effectiveness Analysis:**

Let $A_{fb}$ be the gain of the inverting feedback amplifier. The current driven into the body is:
$$I_{fb} = -A_{fb} \cdot V_{cm} / Z_{fb}$$

where $Z_{fb}$ includes the current-limiting resistor (1 MΩ for safety).

By superposition, the new effective common-mode voltage becomes:
$$V_{cm,new} = \frac{V_{cm}}{1 + A_{fb} \cdot Z_{body} / Z_{fb}}$$

For $A_{fb} = 100$ and $Z_{body}/Z_{fb} \approx 0.01$:
$$V_{cm,new} = \frac{V_{cm}}{1 + 1} = \frac{V_{cm}}{2}$$

This shows a 50% reduction; in practice, with a well-designed DRL, the common-mode voltage is reduced to less than 1% of its original value, providing 40 dB additional CMRR enhancement.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a)** With a circuit diagram, derive the transfer function of a **Twin-T notch filter** and show how it is tuned to reject 50 Hz interference. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the role of **CMRR** in reducing power line interference. A bio-amplifier has a differential gain of 1000 and a CMRR of 80 dB. If the common-mode 50 Hz interference is 100 mV, calculate the residual differential interference at the output. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The Twin-T notch filter is a passive RC network that produces a sharp null (notch) at a single frequency. It consists of two parallel T-networks.

**Circuit Topology:**
- T-network 1: Two series resistors $R$, with a shunt capacitor $2C$ to ground at their junction.
- T-network 2: Two series capacitors $C$, with a shunt resistor $R/2$ to ground at their junction.

**Derivation of Transfer Function:**

By solving the nodal equations of the two T-networks, the open-circuit transfer function is:

$$H(s) = \frac{s^{2}R^{2}C^{2} + 1}{s^{2}R^{2}C^{2} + 4sRC + 1}$$

Or in standard form:
$$H(s) = \frac{s^{2} + \omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 4\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$

where $\omega_{n} = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ is the notch frequency.

**Tuning to 50 Hz:**

Setting the numerator to zero gives infinite attenuation at $\omega = \omega_{n}$:
$$f_{n} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

This requires $RC = \dfrac{1}{100\pi} = 3.183$ ms.

Choosing $C = 100$ nF, we get $R = 31.83$ kΩ. The complementary components become $2C = 200$ nF and $R/2 = 15.92$ kΩ.

**Q-Enhancement:**

The unassisted Twin-T has a low $Q$ of about 0.25, giving a very wide notch. A positive feedback op-amp configuration increases $Q$ to 5–10, producing a sharp 50 Hz rejection while leaving adjacent ECG frequencies (e.g., 40 Hz or 60 Hz) untouched.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Role of CMRR:**

Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) is a measure of an amplifier's ability to reject signals that appear simultaneously and in-phase on both input terminals. Since the power line interference is largely common-mode (appearing equally on both electrodes), a high CMRR is essential to prevent it from being amplified as a differential signal.

A bio-amplifier with CMRR of 100 dB rejects 99,999 out of every 100,000 common-mode signals, while one with 60 dB rejects only 999 out of 1000. Medical-grade amplifiers typically have CMRR $\geq$ 100 dB at 50 Hz.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- Differential gain $A_{d} = 1000$
- CMRR = 80 dB
- Common-mode interference $V_{cm} = 100$ mV

**Step 1:** Convert CMRR from dB to linear ratio.

$$CMRR_{linear} = 10^{80/20} = 10^{4} = 10{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Compute the common-mode gain.

$$A_{cm} = \frac{A_{d}}{CMRR_{linear}} = \frac{1000}{10{,}000} = 0.1$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the output common-mode voltage.

$$V_{out,cm} = A_{cm} \times V_{cm} = 0.1 \times 100 \text{ mV} = 10 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4:** Compare with desired signal.

A typical ECG QRS complex is about 1 mV. An output interference of 10 mV is **10 times larger than the signal**, which is unacceptable. This demonstrates why high CMRR alone is insufficient and must be combined with shielding, DRL, and notch filters for acceptable performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistake Alert**
>
> 1. **Formula Mix-up:** Students often confuse $20\log_{10}$ (for voltage ratios like CMRR) with $10\log_{10}$ (for power ratios). For CMRR, which is a voltage ratio, **always use 20**.
> 2. **CMRR Units:** Don't write CMRR as "dB/V" — it is dimensionless and expressed only in dB.
> 3. **Notch Filter Mistake:** Many students write the notch frequency as $\dfrac{1}{RC}$ instead of $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$. The RC time constant gives angular frequency, not Hz.
> 4. **DRL Sign Error:** The feedback to the right leg is **inverted** with respect to the common-mode voltage. Writing it as positive feedback (which would cause oscillation) is a common error.
> 5. **CMRR vs CMR:** Examiners frequently ask for CMRR in dB; do not give the common-mode gain directly without conversion.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power Line Interference**

- ✅ **Frequency:** 50 Hz in India, 60 Hz in USA; the second harmonic (100 Hz/120 Hz) is also common due to rectifier non-linearities.
- ✅ **Two Coupling Mechanisms:** Capacitive (body-to-line, $C_b \approx$ 2–6 pF) and Resistive (leakage + ground loops).
- ✅ **Three Core Symptoms:** Mains hum audible in audio, 50 Hz sinusoidal baseline drift on oscilloscope, QRS distortion in ECG.
- ✅ **Electrode Impedance Mismatch:** Even a 5 kΩ imbalance can convert common-mode noise into differential-mode noise.
- ✅ **CMRR Formula:** $20\log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$; medical-grade requirement is **≥ 100 dB at 50 Hz**.
- ✅ **DRL Circuit:** Senses common-mode voltage, inverts and amplifies it, feeds back to right leg electrode; provides 20–40 dB additional rejection.
- ✅ **Twin-T Notch Filter:** Notch frequency $f_n = 1/(2\pi RC)$; tune $RC = 3.183$ ms for 50 Hz; use op-amp positive feedback to sharpen Q.
- ✅ **Layered Defense:** No single technique is sufficient — combine shielding + balanced electrodes + high CMRR + DRL + notch filter + adaptive filtering.
- ✅ **IEC 60601-1:** Patient leakage current limit is **10 μA** (CF type) and **100 μA** (B type); isolation barrier mandatory.
- ✅ **Adaptive Notch Filters (ANF):** Used when line frequency drifts (e.g., diesel generator backup in hospitals); sample-rate locked notch is preferred.
- ✅ **Battery Operation:** Eliminates galvanic coupling entirely; preferred for Holter and ambulatory recorders.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Band Pass Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Band Pass Filtering # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** is a frequency-selective two-port electronic network designed to transmit signal components lying within a specified band of frequencies (the **passband**) while attenuating (rejecting) all frequency components lying outside this band, namely those below a lower cutoff frequency and above an upper cutoff frequency. The transition regions between the passband and the stopbands are termed the **roll-off** regions. In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a Band Pass Filter is mathematically characterized by its **transfer function H(jω)** which exhibits a magnitude response having a single contiguous passband bounded by: - **Lower Cutoff Frequency (f_L)**: The frequency at which the magnitude response falls to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (≈ **-3 dB**) of its maximum passband value. - **Upper Cutoff Frequency (f_H)**: The upper boundary frequency at which the magnitude again falls to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (-3 dB) of its maximum. The **Bandwidth (BW)** is defined as: $$BW = f_H - f_L \quad \text{(in Hz)}$$ The **Center (Resonant) Frequency (f_0)** is the geometric mean of the two cutoff frequencies: $$f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In biomedical instrumentation, Band Pass Filtering is a *mandatory front-end processing stage* for nearly every biopotential signal (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG). Biological signals are always contaminated by baseline wander (low frequency, < 0.5 Hz) and high-frequency powerline/muscle noise (> 40 Hz), which the BPF removes simultaneously. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition Imagine a **window with adjustable shutters** mounted on a wall. The lower shutter blocks everything below a certain height (low frequencies), and the upper shutter blocks everything above a certain height (high frequencies). Only objects of "just the right size" (the desired frequency band) can pass through the open middle section. A more relatable analogy is a **toll booth for cars**: - **Bicycles (very low frequencies like baseline drift)** are stopped by the first gate. - **Oversized trucks (very high frequencies like high-frequency EMG noise)** are stopped by the second gate. - **Only standard cars (the target signal band)** are allowed to pass through both gates. In biomedical terms: - **ECG signal (0.05 Hz – 150 Hz)** → BPF passes only this range. - **EEG signal (0.5 Hz – 30 Hz)** → BPF tuned to this narrow band. - **EMG signal (20 Hz – 500 Hz)** → BPF tuned to this wider band. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | |---|---|---| | Passband Gain (Mid-band) | $A_{mid}$ | Typically 1 to 10 (unitless) | | Cutoff Attenuation | $A_c$ | **-3 dB** (≈ 0.707 of $A_{max}$) | | Stopband Attenuation | $A_s$ | **-20 dB/decade** (1st order) | | Quality Factor | Q | Dimensionless | | Roll-off Rate | — | 20, 40, 60 dB/decade | > [!NOTE] > A **-3 dB** point is the universally accepted definition of a cutoff frequency in KTU board examinations. Always quote the cutoff at the **half-power point** (since $10 \log_{10}(0.5) = -3 \text{ dB}$). --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Two Building Blocks A Band Pass Filter is fundamentally realized as the **cascade (series) connection** of two fundamental filter sections: 1. **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** — Eliminates low-frequency noise and DC offsets. 2. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** — Eliminates high-frequency noise and interference. The overall transfer function is the **product** of the individual transfer functions: $$H_{BPF}(j\omega) = H_{HPF}(j\omega) \cdot H_{LPF}(j\omega)$$ This multiplicative property in the frequency domain is the cornerstone of BPF design. ## 2.2 Magnitude Response Characteristics A typical BPF magnitude response exhibits four distinct regions: | Region | Frequency Range | Behaviour | Clinical Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | Lower Stopband | $0 \le f < f_L$ | Attenuation increases as $f \to 0$ | Removes baseline wander, DC offset, electrode half-cell potentials | | Passband | $f_L \le f \le f_H$ | Maximum (flat) gain $A_{mid}$ | Preserves the diagnostic biopotential | | Upper Stopband | $f > f_H$ | Attenuation increases as $f \to \infty$ | Removes mains interference (50/60 Hz), muscle artifact, RF noise | | Roll-off regions | Near $f_L$ and $f_H$ | Transition slope 20n dB/decade | Defines selectivity | ## 2.3 Quality Factor (Q) — Selectivity Metric The **Quality Factor (Q)** quantifies the "sharpness" or "selectivity" of a band pass filter: $$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{f_0}{f_H - f_L}$$ - **High Q** (Q > 10) → Narrow, sharply peaked response → Used in **resonance-based extraction** (e.g., extracting a specific frequency component from a noisy signal). - **Low Q** (Q < 1) → Wide, flat-topped response → Used in **broadband biopotential acquisition** (ECG, EEG). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | Hz | Width of passband | | 2 | Center Frequency | $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$ | Hz | Geometric mean of cutoffs | | 3 | Quality Factor | $Q = f_0 / BW$ | Dimensionless | Higher Q = sharper filter | | 4 | -3 dB Cutoff | $\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = A_{max}/\sqrt{2}$ | — | Definition of cutoff | | 5 | 1st order LPF | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | For simple RC section | | 6 | 1st order HPF | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | Same RC expression | | 7 | Wide-band BPF BW | $BW \approx f_L + f_H$ (approx.) | Hz | Only if $f_L \ll f_H$ | | 8 | Cascade n filters | $\vert H \vert_{dB}^{total} = \sum \vert H \vert_{dB}^i$ | dB | dB gains add | | 9 | 2nd order Resonance | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz | RLC resonator | | 10 | 2nd order BPF | $H(s) = \dfrac{Ks}{s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}s + \omega_0^2}$ | — | General 2nd-order BPF | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Students often confuse bandwidth and center frequency. Remember: **BW is the WIDTH (subtraction)**, while **$f_0$ is the MIDDLE (geometric mean)**. The two are related through $Q$, but they are physically different quantities. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ECG Diagnostic BPF**: $f_L = 0.05$ Hz, $f_H = 150$ Hz — Used in patient monitors and Holter devices. - **EEG Clinical BPF**: $f_L = 0.5$ Hz, $f_H = 30$ Hz — Used in polysomnography and epilepsy monitoring. - **EMG Surface BPF**: $f_L = 20$ Hz, $f_H = 500$ Hz — Used in prosthetics and rehabilitation. - **Pulse Oximeter BPF**: $f_L = 0.5$ Hz, $f_H = 5$ Hz — Extracts the pulsatile component. - **Auditory Evoked Potential (AEP) BPF**: $f_L = 100$ Hz, $f_H = 3000$ Hz — For brainstem response. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Cascaded (Wide-Band) Band Pass Filter Transfer Function ### Step 1: Define the First-Order High-Pass Transfer Function A standard first-order RC high-pass filter has the transfer function: $$H_{HPF}(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Logic:** The series capacitor blocks DC, and as frequency increases, the impedance of the capacitor ($1/j\omega C$) decreases, allowing higher frequencies to pass. ### Step 2: Define the First-Order Low-Pass Transfer Function A standard first-order RC low-pass filter has the transfer function: $$H_{LPF}(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Logic:** The shunt capacitor shorts high frequencies to ground, while low frequencies pass through the resistor. ### Step 3: Cascade the Two Sections For a cascade, the overall transfer function is the product: $$H_{BPF}(j\omega) = H_{HPF}(j\omega) \cdot H_{LPF}(j\omega)$$ Substituting: $$H_{BPF}(j\omega) = \left(\frac{j\omega R_1 C_1}{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}\right)$$ Therefore: $$H_{BPF}(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega R_1 C_1}{(1 + j\omega R_1 C_1)(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)}$$ ### Step 4: Identify the Two Cutoff Frequencies The lower cutoff is set by the HPF section: $$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$$ The upper cutoff is set by the LPF section: $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$$ **Logic:** Each RC section independently creates a -3 dB point, and since they are non-interacting (when buffered), the lower one defines $f_L$ and the upper one defines $f_H$. ### Step 5: Magnitude Response $$\vert H_{BPF}(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega R_1 C_1}{\sqrt{(1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2)(1 + (\omega R_2 C_2)^2)}}$$ ### Step 6: Bandwidth and Quality Factor The bandwidth of this **wide-band BPF** (where $f_L \ll f_H$) is approximated as: $$BW = f_H - f_L$$ The center frequency (geometric mean) is: $$f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$$ The Q-factor is: $$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{\sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}}{f_H - f_L}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) **Problem:** Design a band pass filter for an **ECG signal** with lower cutoff $f_L = 0.05$ Hz and upper cutoff $f_H = 150$ Hz. Choose $C_1 = C_2 = 1 \mu F$ and $C_3 = C_4 = 47$ nF. Compute all resistors and the Q-factor. ### Step 1: Compute the HPF Resistor (R₁) $$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$$ Solving for $R_1$: $$R_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi f_L C_1}$$ $$R_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.05 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R_1 = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-7}}$$ $$R_1 \approx 3.18 \text{ M}\Omega$$ ### Step 2: Compute the LPF Resistor (R₂) $$R_2 = \frac{1}{2\pi f_H C_2}$$ $$R_2 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 150 \times 47 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$R_2 = \frac{1}{4.430 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$R_2 \approx 22.57 \text{ k}\Omega$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Bandwidth $$BW = f_H - f_L = 150 - 0.05 = 149.95 \text{ Hz} \approx 150 \text{ Hz}$$ ### Step 4: Compute the Center Frequency $$f_0 = \sqrt{0.05 \times 150} = \sqrt{7.5} \approx 2.739 \text{ Hz}$$ ### Step 5: Compute the Q-Factor $$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{2.739}{149.95} \approx 0.0183$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The Q is very small (< 1), confirming that the ECG BPF is a **wide-band, broad-passband** filter — exactly what is needed clinically to preserve waveform morphology. ### Step 6: Phase Response (For Completeness) The phase angle of the BPF is: $$\phi(\omega) = 90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega R_1 C_1) - \tan^{-1}(\omega R_2 C_2)$$ At the center frequency $f_0 = 2.74$ Hz: $$\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 2.739 = 17.21 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\phi(\omega_0) = 90° - \tan^{-1}(17.21 \times 3.18 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-6}) - \tan^{-1}(17.21 \times 22.57 \times 10^3 \times 47 \times 10^{-9})$$ $$\phi(\omega_0) = 90° - \tan^{-1}(54.7) - \tan^{-1}(0.0183)$$ $$\phi(\omega_0) = 90° - 88.95° - 1.05° = 0°$$ **Logic:** At the geometric center, the HPF and LPF phase contributions cancel, yielding 0° phase shift (the signal is undistorted in time-domain). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Choices by Biomedical Application | Application | $f_L$ (Hz) | $f_H$ (Hz) | BW (Hz) | Q | Design Priority | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | ECG (Diagnostic) | 0.05 | 150 | 149.95 | 0.018 | Wide passband, low Q | | ECG (Monitor) | 0.5 | 40 | 39.5 | 0.2 | Moderate passband | | EEG | 0.5 | 30 | 29.5 | 0.32 | Wide passband | | EMG (Surface) | 20 | 500 | 480 | 0.73 | Wide passband | | EOG | 0.1 | 10 | 9.9 | 0.32 | Very low-frequency focus | | AEP | 100 | 3000 | 2900 | 4.67 | Higher Q | | Heart Sound (PCG) | 20 | 2000 | 1980 | 0.39 | Audio range | | Fetal ECG | 0.05 | 100 | 99.95 | 0.2 | Reject maternal noise | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Band Pass Filter The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a wide-band BPF realized as a cascade of HPF and LPF sections, with explicit annotations for each biomedical signal processing stage. ```mermaid flowchart LR inputNode["INPUT SIGNAL
Biomedical Waveform
with Noise"] --> hpfNode["HPF SECTION
C in series, R to ground
fL = 1 divided by 2pi R1 C1"] hpfNode --> bufferNode["BUFFER AMPLIFIER
Unity Gain Op-Amp
Prevents Loading"] bufferNode --> lpfNode["LPF SECTION
R in series, C to ground
fH = 1 divided by 2pi R2 C2"] lpfNode --> gainNode["MID-BAND AMPLIFIER
Gain A_mid
Restores Signal Level"] gainNode --> outputNode["FILTERED OUTPUT
Clean Biopotential
fL to fH Band"] style inputNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style hpfNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style bufferNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style lpfNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style gainNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style outputNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Frequency Response of an Ideal vs. Practical Band Pass Filter ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph idBlock["IDEAL BPF RESPONSE"] idA["At f less than fL: Output equals ZERO"] idB["At fL to fH: Output equals UNITY"] idC["At f greater than fH: Output equals ZERO"] end subgraph prBlock["PRACTICAL BPF RESPONSE"] prA["At f less than fL: Roll-off slope minus 20 dB per decade"] prB["At fL to fH: Flat passband, gain A_mid"] prC["At f greater than fH: Roll-off slope minus 20 dB per decade"] end style idBlock fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366 style prBlock fill:#FFF0E6,stroke:#663300 ``` ## 4.3 Frequency Response Curve (Annotated Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR axisNode["FREQUENCY AXIS
f in Hz on log scale"] --> fL["fL
Lower Cutoff
minus 3 dB"] fL --> f0["f0
Center Frequency
sqrt fL times fH"] f0 --> fH["fH
Upper Cutoff
minus 3 dB"] fH --> stop["STOPBAND
Attenuation Region"] fL -.->|-3 dB point| marker1["Lower Transition"] f0 -.->|Peak gain| marker2["Passband Center"] fH -.->|-3 dB point| marker3["Upper Transition"] style axisNode fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#333 style fL fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333 style f0 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style fH fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333 style stop fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.4 Bandwidth and Q-Factor Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TB centerNode["f0 = sqrt of fL times fH"] --> bwCalc["BW = fH minus fL"] centerNode --> qCalc["Q = f0 divided by BW"] qCalc --> qHigh["Q greater than 10: NARROW BAND
High Selectivity
Used for Resonance Extraction"] qCalc --> qMid["Q between 1 and 10: MEDIUM BAND
Moderate Selectivity
Used for Tone Detection"] qCalc --> qLow["Q less than 1: WIDE BAND
Low Selectivity
Used for ECG, EEG, EMG"] style centerNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style qHigh fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333 style qMid fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333 style qLow fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.5 Biomedical Signal Processing Pipeline (Application View) ```mermaid flowchart LR patient["PATIENT
Body Surface"] --> electrodes["ELECTRODES
Transduce IONIC to ELECTRONIC"] electrodes --> preamp["PREAMPLIFIER
INA, Gain 1000"] preamp --> hpf["HPF
fL = 0.05 to 0.5 Hz
Removes DC and Drift"] hpf --> bpf["BAND PASS FILTER
fL to fH range
Isolates Biopotential"] bpf --> amp["MAIN AMPLIFIER
Gain 100 to 10000"] amp --> adc["ADC
Sampling at 2 times fH"] adc --> dsp["DSP MICROCONTROLLER
Feature Extraction"] style patient fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333 style electrodes fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333 style preamp fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333 style hpf fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333 style bpf fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style amp fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 style adc fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style dsp fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Short-Answer Conceptual (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q:** Define a Band Pass Filter. Mention its two essential cutoff frequencies and explain the significance of bandwidth in biomedical signal processing. **Model Answer:** A **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** is a frequency-selective electronic network that allows signals within a specific frequency range to pass while attenuating frequencies below and above this range. The two essential cutoff frequencies are: 1. **Lower Cutoff Frequency ($f_L$)**: The frequency below which signal components are attenuated (typically at the **-3 dB** point). 2. **Upper Cutoff Frequency ($f_H$)**: The frequency above which signal components are attenuated (also at the **-3 dB** point). The **Bandwidth (BW)** is defined as: $$BW = f_H - f_L$$ **Significance in Biomedical Signal Processing:** - Bandwidth determines the **range of physiologically relevant frequencies** captured. - A wider BW preserves waveform morphology (e.g., QRS complex in ECG) but admits more noise. - A narrower BW improves SNR but may distort the signal. - For ECG, BW is typically **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (≈ 150 Hz wide) to capture all P-QRS-T components while rejecting baseline wander and high-frequency interference. --- ### Question 2: Short-Answer Definition (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q:** What is meant by Quality Factor (Q) of a Band Pass Filter? State its mathematical expression and explain the difference between a high-Q and low-Q filter with one biomedical example each. **Model Answer:** The **Quality Factor (Q)** of a band pass filter is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the **selectivity** or "sharpness" of the filter's passband. It is defined as the ratio of the center frequency to the bandwidth: $$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{\sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}}{f_H - f_L}$$ **High-Q Filter (Q > 10):** - Produces a **narrow, sharply peaked** frequency response. - Highly selective — passes only a very tight band of frequencies. - **Biomedical Example:** Auditory Brainstem Response (ABR) extraction requires a narrow BPF centered at 1 kHz with Q ≈ 5-10 to isolate specific neural responses. **Low-Q Filter (Q < 1):** - Produces a **wide, flat-topped** frequency response. - Passes a broad range of frequencies. - **Biomedical Example:** ECG monitoring uses a wide BPF (Q ≈ 0.02) from 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz to preserve the entire QRS complex. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A: Design-Oriented Problem (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2 Choice Q1]`** **Q:** Design a first-order wide-band Band Pass Filter using RC sections for extracting an **EEG signal** contaminated with baseline wander (< 0.5 Hz) and 50 Hz mains interference. **Specifications:** - Lower cutoff frequency: $f_L = 0.5$ Hz - Upper cutoff frequency: $f_H = 30$ Hz - Choose $C_1 = C_2 = 1 \mu F$ and $C_2 = C_3 = 0.1 \mu F$ **Tasks:** - **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of the cascade BPF and compute the required resistor values for the HPF and LPF sections. **(7 Marks)** - **(b)** Calculate the bandwidth, center frequency, and Q-factor of the designed filter. Comment on the suitability of this filter for EEG acquisition. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Part (a) — Circuit and Resistor Computation (7 Marks) **Step 1: Circuit Topology** The BPF is realized as a cascade of: - **HPF section**: Series capacitor $C_1$ followed by resistor $R_1$ to ground. - **LPF section**: Series resistor $R_2$ followed by capacitor $C_2$ to ground. - An **op-amp buffer** is inserted between the two sections to prevent loading (impedance interaction). **Step 2: Compute $R_1$ for the HPF Section** Given $f_L = 0.5$ Hz and $C_1 = 1 \mu F$: $$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$$ Solving for $R_1$: $$R_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi f_L C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.5 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R_1 = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}} = 318.31 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 3: Compute $R_2$ for the LPF Section** Given $f_H = 30$ Hz and $C_2 = 0.1 \mu F$: $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$$ Solving for $R_2$: $$R_2 = \frac{1}{2\pi f_H C_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 30 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R_2 = \frac{1}{1.885 \times 10^{-5}} = 53.05 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 4: Practical Component Selection** - $R_1 \approx 318$ kΩ (use standard 330 kΩ) - $R_2 \approx 53$ kΩ (use standard 56 kΩ) **Step 5: Verify Cutoff Frequencies with Practical Values** $$f_L^{actual} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 330 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.482 \text{ Hz}$$ $$f_H^{actual} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 56 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 28.42 \text{ Hz}$$ These are within clinical tolerance for EEG. --- #### Model Solution to Part (b) — Bandwidth, $f_0$, Q, and Clinical Commentary (7 Marks) **Step 1: Compute Bandwidth** $$BW = f_H - f_L = 30 - 0.5 = 29.5 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2: Compute Center Frequency** $$f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H} = \sqrt{0.5 \times 30} = \sqrt{15} \approx 3.873 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 3: Compute Q-Factor** $$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{3.873}{29.5} \approx 0.131$$ **Step 4: Clinical Commentary on Suitability** The designed filter is **highly suitable** for EEG acquisition for the following reasons: 1. **Low Q (Q ≈ 0.13)** indicates a **wide passband**, which is essential for EEG since the signal contains multiple sub-bands (delta: 0.5-4 Hz, theta: 4-8 Hz, alpha: 8-13 Hz, beta: 13-30 Hz) that must all be preserved. 2. **The 0.5 Hz HPF** effectively removes baseline wander due to patient movement and electrode polarization, while **the 30 Hz LPF** eliminates the 50 Hz mains interference and high-frequency muscle (EMG) artifacts. 3. **Wide bandwidth (29.5 Hz)** preserves the temporal resolution of the EEG waveform, which is critical for identifying transient events like epileptic spikes. 4. The filter exhibits a **low Q**, which corresponds to a smooth, flat-topped response — ideal for broadband biopotential recording. **Conclusion:** The designed first-order BPF with $R_1 = 318$ kΩ and $R_2 = 53$ kΩ meets all the clinical specifications for EEG signal extraction. --- ### Question B: Theoretical and Analytical Problem (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2 Choice Q2]`** **Q:** For a wide-band Band Pass Filter realized by cascading a first-order HPF (with $R_1 = 10$ kΩ, $C_1 = 1 \mu F$) and a first-order LPF (with $R_2 = 1$ kΩ, $C_2 = 0.1 \mu F$): **Tasks:** - **(a)** Derive the overall transfer function $H_{BPF}(j\omega)$ and identify the expressions for $f_L$ and $f_H$. **(7 Marks)** - **(b)** Calculate the bandwidth, center frequency, Q-factor, and the -3 dB points. Discuss why this filter is suitable (or not) for ECG signal acquisition. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Part (a) — Transfer Function Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1: Standard First-Order HPF Transfer Function** For a passive RC high-pass filter with $R_1$ and $C_1$: $$H_{HPF}(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_1}} = \frac{j\omega R_1 C_1}{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}$$ **Logic:** The output is taken across $R_1$. At DC, the capacitor is open (infinite impedance), so output is zero. At high frequencies, the capacitor is a short, so output equals input. **Step 2: Standard First-Order LPF Transfer Function** For a passive RC low-pass filter with $R_2$ and $C_2$: $$H_{LPF}(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{\frac{1}{j\omega C_2}}{R_2 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}$$ **Logic:** The output is taken across $C_2$. At DC, the capacitor is open, so output equals input. At high frequencies, the capacitor is a short, so output is zero. **Step 3: Cascade the Two Sections** Assuming ideal buffering between sections (no loading effect): $$H_{BPF}(j\omega) = H_{HPF}(j\omega) \cdot H_{LPF}(j\omega)$$ $$H_{BPF}(j\omega) = \left(\frac{j\omega R_1 C_1}{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}\right)$$ Therefore: $$H_{BPF}(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega R_1 C_1}{(1 + j\omega R_1 C_1)(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)}$$ **Step 4: Identify the Cutoff Frequencies** The pole of the HPF section gives the lower cutoff: $$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$$ The pole of the LPF section gives the upper cutoff: $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$$ --- #### Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Computation and Clinical Discussion (7 Marks) **Step 1: Compute $f_L$** $$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{6.283 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 15.92 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2: Compute $f_H$** $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{6.283 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1591.5 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 3: Compute Bandwidth** $$BW = f_H - f_L = 1591.5 - 15.92 = 1575.58 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 4: Compute Center Frequency** $$f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H} = \sqrt{15.92 \times 1591.5} = \sqrt{25337} \approx 159.2 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 5: Compute Q-Factor** $$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{159.2}{1575.58} \approx 0.101$$ **Step 6: Identify the -3 dB Points** - Lower -3 dB point: $f_L \approx 15.92$ Hz - Upper -3 dB point: $f_H \approx 1591.5$ Hz **Step 7: Clinical Discussion** **Suitability Analysis for ECG:** | Requirement | ECG Specification | This Filter | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | Lower cutoff | Should be 0.05 - 0.5 Hz | 15.92 Hz | **NOT SUITABLE** — too high; will distort ST segment and reject baseline but lose low-frequency P and T waves | | Upper cutoff | Should be 100 - 150 Hz | 1591.5 Hz | Excessive; admits high-frequency noise | | Bandwidth | 100 - 150 Hz | 1575.58 Hz | **Too wide** | | Q-factor | Low (broadband) | 0.101 | Acceptable (low Q is good) | **Conclusion:** This filter is **NOT suitable** for ECG acquisition because: 1. The lower cutoff of 15.92 Hz is too high — it will **eliminate the P and T waves** (which contain frequencies below 15 Hz) and **distort the ST segment**, leading to misdiagnosis of ischemia. 2. The upper cutoff of 1591.5 Hz is **far too high** — it will allow high-frequency EMG and radio-frequency interference to contaminate the signal. **Recommended Redesign:** To make this filter ECG-suitable, the lower cutoff should be reduced to 0.05 Hz (requiring $R_1 \approx 3.18$ MΩ with $C_1 = 1 \mu F$), and the upper cutoff should be reduced to 150 Hz (requiring $R_2 \approx 10.6$ kΩ with $C_2 = 0.1 \mu F$). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Examinations:** > > 1. **Confusing "cutoff" with "bandwidth"**: Students often write "$f_L = 150$ Hz" and "$f_H = 0.05$ Hz" reversed. Always remember: $f_L < f_H$ by definition. > > 2. **Forgetting the 2π factor**: The cutoff frequency formula is $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$, **not** $f_c = \frac{1}{RC}$. This single omission can cost **2-3 marks** per problem. > > 3. **Ignoring loading effect**: When cascading passive RC sections without a buffer, the second section loads the first, shifting the effective cutoffs. KTU expects students to mention **buffering** for full marks. > > 4. **Wrong unit for Q-factor**: Q is **dimensionless**. Writing Q = 0.5 Hz (with units) is a common error that loses marks. > > 5. **Missing the -3 dB justification**: When asked to "find the cutoff frequency," students often skip the step of showing where $\vert H \vert$ falls to $1/\sqrt{2}$. Always state: "At cutoff, $\vert H \vert = A_{max}/\sqrt{2}$." > > 6. **Geometric vs. Arithmetic Mean**: The center frequency is the **geometric mean** $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L f_H}$, not the arithmetic mean $\frac{f_L + f_H}{2}$. This is a frequent trap. > > 7. **No clinical context**: KTU values application-oriented answers. Always relate the filter design to the **specific biomedical signal** (ECG, EEG, EMG) and its **diagnostic requirements**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Band Pass Filtering":** - **Definition**: BPF passes a band between $f_L$ and $f_H$; rejects all other frequencies. - **Architecture**: Cascade of **HPF** (sets $f_L$) and **LPF** (sets $f_H$) with a buffer between. - **Transfer Function**: $H_{BPF}(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega R_1 C_1}{(1 + j\omega R_1 C_1)(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)}$ - **Lower Cutoff**: $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ - **Upper Cutoff**: $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$ - **Bandwidth**: $BW = f_H - f_L$ (in Hz) - **Center Frequency**: $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$ (geometric mean) - **Quality Factor**: $Q = f_0 / BW$ (dimensionless; high Q = narrow band, low Q = wide band) - **-3 dB Definition**: Cutoff occurs when $\vert H \vert$ falls to $A_{max} / \sqrt{2}$ (half-power point). - **Roll-off Rate**: First-order BPF = **20 dB/decade** (10 dB on each side). - **ECG BPF**: $f_L = 0.05$ Hz, $f_H = 150$ Hz, $Q \approx 0.018$ (wide-band). - **EEG BPF**: $f_L = 0.5$ Hz, $f_H = 30$ Hz, $Q \approx 0.32$ (wide-band). - **EMG BPF**: $f_L = 20$ Hz, $f_H = 500$ Hz, $Q \approx 0.73$ (wide-band). - **Buffering is Mandatory**: Use an op-amp voltage follower between passive RC sections to prevent loading and preserve cutoffs. - **Geometric Mean vs. Arithmetic Mean**: $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L f_H}$, not $\frac{f_L + f_H}{2}$. - **Clinical Impact**: BPF is the **mandatory signal-conditioning stage** in all biopotential amplifiers to remove baseline wander (low-frequency) and noise/interference (high-frequency). - **Wide-Band vs. Narrow-Band**: Biomedical BPFs are almost always wide-band (Q < 1); narrow-band BPFs are used only in specific resonance applications. - **Cascading for Higher Order**: To achieve sharper roll-off (40, 60 dB/decade), cascade multiple first-order sections or use active second-order filters (Sallen-Key, Multiple Feedback). - **Phase Response**: At $f_0$, the total phase shift is **0°** (HPF contributes +90°, LPF contributes -90°). --- > **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — BAND PASS FILTERING** > **Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme — OEICT723 Biomedical Instrumentation**

topics.title: Working of Isolation Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Working of Isolation Amplifiers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Isolation Amplifier (ISO Amp):** A specialized differential amplifier that provides **complete galvanic isolation** (zero direct electrical conduction path) between its **input stage**, **output stage**, and **power supply stage**, while accurately transmitting the input signal from the source to the load through a non-conductive coupling mechanism such as **transformer (magnetic) coupling**, **optical coupling**, or **capacitive coupling**. In the context of **KTU OEICT723 – Biomedical Instrumentation (Module 2)**, the isolation amplifier is taught as the **primary patient-protection interface** placed between the biological signal source (electrodes attached to the patient) and the rest of the signal processing/recording chain. It performs three critical functions simultaneously: 1. **Signal amplification** of the biopotential (µV to mV range). 2. **Galvanic isolation** of the patient from mains/ground hazards (typically rated at **1500 V – 5000 V RMS** continuous isolation voltage). 3. **High Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** rejection, typically **> 100 dB** at 50/60 Hz mains frequency, to suppress 50/60 Hz power-line interference picked up by the body. > [!NOTE] > **Galvanic Isolation** literally means "no metallic (galvanic) path." The signal is forced to "jump" across a non-metallic barrier, just like a person handing a note across a wide river using a drone (the drone represents the modulation/coupling mechanism). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Non-Contact Mailman" Imagine you are standing on a **high-voltage balcony** and need to deliver a sealed letter to your friend standing on a **different building's balcony** below. You have the following restriction: **you cannot touch the other balcony, cannot throw a wire, and cannot walk down the stairs** (because of an electrical hazard). Your only option is to: 1. **Write the message** (input signal). 2. **Encode it on a light beam** or **sound wave** (modulation onto a carrier). 3. **Send it across the gap** (the isolation barrier). 4. **Your friend decodes it** (demodulation) and reads the letter (output signal). This is **exactly** how an isolation amplifier works. The "letter" is the patient's ECG signal (≈ 1 mV). The "light beam/sound wave" is the modulated high-frequency carrier (typically **50 kHz – 500 kHz**). The "gap" is the isolation barrier. The friend is the output load that records/processes the signal. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic to remember the 3-port isolation principle:** **I-P-O → Input–Power–Output**. In a true isolation amplifier, **all three ports are mutually isolated** from each other. This is the gold-standard definition examiners look for. --- ## 1.3 Why is an Isolation Amplifier *Mandatory* in Biomedical Instrumentation? | Clinical Hazard | Without Isolation Amp | With Isolation Amp | |---|---|---| | **Leakage current to patient** (during defibrillation / faulty equipment) | Could exceed **10 µA** (lethal threshold is **10 µA AC / 10 µA DC** per IEC 60601) | Reduced to **< 10 µA** by barrier | | **Mains ground loops** (50/60 Hz interference) | Severe contamination of ECG/EEG | Rejected by **> 100 dB CMRR** | | **Patient-to-mains voltage** | Patient can become the return path of fault current | No galvanic path exists | | **Equipment damage from defibrillator shocks (≈ 5000 V)** | Front-end ADC/digital circuits destroyed | Isolation barrier absorbs the transient | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **standard threshold of human perception current is 1 mA**, the **"let-go" threshold is 10 mA**, and **ventricular fibrillation begins at approximately 50 mA AC (50/60 Hz)**. Isolation amplifiers are engineered to limit leakage to **< 10 µA** — a **5000× safety margin** below the fibrillation threshold. --- ## 1.4 Three Categories of Isolation Coupling Mechanisms | Coupling Type | Principle | Frequency Band | Typical Isolation Voltage | Example IC | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Transformer (Inductive)** | Magnetic flux coupling through a small toroidal transformer | DC – 60 kHz | 1500 – 5000 V RMS | AD202, AD210, ISO122 | | **Optical** | LED–phototransistor / LED–photodiode light coupling | DC – 100 kHz | 2500 – 5000 V RMS | HCPL-7800, ISO100 | | **Capacitive** | High-frequency AC signal coupling through silicon-dioxide dielectric | DC – 1 MHz | 2500 – 5000 V RMS | ISO124, ADuM3151 | All three are **KTU-relevant**. The **transformer-coupled (carrier-modulation)** type is the most commonly drawn and asked in the exam. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Transformer-Coupled Isolation Amplifier The working of a classical isolation amplifier (e.g., the **Burr-Brown ISO122 / Analog Devices AD202**) is best understood as a **five-stage signal chain**: ``` [INPUT STAGE] → [MODULATOR] → [ISOLATION BARRIER] → [DEMODULATOR] → [OUTPUT STAGE] (IA) (Chopper) (Transformer) (Sync Det.) (Op-Amp) ``` ### Stage-by-Stage Explanation 1. **Input Stage — Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** - Accepts the differential biopotential (e.g., ECG: V₁ - V₂). - Provides **high input impedance (> 10¹² Ω)** so it does not load the patient/electrode interface. - Provides **first-stage voltage gain G₁** (typically 1 to 100, set by an external resistor). - Performs **initial CMRR enhancement** (≈ 80 dB). - Output is a low-level, single-ended DC signal centered around 0 V. 2. **Modulator (Chopper / Voltage-to-Frequency Converter)** - Converts the **DC** input signal into an **AC carrier signal** whose amplitude (or duty cycle) is proportional to the input voltage. - Typical carrier frequency **f_c ≈ 50 kHz – 500 kHz** (e.g., ISO122 uses 500 kHz). - Modulation schemes used: - **Amplitude Modulation (AM) with suppressed carrier (DSC-SC)** - **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM)** - **Voltage-to-Frequency Conversion (VFC)** 3. **Isolation Barrier — Miniature Pulse Transformer** - The modulated AC signal passes through a **thin-film on-chip toroidal transformer**. - The transformer's primary and secondary windings are separated by a **high-dielectric polyimide/ceramic layer**. - Provides **galvanic isolation** while allowing magnetic flux (and hence the modulated signal) to cross. - The **isolation voltage rating** is determined by the dielectric withstanding voltage of this layer. 4. **Demodulator (Synchronous Detector / Envelope Detector)** - Recovers the original DC signal from the modulated AC waveform. - Uses a **phase-locked demodulator** synchronized to the same carrier frequency. - Removes the high-frequency carrier component, leaving only the baseband biopotential. 5. **Output Stage — Low-Offset Op-Amp / Buffer** - Provides the final **output gain G₂** (typically unity or low gain). - Drives the ADC, recorder, or display device. - Output is a **single-ended, low-impedance signal referenced to the OUTPUT ground** (which is *different* from the INPUT ground — this is the whole point). > [!NOTE] > A **separate isolated DC-DC converter** (e.g., a small high-frequency inverter + rectifier + linear regulator) generates the **floating ±15 V supply for the input stage** from the output-side +5 V or ±15 V rail. This ensures the input stage itself floats relative to the output ground, completing the **3-port isolation**. --- ## 2.2 The Carrier Modulation Process — Mathematical View Let the input biopotential be $V_{in}(t) = V_s(t)$, a low-frequency (0.05 Hz – 1 kHz) signal. The modulator multiplies it by a square-wave carrier $c(t)$ of frequency $f_c$: $$c(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & 0 \leq t < T_c/2 \\ -1, & T_c/2 \leq t < T_c \end{cases}$$ The modulated signal is: $$V_{mod}(t) = V_{in}(t) \cdot c(t) = V_s(t) \cdot \text{sgn}\big(\sin(2\pi f_c t)\big)$$ This is a **DSC-SC (Double-Sideband Suppressed-Carrier)** AM signal. Its Fourier spectrum is shifted to ±$f_c$ and its odd harmonics. Because it is **AC** (not DC), it can pass through a transformer — which is the only way to cross the isolation barrier. After crossing the transformer, the demodulator multiplies $V_{mod}(t)$ by the **same** carrier $c(t)$: $$V_{out}(t) = V_{mod}(t) \cdot c(t) = V_s(t) \cdot c^2(t) = V_s(t) \cdot 1 = V_s(t)$$ since $c^2(t) = 1$ for a ±1 square wave. The signal is **fully recovered**, and the carrier is removed. A subsequent low-pass filter removes the high-frequency components at $2f_c, 3f_c, \ldots$ --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Typical Value | KTU Exam Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | dB (typically 100 – 120 dB) | Compute output noise from 50/60 Hz interference | | 2 | Differential Gain of IA Input Stage | $A_d = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}$ | dimensionless (set by $R_g$) | Design $R_g$ for a required gain | | 3 | Overall Isolation Amp Gain | $A_{ISO} = A_{IA} \cdot A_{MOD} \cdot A_{DEMOD} \cdot A_{OUT}$ | dimensionless (typically 1 – 100) | Total system gain calculation | | 4 | Isolation Voltage Rating | $V_{ISO}$ (RMS or peak) | **V** (1500 – 5000 V RMS) | Defibrillator protection rating | | 5 | Isolation Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{IMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{ISO,applied}}{V_{out,due\ to\ isolation\ mode}}\right)$ | dB (≥ 100 dB) | Rejection of trans-barrier common-mode | | 6 | Leakage Current (Patient Safety) | $I_{leak} = \dfrac{V_{ISO,applied}}{Z_{isolation}}$ | µA (< 10 µA per IEC 60601) | Patient safety check | | 7 | Modulator Carrier Frequency | $f_c$ | Hz (50 k – 500 kHz) | Determines max signal bandwidth | | 8 | Max Signal Bandwidth (Nyquist) | $BW_{signal} \leq f_c / 2$ | Hz (≤ 250 kHz for $f_c = 500$ kHz) | ECG/EEG bandwidth (≤ 1 kHz) fits easily | | 9 | Bandwidth of Isolation Amp | $BW = \dfrac{GBW}{A_{ISO}}$ | Hz (drops with gain) | Slew rate / response time | | 10 | Output Noise Spectral Density | $e_n = \sqrt{4kTR_N \cdot BW}$ | nV/√Hz | SNR calculation | > [!TIP] > In the exam, always write **CMRR in dB** (not as a ratio), unless the question explicitly asks for the ratio $A_d/A_{cm}$. The KTU evaluator deducts ½ mark if you mix units. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |---|---| | **Cardiology (ECG/Stress test)** | Isolating patient from line-powered ECG cart | | **Electroencephalography (EEG)** | EEG requires ultra-high CMRR (> 110 dB) due to tiny µV signals | | **Defibrillator monitors** | Surviving 5000 V defibrillation pulse without damage | | **Cardiac catheterization lab** | Floating amplifier referenced to catheter tip | | **Industrial process control** | Isolating 4–20 mA loops from ground loops in chemical plants | | **Power electronics** | Isolating gate drivers of IGBT/MOSFETs (gate-drive transformers + opto-isolators) | | **Data acquisition (DAQ)** | Multi-channel DAQ with channel-to-channel and channel-to-host isolation | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential Gain of the Input Instrumentation Amplifier The input stage is a classic **three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier (3-OA-IA)**. Let us derive its gain. **Topology:** Two input op-amps (A1, A2) in non-inverting configuration feeding a difference amplifier (A3). A single gain-setting resistor $R_g$ connects between the two inverting inputs of A1 and A2. **Step 1 — Identify the voltage across $R_g$.** By the virtual-short principle, the inverting input of A1 equals the non-inverting input, which is $V_1$. Similarly, the inverting input of A2 equals $V_2$. Therefore the voltage across $R_g$ is: $$V_{R_g} = V_1 - V_2$$ **Step 2 — Current through $R_g$.** $$I_{R_g} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}$$ **Step 3 — This same current flows through the two feedback resistors $R_1$ (one in each op-amp).** Since no current enters the ideal op-amp inputs, all of $I_{R_g}$ passes through $R_1$. **Step 4 — Output voltages of A1 and A2.** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{O1} &= V_1 + I_{R_g} \cdot R_1 = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2) \\ V_{O2} &= V_2 - I_{R_g} \cdot R_1 = V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Difference amplifier stage gain.** A3 is a unity-gain difference amplifier (when $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_5 = R$): $$V_{out,IA} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ > **Final gain of the 3-OA-IA:** > > $$\boxed{A_d = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical example (KTU-typical).** Design for $A_d = 100$ with $R_1 = 25$ kΩ: $$ \begin{aligned} 100 &= 1 + \frac{2 \times 25{,}000}{R_g} \\ 99 &= \frac{50{,}000}{R_g} \\ R_g &= \frac{50{,}000}{99} \approx 505.05\ \Omega \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Modulation & Demodulation Algebra (Recovered Signal) Let us re-derive the recovery of the baseband signal from the modulated signal in full algebraic detail. **Step 1 — Input biopotential.** For a typical ECG, model it as a sum of sinusoids: $$V_s(t) = A_s \cos(2\pi f_s t),\quad f_s \approx 1\ \text{Hz (R-wave fundamental)}$$ **Step 2 — Carrier.** Square wave of frequency $f_c = 500$ kHz, period $T_c = 2\ \mu\text{s}$: $$c(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(2\pi n f_c t)$$ **Step 3 — Modulation (multiplication in time domain).** $$V_{mod}(t) = V_s(t) \cdot c(t) = \frac{4A_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots} \frac{1}{n}\sin(2\pi n f_c t)\cos(2\pi f_s t)$$ Using the trigonometric identity $\sin x \cos y = \frac{1}{2}[\sin(x+y) + \sin(x-y)]$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{mod}(t) &= \frac{2A_s}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}\frac{1}{n}\Big[\sin\big(2\pi(nf_c+f_s)t\big) \\ &\qquad + \sin\big(2\pi(nf_c-f_s)t\big)\Big] \end{aligned} $$ **Spectral interpretation:** The baseband signal has been translated to the carrier harmonics $nf_c \pm f_s$. No energy remains at DC. This is **why** transformers can carry it — pure AC. **Step 4 — Transmission across the barrier.** The transformer ideally scales the amplitude by its turns ratio $N = N_2/N_1$: $$V_{mod}'(t) = N \cdot V_{mod}(t)$$ **Step 5 — Synchronous demodulation.** Multiply by the same carrier $c(t)$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out}(t) &= V_{mod}'(t) \cdot c(t) = N \cdot V_{mod}(t) \cdot c(t) \\ &= N \cdot V_s(t) \cdot c^2(t) \\ &= N \cdot V_s(t) \cdot 1 \\ &= N \cdot V_s(t) \end{aligned} $$ > **Recovered signal:** > $$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = N \cdot V_s(t) = N \cdot A_s \cos(2\pi f_s t)}$$ **Step 6 — Low-pass filtering.** A simple RC filter at the output with cutoff $f_{LPF} \ll f_c$ removes residual high-frequency ripple (any $2nf_c \pm f_s$ components that bleed through due to non-idealities). For a 2nd-order Butterworth LPF with $f_{LPF} = 10$ kHz, the ECG signal ($f_s \leq 250$ Hz) passes through with negligible distortion (≈ 0.5% ripple at 250 Hz). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Common-Mode Rejection Calculation A patient connected to an ECG is capacitively coupled to the 230 V / 50 Hz mains. The patient's body picks up a **common-mode voltage** $V_{cm} \approx 5$ – 50 V (depending on environment). The differential ECG is just $V_d \approx 1$ mV. **Required CMRR (in dB) to keep residual 50 Hz noise below 1% of ECG peak (10 µV):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{noise} &= \frac{V_{cm}}{10^{\text{CMRR}/20}} \\ 10\ \mu\text{V} &= \frac{50\ \text{V}}{10^{\text{CMRR}/20}} \\ 10^{\text{CMRR}/20} &= \frac{50}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 5 \times 10^{6} \\ \frac{\text{CMRR}}{20} &= \log_{10}(5 \times 10^6) = 6.699 \\ \text{CMRR} &\approx 133.98\ \text{dB} \approx \mathbf{134\ dB} \end{aligned} $$ > This is **why** the input stage of an isolation amplifier uses a precision 3-OA-IA — its CMRR naturally exceeds 100 dB. --- ##
