3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Coupling Techniques | Parameter | Transformer | Optical | Capacitive | |---|---|---|---| | **Underlying physical principle** | Faraday's law of induction | Photoelectric effect | Displacement current | | **Coupling element** | Thin-film toroid with polyimide dielectric | LED + photodiode, air/dielectric gap | SiO₂ dielectric layer | | **Signal that crosses the barrier** | Magnetic flux (B-field) | Photons (light) | Electric field (E-field) | | **Power transfer across barrier** | Yes (small bias power) | No (signal only) | No (signal only) | | **Bandwidth** | DC – 60 kHz | DC – 100 kHz | DC – 1 MHz | | **Isolation voltage** | 1500 – 5000 V RMS | 2500 – 7500 V RMS | 2500 – 5000 V RMS | | **CMRR** | 100 – 120 dB | 60 – 90 dB | 90 – 110 dB | | **Susceptibility to EMI** | Magnetic-field pickup | Immune | Capacitive pickup | | **Long-term drift / aging** | Low | High (LED aging) | Low | | **Power consumption** | Moderate | Low | Low | | **Cost** | High | Low | Moderate | | **KTU-typical example IC** | AD202, ISO122, AD210 | HCPL-7800, IL300 | ISO124, ADuM3151 | > [!TIP] > For KTU exam, when asked "Why not just use an optocoupler?" answer: **An optocoupler cannot transfer DC (because the LED is biased through a current-limiting resistor, and the photodiode cannot distinguish between LED ON-state due to signal vs LED ON-state due to bias). Hence optocouplers need carrier modulation, and so do capacitive couplers. The transformer is unique in that it can pass modulated AC, but it can also pass analog signals via carrier modulation.** --- ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Leakage Current Through the Barrier The barrier is modeled as a **parallel combination of resistance $R_{iso}$ and capacitance $C_{iso}$** between primary and secondary. For an applied mains voltage $V_m(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f_m t)$ between the two grounds: **Resistive leakage current:** $$I_R = \frac{V_p}{R_{iso}}$$ For $V_p = 230$ V (peak) and $R_{iso} = 10^{10}\ \Omega$ (typical 10 GΩ isolation): $$I_R = \frac{230}{10^{10}} = 23\ \text{nA}\ \text{(DC, negligible)}$$ **Capacitive leakage current:** $$I_C = V_p \cdot 2\pi f_m \cdot C_{iso}$$ For $C_{iso} = 5$ pF, $f_m = 50$ Hz: $$I_C = 230 \times 2\pi \times 50 \times 5 \times 10^{-12} = 3.61 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{A} = 0.36\ \mu\text{A}$$ **Total leakage:** $$I_{leak} = \sqrt{I_R^2 + I_C^2} \approx I_C \approx 0.36\ \mu\text{A} \ll 10\ \mu\text{A (IEC limit)}$$ > **Conclusion:** The capacitive path dominates at 50/60 Hz, and it is still well below the IEC 60601 limit of **10 µA**. ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef input fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef barrier fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef power fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 PAT[Patient Electrodes
V1 V2 V3]:::input IA[3-Op-Amp
Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain Ad up to 1000]:::input LPF1[Anti-alias LPF
fc = 5 kHz]:::input MOD[Modulator / Chopper
DSC-SC AM
Carrier fc = 500 kHz]:::input ISO{{ISOLATION BARRIER
Pulse Transformer / Opto / Capacitor
Viso = 1500 to 5000 V RMS
Ileak under 10 microampere}}:::barrier DEMOD[Synchronous Demodulator
Multiplier plus LPF]:::output LPF2[Output LPF
fc = 1 kHz for ECG]:::output OUTBUF[Output Buffer Op-Amp
Low Z out under 1 ohm]:::output LOAD[ADC / Display / Recorder
Patient Side Equipment]:::output DC_DC[Isolated DC-DC Converter
Floating plus minus 15 V supply]:::power PWRSPLY[Main plus minus 15 V supply
Output side ground]:::power PAT --> IA --> LPF1 --> MOD MOD -. "Modulated AC signal" .-> ISO ISO -. "Modulated AC signal" .-> DEMOD DEMOD --> LPF2 --> OUTBUF --> LOAD PWRSPLY --> DC_DC DC_DC -. "Floating plus minus 15 V" .-> IA DC_DC -. "Floating plus minus 15 V" .-> MOD note1[Input Ground is FLOATING
Output Ground is EARTHED
Patient is PROTECTED]:::barrier note1 ~~~ ISO ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Signal Waveform Diagram (Mermaid Timeline) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input ECG
1 mV peak
0.05 to 250 Hz] --> B[Modulated Signal
500 kHz carrier
Amplitude follows ECG] B --> C[Across Isolation Barrier
Pulse Transformer
Galvanically isolated] C --> D[Demodulated
Baseband recovered
1 mV peak at output ground] D --> E[Filtered Output
Clean ECG
Ready for ADC] ``` ## 4.3 Three-Port Isolation Concept Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IP[INPUT PORT Floating] IE[Electrodes / Patient] IIA[Input Instrumentation Amplifier] end subgraph PP[POWER PORT Isolated DC-DC] ISOPWR[Isolated plus minus 15 V Supply] end subgraph OP[OUTPUT PORT Earthed] OIA[Output Amplifier and Buffer] OLOAD[ADC / Recorder / Host] end ISO((Isolation Barrier
3 kV RMS)) --- IP ISO --- OP ISO --- PP IE --> IIA IIA -->|Modulated signal| ISO ISO -->|Modulated signal| OIA OIA --> OLOAD OLOAD -->|Main plus minus 15 V| ISOPWR ISOPWR -->|Floating plus minus 15 V| IIA ``` > [!NOTE] > **Read the three ports in a clockwise direction:** Input Port (left, floating) → Isolation Barrier (center, the dashed barrier) → Output Port (right, earthed). The Power Port is shown isolated to **both** sides. This is the canonical KTU board-drawing pattern for an isolation amplifier. ## 4.4 Closed-Loop Feedback Isolation Amplifier (AD202 Style) ```mermaid flowchart TB V1[Vin plus] --> S1 V2[Vin minus] --> S2 S1 --> A1[Op-Amp A1] S2 --> A2[Op-Amp A2] A1 --> SUM A2 --> SUM SUM[Modulator Summing Node] --> OSC[Oscillator fc 50 kHz] OSC --> XFMR{{Transformer}} XFMR --> DET[Synchronous Detector] DET --> FB[Feedback Network] FB --> A1 FB --> A2 DET --> OUTBUF[Output Buffer] OUTBUF --> VOUT[Vout referenced to Output Ground] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Closed-loop feedback** across the barrier (via the dashed feedback path) is what makes the AD202/AD210 family have **excellent linearity (0.05% max)** and **stable gain**. Open-loop modulator-demodulator designs (e.g., ISO122) have lower linearity but simpler design. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > **Define an isolation amplifier. List any two of its key specifications.** **Model Answer:** An **isolation amplifier** is a differential amplifier that provides **galvanic isolation** between its input, output, and power supply stages, while accurately transmitting the input signal via **transformer, optical, or capacitive coupling** across a non-conductive barrier. **Two key specifications:** 1. **Isolation Voltage Rating** (typically **1500 V – 5000 V RMS**) — the maximum continuous voltage that can appear across the barrier without breakdown. 2. **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** (typically **> 100 dB** at 50/60 Hz) — the ability to reject interference picked up by the patient. *(Any other valid spec accepted: IMRR, leakage current < 10 µA, bandwidth, gain accuracy, isolation resistance > 10¹⁰ Ω.)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]** > **State the three ports of an isolation amplifier. Why must all three be isolated from each other?** **Model Answer:** The three ports are: 1. **Input port** (signal from the patient/electrodes) 2. **Output port** (signal to ADC/recorder) 3. **Power supply port** (DC-DC converter providing floating power to the input stage) All three must be isolated because: - The **input port** must float relative to earth ground to protect the patient from mains-derived leakage currents. - The **output port** is referenced to earth (system) ground for the ADC. - The **power port** must be isolated so that the mains-derived DC-DC does not provide a sneak path for leakage current to the input. If any one of the three were non-isolated, leakage current could flow to the patient, defeating the very purpose of the isolation amplifier and violating the **IEC 60601-1** patient-leakage limit of **10 µA**. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [OPTION 1] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **transformer-coupled isolation amplifier**. Discuss the role of the carrier modulation and demodulation stages. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** An isolation amplifier is used to condition an ECG signal of amplitude **1 mV peak** at 1 Hz. The CMRR of the input instrumentation amplifier is **120 dB**. If a **50 V, 50 Hz** common-mode interference is capacitively coupled to the patient body, calculate: > - (i) The amplitude of the 50 Hz interference appearing at the input of the IA. > - (ii) The output noise voltage due to this common-mode signal. > - (iii) The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in dB. --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a) — Working of Transformer-Coupled Isolation Amplifier (7 Marks) **Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the full mermaid diagram).** The working of a transformer-coupled isolation amplifier consists of **five stages**: **Stage 1: Input Instrumentation Amplifier (1 Mark)** A 3-op-amp IA accepts the differential biopotential (V₁ – V₂) from the patient electrodes. It provides high input impedance (> 10¹² Ω) and a programmable differential gain: $$A_d = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}$$ **Stage 2: Modulator (Chopper) (1.5 Marks)** The DC output of the IA is multiplied by a **square-wave carrier** $c(t)$ of frequency $f_c \approx 50\ \text{kHz} - 500\ \text{kHz}$ (typically generated by an internal astable oscillator). The result is a **DSC-SC AM signal** whose amplitude envelope is the original biopotential. This AC nature is essential because transformers cannot pass DC. **Stage 3: Isolation Barrier — Pulse Transformer (1.5 Marks)** The modulated AC signal is applied to the **primary winding** of a miniature thin-film pulse transformer. The signal crosses to the secondary winding as a **magnetic flux** through a high-dielectric polyimide insulation layer. There is **no galvanic (electrical) connection** between primary and secondary. The isolation rating is **1500 V – 5000 V RMS**, with isolation resistance > **10¹⁰ Ω** and barrier capacitance ≈ **5 pF**. **Stage 4: Synchronous Demodulator (1.5 Marks)** The secondary-winding signal is multiplied by the **same carrier $c(t)$** (synchronized via phase-locked loop). Because $c(t) \cdot c(t) = 1$, the baseband signal is recovered. A low-pass filter removes residual $2f_c$, $3f_c$ components. **Stage 5: Output Buffer Amplifier (0.5 Mark)** Provides low output impedance (< 1 Ω) to drive the ADC, and additional gain (typically unity to 10). **Auxiliary: Isolated Power Supply (1 Mark)** A high-frequency **DC-DC converter** (built-in or external, e.g., the AD246) generates a **floating ±15 V** supply for the input stage from the output-side +5 V or ±15 V rail. This completes the **three-port isolation**. **Why Carrier Modulation?** - Transformers **cannot pass DC** signals (Faraday's law: induced EMF = $-N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$, which is zero for a constant flux). - Patient biopotentials (ECG, EEG, EMG) are inherently **DC to a few kHz** signals. - Modulation converts the low-frequency signal into a **high-frequency AC** that can be transformer-coupled, and then demodulation restores the original signal faithfully. --- #### Model Solution to Question A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Given:** - ECG amplitude: $V_s = 1\ \text{mV} = 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$ at $f_s = 1\ \text{Hz}$ - CMRR = 120 dB - Common-mode interference: $V_{cm} = 50\ \text{V}$ at $f_m = 50\ \text{Hz}$ **(i) Amplitude of the 50 Hz interference at the IA input: (1 Mark)** Since the common-mode voltage is applied equally to both input terminals, the differential input due to common mode is: $$V_{cm,in} = V_{cm} = 50\ \text{V (peak)}$$ This appears as a **common-mode signal** at the input. **(ii) Output noise voltage due to the common-mode signal: (3 Marks)** CMRR in dB: $$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) = 120\ \text{dB}$$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) &= 120 \\ \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) &= 6 \\ \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} &= 10^6 \end{aligned} $$ Output noise due to common mode: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{noise} &= A_d \cdot \frac{V_{cm}}{10^{\text{CMRR(dB)}/20}} \\ &= A_d \cdot \frac{50\ \text{V}}{10^{6}} \\ &= 50\ \mu\text{V} \cdot \left(\frac{A_d}{A_d}\right) \\ &= 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{V} \times \text{(per unit of differential gain)} \end{aligned} $$ But to find the actual output noise, we need the differential gain. The ECG at the output is amplified by $A_d$ to give $V_{out,signal} = A_d \cdot 1\ \text{mV}$. Assuming $A_d = 1000$ (a common ECG pre-amp setting): $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out,signal} &= 1000 \times 1\ \text{mV} = 1\ \text{V (peak)} \\ V_{out,noise} &= A_d \times \frac{V_{cm}}{10^{6}} = 1000 \times 50\ \mu\text{V} = 50\ \text{mV (peak)} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Signal-to-Noise Ratio in dB: (3 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SNR} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{out,signal}}{V_{out,noise}}\right) \\ &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1\ \text{V}}{50\ \text{mV}}\right) \\ &= 20 \log_{10}(20) \\ &= 20 \times 1.301 \\ &\approx 26.02\ \text{dB} \end{aligned} $$ > **General note for the student:** SNR is independent of $A_d$ in this calculation because both signal and noise scale by the same $A_d$. It is the CMRR (in dB) that determines the SNR directly: > > $$\boxed{\text{SNR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_{cm}}\right) + \text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{-3}}{50}\right) + 120 = -93.98 + 120 \approx 26.02\ \text{dB}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [OPTION 2] **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **transformer, optical, and capacitive** isolation techniques used in isolation amplifiers. Mention one specific IC for each. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A patient is being monitored with an ECG isolation amplifier. The isolation barrier capacitance is **5 pF** and isolation resistance is **10¹⁰ Ω**. The patient is accidentally exposed to a **230 V, 50 Hz** mains voltage. Calculate the resulting patient leakage current and verify whether it is within the **IEC 60601-1** limit of **10 µA**. --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a) — Comparison (7 Marks) | Parameter | Transformer | Optical | Capacitive | |---|---|---|---| | Principle | Magnetic flux (Faraday) | Photons (light) | Displacement current (E-field) | | Bandwidth | DC – 60 kHz | DC – 100 kHz | DC – 1 MHz | | Isolation voltage | 1500 – 5000 V RMS | 2500 – 7500 V RMS | 2500 – 5000 V RMS | | Linearity | Excellent (0.025%) | Poor (needs compensation) | Good (0.05%) | | Power transfer across barrier | Yes (small bias) | No | No | | EMI susceptibility | Magnetic pickup | Immune | Capacitive pickup | | Cost | High | Low | Moderate | | **Example IC** | **AD202 / ISO122** | **HCPL-7800 / IL300** | **ISO124 / ADuM3151** | **(2 marks for the table, 2 marks for explanations, 1 mark for examples, 2 marks for the working principle descriptions.)** Key points the examiner expects: - Transformer isolation **requires** carrier modulation because transformers cannot pass DC. - Optocouplers (HCPL-7800) are **commonly used in motor drives and switching power supplies** because of high isolation voltage. - Capacitive isolation (e.g., ADuM3151 from Analog Devices) is a **modern CMOS-based technique** with very high bandwidth and low power, but sensitive to external E-fields. - For ECG applications, **transformer-coupled ISO amps** dominate because of their high CMRR and built-in isolated power supply (e.g., AD202/AD210 from Analog Devices). --- #### Model Solution to Question B(b) — Leakage Current Calculation (7 Marks) **Given:** - Barrier capacitance: $C_{iso} = 5$ pF = $5 \times 10^{-12}$ F - Barrier resistance: $R_{iso} = 10^{10}\ \Omega = 10^{10}\ \Omega$ - Mains voltage: $V_{rms} = 230$ V at $f = 50$ Hz **Step 1 — Convert to peak voltage: (1 Mark)** $$V_p = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.414 = 325.27\ \text{V (peak)}$$ **Step 2 — Resistive leakage current (DC component, negligible): (1 Mark)** $$I_R = \frac{V_p}{R_{iso}} = \frac{325.27}{10^{10}} = 32.5\ \text{nA (peak)}$$ This is **negligible** (≈ 0.03 µA). **Step 3 — Capacitive leakage current (dominant at 50 Hz): (3 Marks)** For a sinusoidal voltage across a capacitor, the current is: $$ \begin{aligned} I_C &= V_p \cdot 2\pi f \cdot C_{iso} \\ &= 325.27 \times 2\pi \times 50 \times 5 \times 10^{-12} \\ &= 325.27 \times 314.159 \times 5 \times 10^{-12} \\ &= 510.7 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A} \\ &\approx 0.511\ \mu\text{A (peak)} \end{aligned} $$ Converting peak to RMS: $$I_{C,rms} = \frac{I_C}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.511}{1.414} \approx 0.361\ \mu\text{A (RMS)}$$ **Step 4 — Total leakage current: (1 Mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{leak,rms} &= \sqrt{I_R^2 + I_{C,rms}^2} \\ &\approx \sqrt{(0.0325)^2 + (0.361)^2}\ \mu\text{A} \\ &\approx 0.363\ \mu\text{A (RMS)} \end{aligned} $$ (Using peak values, $I_{leak,peak} \approx 0.514\ \mu\text{A}$.) **Step 5 — Verification against IEC 60601-1: (1 Mark)** | Parameter | IEC Limit | Our Calculation | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | Patient leakage current (AC) | **≤ 10 µA RMS** | **0.36 µA RMS** | ✓ **PASS** by factor of ≈ **28×** | > **Conclusion:** The isolation amplifier is **safe for the patient** under the given fault condition. The leakage current is well within the IEC 60601-1 limit. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes KTU students make in isolation amplifier questions (each = 1–2 mark deduction):** > > 1. **Confusing "isolation voltage" with "supply voltage."** Isolation voltage (1500–5000 V RMS) is what the barrier can withstand; supply voltage (±15 V) is the rail. > 2. **Forgetting that a transformer cannot pass DC.** A common board-answer mistake: "The transformer amplifies the ECG signal and isolates it." Wrong — the carrier modulation is the *reason* a transformer can be used. > 3. **Mixing up CMRR (dB) and CMRR (ratio).** Always write **CMRR = 120 dB**, not 10⁶ (the ratio is 10⁶, but dB is the proper unit). > 4. **Drawing the block diagram without the isolated DC-DC converter.** The 3-port isolation requires the power port to also be isolated. Examiners specifically look for this in the diagram (1–2 marks). > 5. **In numerical problems: forgetting to convert peak ↔ RMS.** When mains voltage is given as 230 V **RMS**, convert to peak using $\sqrt{2}$ *before* computing capacitive current. > 6. **Using wrong formula for SNR.** SNR(dB) = 20 log₁₀(V_signal / V_noise). Not 10 log₁₀. (Power SNR uses 10 log; voltage SNR uses 20 log.) > 7. **Stating "isolation amplifier increases CMRR"** — wrong. The IA *provides* high CMRR, and the isolation barrier *preserves* it by removing ground loops. Be precise. > 8. **Writing "opto-coupler" without specifying the coupling mechanism.** Examiner wants: "**LED–phototransistor** pair" or "**LED–photodiode** pair." --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute revision — the 12 must-know points:** > > 1. **Isolation amplifier = differential amp + 3-port galvanic isolation.** No metallic path between input, output, and power. > > 2. **Three isolation mechanisms:** **Transformer (magnetic)**, **Optical (photonic)**, **Capacitive (electric field).** All three are taught in KTU. > > 3. **Why carrier modulation?** Transformers and capacitors cannot pass DC. Carrier modulation converts DC biopotential to AC, transmits it, and demodulates back to DC. > > 4. **Block diagram of transformer-isolated ISO amp:** `IA → Modulator → Pulse Transformer → Demodulator → Output Buffer` + Isolated DC-DC Converter for the input stage supply. > > 5. **Three-port isolation principle:** Input port, Output port, Power port are all mutually isolated. Examiner loves this point. > > 6. **Key specs:** Isolation voltage **1500–5000 V RMS**, CMRR **> 100 dB**, leakage current **< 10 µA** (IEC 60601-1), isolation resistance **> 10¹⁰ Ω**, barrier capacitance **≈ 5 pF**. > > 7. **Formulae to memorize:** > - $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ > - $A_d = 1 + 2R_1 / R_g$ (3-OA-IA) > - $I_{leak} \approx V_p \cdot 2\pi f \cdot C_{iso}$ (capacitive, dominant) > - $\text{SNR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_{cm}) + \text{CMRR (dB)}$ > > 8. **Standard biopotential levels:** ECG ≈ 1 mV, EEG ≈ 50 µV, EMG ≈ 100 µV – 1 mV, EOG ≈ 200 µV, ERG ≈ 100 µV. ISO amp must amplify these without saturating. > > 9. **Patient safety standards:** IEC 60601-1 (general), IEC 60601-2-25 (ECG), IEC 60601-2-26 (EEG). All mandate < 10 µA leakage. Defibrillation-proof isolation must withstand 5000 V pulse. > > 10. **Example ICs to remember for KTU viva/exam:** > - **AD202 / AD210** (Analog Devices) — transformer + isolated power, popular for ECG. > - **ISO122 (Burr-Brown)** — transformer, no built-in isolated power. > - **ISO124 (Burr-Brown)** — capacitive coupling, 1 MHz bandwidth. > - **HCPL-7800 (Avago/Broadcom)** — optical, used in motor drives. > > 11. **Common applications:** Patient monitors, ECG/EEG/EMG machines, defibrillators, electrosurgical units (ESU), cardiac catheterization labs, isolated data acquisition systems, isolated 4–20 mA loops. > > 12. **Clinical safety facts:** Let-go threshold = 10 mA, ventricular fibrillation threshold = 50 mA AC (50/60 Hz), perception threshold = 1 mA. Isolation amp keeps leakage < 10 µA → **5000× safety margin** below fibrillation threshold. --- ✅ **END OF NOTES — Working of Isolation Amplifiers (Module 2, OEICT723)** *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1 – L4).*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transformer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Transformer — Foundation & Operating Principle

## 2.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Transformer** is a static (stationary), passive electromagnetic energy conversion device that transfers electrical energy from one alternating-current (AC) circuit to another through the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction**, without any change in frequency, while the voltage and current levels are transformed according to the turns ratio.

In the context of **Biomedical Instrumentation (OEICT723)**, the transformer is most commonly deployed as an **Isolation Transformer** and a **Step-Down Power Transformer** within medical equipment chassis, where it serves the dual purpose of voltage adaptation and **galvanic isolation** between the mains (line) supply and the patient-connected circuit, thereby preventing microshock and macroshock hazards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEICT723 — Module 2):**
> A transformer is a passive electrical device that transfers electrical energy between two or more circuits through electromagnetic induction. In biomedical systems, isolation transformers are critical safety components used in ECG, EEG, EMG, defibrillators, and electrosurgical units to ensure **patient safety** by isolating the patient from the direct mains supply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Mutual Induction:**
> Mutual induction is the phenomenon by which a changing current in one coil induces an electromotive force (EMF) in a neighbouring coil, owing to the time-varying magnetic flux produced by the first coil linking the second.

---

## 2.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a **water-pump system with two chambers** connected by a shared rotating shaft:

- The **primary coil** is the *input chamber* where the patient (electrical source) pushes the water (current) into the pump.
- The **magnetic core** is the *shared rotating shaft* that carries the mechanical energy (magnetic flux) from the input side to the output side without any physical contact.
- The **secondary coil** is the *output chamber* where the energy emerges in a different pressure/flow configuration depending on the chamber size.

Just as the **shared shaft** allows the two water chambers to be physically separated yet energetically coupled, the **magnetic core** allows the two electrical windings to be **electrically isolated** but **magnetically coupled**. There is no direct electrical connection between the two sides; energy crosses the gap entirely through a time-varying magnetic field.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway for First-Time Readers:**
> A transformer does **not** "create" energy. It merely *redistributes* energy between two circuits at different voltage/current ratios, conserving the apparent power (ideally $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$). For every volt you gain on the secondary, you pay with an amp on the primary, and vice versa — this is the **law of conservation of energy** in action.

---

## 2.3 Why Transformers Matter in Biomedical Engineering

In any hospital or clinical environment, the **patient** is the most vulnerable element in the electrical circuit. The human body, when connected to electrodes (ECG, EEG, EMG, defibrillator pads), becomes a sensitive conductor. A small leakage current of even **10 μA** passing directly through the heart can cause **ventricular fibrillation** — a lethal condition.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Biomedical Safety Fact:**
> The International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) standard **IEC 60601-1** mandates that medical electrical equipment must provide a means of **galvanic isolation** between the patient circuit and the mains supply. The isolation transformer is the primary hardware implementation of this safety requirement.

By placing an isolation transformer between the mains and the patient circuit:
- Any fault current on the primary side **cannot** reach the patient on the secondary side.
- The maximum leakage current is limited by the inter-winding capacitance and insulation resistance, both of which are carefully controlled in medical-grade isolation transformers.
- Patient-connected instruments (ECG, EEG, patient monitor) become intrinsically safer.

---

## 2.4 Symbol and Notation Conventions Used in KTU Examinations

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Brief Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Primary voltage | $V_1$ | Volt (V) | RMS voltage applied to primary winding |
| Secondary voltage | $V_2$ | Volt (V) | RMS voltage induced across secondary |
| Primary current | $I_1$ | Ampere (A) | RMS current in primary winding |
| Secondary current | $I_2$ | Ampere (A) | RMS current in secondary winding |
| Number of turns (primary) | $N_1$ | — | Integer count of primary turns |
| Number of turns (secondary) | $N_2$ | — | Integer count of secondary turns |
| Maximum flux in core | $\Phi_m$ | Weber (Wb) | Peak magnetic flux per cycle |
| Frequency | $f$ | Hertz (Hz) | AC supply frequency (50 Hz in India) |
| Transformation ratio | $K$ | — | Ratio $N_2 / N_1$ |
| Core cross-section area | $A$ | m² | Cross-section of the magnetic core |
| Maximum flux density | $B_m$ | Tesla (T) | Peak flux density in core |
| Transformer rating | $S$ | Volt-Ampere (VA) | Apparent power handling capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> All standard quantities listed above are **bolded constants** in the KTU data book and may be assumed as given in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 2: Transformer — Deep Theory & Formula Sheet

## 2.5 Working Principle — Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction

The operation of every transformer, irrespective of size, rests on **two fundamental electromagnetic laws**:

1. **Faraday's Law of Induction (1831):** Whenever the magnetic flux linking a coil changes with time, an EMF is induced in the coil, the magnitude of which is proportional to the rate of change of flux linkage.

$$e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

2. **Lenz's Law:** The direction of the induced EMF is such that it opposes the very cause (the change in flux) that produced it.

### Operational Logic — Step by Step

- **Step 1:** When an alternating voltage $V_1$ is applied to the primary winding, an alternating current $I_1$ flows through it.
- **Step 2:** This alternating current produces an alternating (time-varying) magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the laminated iron core.
- **Step 3:** The core channels this alternating flux through the secondary winding (which is wound on the same core).
- **Step 4:** Because the flux linking the secondary winding is changing with time, an EMF $E_2$ is induced in the secondary by Faraday's law.
- **Step 5:** If the secondary circuit is closed (load connected), a current $I_2$ flows, delivering power to the load.
- **Step 6:** To sustain the primary flux against the opposing MMF created by $I_2$, the primary draws an additional current from the source — this is how power is transferred.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Insight — "Why does current increase when voltage drops?":**
> Power must be conserved in an ideal transformer. So if voltage is stepped down (e.g., 230 V → 12 V), the current must step up by the same ratio to keep $P = V \cdot I$ constant. This is the fundamental reason a step-down transformer used in a 12 V DC power adapter inside a medical monitor can safely deliver higher current to the internal circuitry.

---

## 2.6 Construction of a Transformer — Physical Anatomy

A practical transformer consists of the following physical and electrical components:

- **Magnetic Core:** Built from laminated silicon-steel sheets (typical lamination thickness 0.35 mm) to reduce eddy-current losses. Two common shapes: **Core-type** (windings surround a part of the core) and **Shell-type** (core surrounds the windings).
- **Primary Winding:** The input winding connected to the AC source. Made of enameled copper wire.
- **Secondary Winding:** The output winding connected to the load. May have multiple taps for different voltages.
- **Insulation:** Paper, mica, or epoxy between windings and between windings and the core — critical for medical isolation transformers where creepage distance and clearance must meet IEC 60601-1.
- **Tank and Cooling:** For higher ratings, the core-coil assembly is immersed in transformer oil for insulation and cooling.
- **Bushings and Terminals:** Provide external electrical connections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lamination — Why is the core laminated?**
> The alternating flux induces eddy currents in the solid core, which dissipate as $I^2R$ heat. By laminating (slicing) the core into thin sheets insulated from one another, the eddy-current path is broken, drastically reducing these losses.

---

## 2.7 Ideal Transformer — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The **ideal transformer** is a lossless, perfectly-coupled theoretical model with no winding resistance, no core losses, and 100% magnetic coupling between windings. Although impossible in practice, it forms the foundation for analysing all real transformers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Equations (Memorise These):**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | KTU Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = K$ | Transformation ratio | Voltage transformation |
| 2 | $\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | Current inverse ratio | Current transformation |
| 3 | $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$ | Power conservation | Apparent power identity |
| 4 | $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m$ | Primary EMF (RMS) | EMF equation derivation |
| 5 | $E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m$ | Secondary EMF (RMS) | EMF equation derivation |
| 6 | $\Phi_m = B_m \cdot A$ | Flux relation | Linking $B_m$ and $\Phi_m$ |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2 + P_i + P_c}$ | Efficiency | Loss calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute-value notations in equations above have been written as \dfrac or in plain form to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax — vertical pipes in LaTeX can collide with the markdown table delimiter.

### The Universal Constant **4.44** — Where Does It Come From?

The number $4.44$ arises from the assumption that the flux varies **sinusoidally** with time:

$$\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The RMS value of a sinusoid is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, and $\omega = 2\pi f$, so the constant becomes:

$$4 \times 0.7071 \times \pi = 4.44$$

This constant appears in **every KTU problem** where you must calculate the induced EMF, and is the single most-tested numerical factor in transformer theory.

---

## 2.8 Types of Transformers Relevant to Biomedical Instrumentation

| Type | Turns Ratio | Biomedical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Step-Down Power Transformer** | $N_1 > N_2$ | Inside medical monitor PSU (e.g., 230 V → 12 V, 5 V) |
| **Step-Up Transformer** | $N_1 < N_2$ | Defibrillator charging circuit, X-ray high-voltage generator |
| **Isolation Transformer (1:1)** | $N_1 = N_2$ | Patient safety barrier in ECG/EEG/EMG, ICU ventilators |
| **Auto-Transformer** | Variable single winding | Variable AC supply in surgical equipment calibration |
| **Current Transformer (CT)** | Step-down for current | Patient current monitoring, leakage-current detection |
| **Pulse Transformer** | Special wideband design | Defibrillator pulse shaping, MRI gradient driver |

---

## 2.9 Losses in a Real Transformer

A real transformer differs from the ideal model due to two principal loss mechanisms:

- **Core (Iron) Losses ($P_i$):** Hysteresis loss + Eddy-current loss. Hysteresis loss $P_h = \eta B_m^{1.6} f V$ and eddy-current loss $P_e = k_e B_m^2 f^2 t^2$. Core losses depend on voltage and frequency, **not** on load current.
- **Copper (Ohmic) Losses ($P_c$):** $I^2R$ heating in the primary and secondary windings. $P_c = I_1^2 R_1 + I_2^2 R_2$. These depend on the load current.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility of Efficiency Analysis:**
> In a hospital-grade isolation transformer, efficiency is typically between **95% and 98%** at full load. Power rating is expressed in **VA (volt-amperes)** rather than watts because the load (e.g., a switch-mode power supply) may have a poor power factor. Biomedical transformers are always conservatively rated to keep internal temperature rise below **Class B insulation limits (80°C)** for patient-safety reasons.

---

## 2.10 Real-World Utility Across Engineering Fields

- **Medical Electronics:** Patient isolation, defibrillator charging, MRI gradient amplifiers.
- **Power Systems:** Long-distance HV transmission, distribution transformers.
- **Audio Engineering:** Microphone isolation, balanced-line drivers, ground-loop elimination.
- **Instrumentation:** Signal coupling in oscilloscopes, galvanic isolation in data acquisition.
- **Renewable Energy:** Step-up at solar/wind farms before grid injection.
- **Electric Vehicles:** Onboard chargers, traction motor drive isolation.

# Module 2: Transformer — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$

This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU transformer problems. The model solution below follows the exact step pattern expected by KTU examiners for full marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions Used in the Derivation:**
> 1. The flux $\Phi(t)$ in the core varies sinusoidally with time.
> 2. The winding is uniformly distributed and fully linked with the core flux.
> 3. The transformer operates in the linear region of the B-H curve (no saturation).

**Step 1: Express the time-varying flux.**

Let the instantaneous flux linking the winding be:

$$\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2: Apply Faraday's Law of Induction.**

The instantaneous EMF induced in a coil of $N$ turns is given by Faraday's law:

$$e(t) = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Differentiate the flux expression.**

Substituting $\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ into Faraday's law:

$$e(t) = -N \dfrac{d}{dt} \big[ \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \big]$$

**Step 4: Carry out the differentiation.**

Using $\dfrac{d}{dt}\sin(\omega t) = \omega \cos(\omega t)$:

$$e(t) = -N \cdot \Phi_m \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 5: Re-express in sine form using phase identity.**

Using $-\cos(\theta) = \sin(\theta - \pi/2)$, but for magnitude analysis we can drop the sign and phase:

$$e(t) = N \Phi_m \omega \sin(\omega t - \pi/2)$$

**Step 6: Identify the maximum (peak) value of the EMF.**

The peak EMF $E_{\max}$ is the coefficient of the sine term:

$$E_{\max} = N \, \Phi_m \, \omega$$

**Step 7: Convert to angular frequency $f$.**

Since $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$E_{\max} = N \, \Phi_m \, (2\pi f) = 2\pi f N \Phi_m$$

**Step 8: Convert peak EMF to RMS EMF.**

The RMS value of a sinusoid is $1 / \sqrt{2}$ times the peak value:

$$E_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{E_{\max}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2\pi f N \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 9: Simplify the numerical constant.**

$$\dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416}{1.4142} = 4.44$$

Therefore, the **RMS EMF equation** of a transformer winding is:

$$\boxed{\,E_{\text{rms}} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This constant **4.44** is exact in the sense of $\sqrt{2} \pi$, and applies to all sinusoidal flux distributions. For square-wave or triangular flux distributions, the numerical constant changes (4.0 for square, 4.44 for sine), which is a frequent KTU "twist" question.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Pattern)

### Numerical Problem 1: Basic EMF Computation

> **Problem:** A single-phase transformer has **1200 turns** on the primary and **80 turns** on the secondary. The cross-sectional area of the core is **80 cm²**, and the maximum flux density is **1.2 T**. The supply frequency is **50 Hz**. Calculate:
> (a) The primary EMF
> (b) The secondary EMF
> (c) The transformation ratio

**Step (a) — Primary EMF:**

Calculate maximum flux:

$$\Phi_m = B_m \cdot A = 1.2 \times 80 \times 10^{-4} = 9.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$$

Apply the EMF equation:

$$E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m = 4.44 \times 50 \times 1200 \times 9.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$E_1 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 1200 \times 0.0096 = 2557.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step (b) — Secondary EMF:**

$$E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m = 4.44 \times 50 \times 80 \times 9.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$E_2 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 80 \times 0.0096 = 170.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step (c) — Transformation ratio:**

$$K = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{80}{1200} = \dfrac{1}{15} = 0.0667$$

This is a **step-down** transformer (Step-down since $N_2 < N_1$).

---

### Numerical Problem 2: Turns Ratio Determination

> **Problem:** A 230 V / 115 V single-phase transformer has 600 primary turns. Find the number of secondary turns. Assume an ideal transformer.

**Step 1:** Apply the turns ratio equation:

$$\dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values:

$$\dfrac{600}{N_2} = \dfrac{230}{115} = 2$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $N_2$:

$$N_2 = \dfrac{600}{2} = 300 \text{ turns}$$

---

### Numerical Problem 3: Efficiency Calculation

> **Problem:** A 5 kVA transformer has iron losses of **50 W** and full-load copper losses of **100 W**. The secondary delivers 4 kW at 0.8 power factor. Calculate the efficiency at this load.

**Step 1:** Output power:

$$P_{\text{out}} = 4 \text{ kW} = 4000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Total losses (assuming copper losses vary as the square of load fraction):

For the given load, the load fraction is 0.8. So:

$$P_c = 100 \times (0.8)^2 = 100 \times 0.64 = 64 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** Total losses:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = P_i + P_c = 50 + 64 = 114 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4:** Efficiency:

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{loss}}} = \dfrac{4000}{4000 + 114} = \dfrac{4000}{4114} = 0.9723$$

$$\eta = 97.23\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Insight:**
> The maximum efficiency of a transformer occurs when **iron loss = copper loss**. In this example, the load fraction at maximum efficiency is $\sqrt{P_i / P_{c,\text{FL}}} = \sqrt{50/100} = 0.707$, i.e., at 70.7% of full load — a frequently tested KTU point.

---

### Numerical Problem 4: Biomedical Context — Isolation Transformer Verification

> **Problem:** A patient monitor uses a 1:1 isolation transformer with 500 primary and 500 secondary turns. The primary is connected to 230 V, 50 Hz mains. The core cross-section is 25 cm² and $B_m = 1.0$ T. Verify the primary EMF and find the secondary voltage.

**Step 1:** Calculate $\Phi_m$:

$$\Phi_m = B_m \cdot A = 1.0 \times 25 \times 10^{-4} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $E_1$:

$$E_1 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 500 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 277.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $E_2$ (1:1 turns ratio):

$$E_2 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 500 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 277.5 \text{ V}$$

The slight mismatch (277.5 V vs. 230 V) is because the model neglects winding resistance, leakage reactance, and the no-load primary current that compensates for these. In a well-designed isolation transformer, the deviation is kept below **5%**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation Summary Matrix

| Derivation | Starting Law | Final Equation | KTU Mark Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| EMF equation | Faraday's law | $E = 4.44 f N \Phi_m$ | **7 marks** (favourite KTU question) |
| Turns ratio | Flux conservation | $V_1 / V_2 = N_1 / N_2$ | **3 marks** (definition-type) |
| Current ratio | Power conservation | $I_1 / I_2 = N_2 / N_1$ | **3 marks** |
| Efficiency | Loss accounting | $\eta = P_{\text{out}} / (P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{loss}})$ | **5 marks** (numerical) |

The diagram below shows the **functional block-level architecture** of a single-phase transformer, mapping the energy flow path, the principal components, and the role of each subsystem in the electromagnetic conversion process. This is the Mermaid-compliant block schematic expected in KTU descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    E --> F[Induced EMF E2 at Output]
    F --> G[Load Patient Circuit or Medical Equipment]
    G --> H[Isolated Output Voltage V2]

    I[Core Loss Hysteresis plus Eddy] --> D

    J --> E

    K[Leakage Reactance X1] --> B
    L[Leakage Reactance X2] --> E

    M[Faraday Law of Induction] --> C

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **blue node (A)** is the input supply.

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Reference Schematic

For a more advanced KTU question on voltage regulation, the equivalent circuit of a real transformer referred to the primary is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[V1 Applied] --> R1[R1 Winding Resistance]
    R1 --> X1[X1 Leakage Reactance]
    X1 --> RC[RC Core Loss Branch]
    RC --> XM[XM Magnetising Reactance]
    XM --> R2P[R2 prime Referred to Primary]
    R2P --> X2P[X2 prime Referred to Primary]

    style LOAD fill:#d4f4d4,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $R_1$ and $X_1$ model the primary winding copper loss and leakage flux. $R_c$ and $X_m$ model the no-load core loss and magnetising current. $R_2'$ and $X_2'$ are the secondary resistance and reactance **referred to the primary side** by multiplying with $K^2$.

# Module 2: Transformer — KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Define a transformer and state the principle on which it works. Mention any two applications of transformers in biomedical instrumentation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A transformer is a static electrical device that transfers electrical energy from one AC circuit to another through **mutual electromagnetic induction**, without any change in frequency. It works on the principle of **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**, in which a time-varying current in the primary winding produces a time-varying magnetic flux in the core, which then induces an EMF in the secondary winding.

Two biomedical applications:

1. **Isolation Transformer:** Used in ECG and EEG machines to provide galvanic isolation between the patient and the mains supply, preventing electric shock.
2. **Step-Up Transformer:** Used in defibrillators and X-ray machines to generate the high voltage (several kV) required for capacitor charging or X-ray tube operation.

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> Derive the EMF equation of a transformer and explain the significance of the constant **4.44**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The EMF equation is derived from Faraday's law. For a sinusoidal flux $\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$$

The constant **4.44** arises from the combination $2\pi / \sqrt{2}$, where $2\pi f$ comes from the angular frequency and $1/\sqrt{2}$ is the form factor converting peak EMF to RMS. Its significance is that it directly links the **physical design parameters** of the transformer — frequency, number of turns, and core cross-section — to the **electrical output** — the induced voltage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a single-phase transformer. State the EMF equation. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 5 kVA, 230 V / 115 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has 600 primary turns. Find: (i) the number of secondary turns, (ii) the maximum flux in the core, and (iii) the full-load primary and secondary currents. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

A single-phase transformer consists of a **laminated silicon-steel core** on which two electrically isolated windings — **primary** ($N_1$ turns) and **secondary** ($N_2$ turns) — are wound. The core is laminated to reduce eddy-current loss. The primary is connected to the AC source, and the secondary delivers power to the load.

When an AC voltage $V_1$ is applied to the primary, it draws a small magnetising current that sets up an alternating flux $\Phi$ in the core. This flux links the secondary winding and, by **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**, induces an EMF $E_2$ in the secondary. The induced EMF equation is:

$$E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m$$

The primary EMF is similarly $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m$. The ratio $E_1 / E_2 = N_1 / N_2$ defines the transformation ratio $K$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Sub-part (i) — Number of secondary turns:**

$$N_2 = N_1 \times \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = 600 \times \dfrac{115}{230} = 300 \text{ turns}$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Maximum flux in the core:**

$$E_1 \approx V_1 = 230 \text{ V (ideal transformer assumption)}$$

$$\Phi_m = \dfrac{E_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1} = \dfrac{230}{4.44 \times 50 \times 600}$$

$$\Phi_m = \dfrac{230}{133200} = 1.727 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb} = 1.727 \text{ mWb}$$

**Sub-part (iii) — Full-load currents:**

$$I_{1,\text{FL}} = \dfrac{S}{V_1} = \dfrac{5000}{230} = 21.74 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{2,\text{FL}} = \dfrac{S}{V_2} = \dfrac{5000}{115} = 43.48 \text{ A}$$

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 — Module Internal Choice]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of an isolation transformer with reference to its use in biomedical equipment. Why is galvanic isolation important for patient safety? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 1 kVA, 230 V / 230 V isolation transformer is connected to a patient monitor drawing 800 W at 0.85 power factor lagging. The transformer has iron losses of 20 W and full-load copper losses of 30 W. Calculate: (i) the efficiency, and (ii) the load fraction at which maximum efficiency occurs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Isolation Transformer (7 marks):**

An **isolation transformer** has a 1:1 turns ratio ($N_1 = N_2$), providing the same voltage at input and output while **electrically isolating** the two circuits. The two windings are coupled only magnetically through the core; there is no conductive path between the primary and secondary.

In biomedical equipment such as ECG, EEG, EMG, and patient monitors, the patient is connected to the equipment via electrodes. If a fault in the mains (e.g., a line-to-ground short) occurs, without isolation, a current would flow directly through the patient to ground, potentially causing **microshock** (currents as low as 10 μA through the heart can cause ventricular fibrillation).

The isolation transformer blocks this fault-current path because the inter-winding insulation is rated for several kV. Any leakage that does occur is limited by the inter-winding capacitance to microampere levels, well below the IEC 60601-1 safety threshold of 10 μA (Type CF) for direct patient-connected circuits.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Sub-part (i) — Efficiency:**

Output power: $P_{\text{out}} = 800$ W

Load fraction: $x = P_{\text{out}} / S_{\text{rated}} = 800 / 1000 = 0.8$

Copper loss at this load: $P_c = 30 \times (0.8)^2 = 30 \times 0.64 = 19.2$ W

Total loss: $P_{\text{loss}} = P_i + P_c = 20 + 19.2 = 39.2$ W

Efficiency:

$$\eta = \dfrac{800}{800 + 39.2} = \dfrac{800}{839.2} = 0.9533 = 95.33\%$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Load fraction at maximum efficiency:**

Maximum efficiency occurs when iron loss = copper loss at that load:

$$P_i = x^2 \cdot P_{c,\text{FL}}$$

$$20 = x^2 \times 30$$

$$x^2 = \dfrac{20}{30} = \dfrac{2}{3}$$

$$x = \sqrt{2/3} = 0.8165 = 81.65\% \text{ of full load}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up primary and secondary turns:** A frequent error is writing $N_2 = N_1 \times V_1 / V_2$ instead of the correct $N_2 = N_1 \times V_2 / V_1$. Marks are lost here in nearly every batch.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 4.44:** Many students write $E = N \Phi_m \omega$ (peak value). The question always asks for RMS unless stated otherwise — always use **4.44**.
> 3. **Using $V$ instead of $E$:** For an ideal transformer $V_1 = E_1$, but in a real transformer, $V_1 > E_1$ due to voltage drop across winding resistance and leakage reactance. The problem must specify whether the transformer is ideal or real.
> 4. **Confusing efficiency at rated load vs. maximum efficiency:** The two are different. Maximum efficiency is a *design* parameter; rated-load efficiency is an *operating* parameter.
> 5. **Ignoring the power factor in efficiency problems:** Efficiency $\eta = P_{\text{out}} / P_{\text{in}}$, where $P_{\text{out}} = V I \cos\phi$. If the problem specifies a power factor, **you must include it**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Points (Print and Stick on Your Wall):**

- **Definition:** A transformer is a static electromagnetic device that transfers AC energy between circuits by **mutual induction** without changing the frequency.
- **Working Law:** **Faraday's law** of electromagnetic induction is the governing principle.
- **EMF Equation (Memorise verbatim):** $\quad E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m \quad$ — applicable to both primary and secondary.
- **Origin of 4.44:** It is $\dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\sqrt{2}\pi$, valid **only for sinusoidal flux**.
- **Turns Ratio Equation:** $\quad \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = K$
- **Power Conservation (Ideal):** $\quad V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2 \quad$ — apparent power in = apparent power out.
- **Core Losses (Iron Losses):** Hysteresis + eddy current; depend on $V$ and $f$, **independent of load**.
- **Copper Losses:** $I^2R$ in windings; vary with the **square of load current**.
- **Efficiency Formula:** $\quad \eta = \dfrac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi + P_i + P_c}$
- **Condition for Maximum Efficiency:** Iron loss = Copper loss (at the corresponding load).
- **Step-Up vs Step-Down:** Step-up has $N_2 > N_1$; step-down has $N_2 < N_1$.
- **Isolation Transformer:** A 1:1 transformer used in medical equipment for **galvanic isolation** to protect the patient.
- **Biomedical Safety Standard:** **IEC 60601-1** mandates isolation between patient circuits and mains.
- **Lamination of Core:** Reduces **eddy-current loss** by breaking the induced current paths.
- **Lamination Thickness:** Typical 0.35 mm silicon-steel sheets; thinner laminations are used at higher frequencies.
- **Why no DC operation?** A transformer requires a **time-varying flux** ($d\Phi/dt \neq 0$). DC flux is constant, so no EMF is induced. Applying DC to a transformer primary causes saturation and excessive current — a common exam trap.
- **Units to remember:** $\Phi$ in **Weber (Wb)**, $B$ in **Tesla (T)**, $f$ in **Hz**, $A$ in **m²**, $E$ in **Volts (V, RMS)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In numerical problems, always work in **SI base units** — convert cm² to m² and mWb to Wb before substituting. A single unit slip can cascade into the wrong final answer and cost you the entire 7 marks allocated to the numerical part.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitive and Optical Isolation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Capacitive and Optical Isolation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Electrical Isolation in Biomedical Systems? **Electrical Isolation** in biomedical instrumentation is a safety technique that completely breaks the galvanic (direct electrical/ohmic) path between two circuits while still allowing the desired signal to pass from one side to the other. The primary purpose is twofold: (i) to protect the patient from dangerous leakage currents originating from the mains-powered measurement equipment, and (ii) to eliminate ground loops that corrupt low-level bioelectric signals (ECG, EEG, EMG). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** > Isolation in biomedical instrumentation refers to the complete electrical separation between the patient-connected circuit and the measurement/recording circuit, achieved through non-galvanic coupling mechanisms such as capacitive (electric field) coupling, optical (light) coupling, or inductive (magnetic field) coupling. ## 1.2 Why Isolation is Non-Negotiable in Patient Monitoring The human body is highly sensitive to electrical currents. As little as **10 µA** of 50/60 Hz leakage current passing through the heart can induce **ventricular fibrillation (microshock)**. Any instrument connected to a patient via electrodes (e.g., ECG, EEG) must therefore ensure that no fault current from the mains side can ever reach the patient. Isolation provides this life-saving barrier. ## 1.3 The Two Main Isolation Techniques Covered in This Module ### A. Capacitive Isolation **Formal Definition:** Capacitive isolation is a method of galvanic isolation that uses a **capacitor** as the coupling element. Since a capacitor blocks DC and passes AC based on its reactance, the signal of interest (which is modulated onto a high-frequency carrier) is transmitted across an insulating dielectric, while DC fault currents are physically blocked. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The "Treasure Chest with a Glass Lid":** > Imagine a sealed treasure chest (the patient circuit) containing precious jewels (bioelectric signal). A glass lid is placed on top of the chest — you can *see* the jewels (signal transfer) but thieves (fault currents/leakage) cannot reach in with their hands to steal them. The glass lid behaves like a capacitor's dielectric: light (high-frequency signal) passes through, but physical intrusion (DC current) is blocked. ### B. Optical Isolation **Formal Definition:** Optical isolation uses an **optocoupler (opto-isolator)** consisting of a light-emitting diode (LED) on the input side and a photodetector (phototransistor, photodiode, or phototriac) on the output side, separated by a transparent insulating barrier (air or transparent epoxy). The input signal modulates the LED's light intensity, which is then detected on the output side and converted back to an electrical signal. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "Two Islands Connected by a Lighthouse":** > Picture two islands separated by a deep ocean (the isolation barrier). A lighthouse on Island A shines a beam of light (the modulated signal) across the water. A mirror on Island B catches the light and translates it back into a usable form. The two islands share *no physical bridge or wire* — they communicate only via light. This is exactly how an optocoupler works: zero electrical path, total patient safety. ## 1.4 Key Isolation Performance Metrics (Must Memorize) > [!IMPORTANT] > | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Engineering Meaning | > |---|---|---|---| > | Isolation Voltage | $V_{iso}$ | **2.5 kV – 7.5 kV** | Maximum voltage the barrier can withstand without breakdown | > | Leakage Current | $I_{leak}$ | **< 10 µA** | Current that manages to sneak across the barrier (must be sub-microamp for cardiac apps) | > | Isolation Resistance | $R_{iso}$ | **> 10¹² Ω** | Resistance of the insulating barrier (ideally infinite) | > | Coupling Capacitance | $C_{iso}$ | **2 – 10 pF** | Stray capacitance across the barrier (lower = better) | > | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | CMRR | **> 100 dB** | Ability to reject voltages common to both sides | > | Current Transfer Ratio | CTR | **20 % – 600 %** | (Optocoupler only) Ratio of output phototransistor current to input LED current | ## 1.5 Where These Are Used in Biomedical Instruments - **ECG / EEG / EMG amplifiers** → isolate patient from line-powered recording equipment. - **Defibrillators** → protect the recorder from the high-voltage discharge pulse. - **Patient monitors** → prevent ground loops between multiple instruments connected to the same patient. - **Electrosurgical units (ESU)** → isolate the high-frequency cutting current from the rest of the monitor. - **Blood pressure / SpO₂ monitors** → isolate the transducer/sensor electronics. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Capacitive Isolation — The Physics Deep-Dive ### 2.1.1 Why a Capacitor Blocks DC but Passes AC A capacitor consists of two conductive plates separated by a dielectric (insulator). For DC, once the capacitor is fully charged, current flow ceases completely — it behaves like an open circuit. For AC, the voltage across the plates is continuously changing, causing a continuous displacement current through the dielectric. The opposition offered to this AC is called **capacitive reactance**, $X_C$. ### 2.1.2 Capacitive Reactance — The Central Equation The capacitive reactance is given by: $$X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C}$$ where: - $X_C$ = capacitive reactance in ohms ($\Omega$) - $f$ = frequency of the AC signal in hertz (Hz) - $C$ = capacitance in farads (F) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** As $f \to 0$ (DC), $X_C \to \infty$ (open circuit). As $f \to \infty$, $X_C \to 0$ (short circuit). This is the fundamental basis of capacitive isolation — a low-frequency bioelectric signal cannot cross the capacitor directly; it must first be **modulated onto a high-frequency carrier**. ### 2.1.3 The Modulation–Demodulation Requirement (Critical for KTU) Bioelectric signals have bandwidths of: - ECG: **0.05 – 150 Hz** - EEG: **0.5 – 100 Hz** - EMG: **20 – 500 Hz** Since these are low-frequency, they cannot pass through the isolation capacitor efficiently. Hence, a **chopper / carrier modulation** technique is used: **Step-by-step Logic:** 1. The low-frequency bio-signal is multiplied by a high-frequency carrier (typically **50 kHz – 500 kHz**). 2. The modulated signal (now containing high-frequency components) easily passes through the small isolation capacitor because $X_C$ is small at high $f$. 3. On the output side, a **synchronous demodulator** recovers the original low-frequency signal. 4. The DC / mains fault components (at 50/60 Hz) are *deliberately* at low frequency and therefore blocked by the capacitor. ### 2.1.4 The Stray Coupling Capacitance Problem In any practical isolation barrier, a small parasitic capacitance $C_{iso}$ exists across the barrier. This stray capacitance forms a path for **common-mode (CM) interference** — typically the 50/60 Hz mains hum picked up by the patient cables. The CM voltage divider effect means: $$V_{out,CM} = V_{CM} \cdot \frac{C_{iso}}{C_{iso} + C_{in}}$$ where $C_{in}$ is the input capacitance to the isolated amplifier. To minimize CM pickup, we want $C_{iso}$ as small as possible (typically **2–10 pF** in medical-grade isolators). ### 2.1.5 Isolation Amplifier Topology (Capacitive) The classic three-op-amp isolation amplifier (e.g., Texas Instruments ISO124, Analog Devices AD215) uses: - **Input stage**: Instrumentation amplifier that buffers and amplifies the bio-signal. - **Modulator**: Converts DC/low-freq signal to a high-frequency pulse-width-modulated (PWM) square wave. - **Isolation barrier**: Two matched capacitors (differential capacitive coupling). - **Demodulator + Output stage**: Recovers the signal and filters out the carrier ripple. - **Isolated DC-DC converter**: Powers the input stage without breaking the isolation barrier. ## 2.2 Optical Isolation — The Physics Deep-Dive ### 2.2.1 The Optocoupler Block Diagram An optocoupler integrates four functional blocks: 1. **Input LED (Infrared, λ ≈ 850 – 940 nm)** — converts input current to light. 2. **Transparent insulating gap (typically 0.4 – 1.0 mm of epoxy/air)** — provides the galvanic barrier. 3. **Photodetector** — converts the received light back to electrical signal. Common variants: - Phototransistor (most common, low cost) - Photodarlington (high sensitivity) - Photodiode (fast, linear) - Phototriac (for AC switching) 4. **Output amplifier** (integrated in the same package). ### 2.2.2 Current Transfer Ratio (CTR) — The Optocoupler's Figure of Merit The CTR quantifies how efficiently the optocoupler transfers current from input to output: $$CTR = \frac{I_C}{I_F} \times 100 \,\%$$ where: - $I_F$ = forward current through the input LED (typically **5 – 20 mA**) - $I_C$ = collector current at the output phototransistor > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Ranges:** > - Standard phototransistor optocoupler: CTR = **20 % – 300 %** > - Photodarlington optocoupler: CTR = **100 % – 600 %** (higher gain, but slower) > - Photodiode optocoupler: CTR ≈ **0.1 % – 1 %** (low, but very fast — MHz range) ### 2.2.3 Why CTR Varies and Why It Matters Biologically CTR is highly dependent on: - **Temperature**: falls by about **−0.5 %/°C** above 25 °C. - **LED aging**: degrades by up to **50 % over 10,000 hours**. - **Forward current $I_F$**: non-linear at low currents. For biomedical safety, a high CTR means the LED needs less current to drive the output — keeping the input-side power low, which is good for battery-powered, patient-isolated designs (e.g., Holter monitors). ### 2.2.4 Bandwidth Limitation of Optocouplers The phototransistor's base-collector junction capacitance forms a low-pass filter with the load resistor. The **−3 dB bandwidth** is approximately: $$f_{-3dB} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_L C_{BC}}$$ Typical phototransistor optocouplers have bandwidths of **10 kHz – 200 kHz**, which is more than adequate for ECG/EEG/EMG. ### 2.2.5 The DC Problem in Optical Isolation Optocouplers have a notorious problem: they cannot pass pure DC signals faithfully. This is because the LED's optical output and the phototransistor's dark current are both temperature-dependent, causing a **DC drift** at the output. Solution: **chopping** the DC input to AC before applying it to the optocoupler, then demodulating on the output side — analogous to capacitive isolation. ## 2.3 Comparison of Capacitive vs. Optical Isolation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet** > > | Parameter | Capacitive Isolation | Optical Isolation | > |---|---|---| > | Coupling Medium | Electric field across dielectric | Light photons across air/epoxy | > | Barrier Element | Capacitor (2 matched caps) | LED + photodetector pair | > | Isolation Voltage | 2.5 – 5 kV | 2.5 – 7.5 kV | > | Bandwidth | DC to ~100 kHz (with chopper) | DC to ~200 kHz (with chopper) | > | Coupling Capacitance | 2 – 10 pF | < 1 pF | > | Linearity | Excellent (with modulator) | Moderate (CTR is non-linear) | > | Temperature Drift | Very low | High (CTR varies with T) | > | Power Consumption | Low (carrier-based) | Moderate (LED needs mA) | > | Typical ICs | ISO124, AD215, ADuM3151 | PC817, 6N136, MOC3021 | > | Best Biomedical Use | ECG/EEG signal path isolation | Switching patient-side relays, defib sync | > | Key Formula | $X_C = 1 / (2 \pi f C)$ | $CTR = (I_C / I_F) \times 100 \,\%$ | > | CMRR | > 100 dB | > 60 dB | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Capacitive Reactance from First Principles **Goal:** Derive the formula $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C}$ starting from the definition of capacitance. **Step 1 — Start with the definition of capacitance.** By definition, a capacitor stores charge $Q$ proportional to the voltage $V$ applied across it. The constant of proportionality is the capacitance $C$: $$Q = C V$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to time to obtain current.** Since current is the rate of flow of charge, $i = \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$, and assuming a sinusoidal voltage $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$: $$\frac{dQ}{dt} = C \frac{dv}{dt}$$ **Step 3 — Perform the differentiation.** Substitute $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$: $$\frac{dv}{dt} = \omega V_m \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 4 — Write the resulting current expression.** $$i(t) = C \cdot \omega V_m \cos(\omega t) = \omega C V_m \sin\!\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Identify the amplitude and phase.** The current leads the voltage by **90°** (π/2 radians) — a signature property of a pure capacitor. The peak current is $I_m = \omega C V_m$. **Step 6 — Define capacitive reactance as the ratio $V_m / I_m$.** By analogy with Ohm's law for resistors, the opposition to AC current offered by the capacitor is: $$X_C = \frac{V_m}{I_m} = \frac{V_m}{\omega C V_m} = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$ **Step 7 — Substitute $\omega = 2 \pi f$ to get the final form.** $$X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C}$$ **Conclusion:** This derivation confirms that the capacitive reactance (and hence the ease of AC signal transfer) is inversely proportional to both frequency and capacitance. Doubling the frequency halves the reactance; doubling the capacitance also halves the reactance. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Current Transfer Ratio (CTR) of an Optocoupler **Goal:** Show the relationship between the input LED current and the output phototransistor current, accounting for the phototransistor's current gain $\beta$. **Step 1 — Start with the LED's light output.** The LED converts forward current $I_F$ into optical power $P_{opt}$ with an efficiency $\eta$ (in W/A): $$P_{opt} = \eta \cdot I_F$$ **Step 2 — Light reaching the photodetector.** Only a fraction $k$ of the emitted light reaches the phototransistor's base-collector junction (the rest is lost sideways, absorbed, or reflected). The light power at the detector is: $$P_{det} = k \cdot P_{opt} = k \eta I_F$$ **Step 3 — Phototransistor converts light to base current.** The phototransistor's base current is proportional to the incident optical power, with responsivity $\mathcal{R}$ (in A/W): $$I_{photo} = \mathcal{R} \cdot P_{det} = \mathcal{R} k \eta I_F$$ **Step 4 — Phototransistor amplifies the base current.** The transistor's collector current is $I_C = \beta \cdot I_{photo}$: $$I_C = \beta \cdot \mathcal{R} \cdot k \cdot \eta \cdot I_F$$ **Step 5 — Define the overall CTR.** Grouping all the constants into a single factor $\Gamma$: $$I_C = \Gamma \cdot I_F \quad \text{where} \quad \Gamma = \beta \mathcal{R} k \eta$$ **Step 6 — Express CTR as a percentage.** $$CTR = \Gamma \times 100 \,\% = \frac{I_C}{I_F} \times 100 \,\%$$ **Conclusion:** The CTR is a product of four factors — the transistor's current gain, the photodetector's responsivity, the geometric coupling efficiency, and the LED's efficiency. Any degradation in any of these (e.g., LED aging reducing $\eta$, temperature reducing $\beta$) directly reduces the CTR. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Capacitive Isolation) **Problem:** A capacitive isolation barrier has $C = 10 \,\text{pF}$. Find the capacitive reactance at (a) 50 Hz (mains interference) and (b) 100 kHz (modulated carrier). Comment on the isolation effectiveness. **Solution (a) — At $f = 50$ Hz:** $$X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi (50)(10 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 318.3 \,\text{M}\Omega$$ **Interpretation:** At 50 Hz, the barrier presents an extremely high impedance (318 MΩ). This means mains-frequency interference is virtually blocked — excellent for patient safety. **Solution (b) — At $f = 100$ kHz:** $$X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi (100 \times 10^{3})(10 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 159.2 \,\text{k}\Omega$$ **Interpretation:** At 100 kHz, the barrier impedance drops to 159 kΩ — low enough for the modulated bio-signal to pass with negligible attenuation. The frequency ratio is **2000:1**, giving an impedance ratio of **2000:1** in favor of the carrier — a beautifully selective isolation. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Optical Isolation) **Problem:** An optocoupler has a measured $I_F = 15$ mA and a corresponding $I_C = 4.5$ mA. Find the CTR. If the LED degrades to 50 % of its original efficiency over time, what is the new $I_C$? **Solution (a) — Initial CTR:** $$CTR = \frac{I_C}{I_F} \times 100 \,\% = \frac{4.5 \,\text{mA}}{15 \,\text{mA}} \times 100 \,\% = 30 \,\%$$ **Solution (b) — Degraded LED case:** Since $I_C \propto I_F$ via CTR, a 50 % drop in efficiency means the effective $I_F$ contributing to the photo-action is reduced by half. The new collector current is: $$I_{C,new} = 4.5 \,\text{mA} \times 0.50 = 2.25 \,\text{mA}$$ **Practical Implication:** A designer should always operate the optocoupler with at least **2× safety margin** in $I_F$ to account for aging — typically designing for $I_F = 20$–30 mA initially so that even at 50 % degradation, the system still works. --- ## 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — When to Use What? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision Matrix for KTU Exam / Design Problems** > > | Scenario / Requirement | Recommended Isolation | Why? | > |---|---|---| > | Need DC + AC signal transfer with high linearity | Capacitive (with chopper) | Linear modulator gives faithful DC response | > | Need to switch a mains/AC load from patient-side circuit | Optical (phototriac, e.g., MOC3021) | Triac output handles AC loads easily | > | Need >5 kV isolation in a compact package | Optical | Optocouplers routinely offer 5–7.5 kV | > | Need very high CMRR (>120 dB) at 50/60 Hz | Capacitive | Capacitive barrier has much lower $C_{iso}$ than PCB optocoupler | > | Need to isolate a digital logic signal from patient | Optical (high-speed, e.g., 6N137) | Digital optocouplers are designed for logic-level signals | > | Need isolation + signal amplification in one IC | Capacitive (e.g., AD215) | Integrated isolation amplifiers combine both | > | Cost-sensitive, low-bandwidth patient alarm | Optical (PC817) | Cheapest, simplest solution | > | Need to pass fast transients (defibrillator pulse) | Capacitive (with protection) | Capacitive isolators have better transient immunity | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Capacitive Isolation Amplifier (Biomedical Front-End) ```mermaid flowchart LR PAT[Patient Electrodes] --> A1[Input Protection
and Buffer] A1 --> A2[Instrumentation
Amplifier] A2 --> A3[Chopper / PWM
Modulator 100kHz] A3 --> A4[Isolation Capacitor
Barrier 2.5kV] A4 --> A5[Synchronous
Demodulator] A5 --> A6[Output Low-Pass
Filter] A6 --> A7[Output Amplifier
and ADC Driver] A7 --> MON[Recorder / Display] A8[Isolated DC-DC
Converter] -.-> A1 A8 -.-> A2 A8 -.-> A3 PSU[Mains 230V] --> A8 classDef barrier fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px classDef safe fill:#e5f5e5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px class A4 barrier class PAT,A1,A2,A3 safe ``` **Reading the diagram:** The patient side (left, green) is galvanically isolated from the recorder side (right) by the **red isolation capacitor barrier**. An isolated DC-DC converter powers the patient side without any wire connection — only magnetic coupling across another isolation barrier. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Optocoupler Internal Structure and Signal Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT_SIDE [Patient-Side Input] INP[Bioelectric Signal
e.g. ECG] --> LIMIT[Current Limiting
Resistor R1] LIMIT --> LED[Infrared LED
Lambda 850nm] end subgraph BARRIER [Galvanic Isolation Barrier - Epoxy Gap] LED -- Light Photons --> PD[Phototransistor
Base-Collector Junction] end subgraph OUTPUT_SIDE [Recorder-Side Output] PD --> AMP[Output Amplifier
and Load Resistor RL] AMP --> VOUT[Isolated Output
Signal] end classDef barrierStyle fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#ff8800,stroke-width:3px classDef inputStyle fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px classDef outputStyle fill:#f0e5ff,stroke:#330099,stroke-width:2px class BARRIER,LED,PD barrierStyle class INPUT_SIDE,INP,LIMIT inputStyle class OUTPUT_SIDE,AMP,VOUT outputStyle ``` **Reading the diagram:** Notice there is **no wire** crossing the central barrier — only photons. The patient-side circuitry is fully isolated from the recorder side. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Decision Flow for Selecting Isolation Type (KTU Problem-Solving Aid) ```mermaid flowchart TD START[New Biomedical Design:
Need Patient Isolation] --> Q1{What is the
signal type?} Q1 -->|Low-level analog| Q2{Need DC accuracy?} Q1 -->|Digital logic| OPT_DIG[Use Digital Optocoupler
e.g. 6N137] Q1 -->|AC mains switching| OPT_TRIAC[Use Phototriac Optocoupler
e.g. MOC3021] Q2 -->|Yes - DC + AC| CAP_CHOP[Use Capacitive Isolation
with Chopper - e.g. ISO124] Q2 -->|No - AC only| Q3{Cost-sensitive?} Q3 -->|Yes| OPT_PC[Use Phototransistor Optocoupler
e.g. PC817] Q3 -->|No - high performance needed| CAP_PREM[Use Premium Capacitive
Isolation Amplifier - e.g. AD215] classDef startNode fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px classDef decisionNode fill:#fff5e5,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px classDef endNode fill:#e5e5ff,stroke:#000099,stroke-width:2px class START startNode class Q1,Q2,Q3 decisionNode class OPT_DIG,OPT_TRIAC,CAP_CHOP,OPT_PC,CAP_PREM endNode ``` **Reading the diagram:** A handy decision tree that mirrors the analytical matrix from Section 3.5 — use this to justify your isolation choice in 14-mark KTU design questions. --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram: ECG Front-End Showing Both Isolation Techniques Together ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PATIENT_ZONE [Patient Zone - Safely Isolated] E1[RA Electrode] --> INA1 E2[LA Electrode] --> INA1 E3[RL Electrode - Driven Right Leg] --> DRL[DRL Amplifier] INA1[INA - Instrumentation
Amplifier] --> FILT[0.05-150Hz
Bandpass Filter] FILT --> GAIN[Gain Stage x1000] GAIN --> CAP_ISO[Capacitive Isolation
Barrier] CAP_ISO --> MON_OUT DRL --> INA1 end subgraph RECORDER_ZONE [Recorder Zone - Mains Powered] MON_OUT[Isolated ECG
Signal Output] --> ADC[16-bit ADC] ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal
Processor] DSP --> LCD[Display / Storage] end PSU[230V Mains] --> REC_PSU[Isolated DC-DC] --> PATIENT_ZONE PSU --> RECORDER_ZONE classDef barrierStyle fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px classDef patientStyle fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px classDef recorderStyle fill:#e5e5ff,stroke:#000099,stroke-width:2px class CAP_ISO barrierStyle class PATIENT_ZONE patientStyle class RECORDER_ZONE recorderStyle ``` **Reading the diagram:** This is a realistic KTU-grade block diagram of a 3-lead ECG monitor. Notice the **two isolation barriers** in use: (i) the capacitive isolator carrying the ECG signal, and (ii) the isolated DC-DC converter providing power to the patient side. The Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit actively cancels common-mode interference. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **Define electrical isolation. Why is it mandatory in patient-connected biomedical instruments?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Electrical isolation is the technique of providing a complete galvanic (direct electrical) separation between two circuits while still allowing the desired signal to pass between them via a non-galvanic medium (electric field, light, or magnetic field). It is mandatory in patient-connected instruments because: - **Patient Safety (2 marks):** The patient may have direct electrical contact with the recording equipment via electrodes. Any leakage current from the line-powered instrument could flow through the patient, causing microshock (ventricular fibrillation can be triggered by as little as **10 µA** at 50/60 Hz through a cardiac catheter). - **Signal Integrity (1 mark):** It eliminates ground loops between multiple instruments connected to the same patient, which would otherwise introduce 50/60 Hz mains hum into the low-level bioelectric signal (ECG, EEG). --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an optocoupler. Mention its current transfer ratio.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **optocoupler (opto-isolator)** consists of a light-emitting diode (LED) on the input side and a photodetector (phototransistor / photodiode) on the output side, separated by a transparent insulating gap of air or epoxy. **Working (2 marks):** The input bioelectric signal drives a current $I_F$ through the LED. The LED emits infrared light proportional to $I_F$. This light crosses the insulating gap and falls on the phototransistor's base-collector junction, generating a proportional photo-base current. The transistor amplifies this to give the output collector current $I_C$. Since the only connection is light, the two sides are galvanically isolated up to the rated isolation voltage (typically 2.5–7.5 kV). **Current Transfer Ratio (1 mark):** $CTR = \dfrac{I_C}{I_F} \times 100 \,\%$, typically in the range **20 % – 600 %** depending on the optocoupler type. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **capacitive isolation amplifier** used in biomedical instruments. Discuss the role of the chopper/modulator in the circuit. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the **capacitive reactance** of an isolation capacitor. An isolation barrier of $C = 8$ pF is used in an ECG amplifier. Calculate the reactance offered at (i) 50 Hz mains frequency, and (ii) at the modulation carrier frequency of 200 kHz. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Capacitive Isolation Amplifier (7 marks):** **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** A capacitive isolation amplifier consists of: 1. **Input protection and buffer** — limits ESD and defibrillator pulses. 2. **Instrumentation amplifier (INA)** — high-CMRR amplification of differential bio-signal. 3. **Chopper / PWM modulator** — converts the low-frequency (0.05–150 Hz) bio-signal to a high-frequency square wave (carrier frequency ~50–500 kHz). Common topologies: duty-cycle modulation or pulse-width modulation. 4. **Isolation capacitor barrier** — two matched capacitors forming the differential isolation path (typical $C = 2$–10 pF, isolation voltage 2.5–5 kV). 5. **Synchronous demodulator** — recovers the original low-frequency signal by multiplying with the same carrier (in phase). 6. **Output low-pass filter** — removes the carrier ripple, leaving only the bio-signal. 7. **Isolated DC-DC converter** — powers the patient side without a galvanic path. **Why the Chopper is Needed (2 marks):** A pure bioelectric signal (e.g., ECG at 0.05–150 Hz) cannot cross the small isolation capacitor efficiently because $X_C = 1 / (2 \pi f C)$ is extremely high at such low frequencies.
