The chopper modulates the signal onto a high-frequency carrier, dramatically reducing $X_C$ and enabling efficient transfer across the barrier. **Why this is safe (1 mark):** The 50/60 Hz mains leakage, being a low-frequency signal, is blocked by the high $X_C$ at 50/60 Hz. Only the high-frequency carrier crosses; the demodulator rejects the carrier and any residual low-frequency interference. **Why the Modulator is Linear (1 mark):** A well-designed chopper uses a fixed-amplitude square wave, with the bio-signal modulating either the duty cycle (PWM) or the amplitude (AM). The synchronous demodulation is mathematically equivalent to multiplying by the same carrier, followed by low-pass filtering, which faithfully recovers the original signal. --- **Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical (7 marks):** **Derivation (4 marks):** *(Write the following step-by-step on your answer sheet.)* Starting from the capacitance definition: $$Q = C V$$ Differentiating with respect to time: $$i = \frac{dQ}{dt} = C \frac{dv}{dt}$$ For a sinusoidal input $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$: $$i(t) = C \cdot \omega V_m \cos(\omega t) = \omega C V_m \sin\!\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$ This shows current leads voltage by 90°. The ratio of peak voltage to peak current gives the capacitive reactance: $$X_C = \frac{V_m}{I_m} = \frac{V_m}{\omega C V_m} = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$ Substituting $\omega = 2 \pi f$: $$\boxed{X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C}}$$ **Numerical Calculation (3 marks):** **Case (i) — At mains frequency $f = 50$ Hz:** $$X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi (50)(8 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{1}{2.513 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 397.9 \,\text{M}\Omega$$ **Case (ii) — At carrier frequency $f = 200$ kHz:** $$X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi (200 \times 10^{3})(8 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{1}{1.005 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 99.5 \,\text{k}\Omega$$ **Comment:** The reactance at 50 Hz is approximately **4000 times higher** than at 200 kHz. This means mains-frequency leakage current is heavily attenuated (almost completely blocked), while the modulated bio-signal at 200 kHz passes through with reasonable ease. This is precisely the mechanism by which capacitive isolation protects the patient while transmitting the bio-signal — a beautifully selective frequency-dependent barrier. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the construction and working of an **optical isolator (optocoupler)** with a block diagram. Discuss the significance of the Current Transfer Ratio (CTR) and the factors that affect it. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An optocoupler is operated with an input LED current of $I_F = 12$ mA. The datasheet specifies $CTR = 60 \,\%$ initially, and the LED degrades to 70 % of its original efficiency after 5 years of continuous use. Calculate (i) the initial output collector current, (ii) the output current after degradation, and (iii) suggest a design modification to compensate for this aging effect. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Optical Isolator (7 marks):** **Construction (2 marks):** An optocoupler integrates an **infrared LED** (typically GaAs, λ ≈ 850–940 nm) and a **photodetector** (phototransistor / photodiode / photodarlington / phototriac) inside a single opaque package. The two are separated by a transparent insulating medium (epoxy resin or air gap) of typically 0.4–1.0 mm. The package is designed so that no external light can reach the photodetector. **Working (3 marks):** 1. The input bioelectric signal drives a current $I_F$ through the LED via a current-limiting resistor. 2. The LED emits infrared light whose intensity is approximately proportional to $I_F$. 3. The light crosses the insulating gap and strikes the phototransistor's base-collector junction. 4. The photons generate electron-hole pairs, producing a photo-base current. 5. The phototransistor amplifies this base current by its current gain $\beta$ to produce the output collector current $I_C$. 6. Since the only physical link is light, the two circuits share no common ground — full galvanic isolation up to 5–7.5 kV. **Current Transfer Ratio and its factors (2 marks):** $$CTR = \frac{I_C}{I_F} \times 100 \,\%$$ CTR depends on: - **LED efficiency ($\eta$):** degrades with age and temperature. - **Geometric coupling ($k$):** determined by the lens design inside the package. - **Photodetector responsivity ($\mathcal{R}$):** depends on wavelength match and material. - **Transistor current gain ($\beta$):** decreases with temperature and age. CTR values: standard phototransistor **20–300 %**; photodarlington **100–600 %**; photodiode **0.1–1 %** (low but very fast). --- **Part (b) — Numerical and Design (7 marks):** **Given:** $I_F = 12$ mA, initial $CTR = 60 \,\%$, LED efficiency retention = 70 %. **Part (i) — Initial output current (2 marks):** $$I_{C,0} = CTR \times I_F = 0.60 \times 12 \,\text{mA} = 7.2 \,\text{mA}$$ **Part (ii) — Output current after degradation (3 marks):** The CTR is proportional to the LED's optical efficiency. A 30 % loss in efficiency means the effective CTR becomes: $$CTR_{aged} = 60 \,\% \times 0.70 = 42 \,\%$$ $$I_{C,aged} = 0.42 \times 12 \,\text{mA} = 5.04 \,\text{mA}$$ **Part (iii) — Design Modification (2 marks):** **Solution 1 — Over-drive the LED:** Use a higher initial $I_F$. To maintain $I_C \geq 7.2$ mA at end-of-life, we need: $$I_{F,new} = \frac{7.2 \,\text{mA}}{0.42} = 17.14 \,\text{mA}$$ This provides safety margin for the 30 % aging. **Solution 2 — Active CTR compensation:** Use a closed-loop circuit that monitors $I_C$ and adjusts $I_F$ to keep $I_C$ constant. **Solution 3 — Use a photodarlington optocoupler** (CTR ≈ 300 %) — even after 30 % degradation, the CTR is 210 %, which is more than enough to meet the requirement with a small $I_F$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Before Writing the Exam):** > > 1. **Forgetting the role of the chopper:** Many students draw a capacitive isolation block diagram but forget to explain *why* the chopper is necessary. Without the chopper, the bio-signal cannot cross the capacitor. **Lose 2 marks.** > 2. **Confusing capacitive reactance with impedance:** $X_C$ is purely reactive (resistanceless). Don't confuse it with $Z = R + jX$. **Lose 1 mark.** > 3. **Using CTR in dB:** CTR is a percentage, NOT in dB. Converting it to dB is a common mistake. **Lose 1 mark.** > 4. **Drawing optocoupler LED in forward bias correctly:** The LED anode must be at higher potential. Drawing it reversed will lose 1 mark. > 5. **Numerical mistakes in $X_C$ calculation:** Forgetting to convert pF to F (multiply by $10^{-12}$) is the most common unit error. Always write units explicitly in every step. **Lose 1 mark.** > 6. **Omitting the "Comment" in numerical problems:** KTU examiners explicitly look for an interpretation of the numerical result. Don't end with just a number — always add a one-line "Comment" connecting the calculation to the underlying physics. > 7. **Skipping the derivation steps:** "Jumping" from the formula to the substitution will lose you the 3 process marks. Always show the algebraic transition. > 8. **Forgetting the 70 % efficiency factor in the aging problem:** The 30 % loss is in the LED's efficiency, NOT directly in the output current. The CTR must be recalculated first. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Electrical isolation** = galvanic separation between patient and instrument; mandatory for microshock protection (**< 10 µA leakage**). > - **Capacitive isolation** uses a **capacitor** as the barrier; passes AC based on $X_C = 1 / (2 \pi f C)$. > - **Optical isolation** uses an **LED + photodetector** separated by an insulating gap; only photons cross. > - **Chopper/modulator** is **always required** for capacitive isolation because bio-signals are too low-frequency to pass directly. > - **CTR** = $(I_C / I_F) \times 100 \,\%$; standard optocoupler range **20 % – 300 %**; photodarlington up to **600 %**. > - **Coupling capacitance** across the barrier must be **< 10 pF** for good CMRR. > - **Isolation voltage** for medical-grade isolators: **2.5 kV minimum**, often **5 kV** for cardiac applications. > - **Capacitive isolators** give **better linearity and CMRR**; **optical isolators** give **higher isolation voltage** and are **cheaper**. > - **DC drift** in optocouplers is a major problem; solved by **chopping the DC input** before applying to the optocoupler. > - **Design for aging:** always operate the optocoupler LED at **at least 1.5–2×** the minimum required current. > - **Common ICs to remember:** ISO124 / AD215 (capacitive isolation amplifier); PC817 / 6N136 / MOC3021 (optocouplers). > - **Key biomedical applications:** ECG, EEG, EMG amplifiers; defibrillator-protected monitors; electrosurgical unit interfaces; patient-side relay drivers. > - **Decision rule of thumb:** Capacitive for analog signal paths, Optical for digital/switching applications.

topics.title: Isolated DC Amplifier and AC Carrier Amplifier (Circuit Diagram and Working Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Isolated DC Amplifier and AC Carrier Amplifier (Circuit Diagram and Working Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Isolated DC Amplifier

### Formal Definition
An **Isolated DC Amplifier** is a special-purpose differential amplifier in which the input stage is **galvanically isolated** from the output stage and from the power supply ground. Isolation is achieved using optical, transformer (electromagnetic), or capacitive coupling techniques, allowing the amplifier to faithfully amplify very low-frequency (down to **0 Hz / DC**) bioelectric signals (such as ECG, EEG, EMG, and DC potentials from ion-selective electrodes) while providing complete electrical separation between the patient and the rest of the instrument.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The amplifier must provide patient isolation typically rated between **5000 V rms** and **7500 V rms** (per IEC 60601-1 medical safety standard) between input and output, ensuring no direct conductive path exists for leakage current to flow through the patient's body.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **glass wall between two rooms**. People in Room A (the patient connected to electrodes) can talk to people in Room B (the electronic instrument and display) through the glass — they can see the mouth movements and hear the words. However, if Room A catches fire, the flames cannot jump through the glass into Room B. Similarly, the **isolation barrier** allows the tiny electrical "voice" (millivolt-range biopotential) to pass from the patient to the instrument, but blocks any dangerous electrical "fire" (leakage current, mains voltage fault, or defibrillator discharge) from ever reaching the patient.

## 1.2 AC Carrier Amplifier

### Formal Definition
An **AC Carrier Amplifier** (also called a *carrier-amplifier* or *chopper amplifier*) is an amplifier in which a low-frequency or DC input signal is first **modulated onto a high-frequency carrier wave** (typically **400 Hz to 25 kHz**), then amplified by a stable AC amplifier, and finally **demodulated** back to the original baseband signal. The carrier technique eliminates the **offset voltage** and **drift** problems of conventional direct-coupled DC amplifiers.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **small boat crossing a wide, choppy river**. The boat alone would be tossed about and might capsize. However, if you strap the small boat onto a **large, stable cargo ship (the carrier)**, it can cross safely. Once across, you unstrap the small boat. The "cargo ship" is the high-frequency carrier, the "small boat" is the DC signal, and the river is the noisy/offset-prone DC amplifier environment. The carrier provides a stable, noise-free pathway for the tiny signal to be amplified.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** In Module 2, examiners specifically test the *block diagram* and *step-by-step working* of these amplifiers — not deep mathematical design. Memorize the signal-flow chain: **Input → Chopper/Modulator → AC Amplifier → Demodulator → Output**.

---

# 2. Why Are These Amplifiers Required in Biomedical Systems?

| Problem in Direct-Coupled DC Amplifier | Solution Provided |
|---|---|
| **Drift** due to temperature changes in transistors | Carrier/chopper modulation translates DC to AC, where drift is negligible |
| **DC offset voltage** at the output with zero input | AC coupling between stages blocks any DC offset |
| **Patient safety**: risk of microshock (< 10 mA leakage) | Galvanic isolation barrier breaks the conductive path to mains ground |
| **High Common-Mode Voltages** (e.g., 50 Hz mains pickup on body) | Isolation provides very high CMRR (often > **100 dB**) |
| **Low-frequency noise (1/f noise)** | Modulating to a higher carrier frequency moves signal away from 1/f noise corner |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric:** Patient-connected isolation amplifier leakage current must be ≤ **10 µA (normal condition)** and ≤ **50 µA (single-fault condition)** as per IEC 60601-1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 3. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 3.1 Isolated DC Amplifier — Block Architecture

An isolated DC amplifier is typically built by **cascading three sub-systems**:

1. **Input Isolated Pre-amplifier** (often an instrumentation amplifier coupled to a transformer/optical isolator)
2. **High-Gain AC Amplifier** (operating at the carrier frequency)
3. **Output Demodulator / Synchronous Rectifier + Low-Pass Filter**

The isolation barrier can be implemented using **three physical principles**:

| Isolation Method | Principle | Bandwidth | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transformer Isolation** | Magnetic coupling across a pot-core | DC to **5 kHz** | Most common in medical amplifiers |
| **Optical Isolation** | Light coupling via opto-coupler/LED-photodiode | DC to **100 kHz** | Compact designs, ECG/EEG |
| **Capacitive Isolation** | Electric field coupling across a capacitor | DC to **1 MHz** | Modern IC isolators (e.g., Analog Devices ADuM series) |

## 3.2 AC Carrier Amplifier — Working Steps

The AC carrier amplifier operates on the **Modulation → Amplification → Demodulation** chain:

### Step-by-Step Working
1. **Chopper/Modulator Stage:** A low-frequency or DC input signal $v_{in}(t)$ is multiplied (chopped) by a square-wave carrier $c(t)$ of frequency $f_c$, producing a modulated signal whose spectrum is centered on $f_c$.
2. **AC Amplification Stage:** The modulated signal is amplified by a stable, drift-free AC amplifier (typically with gain $A$ of **100 to 10,000**).
3. **Demodulation Stage:** A synchronous demodulator (phase-sensitive detector) multiplies the amplified signal by the *same* carrier $c(t)$, recovering the original baseband signal.
4. **Low-Pass Filtering Stage:** A low-pass filter (cutoff $\ll f_c$) removes the high-frequency carrier residue, producing a clean amplified DC/low-frequency output.

## 3.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $v_{in}(t)$ | Input bioelectric signal | $\le \pm 5$ (typical ECG) | mV |
| $c(t)$ | Square-wave carrier | $c(t) = \pm 1$ alternating at $f_c$ | — |
| $f_c$ | Chopper / carrier frequency | **400 Hz to 25 kHz** (typical biomedical) | Hz |
| $f_{sig}$ | Highest signal frequency | ECG: 150; EEG: 100; EMG: 500 | Hz |
| $v_{mod}(t)$ | Modulated signal | $v_{mod}(t) = v_{in}(t) \cdot c(t)$ | V |
| $A_v$ | AC amplifier voltage gain | $10^2$ to $10^4$ | V/V |
| $v_{out}(t)$ | Demodulated & filtered output | $v_{out}(t) = A_v \cdot v_{in}(t)$ | V |
| $V_{iso}$ | Isolation voltage rating | $\ge 5000$ | V rms |
| $I_{leak}$ | Patient leakage current | $\le 10$ (normal) / $\le 50$ (fault) | µA |
| CMRR | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\ge 100$ (medical grade) | dB |
| $\tau$ | LPF time constant | $\tau = RC$ | s |
| $f_{-3dB}$ | LPF cutoff frequency | $f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz |
| $f_{3dB, sys}$ | Overall system bandwidth | $f_{3dB, sys} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{in} C_{in}}$ | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The chopper frequency $f_c$ must be chosen such that $f_c \gg f_{sig,max}$ (typically at least **5× to 10× higher**) to allow easy separation in the demodulation low-pass filter. Setting $f_c$ too low causes aliasing of the signal spectrum back into baseband.

## 3.4 Engineering Utility

* **Patient Monitoring Systems:** ECG, EEG, EMG machines all use isolated DC amplifiers so that a single fault (mains wire touching the patient cable) cannot cause electrocution.
* **Defibrillator Protection:** Isolation allows the patient to be defibrillated (a **360 J shock, several kV transient**) without destroying the monitoring amplifier.
* **Long-term Signal Recording:** The near-zero drift of carrier amplifiers allows DC and very-low-frequency potentials (e.g., galvanic skin response, **0.01 Hz**) to be recorded accurately over hours.
* **Industrial Bio-sensing:** Ion-selective electrodes (pH, pO₂, pCO₂) generate pure DC potentials in the range of **± 100 mV**, which are impossible to amplify accurately with direct-coupled amplifiers due to drift.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 4. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation

## 4.1 Mathematical Model of Chopper Modulation

Let the input bioelectric signal be approximated as a slowly varying DC level $V_{DC}$ (for the purpose of analyzing the modulation principle).

Let the square-wave carrier be:

$$
c(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & 0 \le t < \dfrac{T_c}{2} \\[4pt] -1, & \dfrac{T_c}{2} \le t < T_c \end{cases}
$$

where the carrier period is $T_c = \dfrac{1}{f_c}$.

### Step 1 — Modulation
The chopper (essentially an analog multiplier or a MOSFET switch) multiplies the input with the carrier:

$$
v_{mod}(t) = V_{DC} \cdot c(t)
$$

This is an **amplitude-reversed square wave** that swings between $+V_{DC}$ and $-V_{DC}$ at frequency $f_c$.

### Step 2 — Fourier Expansion of the Modulated Signal
The square wave $c(t)$ has the well-known Fourier series:

$$
c(t) = \dfrac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n} \sin(2\pi n f_c t)
$$

Therefore the modulated signal spectrum is:

$$
v_{mod}(t) = \dfrac{4 V_{DC}}{\pi} \left[ \sin(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{1}{3}\sin(2\pi \cdot 3 f_c t) + \dfrac{1}{5}\sin(2\pi \cdot 5 f_c t) + \dots \right]
$$

**Interpretation:** The original DC level $V_{DC}$ now appears as spectral lines at $f_c, 3f_c, 5f_c, \dots$ — it has been **translated to a high-frequency band**.

### Step 3 — AC Amplification
The AC amplifier (band-pass centered on $f_c$) amplifies the dominant component at $f_c$ with voltage gain $A_v$:

$$
v_{amp}(t) = A_v \cdot \dfrac{4 V_{DC}}{\pi} \sin(2\pi f_c t) + \text{(residual harmonics)}
$$

### Step 4 — Synchronous Demodulation
The amplified signal is multiplied again by the *same in-phase* carrier $c(t)$. Multiplying two square waves of identical frequency and phase gives:

$$
c(t) \cdot c(t) = 1 \quad \text{(a constant DC value)}
$$

Hence the demodulated signal becomes:

$$
v_{demod}(t) = A_v \cdot \dfrac{4 V_{DC}}{\pi} \sin(2\pi f_c t) \cdot c(t)
$$

Using the identity $\sin(\theta) \cdot \text{sgn}(\sin\theta) = \vert \sin\theta \vert$, and then applying the LPF with cutoff $f_{LPF} \ll f_c$:

$$
v_{out}(t) = \dfrac{2 A_v \cdot 4 V_{DC}}{\pi^2} = \dfrac{8 A_v V_{DC}}{\pi^2} \approx 0.81 \, A_v \, V_{DC}
$$

### Step 5 — Net DC Gain
The overall DC gain of the carrier amplifier is therefore:

$$
A_{DC,\,net} = \dfrac{v_{out}}{V_{DC}} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot A_v \approx 0.81 \, A_v
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Insight:** The factor $8/\pi^2 \approx 0.81$ accounts for the harmonic content lost in the chopping process. Some texts round this to **0.637** when only the fundamental $f_c$ component is considered (i.e., $\dfrac{2}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{4}{\pi} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2}$ in the full square-wave case, or $\dfrac{2}{\pi}$ in the single-tone-sinusoid case). Examiners commonly accept the intuitive form $A_{net} \approx A_v$.

### Step 6 — Why Drift is Eliminated
Any slow DC drift $\Delta V_{drift}$ from the AC amplifier is *also* modulated by the second chopper, producing a signal at frequency $2f_c$, which is **rejected by the LPF**. Therefore drift contributions are attenuated by a factor proportional to $\dfrac{f_{LPF}}{f_c}$, which is extremely small — typically **0.001 or less**.

$$
\text{Drift Rejection Factor} = \dfrac{f_{LPF}}{f_c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C \cdot f_c}
$$

For $f_c = 400$ Hz and $f_{LPF} = 150$ Hz, the factor is $\dfrac{150}{400} = 0.375$, and drift is reduced to less than **1 %** of its original value.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 5. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 5.1 Block Diagram — Isolated DC Amplifier (Using Transformer Isolation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PATIENT["PATIENT SIDE (Isolated Domain)"]
        A1[Bioelectric Source e.g. ECG Electrodes] --> A2[Instrumentation Amplifier Input Gain 10]
        A2 --> A3[Modulator Chopper MOSFET f_c equals 400 Hz]
    end

    ISOBARRIER{{Galvanic Isolation Barrier Transformer 1 to 1 Turns Ratio}}

    A3 --> ISOBARRIER
    ISOBARRIER --> B1[AC Amplifier Gain 1000 Band Pass f_c]

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT SIDE (Mains-Referenced Domain)"]
        B1 --> B2[Synchronous Demodulator Phase Sensitive Detector]
        B2 --> B3[Low Pass Filter Cutoff 150 Hz]
        B3 --> B4[Output Buffer Gain 1 Unity]
        B4 --> B5[Display or Recorder or ADC]
    end

    CARRIER[Local Oscillator f_c equals 400 Hz Square Wave] -.modulation drive.-> A3
    CARRIER -.synchronous reference.-> B2

    POWER1[Isolated DC DC Converter 5 kV rms] -.powers.-> PATIENT
    POWER2[Mains Derived DC Supply] -.powers.-> OUTPUT
```

**Reading the diagram:** The double-line barrier `ISOBARRIER` represents the transformer that physically separates the patient-touching circuitry from the mains-referenced circuitry. The same oscillator drives **both** the chopper and the demodulator to keep them **phase-locked**.

## 5.2 Block Diagram — AC Carrier Amplifier (General Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[v_in(t) DC or Low Frequency Bio Signal] --> CHOP[Chopper Modulator Switch Network]
    OSC[Oscillator f_c between 400 Hz to 25 kHz] --> CHOP
    CHOP --> AMP[AC Amplifier High Gain Low Drift]
    AMP --> DEMOD[Synchronous Demodulator Phase Sensitive Detector]
    OSC --> DEMOD
    DEMOD --> LPF[Low Pass Filter RC Cutoff below 150 Hz]
    LPF --> OUT[v_out(t) Amplified DC or Low Frequency Signal]

    style IN fill:#e1f5ff
    style OUT fill:#d4f4dd
    style OSC fill:#fff4cc
    style CHOP fill:#ffe1e1
    style AMP fill:#ffe1e1
    style DEMOD fill:#ffe1e1
    style LPF fill:#ffe1e1
```

## 5.3 Signal-Waveform Visualization at Each Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph W1["Node 1: v_in(t)"]
        S1["Slow varying DC level constant 1 mV"]
    end
    subgraph W2["Node 2: v_mod(t) = v_in times c(t)"]
        S2["Square wave alternating between plus 1 mV and minus 1 mV at 400 Hz"]
    end
    subgraph W3["Node 3: v_amp(t)"]
        S3["Same square wave scaled by gain A_v equals 1000 so plus 1 V and minus 1 V"]
    end
    subgraph W4["Node 4: v_demod(t)"]
        S4["Rectified version all positive pulses at 800 Hz repetition"]
    end
    subgraph W5["Node 5: v_out(t) after LPF"]
        S5["Clean DC level of plus 1 V proportional to input"]
    end

    W1 --> W2 --> W3 --> W4 --> W5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to draw the waveforms, students often forget to label the **time axis** and the **amplitude axis values**. Always annotate the waveform: "Square wave of amplitude $\pm 1$ mV at chopper frequency $f_c = 400$ Hz" — partial marks are recovered by correct labels even if the sketch is not to scale.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 6. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the need for an isolated DC amplifier in biomedical instrumentation. Mention any two methods of achieving isolation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In biomedical instrumentation, the patient is directly connected to the recording equipment. Any leakage current above **10 µA** through the patient can cause ventricular fibrillation. An isolated DC amplifier provides a **galvanic isolation** barrier (typically rated **≥ 5000 V rms**) between the patient-side input and the mains-referenced output, breaking any conductive path for leakage current while still allowing the bioelectric signal (e.g., ECG at **1–5 mV**) to be amplified faithfully.

Two methods of achieving isolation:
1. **Transformer (electromagnetic) isolation** — using a 1:1 audio-frequency transformer as the coupling element.
2. **Optical isolation** — using an opto-coupler (LED + photodiode pair) so that the signal is transferred as light.

*(1 mark — patient safety/leakage need; 1 mark — definition; 1 mark — two methods with brief description.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is an AC carrier amplifier? Why is a chopper circuit used at the input stage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **AC carrier amplifier** is an amplifier in which a low-frequency or DC input signal is first modulated onto a high-frequency carrier wave, then amplified by a stable AC-coupled amplifier, and finally demodulated back to the original baseband.

A **chopper circuit** is used at the input because:
1. It converts the DC/low-frequency input signal into an **AC signal** (square wave) at the chopper frequency $f_c$, allowing drift-free AC amplification.
2. This **eliminates the DC offset and thermal drift** problems of direct-coupled amplifiers, since drift contributions are also modulated to a high frequency and rejected by the output low-pass filter.
3. It shifts the signal spectrum **away from the 1/f noise corner** of transistors, improving SNR.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — chopper function 1; 1 mark — chopper function 2/3.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **working of an isolated DC amplifier** used in biomedical instrumentation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **working of an AC carrier amplifier** with a block diagram and explain how it eliminates the drift of a conventional DC amplifier. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Bioelectric Source] → [Instrumentation Amplifier] → [Chopper / Modulator]
                                                              ↓
                          [AC Amplifier] ← [Isolation Barrier (Transformer)]
                                ↓
                    [Synchronous Demodulator] → [Low-Pass Filter] → [Output]
```

**Working Explanation (step-by-step, 1 mark per point):**

1. The **bioelectric signal** (e.g., ECG ≈ 1 mV, bandwidth 0.05–150 Hz) is picked up by surface electrodes and fed into a high-CMRR **instrumentation amplifier** (gain typically 10).
2. The amplified signal is then **chopped/modulated** by a square-wave carrier of frequency $f_c = 400$ Hz, producing an AC signal whose amplitude envelope represents the input.
3. This modulated AC signal is transferred across the **isolation barrier** (e.g., 1:1 transformer or opto-coupler) which provides galvanic isolation rated at **5000 V rms**.
4. On the output side, the AC signal is amplified by a **stable, drift-free AC amplifier** with gain of **1000**.
5. A **synchronous demodulator** (phase-sensitive detector), driven by the same oscillator, recovers the original baseband signal.
6. A **low-pass filter** (cutoff ≈ 150 Hz) removes the carrier residue, producing a clean amplified output proportional to the input bioelectric signal.

**Why Isolation is Essential (1 mark):**
- Patient safety: prevents leakage current > 10 µA from reaching the patient.
- Defibrillator protection: the barrier can withstand high transient voltages.
- Ground-loop elimination: prevents 50 Hz mains interference.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (refer to the AC carrier amplifier block diagram in Section 5.2 above):**

**Working (1 mark per major step):**

1. **Chopper/Modulator:** The input DC/low-frequency signal $v_{in}(t)$ is multiplied by a square-wave carrier $c(t)$ of frequency $f_c$ (typically **400 Hz to 25 kHz**), producing $v_{mod}(t) = v_{in}(t) \cdot c(t)$.
2. **AC Amplifier:** This modulated signal is amplified by a high-gain, low-drift AC amplifier. Gain $A_v$ of **1000** is typical.
3. **Synchronous Demodulator:** The amplified signal is multiplied by the *same in-phase* carrier, recovering the baseband signal.
4. **Low-Pass Filter:** A passive RC LPF (cutoff $f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, chosen to be **just above** the maximum signal frequency) removes the high-frequency carrier residue.

**Drift Elimination Mechanism (2 marks):**
In a conventional direct-coupled DC amplifier, slow thermal drift $\Delta V_{drift}$ appears as a false DC output. In a carrier amplifier, this drift is *also* chopped to the carrier frequency and then **demodulated to $2f_c$**, where it is rejected by the LPF. The drift rejection factor is approximately:

$$
\text{Drift Rejection} = \dfrac{f_{LPF}}{f_c}
$$

For $f_c = 1$ kHz and $f_{LPF} = 150$ Hz, this gives a rejection of **0.15**, reducing drift by ~ **85 %** in a single-stage system. In two-stage chopper-stabilized designs, drift can be reduced by factors of **1000× or more**, achieving effective drift rates below **0.1 µV/°C**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of an **AC carrier amplifier** and explain the function of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **transformer isolation** and **optical isolation** methods for biomedical amplifiers in terms of principle, bandwidth, voltage rating, and typical applications. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**
*Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 5.2 of these notes.*

**Function of Each Block (1 mark per block):**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Input (v_in)** | DC or low-frequency bioelectric signal — e.g., 1 mV ECG, ± 100 mV from pH electrode. |
| **Chopper / Modulator** | Multiplies $v_{in}$ with the carrier $c(t)$ at $f_c$ to translate the signal spectrum to $f_c$. Uses a MOSFET switch driven by the oscillator. |
| **Oscillator** | Generates a stable square-wave carrier at $f_c$ (typically **400 Hz to 25 kHz**). Frequency must be $\ge 5\times$ the maximum signal frequency. |
| **AC Amplifier** | Provides high voltage gain (e.g., **1000**) with no DC drift because it is AC-coupled. Dominant component at $f_c$ is amplified. |
| **Synchronous Demodulator** | A phase-sensitive detector that multiplies the amplified signal by the same carrier, recovering the baseband envelope. Also called a *lock-in detector*. |
| **Low-Pass Filter** | Removes the high-frequency components at $f_c, 2f_c, 3f_c, \dots$, passing only the baseband. Cutoff set just above the highest signal frequency. |
| **Output (v_out)** | Clean amplified DC/low-frequency signal — typically **1 V** for a 1 mV input with overall gain 1000. |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks for table + 4 marks for explanation):**

| Parameter | Transformer Isolation | Optical Isolation |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Magnetic flux coupling between two windings on a common ferrite core | Light emitted by an LED is detected by a photodiode/transistor on the other side of a transparent barrier |
| **Bandwidth** | Limited to **5–10 kHz** (due to transformer core size) | **DC to 100 kHz** (limited by photodiode speed) |
| **Isolation Voltage** | **5 kV rms** typical, up to **10 kV** with special design | **2.5–5 kV rms** typical |
| **Power Transfer** | **Can** transfer power as well as signal (using additional windings) | **Cannot** efficiently transfer power (very inefficient) |
| **Linearity** | Good, but subject to core saturation | Can have non-linearity in LED-photodiode coupling |
| **Typical Applications** | Mains-powered ECG/EEG machines, defibrillator-protected monitors | Compact battery-powered recorders, signal-level isolation in hybrid circuits |
| **Susceptibility to EMI** | Susceptible to external magnetic fields | Immune to magnetic fields (light-based) |
| **Size & Cost** | Bulky and expensive (ferrite core) | Compact, cheap, and integrable as ICs (e.g., ISO-series) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For high-voltage, high-power applications (e.g., line-powered bedside monitors), **transformer isolation** is preferred. For compact, portable, or DC-coupled applications, **optical isolation** is preferred.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the isolation voltage rating** (5000 V rms) when describing isolated amplifiers — *−1 mark*.
> 2. **Confusing "chopper" with "modulator"** — they are functionally equivalent in this context, but a chopper specifically uses a square-wave carrier, not a sine wave. Mentioning it is a *square-wave* carrier fetches the full mark.
> 3. **Not explaining the role of the Low-Pass Filter** — students often treat it as obvious, but the LPF is what removes the carrier residue and gives clean DC output. Always explain it explicitly.
> 4. **Missing the "phase-locked" requirement** between modulator and demodulator — the same oscillator must drive both; otherwise the signal is severely attenuated or distorted.
> 5. **No labels on block diagrams** — every block must be clearly named and the direction of signal flow must have **arrows**. Unlabeled diagrams fetch only 50% marks at most.
> 6. **Confusing CMRR with isolation voltage** — CMRR is for common-mode noise rejection (in dB), isolation voltage is for safety (in V rms). These are different quantities.

---

# 7. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Isolated DC Amplifier & AC Carrier Amplifier**

- **Isolated DC Amplifier = DC amplifier + Galvanic isolation barrier** (transformer / optical / capacitive).
- **Primary purpose:** **Patient safety** (limit leakage current to ≤ **10 µA**); secondary purpose: **drift elimination** and **defibrillator protection**.
- **Isolation voltage rating:** must be **≥ 5000 V rms** per medical safety standards.
- **AC Carrier Amplifier = Chopper → AC Amplifier → Demodulator → LPF.**
- **Carrier frequency $f_c$:** typically **400 Hz to 25 kHz**; must be **≥ 5× max signal frequency** for clean demodulation.
- **Chopper action:** converts DC to AC by multiplying input with square-wave carrier → signal spectrum shifted to $f_c, 3f_c, 5f_c, \dots$
- **Drift rejection factor:** $\dfrac{f_{LPF}}{f_c}$; lower LPF cutoff relative to carrier gives better drift rejection.
- **Synchronous demodulator** must be driven by the **same oscillator** as the chopper (phase-locked) for proper signal recovery.
- **Net DC gain** of carrier amplifier ≈ **0.81 × $A_v$** (when using square-wave carrier).
- **Why AC amplification avoids drift:** AC-coupled stages have no DC path, hence no DC drift accumulation; any drift is modulated to high frequency and filtered out.
- **Applications in KTU syllabus context:** ECG, EEG, EMG amplifiers, patient monitoring systems, defibrillator-protected equipment, ion-selective electrode amplifiers.
- **Three isolation methods to memorize:** Transformer (magnetic), Optical (light), Capacitive (electric field).
- **Block-diagram rule for full marks:** Always include **all arrows**, **block names**, the **oscillator driving both modulator and demodulator**, and the **isolation barrier** drawn as a double line or a shaded gap.
- **Key formula to remember for derivations:** $v_{mod}(t) = v_{in}(t) \cdot c(t)$ and $f_{-3dB,LPF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature and Pulse Rate Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temperature and Pulse Rate Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Temperature Measurement — The Engineering Definition

**Temperature** in biomedical instrumentation is defined as the degree of hotness or coldness of a body, which is a thermodynamic property directly related to the average kinetic energy of the molecules within the body. In the clinical context, the measurement of body temperature (core, skin, tympanic, and rectal) is critical because deviation from the homeostatic set-point of **37 °C (98.6 °F)** is a primary indicator of infection, inflammation, hypothermia, hyperthermia, and metabolic disorders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Temperature is a *scalar* physical quantity representing the average translational kinetic energy of particles. It is the only quantity that reads identically regardless of the type of thermometer used (Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics), provided the system is in thermal equilibrium.

### 1.2 Pulse Rate Measurement — The Engineering Definition

**Pulse Rate** (or Heart Rate, HR) is defined as the number of arterial pressure pulses (caused by ventricular contractions) detected per minute at a peripheral site such as the wrist (radial artery), neck (carotid artery), or earlobe. The normal adult resting pulse rate is **60–100 beats per minute (bpm)**, while a trained athlete may exhibit **40–60 bpm** (bradycardia). Tachycardia is defined as > 100 bpm at rest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Note:** The KTU 2024 Module-3 specifically focuses on *electronic* and *transducer-based* measurements. Mercury-in-glass clinical thermometers are only briefly mentioned for historical context, with the focus on **thermocouples, thermistors, RTDs, infrared (IR) sensors, and photo-plethysmographic (PPG) optical pulse sensors**.

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Body as a Heated Engine"

Think of the human body as a continuously running low-temperature combustion engine. Like a car's dashboard, which has a temperature gauge (measuring engine coolant heat) and a tachometer (measuring crankshaft revolutions per minute — i.e., the "pulse" of the engine), the human body has:

| Car Dashboard | Human Body Equivalent | Transducer Used |
|---|---|---|
| Coolant Temperature Gauge | Core Body Temperature | Thermocouple / Thermistor / RTD |
| Tachometer (RPM) | Heart Rate (BPM) | PPG / ECG Electrodes / Piezoelectric |
| Oil Pressure Light | Blood Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂) | Pulse Oximeter |

- **Why temperature?** Because the body is constantly producing metabolic heat (≈ 100 W at rest). When the radiator (skin) fails or the engine (mitochondria) overheats, temperature changes. Detecting this *non-invasively* requires a transducer that converts heat (thermal energy) into an electrical signal (voltage or resistance change).

- **Why pulse rate?** Every heartbeat ejects a bolus of blood (≈ 70 mL per stroke at rest). When this bolus passes through a peripheral artery, the artery wall expands slightly. A transducer placed on the skin detects this micro-expansion (mechanical pulse) or the associated change in light absorption (optical pulse) and converts it to a countable electrical signal.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Connection:** Modern smartwatches (Apple Watch, Fitbit) use exactly the two principles covered in this module: (1) a **thermistor** or contact **RTD** for skin temperature, and (2) a **PPG (photo-plethysmography)** optical sensor for pulse rate. This makes Module 3 directly relevant to consumer wearable technology.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Biomedical Temperature Transducers

**A. Contact-Type (require physical touch with the body):**
1. **Thermocouple** — uses Seebeck effect of dissimilar metals.
2. **Thermistor** — uses resistance change of semiconductor oxides.
3. **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)** — uses resistance change of pure metal (typically platinum Pt-100 / Pt-1000).
4. **Semiconductor (IC) sensor** — e.g., LM35, AD590, DS18B20.

**B. Non-Contact-Type (radiation-based):**
1. **Infrared (IR) Pyrometer / Tympanic Thermometer** — uses Stefan-Boltzmann law of blackbody radiation.
2. **Thermal Imaging Camera** — uses Planck's law for spectral radiance.

---

### 1.5 Classification of Biomedical Pulse Rate Transducers

1. **Photo-Plethysmography (PPG)** — optical: LED + photodetector.
2. **Piezoelectric Sensor** — mechanical: detects arterial wall displacement.
3. **Strain Gauge / Capacitive** — mechanical: detects volume changes.
4. **ECG-based** — electrical: detects R-R interval.
5. **Doppler Ultrasound** — acoustic: detects blood cell velocity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Thermocouple — Seebeck Effect Principle

**Operating Principle:** When two dissimilar metals (A and B) are joined to form two junctions — a *measuring junction* (hot, T_hot) and a *reference junction* (cold, T_cold) — a continuous, temperature-dependent electromotive force (EMF, in volts) is generated due to the **Seebeck Effect** (discovered by Thomas Seebeck in 1821).

**Physical "Why":** Electrons in different metals have different Fermi energy levels. When one junction is heated, electrons diffuse from the hotter metal to the colder metal at a different rate in each leg, producing a net charge accumulation and a measurable DC voltage.

**Key KTU Points:**
- Thermocouples are **active transducers** (self-generating — no excitation voltage required).
- The output is a small DC voltage: typically **40 µV/°C** for a Type K (Chromel-Alumel) thermocouple.
- **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Inserting a third metal into the circuit does NOT affect the EMF, *provided* both ends of the third metal are at the same temperature.
- **Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** The EMF for T₁ → T₃ equals the sum of EMFs for T₁ → T₂ and T₂ → T₃. This is the basis of *software cold-junction compensation*.

**Standard Thermocouple Types (ISA/IEC Color Code):**

| Type | Materials | Range (°C) | Sensitivity | Use in Biomedical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T | Copper-Constantan | -200 to 350 | 43 µV/°C | Cryosurgery probes |
| J | Iron-Constantan | 0 to 760 | 50 µV/°C | Skin probes |
| **K** | **Chromel-Alumel** | **-200 to 1260** | **40 µV/°C** | **Most common in biomedical** |
| E | Chromel-Constantan | -200 to 870 | 68 µV/°C | High sensitivity |
| R/S | Pt-Pt/Rh | 0 to 1480 | 10 µV/°C | Hyperthermia probes |

---

### 2.2 The Thermistor — Semiconductor Temperature Sensor

**Operating Principle:** A **thermistor** (THERMally sensitive resISTOR) is a sintered semiconductor device (usually made from metal oxides of Mn, Ni, Co, Fe) whose electrical resistance varies **strongly and non-linearly** with temperature.

**Two Types:**
- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *decreases* as temperature *increases*. Most common in biomedical.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *increases* as temperature *increases*. Used in overcurrent protection, not for measurement.

**Physical "Why":** In semiconductors, the number of charge carriers (electron-hole pairs) increases exponentially with temperature. Since conductance is proportional to carrier density, resistance drops exponentially.

**Steinhart-Hart Equation (High-Accuracy Model):**
$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C (\ln(R))^3$$
where T is in Kelvin, R is in ohms, and A, B, C are Steinhart-Hart coefficients specific to the thermistor.

**β-Parameter Model (Simplified, common in KTU problems):**
$$R(T) = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$
where R₀ is resistance at reference temperature T₀ (usually 298 K = 25 °C), and β is the material constant (typically 3000–5000 K for biomedical NTC thermistors).

**Sensitivity:** For an NTC thermistor with β = 4000 K, sensitivity α = -β/T² ≈ **-4.4 %/°C at 25 °C** — about **10× more sensitive** than an RTD. This makes thermistors ideal for measuring small body temperature differentials (e.g., 0.1 °C changes during inflammation).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Thermistor sensitivity is *not* constant; it varies with T. Do not use α = constant — it is defined as α = (1/R)(dR/dT) and equals -β/T², which is a function of T.

---

### 2.3 The RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)

**Operating Principle:** A pure metal (Pt, Ni, Cu) exhibits a **linear and positive** change in resistance with temperature due to increased lattice phonon scattering of conduction electrons.

**Most Common:** **Pt-100** (Platinum, 100 Ω at 0 °C) and **Pt-1000** (1000 Ω at 0 °C). Platinum is preferred because it is chemically stable, has a high melting point (1768 °C), and a nearly linear R-T relationship.

**Callendar-Van Dusen Equation (Full Model):**
$$R(T) = R_0 \left[ 1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100) T^3 \right]$$
For T > 0 °C, the C term vanishes: R(T) = R₀(1 + AT + BT²)

**Linearized (Approximate) Form — used in KTU problems:**
$$R(T) \approx R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \, \Delta T \right]$$
where ΔT = T - T₀ and α is the temperature coefficient of resistance. For platinum, **α = 0.00385 Ω/Ω/°C** (IEC 60751 standard).

**Sensitivity:** Approximately **+0.385 Ω/°C** for Pt-100. Much lower than thermistor, but **linear**, **stable**, and **accurate over wide range**.

---

### 2.4 Infrared (IR) Pyrometers — Non-Contact Thermometry

**Operating Principle:** Every object above absolute zero (0 K) emits **thermal (blackbody) radiation**. The intensity and spectral distribution of this radiation depend on the object's temperature. By measuring the radiated IR power (typically in the 8–14 µm wavelength band, where the human body emits peak radiation at 37 °C), the surface temperature can be inferred *without physical contact*.

**Stefan-Boltzmann Law:**
$$W = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4$$
where:
- W = total radiated power per unit area (W/m²)
- ε = emissivity (≈ 0.98 for human skin)
- σ = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** (Stefan-Boltzmann constant)
- T = absolute temperature in Kelvin

**Wien's Displacement Law:**
$$\lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{b}{T}$$
where b = **2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K** is Wien's displacement constant.

For T = 310 K (37 °C body temperature):
$$\lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{310} \approx 9.35 \, \mu m$$
This falls in the **8–14 µm atmospheric window** — the reason IR thermometers use thermopile detectors with 8–14 µm optical filters.

**Detector Used:** **Thermopile** (a series-connected array of miniature thermocouples) — converts incident IR radiation into a measurable EMF. Typical sensitivity: **50–100 µV/°C of target temperature**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Application:** Tympanic (ear) thermometers measure IR radiation from the tympanic membrane, which shares blood supply with the hypothalamus (the body's thermoregulatory center). This gives a near-core temperature reading in < 1 second.

---

### 2.5 Photo-Plethysmography (PPG) — Optical Pulse Rate Sensor

**Operating Principle:** PPG illuminates the skin with an LED (typically green at 530 nm or red at 660 nm) and measures the reflected/transmitted light intensity with a photodiode. During each heartbeat:
- **Systole (heart contraction):** Blood volume in the arterioles increases → more light absorption → *less* light reaches the photodetector → a **valley** in the PPG signal.
- **Diastole (heart relaxation):** Blood volume decreases → *more* light reaches the photodetector → a **peak** in the PPG signal.

The PPG signal has two components:
- **DC component:** Average light absorbed by skin, bone, tissue, venous blood, and non-pulsatile arterial blood.
- **AC component:** Small pulsatile variation (≈ 1–2 % of DC) due to arterial blood volume change. The **AC peak-to-peak interval corresponds to one cardiac cycle (R-R equivalent).**

**Pulse Rate Calculation:**
$$\text{BPM} = \frac{60}{T_p}$$
where T_p is the time interval (in seconds) between two consecutive PPG peaks.

**Advantages:** Non-invasive, low-cost, suitable for wearables.
**Limitations:** Sensitive to motion artifacts; ambient light interference.

---

### 2.6 Piezoelectric Pulse Sensor

**Operating Principle:** A piezoelectric crystal (e.g., PVDF — polyvinylidene fluoride) or PZT ceramic generates a charge/voltage when mechanically stressed. When placed on the skin over an artery (radial, carotid), the arterial wall displacement (≈ 0.1–1 mm) during each heartbeat applies a periodic stress to the piezo element, producing a small AC voltage signal at the heart-rate frequency.

**Sensitivity:** Typical PVDF sensor produces ≈ 1 mV per mm of displacement. The signal is in the 0.5–5 Hz band (corresponding to 30–300 bpm).

**Charge Amplifier Required:** Because the piezo element behaves as a charge source with very high output impedance, a charge amplifier (op-amp with feedback capacitor) is mandatory for signal conditioning.

---

### 2.7 📋 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is the most essential revision resource for KTU ESE Module 3 problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seebeck EMF | $V = \alpha_S (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})$ | α_S in µV/°C; Type K ≈ 40 |
| 2 | Thermistor (β-model) | $R(T) = R_0 e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | T in K, β in K (3000–5000) |
| 3 | Thermistor sensitivity | $\alpha = \frac{1}{R}\frac{dR}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$ | per °C, negative for NTC |
| 4 | RTD linearized | $R(T) \approx R_0[1 + \alpha \Delta T]$ | α = 0.00385 /°C for Pt |
| 5 | RTD sensitivity | $\frac{dR}{dT} = R_0 \alpha$ | Ω/°C, positive |
| 6 | Stefan-Boltzmann | $W = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | σ = 5.67e-8 W/m²K⁴ |
| 7 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{\text{peak}} = b / T$ | b = 2.898e-3 m·K |
| 8 | Pulse Rate (PPG/Piezo) | $\text{BPM} = 60 / T_p$ | T_p in seconds |
| 9 | Sensitivity of any temp sensor | $S = \Delta V / \Delta T$ or $\Delta R / \Delta T$ | V/°C or Ω/°C |
| 10 | Cold-junction compensation | $V_{T_1 \to T_3} = V_{T_1 \to T_2} + V_{T_2 \to T_3}$ | Law of intermediate T |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX SAFETY NOTE:** All absolute-value bars in the formulas above (e.g., for $\vert \alpha \vert$) are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the surrounding markdown table pipe `|` characters. The KTU examiner may deduct marks for sloppy notation.

---

### 2.8 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

| Application | Sensor Used | Why This Sensor? |
|---|---|---|
| **Digital Clinical Thermometer (oral/axillary)** | NTC Thermistor (10 kΩ at 25 °C) | High sensitivity (4 %/°C) allows ±0.1 °C accuracy in < 60 s |
| **Tympanic (ear) Thermometer** | IR Thermopile | Measures core hypothalamic temperature in 1 s, no cross-infection |
| **Skin/Surface Probe (incubator)** | Type K Thermocouple | Wide range, fast response, cheap, sterile single-use |
| **Hyperthermia Cancer Treatment** | Pt-100 RTD or Fiber-optic probe | High accuracy, RF-immune; fiber-optic avoids EM heating artifact |
| **Cryosurgery Probe** | Type T Thermocouple | Works down to -200 °C, low error at very low T |
| **Smartwatch (Apple/Fitbit)** | PPG optical + NTC thermistor | Wearable, battery-friendly, optical + thermal fusion |
| **ICU Pulse Monitor** | PPG (ear/finger clip) | Continuous SpO₂ + HR monitoring |
| **Bedside HR Monitor** | Piezoelectric (PVDF) | Detects mechanical arterial wall pulse; cheap, robust |
| **ECG Holter Monitor** | Ag/AgCl electrodes | Electrical pulse via R-R interval — gold standard |
| **Fetal Heart Rate Monitor** | Doppler Ultrasound | Non-invasive, deep tissue penetration, safe for fetus |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermistor Resistance at a New Temperature

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A thermistor has R₀ = 10 kΩ at T₀ = 298 K (25 °C) and β = 4000 K. Find its resistance at body temperature T = 310 K (37 °C). Also find the temperature coefficient of resistance (sensitivity) at this new temperature.

**Given:**
- R₀ = 10,000 Ω
- T₀ = 298 K
- β = 4000 K
- T = 310 K (which is 37 °C, the clinical baseline)

**Step 1 — State the governing equation:**
The β-model equation for an NTC thermistor is:
$$R(T) = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the reciprocal-temperature difference:**
$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{310} - \frac{1}{298}$$

$$\frac{1}{310} = 0.0032258 \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{298} = 0.0033557 \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{310} - \frac{1}{298} = 0.0032258 - 0.0033557 = -0.0001299 \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by β:**
$$\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) = 4000 \times (-0.0001299) = -0.5196$$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponential:**
$$e^{-0.5196} = 0.5949$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final resistance:**
$$R(310 \, \text{K}) = 10000 \times 0.5949 = 5949 \, \Omega \approx 5.95 \, \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Compute the temperature coefficient of resistance (sensitivity) at T = 310 K:**
The general sensitivity definition is:
$$\alpha = \frac{1}{R} \frac{dR}{dT}$$

Starting from R(T) = R₀ · e^{β(1/T - 1/T₀)}:
$$\frac{dR}{dT} = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)} \cdot \frac{d}{dT} \left[ \beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) \right]$$

$$\frac{d}{dT} \left[ \beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) \right] = \beta \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{T^2} \right) = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

So:
$$\frac{dR}{dT} = R(T) \cdot \left( -\frac{\beta}{T^2} \right)$$

Therefore:
$$\alpha = \frac{1}{R} \frac{dR}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

Substituting β = 4000 K, T = 310 K:
$$\alpha = -\frac{4000}{(310)^2} = -\frac{4000}{96100} = -0.04162 \, \text{per °C} = -4.162 \, \%\text{per °C}$$

**Final Answer:**
- **R(310 K) = 5949 Ω ≈ 5.95 kΩ**
- **α (310 K) = -4.16 %/°C**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Resistance drops from 10 kΩ to 5.95 kΩ as temperature rises by 12 °C (from 25 °C to 37 °C). The sensitivity is -4.16 %/°C, meaning for every 1 °C rise, the resistance drops by 4.16 % of its current value. This is why thermistors need linearization in a Wheatstone bridge.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pt-100 RTD Resistance at Body Temperature

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A Pt-100 RTD (R₀ = 100 Ω at 0 °C) is used to measure body temperature. Find its resistance at 37 °C and 40 °C. The temperature coefficient α = 0.00385 /°C (IEC standard).

**Given:**
- R₀ = 100 Ω at T₀ = 0 °C
- α = 0.00385 /°C

**Step 1 — Apply the linearized RTD equation:**
$$R(T) = R_0 [1 + \alpha \, (T - T_0)]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute T = 37 °C:**
$$R(37) = 100 \times [1 + 0.00385 \times 37]$$
$$R(37) = 100 \times [1 + 0.14245]$$
$$R(37) = 100 \times 1.14245 = 114.245 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Substitute T = 40 °C:**
$$R(40) = 100 \times [1 + 0.00385 \times 40]$$
$$R(40) = 100 \times [1 + 0.154] = 100 \times 1.154 = 115.4 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity calculation:**
$$\frac{dR}{dT} = R_0 \alpha = 100 \times 0.00385 = 0.385 \, \Omega / °C$$

**Step 5 — Voltage equivalent in a 1 mA constant-current source:**
If the RTD is excited with I = 1 mA, the output voltage at 37 °C:
$$V = I \cdot R = 10^{-3} \times 114.245 = 0.11425 \, \text{V} = 114.25 \, \text{mV}$$

The voltage change per °C:
$$\Delta V = I \times 0.385 \, \Omega = 0.385 \, \text{mV/°C}$$

**Final Answer:**
- **R(37 °C) = 114.245 Ω**  (matches industry Pt-100 lookup tables)
- **R(40 °C) = 115.4 Ω**
- **Sensitivity: 0.385 Ω/°C** (or 0.385 mV/°C at 1 mA excitation)

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Peak IR Emission Wavelength from Human Skin at 37 °C

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** Find the peak wavelength of thermal radiation emitted by human skin at 37 °C. State which IR atmospheric window this falls in, and which optical filter an IR thermometer should use.

**Given:**
- T = 37 °C = 37 + 273 = 310 K
- Wien's displacement constant b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K

**Step 1 — State Wien's Displacement Law:**
$$\lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{b}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$\lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m·K}}{310 \, \text{K}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**
$$\lambda_{\text{peak}} = 9.348 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 9.35 \, \mu m$$

**Step 4 — Identify the IR atmospheric window:**
The earth's atmosphere has two main transmission windows: **3–5 µm** and **8–14 µm**. The 9.35 µm peak falls squarely in the **8–14 µm atmospheric window** — meaning that IR radiation from the human body is not absorbed by atmospheric water vapor/CO₂ and can reach an IR thermometer outside the body.

**Step 5 — Optical filter selection:**
An IR thermometer for human body should use a **bandpass optical filter centered at 8–14 µm** (e.g., 10 µm center, with 1 µm bandwidth). This rejects visible/UV light and accepts only the body's IR emission band.

**Final Answer:**
- **λ_peak = 9.35 µm**
- **Filter band: 8–14 µm**
- **Practical use: Tympanic / forehead IR thermometers**

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Pulse Rate Calculation from PPG Signal

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A PPG sensor placed on a fingertip records peaks at the following timestamps (in seconds): t₁ = 0.000, t₂ = 0.870, t₃ = 1.700, t₄ = 2.550, t₅ = 3.400. Determine (a) the average period of the cardiac cycle, and (b) the pulse rate in BPM.

**Step 1 — Compute intervals between consecutive peaks:**
- T₁ = t₂ - t₁ = 0.870 - 0.000 = 0.870 s
- T₂ = t₃ - t₂ = 1.700 - 0.870 = 0.830 s
- T₃ = t₄ - t₃ = 2.550 - 1.700 = 0.850 s
- T₄ = t₅ - t₄ = 3.400 - 2.550 = 0.850 s

**Step 2 — Compute the average period T_avg:**
$$T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4}{4} = \frac{0.870 + 0.830 + 0.850 + 0.850}{4}$$

$$T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{3.400}{4} = 0.850 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute pulse rate in BPM:**
$$\text{BPM} = \frac{60 \, \text{s/min}}{T_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{60}{0.850}$$

$$\text{BPM} = 70.59 \approx 71 \, \text{bpm}$$

**Step 4 — Check for arrhythmia:**
- Standard deviation of intervals: σ ≈ 0.014 s. The R-R interval variation is < 10 %, so the rhythm is **regular** (no arrhythmia).

**Final Answer:**
- **Average period T_avg = 0.850 s**
- **Pulse rate ≈ 71 bpm** (within normal 60–100 bpm range)

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Thermocouple EMF for Body Temperature

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A Type K thermocouple is used with an ice-bath reference junction (T_cold = 0 °C = 273 K). The measuring junction is placed under the tongue (T_hot = 37 °C = 310 K). The Seebeck coefficient is α_S = 40 µV/°C. Find the output EMF.

**Step 1 — State the Seebeck equation:**
$$V = \alpha_S \times (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature difference in °C:**
$$\Delta T = 37 - 0 = 37 \, °C$$

**Step 3 — Convert α_S to consistent units:**
$$\alpha_S = 40 \, \mu V/°C = 40 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{V/°C}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the EMF:**
$$V = 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 37 = 1480 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{V} = 1.48 \, \text{mV}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using Kelvin:**
$$V = 40 \times 10^{-6} \times (310 - 273) = 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 37 = 1.48 \, \text{mV} \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:**
- **V_out = 1.48 mV** (this small signal requires a precision instrumentation amplifier with gain ≈ 1000 to reach 1.48 V full-scale for ADC)

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Temperature Sensors

| Property | Thermocouple | Thermistor (NTC) | RTD (Pt-100) | IC Sensor (LM35) | IR Pyrometer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Active/Passive** | Active (self-gen) | Passive (needs excitation) | Passive (needs excitation) | Active (self-gen) | Active (self-gen) |
| **Output** | µV–mV | kΩ change | Ω change | 10 mV/°C | µV–mV |
| **Sensitivity** | Low (40 µV/°C) | Very High (-4 %/°C) | Medium (0.385 Ω/°C) | High (10 mV/°C) | Medium (50 µV/°C) |
| **Linearity** | Moderate | Poor (logarithmic) | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate (T⁴ law) |
| **Range (°C)** | -200 to 1260 | -55 to 150 | -200 to 850 | -55 to 150 | -50 to 2000 |
| **Accuracy** | ±1–2 °C | ±0.1–0.5 °C | ±0.1 °C | ±0.5–1 °C | ±0.5–1 °C |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | High | Very Low | High |
| **Response Time** | Fast (ms) | Fast (s) | Moderate | Fast | Very Fast (ms) |
| **Contact?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Biomedical Use** | Skin probes, hyperthermia | Digital clinical thermometer | ICU, hyperthermia | Wearable thermometers | Tympanic, forehead |
| **KTU Pick (Exam)** | **Very common** | **Very common** | **Common** | Rare | Common |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Body Temperature Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Body Heat Source\nSkin, Tympanic\nMembrane, Rectum] --> B[Temperature\nTransducer\nTC / Thermistor /\nRTD / IR Detector]
    B --> C[Signal\nConditioning\nAmplifier + Filter]
    C --> D[ADC\nAnalog to Digital\nConverter]
    D --> E[Microcontroller\nLinearization +\nCalibration]
    E --> F[Display\nLCD / 7-Segment]
    E --> G[Alarm / Data\nLogger]

    classDef src fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef transducer fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef sigcond fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000

    class A src
    class B transducer
    class C sigcond
    class D,E process
    class F,G output
```

**Explanation of the Flow:**
- The **Body Heat Source** (skin/tissue) is the physical measurand.
- The **Transducer** converts thermal energy → electrical signal (EMF/resistance change).
- The **Signal Conditioning** stage amplifies the small µV/mV signal and filters 50/60 Hz mains interference.
- The **ADC** digitizes the analog signal (12-bit or 16-bit).
- The **Microcontroller** applies linearization (e.g., Steinhart-Hart for thermistor) and converts to °C.
- The **Display** shows the temperature; the **Alarm** triggers if T > 38 °C (fever) or T < 35 °C (hypothermia).

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — PPG-Based Pulse Rate Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[LED Driver\nGreen 530 nm\nor Red 660 nm] --> A2[LED\nLight Source]
    A2 --> A3[Arterial Blood\nin Fingertip /\nEarlobe]
    A3 --> A4[Photodetector\nPhotodiode /\nPhototransistor]
    A4 --> B1[Transimpedance\nAmplifier\nCurrent to Voltage]
    B1 --> B2[Bandpass Filter\n0.5 Hz to 5 Hz\n30 to 300 BPM]
    B2 --> C1[Peak Detector\nCircuit]
    C1 --> C2[Microcontroller\nR-R Interval\nMeasurement]
    C2 --> D1[Pulse Rate\nDisplay BPM]
    C2 --> D2[Heart Rate\nVariability\nHRV Analysis]

    classDef light fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef tissue fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef cond fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef display fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#e65100,color:#000

    class A1,A2 light
    class A3,A4 tissue
    class B1,B2 cond
    class C1,C2 process
    class D1,D2 display
```

**Explanation:**
- The **LED** (driven by a constant-current source for stable intensity) shines light into the tissue.
- **Arterial blood** in the capillaries absorbs light; pulsatile volume changes modulate the absorption.
- The **Photodetector** converts the transmitted/reflected light to a current signal.
- The **Transimpedance Amplifier** converts this small current to a usable voltage.
- The **Bandpass Filter** isolates the heart-rate frequency band (0.5–5 Hz).
- The **Peak Detector** identifies systolic peaks, and the **Microcontroller** measures the time between peaks to compute BPM.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Comparative Transducer Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Biomedical Temperature\nMeasurement Required]) --> Q1{Contact\nAllowed?}
    Q1 -->|No| IR[Infrared Pyrometer\nTympanic / Forehead\nThermopile Detector]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Accuracy\nRequired?}
    Q2 -->|±0.1 °C| Q3{Range?}
    Q2 -->|±1 °C OK| TC[Type K Thermocouple\nSkin / Disposable\nProbes]
    Q3 -->|Narrow\n0 to 50 °C| NTC[NTC Thermistor\nDigital Clinical\nThermometer]
    Q3 -->|Wide\n-200 to 850 °C| RTD[Pt-100 / Pt-1000\nRTD\nICU / Hyperthermia]
    IR --> Out([Output: Patient\nTemperature in °C])
    TC --> Out
    NTC --> Out
    RTD --> Out

    classDef start fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef tech fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef endnode fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000

    class Start start
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 decision
    class IR,TC,NTC,RTD tech
    class Out endnode
```

**Decision Logic Explained:**
- If the measurement must be **non-contact** (e.g., a sleeping infant, a screening station, a tympanic reading), the only viable option is an **IR Pyrometer**.
- If **contact is allowed** and only **moderate accuracy** is needed (e.g., a disposable skin probe during surgery), a **Type K Thermocouple** is the cheapest, fastest option.
- If **high accuracy** is required in a **narrow body-temperature range** (0–50 °C), the **NTC thermistor** is the gold standard — used in 90 % of digital clinical thermometers.
- If **wide range** and **linearity** are needed (e.g., cryosurgery at -80 °C or hyperthermia at 80 °C), the **Pt-100 RTD** is mandatory.

---

### 4.4 Signal Waveform — PPG Output and Peak Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Time 0.0 s\nPeak 1\nSystole] -.T1 = 0.85 s.-> P2[Time 0.85 s\nPeak 2\nSystole]
    P2 -.T2 = 0.83 s.-> P3[Time 1.68 s\nPeak 3\nSystole]
    P3 -.T3 = 0.85 s.-> P4[Time 2.53 s\nPeak 4\nSystole]
    P4 -.T4 = 0.86 s.-> P5[Time 3.39 s\nPeak 5\nSystole]

    P1 --> Calc[Microcontroller\nT_avg = Sum Ti / N\n= 0.8475 s]
    P2 --> Calc
    P3 --> Calc
    P4 --> Calc
    P5 --> Calc
    Calc --> BPM[Pulse Rate = 60 / T_avg\n= 60 / 0.8475\n= 70.8 BPM]

    classDef peak fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000

    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 peak
    class Calc calc
    class BPM result
```

**Waveform Interpretation:** The PPG signal shows a characteristic double-peak per cardiac cycle — a main systolic peak (larger) and a secondary dicrotic notch peak (smaller) corresponding to aortic valve closure. For pulse rate calculation, only the main systolic peaks are used.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, OEICT723]**
Define Seebeck effect. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a thermocouple-based temperature measurement system. Why is a reference junction required?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Seebeck Effect (1 mark):** The Seebeck effect is the generation of an electromotive force (EMF) across two junctions of dissimilar metals when those junctions are maintained at different temperatures. The magnitude of EMF is proportional to the temperature difference between the hot and cold junctions.

**Working (1.5 marks):** A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar metal wires (e.g., Chromel and Alumel for Type K) joined at one end to form the **measuring (hot) junction**, which is placed in contact with the body. The other ends are connected to the measuring instrument, forming the **reference (cold) junction**, typically maintained at 0 °C using an ice bath or by electronic cold-junction compensation. The temperature difference (T_hot - T_cold) generates a small DC voltage (typically 40 µV/°C for Type K), which is amplified by an instrumentation amplifier, digitized by an ADC, and displayed as a temperature reading.

**Need for Reference Junction (0.5 mark):** A thermocouple measures *temperature difference*, not absolute temperature. The reference junction provides a known, stable baseline (0 °C) so that the measured EMF can be unambiguously converted to the absolute temperature of the measuring junction using standard EMF-vs-T lookup tables.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEICT723]**
Differentiate between active and passive transducers. Give two examples of each used in temperature measurement.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Transducer | Passive Transducer |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Generates electrical output *without* external power | Requires external excitation (voltage/current) to operate |
| **Energy Conversion** | Self-generating (measurand → electrical) | Modulates externally supplied energy |
| **Examples in Temperature** | **Thermocouple** (heat → EMF), **IR Pyrometer / Thermopile** (radiation → EMF) | **NTC Thermistor** (resistance changes with T), **Pt-100 RTD** (resistance changes with T) |

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for table-style contrast, 1 mark for examples.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — On Thermistor & Thermocouple

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**Q3 (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a thermistor-based digital clinical thermometer. Derive the expression for thermistor resistance variation with temperature using the β-parameter model. A thermistor has R₀ = 50 kΩ at 25 °C and β = 3950 K. Calculate its resistance at 50 °C and find the temperature coefficient of resistance at 50 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of Digital Clinical Thermometer (3 marks):**

A digital clinical thermometer consists of:
1. **NTC thermistor** (the sensing tip, e.g., 10 kΩ or 50 kΩ at 25 °C).
2. **Wheatstone bridge** — the thermistor forms one arm; three precision resistors form the other three arms. A reference voltage V_ref is applied across the bridge.
3. **Differential amplifier** (instrumentation amplifier) — amplifies the bridge imbalance voltage.
4. **ADC + Microcontroller** — converts the analog voltage to digital code, applies Steinhart-Hart linearization, and computes T in °C.
5. **LCD display** — shows the temperature to 0.1 °C resolution.
6. **Buzzer/LED** — signals when the reading is stable (peak-hold algorithm).

The thermistor is placed under the tongue or in the axilla. As body heat transfers to the thermistor, its resistance drops (NTC behavior), unbalancing the bridge. The microcontroller detects the imbalance, converts to temperature, and displays the result.

**Derivation of β-Parameter Model (2 marks):**

For an NTC thermistor, the carrier density n(T) follows an Arrhenius-type exponential:
$$n(T) = n_0 e^{-E_g / (2kT)}$$
Since conductance σ ∝ n, and resistance R = 1/σ, we get R ∝ e^{+E_g/(2kT)}. Writing E_g/(2k) as β (material constant):
$$R(T) = R_0 e^{\beta (1/T - 1/T_0)}$$
where R₀ is resistance at reference T₀. (Note: T must be in Kelvin.)

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given R₀ = 50 kΩ, T₀ = 298 K, β = 3950 K, T = 50 °C = 323 K.

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{323} - \frac{1}{298} = 0.0030960 - 0.0033557 = -0.0002597 \, K^{-1}$$

$$\beta \times (-0.0002597) = 3950 \times (-0.0002597) = -1.0258$$

$$R(323 \, K) = 50000 \times e^{-1.0258} = 50000 \times 0.3585 = 17925 \, \Omega \approx 17.9 \, k\Omega$$

**Temperature coefficient at 50 °C (T = 323 K):**
$$\alpha = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} = -\frac{3950}{(323)^2} = -\frac{3950}{104329} = -0.03786 \, /°C = -3.79 \, \%/°C$$

**Final Answer:** R(50 °C) ≈ **17.9 kΩ**, α ≈ **-3.79 %/°C**

---

**Q3 (b)** Explain the working of a Type K thermocouple. What is cold-junction compensation? A Type K thermocouple with α_S = 40 µV/°C has a hot junction at 100 °C and a cold junction at 25 °C. Calculate the output EMF. If a copper lead of 1 m length is introduced in the circuit (Law of Intermediate Metals), what will be the new EMF? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of Type K Thermocouple (2 marks):**
A Type K thermocouple uses **Chromel** (90 % Ni, 10 % Cr) and **Alumel** (95 % Ni, 2 % Al, 2 % Mn, 1 % Si) wires joined at the measuring (hot) junction. When this junction is heated, electrons diffuse at different rates in the two metals due to their different Seebeck coefficients, producing a net DC voltage at the open ends (cold junction). This EMF is measured by a high-impedance voltmeter or instrumentation amplifier. The output is approximately linear over 0–500 °C with sensitivity 40 µV/°C. Application in biomedical: disposable skin probes, hypodermic needle probes for tissue temperature.

**Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC) (2 marks):**
Since a thermocouple measures *temperature difference*, the cold-junction temperature must be known. In modern instruments, the ice-bath is replaced by a **second temperature sensor** (a thermistor or IC sensor) at the terminal block where the thermocouple wires connect to the instrument. The instrument measures the cold-junction temperature T_cj and adds the corresponding EMF (from standard tables) to the measured thermocouple EMF, effectively "compensating" so that the reading corresponds to T_hot. This is called **software cold-junction compensation** and uses the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures**.

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

EMF for ΔT = 100 - 25 = 75 °C:
$$V_1 = \alpha_S \times \Delta T = 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 75 = 3000 \times 10^{-6} \, V = 3 \, mV$$

When a copper lead is introduced (Law of Intermediate Metals): **EMF remains unchanged = 3 mV**, because both ends of the copper lead are at the same temperature (T_cold = 25 °C), and the law states that inserting a third metal anywhere in the circuit (with both its ends at the same T) does not affect the EMF.

**Final Answer:** V_EMF = **3 mV** (unchanged with copper lead).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — On IR Pyrometer & PPG Pulse Sensor

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**Q4 (a)** Explain the working of an infrared tympanic thermometer using the Stefan-Boltzmann and Wien's Displacement laws. A human body at 37 °C emits IR radiation. Calculate: (i) the peak emission wavelength, and (ii) the radiated power per unit area assuming ε = 0.98. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of IR Tympanic Thermometer (3 marks):**
The tympanic membrane (eardrum) shares blood supply with the hypothalamus (the body's thermoregulatory center), so its temperature is a true representation of core body temperature. The IR thermometer has:
1. A **waveguide / speculum** inserted into the ear canal.
2. An **optical bandpass filter** (8–14 µm) that admits only the body's IR radiation and blocks visible light.
3. A **thermopile detector** — an array of 50–100 miniature thermocouples in series. The incident IR radiation heats the hot junctions; the cold junctions are thermally anchored to the housing. The temperature difference produces an EMF proportional to the 4th power of target temperature.
4. An **amplifier + ADC + microcontroller** — converts EMF to T (using the inverse T⁴ relation, with Stefan-Boltzmann constant and emissivity correction).
5. A **LCD display** showing the temperature in < 1 second.

**Wien's Displacement Law (1 mark):**
$$\lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{b}{T}, \quad b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K$$

At T = 310 K:
$$\lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{310} = 9.35 \times 10^{-6} \, m = 9.35 \, \mu m$$

**Stefan-Boltzmann Law (1 mark):**
$$W = \varepsilon \sigma T^4, \quad \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, W/(m^2 K^4)$$

At T = 310 K, ε = 0.98:
$$W = 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (310)^4$$

Compute (310)⁴:
$$310^2 = 96100; \quad 310^4 = (96100)^2 = 9.235 \times 10^9$$

$$W = 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.235 \times 10^9$$

$$W = 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 92.35$$

$$W = 0.98 \times 523.6 = 513.1 \, W/m^2$$

**Final Answers:** **λ_peak = 9.35 µm**; **W ≈ 513 W/m²**

---

**Q4 (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a PPG-based pulse rate measurement system. A PPG sensor records peak-to-peak intervals (in ms) of: 850, 870, 830, 860, 845. Calculate the average pulse rate in BPM and comment on whether the rhythm is regular. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram & Working (4 marks):** *(See Section 4.2 for the detailed Mermaid diagram. Describe verbally in exam:)*

The PPG system consists of:
1. **LED source** (green 530 nm for surface; red 660 nm for deeper penetration) — illuminates the fingertip or earlobe.
2. **Tissue + arterial blood** — pulsatile blood volume modulates light absorption.
3. **Photodiode / phototransistor** — detects the transmitted/reflected light and converts to a small current.
4. **Transimpedance amplifier** — converts current to voltage with high gain.
5. **Bandpass filter (0.5–5 Hz)** — extracts the heart-rate frequency band and rejects DC and high-frequency noise.
6. **Peak detector** — identifies systolic peaks.
7. **Microcontroller** — measures the time between consecutive peaks (R-R interval equivalent) and computes BPM = 60/T_avg.
8. **Display** — shows BPM.

The DC component of the PPG signal contains the constant absorption due to skin, bone, and venous blood. The **AC component** (~1–2 % of DC) contains the pulsatile arterial information. Each cardiac cycle produces one AC peak.

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given intervals (in ms): 850, 870, 830, 860, 845.

**Sum:** 850 + 870 + 830 + 860 + 845 = **4255 ms**

**Average period T_avg:**
$$T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{4255}{5} = 851 \, \text{ms} = 0.851 \, \text{s}$$

**Average pulse rate:**
$$\text{BPM} = \frac{60}{0.851} = 70.5 \, \text{bpm}$$

**Rhythm regularity check (1 mark):**
- Mean = 851 ms
- Standard deviation σ:
  - Deviations: -1, +19, -21, +9, -6
  - Squared: 1, 361, 441, 81, 36 = sum 920
  - σ = √(920/5) = √184 ≈ 13.6 ms
- Coefficient of variation CV = σ/mean = 13.6/851 = **1.6 %**

**Comment:** Since CV < 10 %, the rhythm is **regular (normal sinus rhythm)**. No arrhythmia is present.

**Final Answer:** **Average pulse rate ≈ 70–71 BPM**, **rhythm is regular**.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin in the thermistor equation.** The β-model equation *requires* absolute temperature (K). Writing T = 37 instead of T = 310 will yield a wildly wrong R value and full mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Using the linearized RTD equation outside its valid range.** The linear approximation R = R₀(1 + αΔT) is only valid for T > 0 °C. For T < 0 °C, the cubic Callendar-Van Dusen term must be included.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Stefan-Boltzmann units.** σ is in **W/(m²·K⁴)**, NOT W/(m²·°C⁴). The temperature must be in Kelvin, not Celsius. A student writing W = εσ(37)⁴ instead of W = εσ(310)⁴ will lose 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing peak wavelength (Wien) with peak frequency.** Wien's law gives the wavelength at which spectral radiance is maximum. Do not invert b to get T from frequency — they are different.
>
> 5. **Reporting pulse rate without specifying the time interval source.** In ECG, the R-R interval is used. In PPG, the peak-to-peak interval is used. Mixing these up or not stating the source loses a definition mark.
>
> 6. **Drawing block diagrams without arrow labels.** Every arrow in a KTU block diagram must be labeled (e.g., "PPG signal", "EMF in mV"). Unlabeled arrows lose 0.5–1 mark per diagram.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to mention the role of the bandpass filter in PPG.** Students often write "filter the noise" but should specifically say "0.5–5 Hz bandpass filter to isolate the heart-rate frequency band and reject ambient light / motion artifacts / 50 Hz mains interference."
>
