1. **Catheter and Dome:** A heparinized saline-filled catheter is introduced into the artery. The catheter transmits hydrostatic pressure to a domed chamber housing the diaphragm. All air bubbles must be removed (de-bubble) to prevent damping artifacts.

2. **Pressure Transducer (Diaphragm + Strain Gauges):** A thin silicon diaphragm (10–25 µm) deflects under pressure. Four semiconductor strain gauges diffused on the diaphragm convert this deflection into resistance changes via the piezoresistive effect.

3. **Wheatstone Bridge:** The four gauges are connected in a bridge configuration excited by a stable DC voltage (5–10 V). The differential output is proportional to pressure.

4. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** A high-CMRR (> 80 dB) amplifier boosts the millivolt-level bridge output to a usable 0–5 V signal.

5. **Filter and Calibration:** A low-pass filter (20–40 Hz) removes high-frequency noise. A zero-balance circuit cancels baseline offset, and a span adjustment sets the calibration.

6. **Display and Recorder:** The conditioned signal drives a digital display showing systolic/diastolic values and a recorder displaying the pressure waveform.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Derivation of four-active-arm bridge output:**

With all four arms active, two diagonally opposite arms (say R₁ and R₃) experience tensile strain (+ΔR) and the other two (R₂ and R₄) experience compressive strain (−ΔR). The bridge becomes:

$$
V_o \;=\; V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R_2 - \Delta R}{(R_1 + \Delta R) + (R_2 - \Delta R)} - \frac{R_4 - \Delta R}{(R_3 + \Delta R) + (R_4 - \Delta R)} \right)
$$

For a balanced bridge where all $R_i = R$, after simplification:

$$
\boxed{\,V_o \;=\; V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}\,}
$$

This is double the single-active-arm output.

**Numerical Calculation:**

- $G_F = 110$, $V_{ex} = 6$ V, $a = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $t = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $E = 1.9 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $\nu = 0.28$
- $P = 120 \times 133.32 = 15998.4$ Pa

**Step 1: $a^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-6}$ m²; $t^2 = 4 \times 10^{-10}$ m²; $1 - \nu^2 = 1 - 0.0784 = 0.9216$**

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{3 \times 15998.4 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.9216}{8 \times 1.9 \times 10^{11} \times 4 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{0.09960}{608} \;\approx\; 1.638 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 2:**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; 110 \times 1.638 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 0.01802
$$

**Step 3:**

$$
V_o \;=\; 6 \times 0.01802 \;=\; 0.1081\ \text{V} \;\approx\; 108.1\ \text{mV}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the piezoresistive effect. Derive the relationship between fractional change in resistance and applied strain, and discuss why semiconductor strain gauges are preferred over metallic strain gauges in electronic manometers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A capacitive transducer is used as an electronic manometer. The transducer has plate area $A = 50$ mm² and initial gap $d = 0.1$ mm. The dielectric is air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$). If the diaphragm deflects by 5 µm under arterial pressure, calculate: (i) the initial capacitance, (ii) the change in capacitance, and (iii) the fractional change $\Delta C / C$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Piezoresistive Effect:** The piezoresistive effect is the change in electrical resistivity of a semiconductor or metal when subjected to mechanical strain. When a semiconductor crystal is stressed, its band structure changes, altering the mobility and concentration of charge carriers, and hence its resistivity changes significantly.

**Derivation of ΔR/R relation:**

The resistance of a uniform conductor is $R = \rho L / A$. Taking the logarithmic differential:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}
$$

For small strains, $\Delta L / L = \varepsilon$ (longitudinal strain) and $\Delta A / A = -2\nu \varepsilon$ (lateral strain, Poisson effect). Substituting:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon (1 + 2\nu)
$$

The total fractional resistance change equals the piezoresistive component (Δρ/ρ) plus the dimensional component $\varepsilon(1 + 2\nu)$.

Defining the **gauge factor**:

$$
G_F \;=\; \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} \;=\; 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}
$$

The gauge factor is defined as the ratio of fractional resistance change to applied strain. The first two terms are the geometric contribution (~1.6 for metals), and the third term is the piezoresistive contribution.

**Why Semiconductor Preferred:**

| Property | Metal Gauge | Semiconductor Gauge |
|---|---|---|
| Gauge factor $G_F$ | ~2.0 | **100–200** (50–100× higher) |
| Sensitivity | Low | **High** (smaller deflections detectable) |
| Size | Larger | **Miniature** (can be diffused on diaphragm) |
| Hysteresis | Low | Slightly higher |

The high $G_F$ of semiconductors enables detection of very small diaphragm deflections, which is critical for measuring low-amplitude pressures accurately.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $A = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m², $d = 0.1 \times 10^{-3}$ m = $10^{-4}$ m, $\varepsilon_r = 1$, $\Delta d = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m.

**Step (i): Initial Capacitance**

$$
C \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \;=\; \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-4}}
$$

$$
C \;=\; \frac{4.427 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-4}} \;=\; 4.427 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F} \;\approx\; 4.43\ \text{pF}
$$

**Step (ii): Change in Capacitance**

For small gap decrease ($\Delta d \ll d$):

$$
\Delta C \;\approx\; -C \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d} \;=\; -4.43 \times 10^{-12} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\Delta C \;=\; -4.43 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.05 \;=\; -0.2215 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F} \;\approx\; -0.222\ \text{pF}
$$

**Step (iii): Fractional Change**

$$
\frac{\Delta C}{C} \;=\; -\frac{\Delta d}{d} \;=\; -0.05 \quad \text{or} \quad 5\%
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Electronic Manometer (Module 3)**

- ✅ Electronic manometer = **direct, invasive, transducer-based** BP measurement; uses **fluid-filled catheter + strain gauge transducer + Wheatstone bridge + amplifier**.
- ✅ **Direct method** = measures **true intra-arterial/intracardiac** pressure; **Indirect method** = cuff-based (auscultatory / oscillometric).
- ✅ Working principle: **Pressure → Diaphragm deflection → Resistance change → Bridge output voltage → Amplification → Display**.
- ✅ Key equation: $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$ (four-active-arm bridge) or $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R)$ (single-active-arm).
- ✅ Gauge factor: **G_F = 2** (metals), **G_F = 100–200** (semiconductors). Higher G_F = higher sensitivity.
- ✅ Sensitivity increases with: **larger diaphragm radius $a$**, **thinner diaphragm $t$**, **higher excitation $V_{ex}$**, **higher gauge factor $G_F$**.
- ✅ Catheter–dome acts as a **second-order underdamped system** with natural frequency **20–40 Hz** and optimal damping **ζ ≈ 0.707**.
- ✅ **Fast-flush test** is used clinically to verify damping characteristics.
- ✅ Common errors: **air bubbles** (under-damping), **clots** (over-damping), **catheter whip artifact** (high-frequency noise).
- ✅ Conversion: **1 mmHg ≈ 133.32 Pa**; **MAP = Pd + ⅓(Ps − Pd)**.
- ✅ Transducer types: **Strain gauge** (most common), **Capacitive** (high accuracy), **Piezoelectric** (AC only), **Semiconductor diffused** (modern disposable).
- ✅ Always use **instrumentation amplifier** (CMRR > 80 dB) to amplify the millivolt-level bridge signal.
- ✅ Typical calibration: **1 V = 100 mmHg** output scaling.
- ✅ Clinical applications: **Cardiac catheterization, ICU monitoring, OR monitoring, PCWP measurement, CardioMEMS implants**.
- ✅ Advantages over auscultatory: **continuous, true pressure, waveform-based, suitable for all patients**.
- ✅ Disadvantages: **invasive, infection risk, requires skilled operator, expensive, time-consuming setup**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pressure Amplifiers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Pressure Amplifier** is a specialized signal conditioning electronic circuit designed to amplify the low-level electrical output generated by a **biomedical pressure transducer** (e.g., strain-gauge bridge, capacitive sensor, or piezoelectric crystal) to a level suitable for further processing, display, recording, or analog-to-digital conversion. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the pressure amplifier is treated as a critical subsystem within the **cardiovascular and respiratory measurement chain**, where physiological pressures (e.g., arterial blood pressure of ~120/80 mmHg, intracranial pressure of ~10–15 mmHg, or intrauterine pressure) must be transduced, amplified with high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**, and isolated for patient safety (per **IEC 60601-1** medical safety standard). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The pressure amplifier is conventionally realized using an **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** topology (three op-amp configuration) or a **Carrier Amplifier** (chopper/AC-excited bridge amplifier) when the transducer is a strain-gauge Wheatstone bridge. ### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine you are trying to hear a whisper in a noisy stadium. The whisper is the **millivolt-level signal** coming from a pressure sensor placed inside an artery, and the stadium roar is the **electrical noise, mains hum (50 Hz), and motion artifacts**. A **pressure amplifier** acts like a **highly directional, noise-cancelling microphone** placed right next to the whispering person. It: 1. **Boosts** the whisper to a loud, audible level (amplification of mV to V). 2. **Ignores** the background roar (high CMRR rejects common-mode noise). 3. **Listens to only one person** even if many are talking (differential input rejects interference common to both input lines). For a clinical example: when a catheter-tip pressure sensor measures the blood pressure waveform inside the heart, the signal is about **1–5 mV** per 100 mmHg. The pressure amplifier boosts this to a **0–5 V** range that an ICU monitor can graph on its screen. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard excitation voltage** for strain-gauge bridges: **$V_{ex} = 5\text{ V DC}$** or **$V_{ex} = 5\text{ V RMS}$ at 400 Hz** (carrier mode). - **Full-scale physiological pressure** (arterial): **$P_{FS} = 300\text{ mmHg}$** ≈ **$40\text{ kPa}$**. - **Strain gauge gauge factor**: **$G_F \approx 2.0$** for constantan alloy. - **Patient isolation standard**: leakage current **$< 10\,\mu\text{A}$** (CF-type cardiac floating, IEC 60601-1). > [!NOTE] > **Why CMRR matters in biomedical amplifiers:** A pressure transducer sitting on a patient is essentially an antenna picking up 50/60 Hz mains interference. Since the interference appears equally on both input leads (common-mode), an amplifier with **CMRR > 100 dB** is non-negotiable for clinical-grade pressure measurement. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Pressure Measurement Signal Chain A biomedical pressure measurement system follows this cascade: 1. **Pressure Source** → (Physiological event, e.g., systolic contraction) 2. **Transducer** → (Strain-gauge bridge / capacitive / piezoelectric element) 3. **Pressure Amplifier** → (Instrumentation amplifier / carrier amplifier) 4. **Filter** → (Low-pass to remove high-frequency noise) 5. **Display / Recorder** → (Oscilloscope, chart recorder, digital monitor) ### 2.2 Why Standard Op-Amps Fail for Pressure Signals A plain op-amp (e.g., $\mu$A741) cannot be used directly because: - **Input impedance is finite** → loads the high-impedance transducer bridge, causing gain error. - **CMRR is moderate (~70 dB)** → 50 Hz mains interference corrupts the reading. - **No built-in gain trimming** → calibration across transducers is tedious. - **No patient isolation** → unsafe for direct cardiac contact. Hence, the **three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** is the gold standard. ### 2.3 The Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) — Operation Logic The IA consists of two distinct stages: **Stage 1: Buffering + Differential Gain (the front-end pair)** - Two op-amps ($A_1$, $A_2$) buffer the input signals via non-inverting inputs. - A single external gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between the two inverting inputs sets the differential gain. - Negative feedback through resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ creates a balanced difference amplifier. **Stage 2: Difference Amplifier (the subtractor)** - A third op-amp ($A_3$) configured as a unity-gain difference amplifier with four matched resistors ($R_3$, $R_3$, $R_4$, $R_4$). - It rejects any common-mode voltage that survived Stage 1 and converts the differential signal to a single-ended output referenced to ground. ### 2.4 The Carrier Amplifier — Concept Used when the transducer is a **Wheatstone strain-gauge bridge**. The bridge is excited by an **AC carrier signal** (typically 400 Hz to 5 kHz sine wave), not DC. The output is an **amplitude-modulated (AM) carrier** whose envelope carries the pressure information. Advantages: - Eliminates **DC drift and 1/f noise**. - Allows easy **chopper stabilization** for ultra-low offset. - Enables **transformer isolation** between patient and mains-powered circuitry (essential for cardiac catheters). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Carrier amplifiers are the workhorse of **invasive blood pressure (IBP) monitors** in cardiac catheterization labs, ICU ventilators, and anesthesia workstations. They offer nano-volt-level resolution needed for low-pressure measurements (e.g., esophageal pressure ~5 mmHg). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Differential gain of 3-op-amp IA | $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | Dimensionless, matched $R_3/R_2$ | | 2 | Common-mode gain (ideal IA) | $A_{cm} = 0$ | Only if $R_3/R_2$ ratio perfectly matched | | 3 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Expressed in **dB**; biomedical target $\geq 100$ dB | | 4 | Bridge output (1 active gauge) | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Wheatstone bridge, single variable resistor | | 5 | Bridge output (2 active gauges) | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{2}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Adjacent arms, opposite strain directions | | 6 | Bridge output (4 active gauges) | $V_o = V_{ex}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Full bridge — maximum sensitivity | | 7 | Gauge factor relation | $G_F = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\epsilon}$ | $\epsilon$ = mechanical strain (dimensionless) | | 8 | Pressure to strain (membrane) | $\epsilon = \dfrac{3PR^2}{8Eh^2}\,(1-\nu^2)$ | Thin circular diaphragm, $E$ = Young's modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | | 9 | Carrier amp demodulation | $V_{out} = K \cdot V_{carrier} \cdot V_{mod}$ | Synchronous demodulator output | | 10 | Input bias current effect | $V_{offset} = I_B \cdot R_s$ | $R_s$ = source resistance, $I_B$ in **pA to nA** for FET-input IA | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Differential Gain of the 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier **Given circuit:** Two input buffer op-amps $A_1$, $A_2$ with feedback resistors $R_1$, gain-setting resistor $R_G$, followed by a difference amplifier with resistors $R_3$ (feedback) and $R_2$ (input). Let the two transducer outputs be $V_1$ and $V_2$ (with $V_2 > V_1$). **Step 1:** The non-inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$ see $V_1$ and $V_2$ directly (high input impedance buffering). **Step 2:** By the virtual-short principle, the inverting inputs are at the same potentials: $$V^-_{A_1} = V_1 \quad \text{and} \quad V^-_{A_2} = V_2$$ **Step 3:** The current flowing through $R_G$ (between the two inverting inputs) is: $$I_{RG} = \dfrac{V_2 - V_1}{R_G} = \dfrac{V_d}{R_G}$$ where $V_d = V_2 - V_1$ is the differential input voltage. **Step 4:** This same current also flows through both $R_1$ resistors (since no current enters the ideal op-amp inputs). Therefore the voltage at the output of $A_1$ is: $$V_{A1} = V_1 - I_{RG}\cdot R_1 = V_1 - \dfrac{V_d R_1}{R_G}$$ **Step 5:** Similarly, the output of $A_2$ is: $$V_{A2} = V_2 + I_{RG}\cdot R_1 = V_2 + \dfrac{V_d R_1}{R_G}$$ **Step 6:** The differential output of Stage 1 is: $$V_{A2} - V_{A1} = (V_2 - V_1) + 2\cdot\dfrac{V_d R_1}{R_G} = V_d\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$$ **Step 7:** This differential signal is fed into the difference amplifier (Stage 2). For a perfectly matched difference amplifier ($R_3/R_2$ identical in both paths), the output is: $$V_{out} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\,(V_{A2} - V_{A1})$$ **Step 8:** Combining Stage 1 and Stage 2, the overall differential gain is: $$ \boxed{\;A_d = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_d} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\cdot\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\;} $$ **Interpretation:** A single resistor $R_G$ controls the gain across a wide range (e.g., $R_G = 100\,\Omega$ to $100\,\text{k}\Omega$ gives gains from ~1 to ~1000), without disturbing the symmetric bridge balance. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: CMRR of the Instrumentation Amplifier **Given:** A finite resistor mismatch $\delta$ in the difference amplifier such that one branch has $R_3(1+\delta)$ and the other $R_2(1-\delta)$. **Step 1:** The ideal difference amplifier has output: $$V_{out} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\,(V_+ - V_-)$$ **Step 2:** With mismatch, the actual transfer is: $$V_{out} = \dfrac{R_3(1+\delta)\,V_+ - R_2(1-\delta)\,V_-}{R_2(1-\delta) + R_3(1+\delta)}$$ **Step 3:** For a common-mode input $V_+ = V_- = V_{cm}$, the common-mode gain becomes (after first-order Taylor expansion in $\delta$): $$A_{cm} \approx \dfrac{\delta\,(R_3/R_2)}{1 + (R_3/R_2)} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\delta}{2} \quad \text{(for matched ratios)}$$ **Step 4:** The CMRR is the ratio of differential to common-mode gain: $$\text{CMRR} = \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \approx \dfrac{2}{\delta}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\!\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$$ **Step 5:** In decibels: $$ \boxed{\;\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{\delta}\right) + 20\log_{10}\!\left[\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\!\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right]\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Insight:** To achieve **CMRR > 100 dB** at 50 Hz, the resistor mismatch $\delta$ must be less than **0.02 %** (i.e., 0.0002). This is why biomedical IAs (e.g., INA128, AD620) use **laser-trimmed, monolithically integrated resistor networks** — discrete resistors cannot achieve this precision. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Output Voltage of a Single-Active-Gauge Wheatstone Bridge **Given:** A Wheatstone bridge with three fixed resistors $R$ and one active strain gauge $R + \Delta R$ excited by $V_{ex}$. **Step 1:** Output voltage is the difference between the two midpoints: $$V_o = V_{ex}\!\left[\dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \dfrac{R}{2R}\right]$$ **Step 2:** Simplify the left fraction: $$\dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} = \dfrac{R(1 + \Delta R/R)}{R(2 + \Delta R/R)} = \dfrac{1 + \Delta R/R}{2 + \Delta R/R}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute and use the series expansion $1/(2+x) \approx 1/2 - x/4$ for small $x = \Delta R/R$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_o &= V_{ex}\!\left[\dfrac{1 + x}{2 + x} - \dfrac{1}{2}\right] \\[4pt] &= V_{ex}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{x}{4} - \dfrac{x^2}{8} + \cdots\right) - \dfrac{1}{2}\right] \\[4pt] &\approx V_{ex}\cdot\dfrac{x}{4} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Using the gauge factor definition $G_F = (\Delta R/R)/\epsilon$: $$ \boxed{\;V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4}\cdot G_F\cdot\epsilon\;} $$ **Numerical check:** For $V_{ex} = 5\text{ V}$, $G_F = 2.0$, $\epsilon = 10^{-3}$ (typical): $$V_o = \dfrac{5}{4}\cdot 2.0\cdot 10^{-3} = 2.5\text{ mV}$$ This tiny output justifies the need for a high-gain, low-noise pressure amplifier. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DC vs. Carrier Pressure Amplifier | Parameter | DC Instrumentation Amplifier | AC Carrier Amplifier | |-----------|------------------------------|----------------------| | Excitation | DC voltage (e.g., **5 V**) | AC sine (e.g., **400 Hz, 5 V RMS**) | | Transducer coupling | Direct | Transformer / capacitive | | Drift performance | Suffers from **1/f noise and thermal drift** | Excellent — no DC drift issues | | Patient isolation | Requires opto-isolator | Natural — transformer isolation | | Bandwidth | DC to ~kHz | Centered on carrier frequency | | Typical application | External cuffs, non-invasive BP | Invasive BP, ICP, IUP catheters | | CMRR at 50 Hz | Limited by 1/f region | Excellent due to frequency shifting | | Complexity | Low (one IA chip) | High (oscillator, demodulator, filter) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Biomedical Pressure Measurement System flowchart LR srcA[Physiological Pressure Source
Arterial Blood Vessel] --> trdA[Pressure Transducer
Strain Gauge Bridge] trdA --> exuA[Excitation Source
DC 5V or 400Hz AC Carrier] exuA --> trdA trdA --> preA[Pressure Amplifier
Instrumentation Amplifier] preA --> fltA[Low Pass Filter
Cutoff approx 40 Hz] fltA --> disA[Display and Recorder
ICU Monitor] preA -.CMRR greater than 100dB.-> fltA disA --> docA[Clinician Decision Support] style srcA fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#333 style trdA fill:#cddafd,stroke:#333 style exuA fill:#cddafd,stroke:#333 style preA fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333 style fltA fill:#cddafd,stroke:#333 style disA fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#333 style docA fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#333 ### 4.2 Schematic — 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Topology flowchart TB in1[Input V1
from bridge] --> op1[Op-Amp A1
Buffer plus Gain] in2[Input V2
from bridge] --> op2[Op-Amp A2
Buffer plus Gain] op1 --- r1a[Feedback Resistor R1] op2 --- r1b[Feedback Resistor R1] op1 --- rg[Gain Resistor RG] op2 --- rg op1 --> da1[Positive Input
of Subtractor] op2 --> da2[Negative Input
of Subtractor] da1 --> op3[Op-Amp A3
Difference Amplifier] da2 --> op3 op3 --- r3fb[Feedback R3] op3 --- r2in[Input Resistor R2] op3 --> vout[Output Vout
Single Ended] vout -.-> mon[ICU Monitor Display] style in1 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#333 style in2 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#333 style op1 fill:#cddafd,stroke:#333 style op2 fill:#cddafd,stroke:#333 style op3 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333 style vout fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#333 style mon fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#333 ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Carrier Amplifier Architecture flowchart LR osc[400Hz Sine Oscillator
Stable Wein Bridge] --> xfmr1[Isolation Transformer
Primary] xfmr1 --> brg[Strain Gauge Bridge
AC Excited] brg --> amp[AC Amplifier
High Gain Bandpass] amp --> demod[Synchronous Demodulator
Phase Sensitive Detector] osc -.Reference.-> demod demod --> lp[Low Pass Filter
Less than 40 Hz Cutoff] lp --> out[DC Output
Proportional to Pressure] out --> iso[Opto Isolator
Patient Safety Barrier] iso --> rec[Recorder and Display] style osc fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333 style brg fill:#cddafd,stroke:#333 style amp fill:#cddafd,stroke:#333 style demod fill:#cddafd,stroke:#333 style lp fill:#cddafd,stroke:#333 style out fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#333 style iso fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#333 style rec fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#333 ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a pressure amplifier. Why is high CMRR critical in biomedical pressure measurements?** **Model Answer:** CMRR is defined as the ratio of differential-mode gain ($A_d$) to common-mode gain ($A_{cm}$), expressed in decibels: $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$$ High CMRR is critical because pressure transducers placed on or inside the human body act as **antennas** for 50/60 Hz mains interference and other electromagnetic noise. This interference appears equally on both input leads (common-mode). A high CMRR (typically $\geq 100$ dB in clinical-grade amplifiers) ensures that this interference is rejected, and only the true differential pressure signal is amplified. Without high CMRR, the display would be swamped by 50 Hz hum, rendering cardiac pressure waveforms unreadable. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between a DC instrumentation amplifier and an AC carrier amplifier used for biomedical pressure measurement.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | DC Instrumentation Amplifier | AC Carrier Amplifier | |---|---|---| | Excitation | DC voltage | AC sine wave (typically 400 Hz) | | Patient isolation | Needs separate opto-isolator | Natural via transformer | | Drift and 1/f noise | Susceptible | Immune (signals modulated away from DC) | | Sensitivity to thermal EMF | High | Negligible | | Typical use | Non-invasive BP cuffs, external sensors | Invasive catheters, ICU pressure modules | The carrier amplifier provides superior drift performance and inherent patient safety, while the DC instrumentation amplifier offers simpler design and lower cost. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024] **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** used as a pressure amplifier. Derive an expression for its differential gain. (7 marks) **(b)** A pressure transducer with a full-scale output of 4 mV is to be amplified to 4 V. Design an instrumentation amplifier using the topology of part (a), assuming the difference-amplifier stage has fixed resistors $R_2 = R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$. Specify the value of the gain-setting resistor $R_G$ and the feedback resistor $R_1$. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Operation and Derivation:** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. The three-op-amp IA consists of two input buffer amplifiers ($A_1$, $A_2$) and a difference amplifier ($A_3$). The transducer output is applied differentially to the non-inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$, providing high input impedance. The gain is set by a single resistor $R_G$ between the two inverting inputs and the matched feedback resistors $R_1$. The derived differential gain is: $$A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\cdot\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$$ **Key advantages** over a single op-amp difference amplifier: - Single-resistor gain control - Symmetric high input impedance on both inputs - Easily trimmed for high CMRR **(b) Numerical Design:** **Step 1:** Required overall gain: $$A_d = \dfrac{V_{out,FS}}{V_{in,FS}} = \dfrac{4\text{ V}}{4\text{ mV}} = 1000$$ **Step 2:** With $R_2 = R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, the second stage gain is: $$A_{d2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} = 1$$ **Step 3:** Therefore, Stage 1 must provide all 1000× gain: $$1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G} = 1000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G} = 999$$ **Step 4:** Choose a convenient value, e.g., $R_1 = 49.95\text{ k}\Omega$ (standard: $R_1 = 50\text{ k}\Omega$): $$R_G = \dfrac{2\cdot 49.95\text{ k}\Omega}{999} \approx 100\,\Omega$$ **Final Design Values:** - $R_1 = 50\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_G \approx 100\,\Omega$. - The single resistor $R_G$ can be a precision multiturn trimpot to allow fine calibration. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 — Alternative] **(a)** Explain the **Wheatstone bridge configuration** used with strain-gauge pressure transducers. Derive the output voltage expression for: (i) one active gauge, (ii) four active gauges in a full bridge. (7 marks) **(b)** A full-bridge pressure transducer with all four gauges having gauge factor $G_F = 2.1$ is bonded to a steel diaphragm ($E = 200\text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$, radius $R = 10\text{ mm}$, thickness $h = 0.5\text{ mm}$). The bridge is excited with 5 V DC. Calculate the bridge output voltage when the applied pressure is 100 mmHg. (Take 1 mmHg = 133.32 Pa.) (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Wheatstone Bridge:** Refer to Section 3.3. The bridge converts small resistance changes ($\Delta R$) in the strain gauges into a differential voltage. - **(i) Single active gauge:** $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ - **(ii) Full bridge (4 active gauges):** $V_o = V_{ex}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ The full-bridge configuration is 4× more sensitive than the single-gauge configuration. **(b) Numerical Calculation:** **Step 1:** Convert pressure: $$P = 100\text{ mmHg} \times 133.32\text{ Pa/mmHg} = 13\,332\text{ Pa}$$ **Step 2:** Compute strain using the thin circular diaphragm formula: $$\epsilon = \dfrac{3PR^2}{8Eh^2}(1-\nu^2)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute: $$\epsilon = \dfrac{3 \cdot 13\,332 \cdot (0.01)^2}{8 \cdot 200\times 10^9 \cdot (0.0005)^2}\cdot(1 - 0.3^2)$$ **Step 4:** Numerator: $$3 \cdot 13\,332 \cdot 10^{-4} = 3.9996 \approx 4.0$$ **Step 5:** Denominator: $$8 \cdot 2\times 10^{11} \cdot 2.5\times 10^{-7} = 4\times 10^{5}$$ **Step 6:** Strain: $$\epsilon = \dfrac{4.0}{4\times 10^5}\cdot(0.91) = 10^{-5}\cdot 0.91 = 9.1\times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 7:** Resistance change ratio: $$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F\cdot\epsilon = 2.1\cdot 9.1\times 10^{-6} = 1.911\times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 8:** Full-bridge output: $$V_o = V_{ex}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = 5 \cdot 1.911\times 10^{-5} = 95.55\,\mu\text{V}$$ **Step 9:** This 95.55 μV signal is amplified by the pressure amplifier (gain ~10,000) to give ~0.96 V at the output — well within the 0–5 V display range. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly (1) forget to convert mmHg to Pa, (2) drop the $(1-\nu^2)$ factor, (3) use the single-gauge bridge formula when the problem states a full bridge (losing 4× sensitivity), or (4) confuse strain $\epsilon$ with stress. Always re-read the bridge configuration and unit system before starting. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - A **pressure amplifier** is a high-CMRR, differential-input signal conditioner for transducer outputs in the mV range. - The **3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** is the workhorse topology: $A_d = (1 + 2R_1/R_G)(R_3/R_2)$. - Single-resistor gain control ($R_G$) is a key advantage; $R_G \to \infty$ gives unity gain. - **High CMRR (≥ 100 dB)** is achieved by laser-trimmed monolithic resistor networks; discrete designs are insufficient. - **Wheatstone bridge** sensitivity scales as: 1/4 (one gauge), 1/2 (two gauges), 1 (full bridge). - **Gauge factor** $G_F = (\Delta R/R)/\epsilon$ — typically 2.0 for constantan, 1.8–2.1 for semiconductor. - **Carrier amplifiers** use AC excitation to avoid 1/f noise and allow transformer isolation. - **Patient isolation** (IEC 60601-1) demands leakage current **$< 10\,\mu\text{A}$** for CF-type cardiac equipment. - For diaphragm-based sensors, strain $\epsilon = 3PR^2(1-\nu^2)/(8Eh^2)$. - **Design sequence**: Bridge sensitivity → Transducer output → IA gain → Output range (typically 0–5 V or $\pm 5$ V). - For invasive blood pressure: typical full scale = 300 mmHg, sensitivity ~5 μV/V/mmHg for a full bridge.

topics.title: Systolic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Systolic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: SYSTOLIC — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Systolic Pressure** is the **peak (maximum) arterial blood pressure** measured in the large arteries (typically the brachial artery) immediately following ventricular systole — the phase of the cardiac cycle during which the left ventricle contracts and ejects blood into the aorta. It represents the highest pressure exerted by the blood on the arterial walls during one complete cardiac cycle.

In formal cardiovascular physiology, Systolic Pressure is one of the two critical endpoints that define the **Blood Pressure (BP) waveform**. It is universally paired with **Diastolic Pressure** (the minimum arterial pressure during ventricular relaxation) to give the familiar clinical reading, e.g., **120/80 mmHg**, where **120 mmHg** is the systolic component.

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Adult Value | Units |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Systolic Arterial Pressure | $P_{sys}$ or **SBP** | **120 mmHg** (normal adult) | mmHg, kPa, cmH₂O |
| Diastolic Arterial Pressure | $P_{dia}$ or **DBP** | **80 mmHg** (normal adult) | mmHg, kPa, cmH₂O |
| Pulse Pressure | $PP = SBP - DBP$ | **40 mmHg** | mmHg |
| Mean Arterial Pressure | $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ | **≈ 93 mmHg** | mmHg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cardiac Cycle Context:** The term *Systolic* literally originates from the Greek word *systole*, meaning "a drawing together or contraction." In the cardiac cycle, **ventricular systole** is the period of ventricular wall contraction (≈ **0.3 s** at rest), during which intraventricular pressure rises sharply, the aortic valve opens, and blood is ejected. The peak of this ejection phase is precisely the **Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose Pump" Model

Imagine a **rubber garden hose connected to a hand-pump sprinkler system**. Each time you push the pump handle down (a "contraction" or **systole**), water is forced into the hose. The peak pressure you feel pushing back against your palm at the moment of maximum push is the **systolic pressure**.

When you release the handle (the "relaxation" or **diastole**), the hose still has water in it under some residual pressure — this is the **diastolic pressure**. If the hose has a narrow kink (analogous to arterial stenosis or peripheral vascular resistance), the systolic pressure reading will rise dramatically, just as it does in a hypertensive patient.

**Key Takeaway for the Student:** Systolic pressure is *not* an average — it is the **instantaneous maximum** pressure the arterial wall experiences during one heartbeat. A healthy artery behaves like a flexible, elastic hose (Windkessel effect), smoothing the pulsatile output of the heart. A stiffened, atherosclerotic artery causes SBP to spike.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Normal Adult SBP Range:** 90 – 120 mmHg (per WHO/AHA guidelines)
> - **Hypertension Threshold (SBP):** $\geq$ **140 mmHg**
> - **Hypotension Threshold (SBP):** $<$ **90 mmHg**
> - **Conversion Factor:** $1\ mmHg = 0.1333\ kPa = 1.3595\ cmH_2O$
> - **Standard Cardiac Output at rest:** $CO \approx 5\ L/min$
> - **Standard Heart Rate at rest:** $HR \approx 60 - 100\ bpm$

The **Systolic Pressure** is fundamentally derived from the haemodynamic relationship governed by the heart's stroke output and the arterial system's compliance. The simplified steady-flow form is often written using the **Ohm's law analogue for fluids**:

$$MAP = CO \times SVR$$

where **CO** is *Cardiac Output* and **SVR** is *Systemic Vascular Resistance*. Since $MAP$ is intrinsically linked to $SBP$ through the Windkessel model, any change in cardiac contractility or arterial stiffness directly modulates the **systolic** value we measure.

---

## 1.4 Why Is Systolic Pressure So Clinically Important?

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Risk Stratification:** Elevated SBP is a *stronger* predictor of stroke and cardiovascular events in adults over **50 years** of age than DBP (per the **SPRINT trial** and **AHA 2017 Guidelines**).
> 2. **Cardiac Workload Index:** High SBP increases the **Left Ventricular Afterload**, forcing the heart to work harder, leading to **Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (LVH)**.
> 3. **Aortic Integrity:** Sustained high SBP contributes to **aortic aneurysm** formation and rupture.
> 4. **Cerebral Perfusion:** Adequate SBP is essential to maintain **cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP)**, especially in head-injury patients.
> 5. **Shock Diagnosis:** An SBP below **90 mmHg** is a clinical hallmark of **circulatory shock**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 3: SYSTOLIC — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Physiological Origin of the Systolic Peak

To truly *understand* the systolic pressure, we must trace it from its mechanical origin — the **left ventricular ejection phase**.

**Step 1 — Isovolumetric Contraction:** The ventricle depolarizes (QRS complex on ECG), pressure inside the chamber rises rapidly. All four valves are closed. No blood moves yet.

**Step 2 — Ventricular Ejection Begins:** When intraventricular pressure exceeds **aortic pressure** (typically ~80 mmHg), the **aortic valve** opens. Blood is forcibly ejected into the aorta.

**Step 3 — Rapid Ejection Phase:** ~70% of stroke volume is ejected in the first **1/3** of systole. The aortic pressure rises sharply, reaching its **peak value** — this peak is the **Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP)**.

**Step 4 — Reduced Ejection Phase:** The remaining ~30% of stroke volume is ejected more slowly. Aortic pressure begins to fall as ejection rate drops below the runoff rate to peripheral arteries.

**Step 5 — Protodiastole & Isovolumetric Relaxation:** The ventricle begins to relax. Aortic pressure continues falling until the **aortic valve closes** (S2 heart sound, dicrotic notch on the BP waveform). Pressure then continues to decay into the **diastolic** phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Peak:** The SBP peak occurs because ejection of blood is faster than the runoff of blood to the periphery during the early part of systole. The aorta, being elastic, distends to accommodate this surge — storing potential energy (the **Windkessel effect**). This is why the systolic pressure measured peripherally (brachial) is slightly *higher* than central aortic systolic pressure (typically by **10–15 mmHg**) in young, healthy adults.

---

## 2.2 The Arterial Pressure Waveform

The full **central aortic pressure waveform** is the foundation of systolic measurement. It has the following clinically identifiable features:

| Waveform Feature | Phase | Clinical Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Anacrotic Notch** (P1) | Early Systole (rising limb) | Marks aortic valve opening |
| **Systolic Peak (SBP)** | Peak of the waveform | **Primary measurement target** |
| **Tidal Wave (P2)** | Late Systole | Reflected wave from periphery; augmented with age |
| **Dicrotic Notch (Incisura)** | End of Systole / Start of Diastole | Marks **aortic valve closure** — corresponds to S2 heart sound |
| **Diastolic Decay** | Diastole | Exponential runoff of blood to periphery |
| **Diastolic Minimum (DBP)** | End-diastolic point | Lowest pressure before next cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Wave Velocity (PWV) Insight:** As arterial walls stiffen with age or atherosclerosis, the pulse wave travels *faster* (typically **5–15 m/s**), causing the reflected wave to return during systole rather than diastole. This **augments** the central systolic pressure — a major reason isolated systolic hypertension (ISH) is common in the elderly.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table lists every clinically and academically relevant formula for systolic pressure analysis. All formulas are mathematically standard and appear consistently in KTU model question papers.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Units | KTU Exam Use |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **Pulse Pressure** | $PP = SBP - DBP$ | $SBP, DBP$ | mmHg | High-yield; basic definition |
| 2 | **Mean Arterial Pressure** | $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3} \cdot PP$ | All BP values | mmHg | Most-frequently tested |
| 3 | **Cardiac Output (Fick)** | $CO = HR \times SV$ | Heart rate, stroke volume | L/min | Indirect SBP analysis |
| 4 | **Haemodynamic Ohm's Law** | $MAP = CO \times SVR$ | CO, SVR | mmHg, L/min, Wood Units | Hypertension physiology |
| 5 | **Stroke Volume (from BP)** | $SV = \frac{PP}{SVR}$ | Pulse pressure, SVR | mL/beat | Windkessel-derived |
| 6 | **Windkessel Compliance** | $C = \frac{SV}{PP}$ | Stroke volume, pulse pressure | mL/mmHg | Arterial stiffness |
| 7 | **Pulse Wave Velocity (Moens-Korteweg)** | $PWV = \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot h}{2 \cdot r \cdot \rho}}$ | E = Young's modulus, h = wall thickness, r = radius, $\rho$ = blood density | m/s | Arterial stiffness quantification |
| 8 | **Auscultatory SBP (Korotkoff Phase I)** | $SBP = P_{cuff}$ at first Korotkoff sound | Cuff pressure | mmHg | Sphygmomanometer theory |
| 9 | **Oscillometric SBP** | $SBP \approx P_{cuff}$ at amplitude = $0.5 \cdot MAP_{osc}$ | Oscillometric envelope | mmHg | Automated BP monitors |
| 10 | **Systolic Amplitude Ratio** | $SAR = \frac{A_{systole}}{A_{diastole}}$ | Oscillometric amplitudes | dimensionless | Algorithm design |
| 11 | **Pressure Conversion** | $1\ mmHg = 0.1333\ kPa$ | — | kPa | Unit-conversion problems |
| 12 | **Pressure Conversion** | $1\ mmHg = 1.3595\ cmH_2O$ | — | cmH₂O | Unit-conversion problems |
| 13 | **Left Ventricular Work (per beat)** | $W = SBP \times SV$ | Pressure, stroke volume | J | Cardiac workload |
| 14 | **Augmentation Index (Aix)** | $Aix = \frac{\Delta P}{PP} \times 100\%$ | $\Delta P = P2 - P1$ | % | Wave reflection analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked to compute MAP, *never* use the simple arithmetic mean $(SBP+DBP)/2$. The correct formula — and the one KTU examiners award full marks for — is $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$. This is because diastole occupies **~2/3** of the cardiac cycle at rest, and the time-weighted average naturally weights DBP more heavily.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The study of systolic pressure is *not* purely academic — it underpins several active engineering domains:

1. **Automated BP Monitor Design (Consumer Electronics):** Every digital wristwatch BP monitor (e.g., Omron, Apple Watch FDA-cleared algorithms) uses **oscillometric peak detection** to estimate SBP.
2. **ICU Invasive Monitoring:** The **arterial line** (e.g., radial artery cannulation) directly displays the SBP peak in real time on a bedside monitor, with **STRAIN GAUGE** or **PIEZO-RESISTIVE** transducers.
3. **Pulse Oximeter Signal Processing:** The **photoplethysmographic (PPG) pulse** amplitude correlates with $PP = SBP - DBP$, allowing cuff-less SBP estimation (an active research area).
4. **Cardiac Pacemaker Feedback Loops:** Modern **rate-responsive pacemakers** use SBP variations (via intracardiac impedance) to adapt pacing rate.
5. **Wearable Health Tech:** Continuous systolic pressure monitoring via **tonometry** (applanation tonometry at the radial artery) is a frontier of biomedical wearables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The systolic peak is essentially the *highest amplitude point* in the arterial pressure time-series. Any signal processing chain (filtering, peak detection, envelope extraction) that *correctly* identifies this peak qualifies as a systolic pressure measurement system. The KTU module specifically tests your ability to describe the **electronic hardware** and **algorithms** that perform this identification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 3: SYSTOLIC — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Mean Arterial Pressure from Systolic and Diastolic Values

This is the **single most-tested derivation** in KTU examinations related to BP.

**Given:**
- A patient has $SBP = 120\ mmHg$ and $DBP = 80\ mmHg$.
- The duration of systole is $T_s$ and diastole is $T_d$.
- At rest, $T_s \approx 0.3\ s$ and $T_d \approx 0.5\ s$ (cardiac cycle = 0.8 s).

**Step 1: Express the time-weighted mean.**

The MAP is the *time-weighted* average of the pressure waveform over one cardiac cycle. Assuming a piecewise-linear approximation of the BP waveform (a trapezoidal rise during systole and an exponential decay during diastole), the area under the curve is:

$$\text{Area} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}(SBP + DBP) \cdot T_s}_{\text{systolic triangle}} + \underbrace{DBP \cdot T_d + \frac{1}{2}(SBP - DBP) \cdot T_s}_{\text{diastolic trapezoid correction}}$$

**Step 2: Apply the time-weighted average.**

$$MAP = \frac{1}{T_s + T_d} \int_0^{T_s + T_d} P(t)\ dt$$

For the trapezoidal approximation:

$$MAP = \frac{(SBP + DBP) \cdot T_s + 2 \cdot DBP \cdot T_d}{2(T_s + T_d)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the resting cardiac cycle ratio.**

At rest, the ratio $T_d / T_s \approx 2$ (because $T_s = 0.3$ s, $T_d = 0.5$ s, total $0.8$ s). Substitute $T_d = 2 T_s$:

$$MAP = \frac{(SBP + DBP) \cdot T_s + 2 \cdot DBP \cdot 2 T_s}{2(T_s + 2 T_s)}$$

$$MAP = \frac{(SBP + DBP) + 4 \cdot DBP}{6}$$

$$MAP = \frac{SBP + DBP + 4 \cdot DBP}{6} = \frac{SBP + 5 \cdot DBP}{6}$$

$$MAP = \frac{SBP}{6} + \frac{5 \cdot DBP}{6}$$

**Step 4: Convert to the standard KTU form.**

Multiply numerator and denominator to match the conventional form. Since $PP = SBP - DBP$:

$$MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$$

$$\boxed{MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3} \cdot PP}$$

**Step 5: Numerical check.**

$$MAP = 80 + \frac{1}{3}(120 - 80) = 80 + 13.33 = 93.33\ mmHg$$

This matches the well-known clinical value. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Stroke Volume from Pulse Pressure (Windkessel Model)

**Background:** The **two-element Windkessel model** treats the arterial system as a single compliance chamber ($C$) in parallel with a single resistance ($R$) representing peripheral runoff. This is the standard simplified model used in KTU problems.

**Step 1: Define compliance.**

Compliance is defined as the *change in volume* per *change in pressure*:

$$C = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta P} = \frac{SV}{PP}$$

Rearranging:

$$SV = C \cdot PP$$

**Step 2: Use the RC time constant.**

In the two-element Windkessel model, the diastolic pressure decays exponentially:

$$P(t) = DBP \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

The time constant $\tau = RC$ represents the time for pressure to fall to $1/e$ of its initial value. For a typical adult at rest, $\tau \approx 1.5$ seconds.

**Step 3: Solve for C in terms of MAP and CO.**

Since the *steady-state* mean flow equals cardiac output:

$$CO = \frac{MAP}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{MAP}{CO}$$

Substituting back:

$$C = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{\tau \cdot CO}{MAP}$$

**Step 4: Combine to express SV in terms of measurable BP parameters.**

$$SV = C \cdot PP = \frac{\tau \cdot CO \cdot PP}{MAP}$$

For the *steady state*, $CO = HR \cdot SV$, leading to a self-consistent relationship:

$$\boxed{SV = \frac{\tau \cdot PP \cdot HR \cdot SV}{MAP} \quad \Rightarrow \quad MAP = \tau \cdot PP \cdot HR}$$

Wait — this is an *approximation* that works only when $\tau \cdot HR \approx 1$ at rest. The more rigorous KTU-accepted formula is:

$$SV = \frac{PP}{SVR} \quad \text{where} \quad SVR = \frac{MAP}{CO}$$

**Step 5: Numerical example.**

Given: $SBP = 130$, $DBP = 85$, $CO = 5$ L/min, $HR = 72$ bpm.

$$PP = 130 - 85 = 45\ mmHg$$

$$MAP = 85 + \frac{1}{3}(45) = 100\ mmHg$$

$$SVR = \frac{100}{5} = 20\ mmHg \cdot min/L$$

$$SV = \frac{45}{20} = 2.25\ L/beat$$

Convert to mL: $SV = 2250$ mL/beat. ❌ **This is too high** — the issue is the unit mismatch.

**Corrected numerical example (using consistent units):**

$$SVR = 20\ mmHg/(L/min) = 20 \times 60\ mmHg/(L/h) = 1200\ mmHg \cdot s/L$$

But the simpler, KTU-canonical relationship is:

$$SV \approx \frac{PP}{SVR} \quad \text{(in arbitrary but consistent units)}$$

For KTU problem-solving, the *qualitative* result is what matters: **higher PP and lower SVR yield higher stroke volume**, which in turn raises SBP on the next beat.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Oscillometric Maximum Amplitude Algorithm

**Problem Setup:** The oscillometric method measures SBP and DBP by analyzing the **cuff pressure oscillations** as the cuff is deflated from above SBP to below DBP. The amplitude of oscillations follows a characteristic envelope.

**Step 1: Define the oscillometric envelope.**

As the cuff deflates, the *peak-to-peak* amplitude of cuff pressure oscillations $A(P_{cuff})$ rises from zero, reaches a maximum, and falls back to zero. The maximum corresponds to **MAP**.

**Step 2: Establish the empirical systolic/diastolic thresholds.**

Empirically validated ratios (used in commercial monitors):

$$SBP \approx P_{cuff} \text{ at } A = 0.45 \text{ to } 0.55 \cdot A_{max}$$

$$DBP \approx P_{cuff} \text{ at } A = 0.65 \text{ to } 0.85 \cdot A_{max}$$

A widely cited KTU-acceptable standard is:

$$A_{systolic} = 0.5 \cdot A_{max} \quad \text{and} \quad A_{diastolic} = 0.7 \cdot A_{max}$$

**Step 3: Numerical illustration.**

Suppose $A_{max} = 8\ mmHg$ at $P_{cuff} = 90\ mmHg$ (which we identify as MAP). Then:

- $A_{systolic} = 0.5 \times 8 = 4\ mmHg$ at some higher cuff pressure $P_{cuff,\ sys}$
- $A_{diastolic} = 0.7 \times 8 = 5.6\ mmHg$ at some lower cuff pressure $P_{cuff,\ dia}$

If the cuff pressure at 4 mmHg amplitude is, say, 120 mmHg, then $SBP = 120$ mmHg.

**Step 4: Algorithmic flow.**

The KTU-typical answer requires explaining the **signal processing chain**:

1. Inflate cuff to ~**180 mmHg** (above expected SBP).
2. Deflate at **2–3 mmHg/s**.
3. Measure cuff pressure $P_c(t)$ with a pressure transducer.
4. High-pass filter to extract the **oscillatory component** $A(t)$.
5. Find $A_{max}$ and corresponding $P_c$ → this is **MAP**.
6. Find cuff pressures at $0.5 A_{max}$ (SBP) and $0.7 A_{max}$ (DBP).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of SBP Measurement Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison table is **mandatory study material** for KTU 14-mark questions comparing invasive vs non-invasive techniques.

| Parameter | **Auscultatory (Manual)** | **Oscillometric (Automated)** | **Invasive (Arterial Line)** | **Tonometric (Applanation)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Principle** | Korotkoff sounds | Cuff oscillation envelope | Direct catheter transduction | Arterial wall compression |
| **SBP Accuracy** | ±5 mmHg | ±5–8 mmHg | **±1 mmHg (gold standard)** | ±5–10 mmHg |
| **Operator Skill** | High (trained) | Low (automated) | High (medical) | Moderate |
| **Cuff Required** | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| **Continuous?** | No | No (spot-check) | **Yes (beat-to-beat)** | Yes |
| **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Non-invasive | **Invasive (arterial puncture)** | Non-invasive |
| **Risks** | None | None | Bleeding, infection, thrombosis | Skin irritation |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | **Very High** | **Very High** | **High** | Medium |
| **Typical Use** | Clinic, bedside | Home, ambulatory monitors | ICU, OR | Research, advanced wearables |

---

## 3.5 Derivation #4: Korotkoff Sound Threshold & Cuff Pressure Mechanics

**Setup:** The auscultatory method requires understanding the relationship between cuff pressure, transmural pressure across the artery wall, and the audible Korotkoff phases.

**Step 1: Transmural pressure.**

The transmural pressure is the difference between intra-arterial pressure and cuff pressure:

$$P_{tm} = P_{artery} - P_{cuff}$$

- If $P_{tm} > 0$: artery is open; blood flows (laminar, silent).
- If $P_{tm} < 0$: artery is fully compressed; no flow.
- If $P_{tm} \approx 0$: artery is partially compressed; turbulent flow → **Korotkoff sounds**.

**Step 2: Phase I (SBP).**

Phase I Korotkoff sounds appear the *instant* $P_{cuff}$ falls just below $SBP$. At this point, blood can *just* squeeze through the partially occluded brachial artery during peak systole, creating a brief spurt of flow → audible "tap."

$$\boxed{P_{cuff} = SBP \text{ at the onset of Phase I Korotkoff sounds}}$$

**Step 3: Phase IV and V (DBP).**

Phase IV: Sounds muffle (artery mostly open, less turbulence).
Phase V: Sounds **disappear** (artery fully open, purely laminar flow).

$$\boxed{P_{cuff} = DBP \text{ at the disappearance of Phase V Korotkoff sounds}}$$

**Step 4: Gap phenomenon (auscultatory gap).**

In some hypertensive patients, Korotkoff sounds may *disappear* between SBP and a lower pressure (typically 20–40 mmHg below SBP), then reappear. This is the **auscultatory gap** and can lead to SBP underestimation if the examiner does not palpate the radial pulse first to estimate SBP.

> [!WARNING]
> **Clinical Pitfall:** Always palpate the radial pulse while inflating the cuff to determine the *true* SBP before auscultation. This avoids the auscultatory gap error.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Pressure Unit Conversion

**Problem (KTU-style):** A patient's systolic pressure is 150 mmHg. Express this in **kPa** and **cmH₂O**.

**Step 1: Convert to kPa.**

$$P_{kPa} = 150\ mmHg \times 0.1333\ \frac{kPa}{mmHg}$$

$$P_{kPa} = 19.995\ kPa \approx 20.0\ kPa$$

**Step 2: Convert to cmH₂O.**

$$P_{cmH_2O} = 150\ mmHg \times 1.3595\ \frac{cmH_2O}{mmHg}$$

$$P_{cmH_2O} = 203.925\ cmH_2O \approx 204\ cmH_2O$$

**Step 3: Sanity check.**

Standard atmospheric pressure: 760 mmHg = 101.325 kPa = 1033 cmH₂O. A systolic of 150 mmHg ≈ 1/5 atmosphere ≈ 20 kPa ≈ 200 cmH₂O. ✅ All values consistent.

---

## 3.7 Numerical Problem: SBP from Pulse Pressure & MAP

**Problem (KTU-style):** A patient's MAP is **100 mmHg** and DBP is **80 mmHg**. Find SBP and PP.

**Step 1: Use the MAP formula.**

$$MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3} \cdot PP$$

$$100 = 80 + \frac{1}{3} \cdot PP$$

$$\frac{1}{3} \cdot PP = 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad PP = 60\ mmHg$$

**Step 2: Calculate SBP.**

$$SBP = DBP + PP = 80 + 60 = 140\ mmHg$$

This indicates **Stage 2 Hypertension** by AHA 2017 criteria. ✅

# MODULE 3: SYSTOLIC — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

> **Define Systolic Blood Pressure. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the central aortic pressure waveform and label all major features.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP) is the *maximum* pressure exerted by blood on the arterial walls during ventricular contraction (systole). It represents the peak of the arterial pressure pulse waveform and is typically measured non-invasively using a sphygmomanometer at the brachial artery.

**Waveform Description (2 marks):** The central aortic pressure waveform consists of:

1. **Rising (anacrotic) limb** — corresponds to ventricular ejection; aortic valve opens.
2. **Systolic Peak (SBP)** — the highest point, occurring during rapid ejection.
3. **Tidal wave (P2)** — reflected wave from the periphery; late-systolic feature.
4. **Dicrotic notch (incisura)** — sharp downward deflection marking **aortic valve closure** (corresponds to S2 heart sound).
5. **Diastolic decay** — exponential pressure fall-off as blood runs off to the periphery.
6. **End-diastolic point (DBP)** — minimum pressure, just before the next cycle begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch should include:** Vertical axis = Pressure (mmHg), Horizontal axis = Time. Label: SBP peak, Dicrotic notch, DBP. Typical values: SBP = 120, DBP = 80.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **List the five phases of Korotkoff sounds. State which phase corresponds to SBP and which to DBP. What is the "auscultatory gap"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Phase | Description | Cuff Pressure Relation |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Phase I** | First appearance of clear, faint tapping sounds | **= SBP** |
| **Phase II** | Sounds become murmur-like (longer) | Between SBP and DBP |
| **Phase III** | Sounds become crisper, louder | Between SBP and DBP |
| **Phase IV** | Sudden muffling of sounds | (Sometimes used as DBP clinically) |
| **Phase V** | Complete disappearance of sounds | **= DBP** |

**Auscultatory Gap (1 mark):** A phenomenon in some hypertensive patients where Korotkoff sounds *temporarily disappear* between Phase I and Phase III (typically 20–40 mmHg below true SBP), then reappear. If the clinician does not palpate the radial pulse first to estimate SBP, this gap can lead to **underestimation of true SBP**. *(2 marks allocated: 1 for phases, 1 for gap explanation, 1 for SBP/DBP identification.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE 14-mark questions in Module 3 follow a standard *internal choice* pattern. The question is presented with two alternatives (Or option), and the student chooses ONE. Each alternative has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

> **A. (a)** Explain the **Auscultatory method** of measuring Systolic Blood Pressure in detail. Describe the instrument used, the procedure, and the role of Korotkoff sounds. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **A. (b)** With a block diagram, describe the **Oscillometric method** of BP measurement. Explain how SBP and DBP are algorithmically derived from the oscillometric envelope. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **A. (a) Model Answer — Auscultatory Method (7 Marks)**

**1. Instrument — Mercury Sphygmomanometer (2 marks):**

The standard instrument consists of:
- **Inflatable rubber cuff** (12–14 cm wide for adults) connected to a rubber bulb pump.
- **Manometer** — either a **mercury column** (gold standard) or an **aneroid gauge** (calibrated dial).
- **Rubber tubing** connecting cuff to manometer and pump.
- **Stethoscope** with diaphragm, placed over the **brachial artery** at the **antecubital fossa**.

**2. Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Wrap cuff snugly around upper arm at heart level (≈ mid-sternal level).
2. Palpate the **radial artery** at the wrist.
3. Inflate cuff rapidly until radial pulse **disappears**, then add ~**20–30 mmHg** more. This is the *estimated SBP*.
4. Place stethoscope diaphragm over brachial artery (do NOT tuck under cuff).
5. Deflate cuff slowly at **2–3 mmHg per second**.
6. Note the **cuff pressure at which the first Korotkoff sound (Phase I) is heard** → this is **SBP**.
7. Continue deflating; note the cuff pressure at which sounds **disappear (Phase V)** → this is **DBP**.
8. Deflate completely; remove cuff.

**3. Role of Korotkoff Sounds (2 marks):**

- Generated by **turbulent blood flow** through the partially occluded brachial artery.
- Phase I (SBP): Cuff pressure just below intra-arterial peak → arterial wall vibrates as blood spurts through during systole only.
- Phase V (DBP): Cuff pressure below intra-arterial minimum → artery fully open → purely **laminar** flow → no sounds.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Auscultatory):** Students commonly lose marks by (1) forgetting to palpate radial pulse first, (2) placing stethoscope under the cuff, (3) deflating too fast (>3 mmHg/s), or (4) using cuff of wrong size. Always mention these precautions.

---

#### **A. (b) Model Answer — Oscillometric Method (7 Marks)**

**1. Block Diagram (2 marks):**

[Cuff] → [Pneumatic pump + deflation valve] → [Pressure transducer] → [Instrumentation amplifier] → [ADC] → [Microcontroller/DSP] → [Display]

**2. Principle (2 marks):**

When the cuff is inflated above SBP and slowly deflated, the **arterial wall oscillates** in response to each heartbeat. These small pressure oscillations (typically 1–8 mmHg amplitude) are superimposed on the slowly deflating cuff pressure baseline. The **envelope** of these oscillations rises, peaks, and falls:

- **Below SBP:** No oscillations (artery fully occluded).
- **At MAP:** Oscillations have **maximum amplitude**.
- **Below DBP:** Oscillations decrease as artery is fully open.

**3. SBP / DBP Algorithmic Derivation (3 marks):**

The maximum amplitude $A_{max}$ corresponds to **MAP** (this is the *empirical* but universally accepted relationship).

Using empirically validated ratios:
- $A_{systolic} = 0.5 \times A_{max}$ → corresponding cuff pressure is **SBP**.
- $A_{diastolic} = 0.7 \times A_{max}$ → corresponding cuff pressure is **DBP**.

**Algorithm Pseudocode (for reference, not in answer):**
1. Sample cuff pressure at **100 Hz** minimum.
2. High-pass filter ($f_c \approx 0.5$ Hz) → extract AC oscillations.
3. Compute peak-to-peak amplitude per heartbeat.
4. Find maximum amplitude → identify MAP.
5. Backtrack through amplitudes to find $0.5 \cdot A_{max}$ (SBP) and $0.7 \cdot A_{max}$ (DBP).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Oscillometric):** Many students mistakenly state that $A_{max}$ corresponds to SBP. The correct fact is that **$A_{max}$ corresponds to MAP**. SBP and DBP are *back-calculated* using fixed ratios. This is the single most common conceptual error and costs up to 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> **B. (a)** Derive the relationship between **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)**, Systolic, and Diastolic pressures. Why is MAP NOT the arithmetic mean of SBP and DBP? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **B. (b)** Explain the **invasive arterial catheter method** of SBP measurement. State its clinical advantages and disadvantages compared to non-invasive methods. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **B. (a) Model Answer — MAP Derivation (7 Marks)**

**1. Why Arithmetic Mean Fails (2 marks):**

The cardiac cycle consists of **systole (~0.3 s)** and **diastole (~0.5 s)** at rest. Diastole is *longer* than systole. The MAP must therefore be a **time-weighted** average, weighting DBP more heavily. The simple arithmetic mean $\frac{SBP+DBP}{2}$ would only be valid if systole and diastole had **equal duration**, which they do not.

**2. Derivation (3 marks):**

[See Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation. The final answer is:]

$$MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3} \cdot (SBP - DBP) = DBP + \frac{1}{3} \cdot PP$$

**3. Numerical Verification (2 marks):**

For a healthy adult: $SBP = 120$, $DBP = 80$:

$$MAP = 80 + \frac{1}{3}(120-80) = 80 + 13.33 = 93.33\ mmHg$$

Versus arithmetic mean: $\frac{120+80}{2} = 100\ mmHg$ — clearly different.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing $MAP = \frac{SBP + DBP}{2}$ is the **classic KTU trap**. Even a partially correct derivation loses full marks if the final boxed equation is wrong. Memorize the $\frac{1}{3}$ factor.

---

#### **B. (b) Model Answer — Invasive Arterial Catheter Method (7 Marks)**

**1. Principle (2 marks):**

A **cannula (catheter)** is inserted into a peripheral artery (typically the **radial artery** at the wrist; alternatively femoral, brachial, or dorsalis pedis). The catheter is connected via **fluid-filled tubing** to an external **strain-gauge pressure transducer**. The transducer converts mechanical pressure into an electrical voltage proportional to the instantaneous arterial pressure. The output is displayed on a bedside monitor as a continuous, real-time pressure waveform.

**2. Procedure (2 marks):**

1. **Allen test** first to confirm adequate ulnar collateral circulation.
2. Insert **20-G cannula** into radial artery using Seldinger technique.
3. Connect via **rigid saline-filled tubing** to transducer.
4. **Zero** the transducer to atmospheric pressure at the level of the right atrium (phlebostatic axis — 4th ICS, mid-axillary line).
5. **Calibrate** and observe continuous waveform.

**3. SBP Determination (1 mark):**

Each cardiac cycle produces a waveform with a sharp systolic peak. **SBP is the maximum value of each beat's waveform** — a peak-detection algorithm in the monitor automatically identifies and displays the systolic value continuously.

**4. Clinical Advantages vs Non-Invasive (1 mark):**

- **Beat-to-beat continuous monitoring** (vs spot-check).
- **Gold-standard accuracy** (±1 mmHg) — used in cardiac surgery, ICU, shock management.
- Detects **beat-to-beat variability** and rapid changes.

**5. Disadvantages (1 mark):**

- **Invasive** — risk of infection, bleeding, arterial thrombosis, distal ischemia.
- Requires **trained personnel** and sterile technique.
- Expensive; not suitable for routine or home use.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Invasive):** Students often forget to mention the **Allen test** (a critical pre-procedure step) and the **phlebostatic axis** leveling for accurate readings. Omitting these costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Systolic Pressure" — KTU 2024 Module 3**

### **Core Concepts to Memorize**

- ☐ **Systolic Pressure = Peak arterial pressure during ventricular contraction**
- ☐ Typical value: **120 mmHg** (range 90–120 normal; ≥140 = hypertension)
- ☐ Phase I Korotkoff sound → **SBP**; Phase V disappearance → **DBP**
- ☐ **MAP formula (NEVER arithmetic mean):** $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$
- ☐ **Pulse Pressure:** $PP = SBP - DBP$
- ☐ **Oscillometric:** $A_{max}$ → MAP; $0.5 A_{max}$ → SBP; $0.7 A_{max}$ → DBP
- ☐ **Windkessel compliance:** $C = SV / PP$
- ☐ **Moens-Korteweg PWV:** increases with arterial stiffness
- ☐ **Conversion factors:** 1 mmHg = 0.1333 kPa = 1.3595 cmH₂O

### **High-Yield Numerical Skills**

- ☐ Compute MAP from SBP/DBP
- ☐ Convert between mmHg, kPa, cmH₂O
- ☐ Identify SBP/DBP from Korotkoff phases
- ☐ Calculate $PP$, $SV$ from given BP and CO
- ☐ Back-calculate SBP/DBP from oscillometric ratios

### **Comparison Matrix Mastery**

- ☐ Auscultatory vs Oscillometric vs Invasive vs Tonometric
- ☐ Know: accuracy, invasiveness, operator skill, clinical use
- ☐ Be able to draw a block diagram of an oscillometric monitor

### **Common Examiner Traps (Avoid These)**

- ☐ ❌ Using $MAP = (SBP+DBP)/2$
- ☐ ❌ Stating $A_{max}$ corresponds to SBP (it is MAP!)
- ☐ ❌ Forgetting the Allen test in invasive technique
- ☐ ❌ Confusing the Korotkoff phases (Phase IV is muffling, NOT DBP)
- ☐ ❌ Omitting units in numerical answers
- ☐ ❌ Forgetting to state cuff width for sphygmomanometer (12–14 cm adult)

### **Clinical Correlations to Mention in 14-Mark Answers**

- ☐ Isolated Systolic Hypertension (ISH) in elderly
- ☐ Windkessel effect in aortic stiffness
- ☐ Korotkoff auscultatory gap in severe hypertension
- ☐ LVH as a consequence of chronic high SBP
- ☐ Pulse pressure as a cardiovascular risk marker

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Module 3 questions on Systolic Pressure **almost always** appear in the format: "Explain the [method] of BP measurement with a block diagram / waveform." Practice drawing clean block diagrams of the oscillometric system, and ALWAYS label: cuff → transducer → amplifier → ADC → processor → display. A well-labelled diagram is worth **at least 2 of 7 marks** in a typical 7-mark sub-question.

---

**END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — SYSTOLIC PRESSURE**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT723 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Diastolic Blood Pressure (DBP)** is defined as the minimum arterial pressure recorded in the systemic circulation during the cardiac cycle, specifically corresponding to the **ventricular diastole** phase — when the heart muscle relaxes and the ventricles fill with blood from the atria.

In the context of KTU OEICT723 (Biomedical Instrumentation), diastolic pressure is the second (lower) numerical value reported in a standard blood pressure reading (e.g., **120/80 mmHg**, where 80 is the diastolic value). It represents the baseline residual pressure that the arterial walls always experience, even when the heart is not actively ejecting blood.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Diastolic pressure is a critical sub-topic under *"Non-invasive blood pressure measurement"* and *"Korotkoff sound analysis."* Examiner's frequently frame questions around the **mechanism of diastolic endpoint detection** in both auscultatory and oscillometric sphygmomanometers.

## 1.2 Standard Physiological Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Adult Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Normal Diastolic BP (DBP) | **60 – 80** | **mmHg** |
| Elevated DBP | 80 – 89 | mmHg |
| Stage 1 Hypertension (DBP) | 90 – 99 | mmHg |
| Stage 2 Hypertension (DBP) | ≥ 100 | mmHg |
| Hypotension (DBP) | < 60 | mmHg |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Model

Imagine a garden hose connected to a municipal water pump. The pump pushes water in bursts (systole → **systolic pressure** = peak surge), and between pushes, the hose is still under tension because of the water still inside it and the closed nozzle resisting flow (diastole → **diastolic pressure** = residual baseline).

The hose's elasticity (analogous to arterial compliance) determines how much pressure drops between surges. A stiff hose (arteriosclerosis) maintains a *high* diastolic pressure; a very flaccid hose drops to near zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Diastolic pressure is essentially the "floor" of the arterial pressure waveform. The difference between systolic and diastolic is called **Pulse Pressure (PP = SBP − DBP)**.

## 1.4 Cardiac Cycle Context

The human cardiac cycle alternates between two phases:

1. **Systole (≈ 0.3 s):** Ventricles contract, ejecting blood. Aortic pressure peaks → **SBP**.
2. **Diastole (≈ 0.5 s):** Ventricles relax, the aortic valve closes (producing the second heart sound, S2). Aortic pressure decays to its lowest point → **DBP**.

The duration of diastole is the period during which coronary arteries perfuse the myocardium — making diastolic pressure physiologically vital for **coronary blood supply**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Origin of Diastolic Pressure — Why Does It Exist?

Arterial diastolic pressure is sustained by three coupled physical phenomena:

- **Arterial Wall Elastic Recoil (Windkessel Effect):** The aorta and large arteries are elastic. During systole they stretch, storing ~50% of the stroke volume. During diastole, they recoil, squeezing blood forward into the systemic circulation, maintaining a continuous downstream flow and residual pressure.
- **Peripheral Vascular Resistance (PVR):** The arterioles act as the dominant resistive element. According to Poiseuille's law, pressure drop across a resistive network is proportional to flow × resistance.
- **Heart Rate & Stroke Volume Interaction:** A faster heart rate shortens the diastolic interval, leaving less time for pressure to decay, which elevates DBP.

## 2.2 The Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) — The Diastolic Backbone

The most clinically important derived parameter involving DBP is **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)**, because organ perfusion pressure depends on the *time-averaged* arterial pressure, not the peak.

Because ~2/3 of the cardiac cycle is spent in diastole, MAP is biased toward DBP:

$$ \text{MAP} = \text{DBP} + \frac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP}) $$

Equivalently:

$$ \text{MAP} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{DBP} + \text{SBP}}{3} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Identity:** The factor **1/3** is an empirical approximation valid for a normal heart rate (~75 bpm). At very high heart rates (tachycardia), MAP should be computed via true waveform integration rather than this formula.

## 2.3 Diastolic Endpoint Detection — The Two Pillars of NIBP

KTU 2024 Module 3 emphasizes **two non-invasive methods** for identifying the diastolic point:

### 2.3.1 Auscultatory Method (Korotkoff Sounds)

Discovered by **Dr. Nikolai Korotkoff (1905)**, this method listens to sounds generated as the cuff pressure is gradually released.

- **Phase I (SBP):** First appearance of clear tapping sounds → cuff pressure ≈ SBP.
- **Phase II & III:** Sounds become murmurs, then louder.
- **Phase IV (Muffling):** Abrupt softening/muffling of sounds → historically used as DBP by some clinicians.
- **Phase V (Disappearance):** Sounds vanish completely → **modern standard for DBP** (recommended by AHA/WHO).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Phase V?** In some patients (e.g., pregnant women, high-output states), sounds may persist to zero cuff pressure. The *disappearance* point is more reproducible than muffling.

### 2.3.2 Oscillometric Method

A modern, fully automatic technique. The cuff senses **oscillations** in arterial wall pulsation as cuff pressure is bled down.

- The amplitude of oscillations peaks at **MAP**.
- Systolic is identified as the cuff pressure where oscillations reach a fraction (e.g., 0.5 of max).
- **Diastolic is identified as the cuff pressure where oscillations drop to a specific fraction (e.g., 0.55 to 0.85 of max amplitude on the descending limb)** — algorithm-dependent.

| Method | DBP Detection Criterion | Reliability |
|---|---|---|
| Auscultatory | Phase V (sound disappearance) | High (gold-standard non-invasive) |
| Oscillometric | Algorithm: oscillation amplitude ratio | Moderate (proprietary) |
| Palpatory | Not detectable (palpation only gives SBP) | Low |
| Invasive (Arterial Line) | Direct transducer readout of waveform trough | Highest |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{PP} = \text{SBP} - \text{DBP}$ | Pulse Pressure | mmHg |
| 2 | $\text{MAP} = \text{DBP} + \tfrac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP})$ | Mean Arterial Pressure | mmHg |
| 3 | $\text{MAP} = \text{CO} \times \text{SVR} + \text{CVP}$ | Hemodynamic identity (CO = cardiac output, SVR = systemic vascular resistance) | mmHg |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi r^{4}}$ | Poiseuille's Law (resistance basis of DBP) | Pa |
| 5 | $\text{SVR} \propto \dfrac{1}{r^{4}}$ | Resistance inversely ∝ radius⁴ | — |
| 6 | $V_{\text{stored}} = C \cdot \Delta P$ | Windkessel: Aortic compliance $C$ | mL |
| 7 | $P(t) = P_d e^{-t / R C}$ | Exponential diastolic decay | mmHg |
| 8 | $\text{DBP}_\text{decay} = \text{SBP} \cdot e^{-T_d / (RC)}$ | Pressure at end of diastolic period $T_d$ | mmHg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table syntax safeguard:** In any markdown row above, the absolute-value or magnitude notation has been replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` to avoid breaking the pipe-delimited table structure.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automated BP Cuffs (Omron, Philips):** Use oscillometric algorithms whose DBP threshold is the single most algorithm-protected IP.
- **ICU Patient Monitors:** Continuous invasive DBP feeds alarms for hypotension (shock, sepsis) and hypertension.
- **Anesthesia Machines:** MAP derived from DBP guides vasopressor dosing.
- **Wearable PPG Devices (smartwatches):** Estimate DBP via pulse transit time and arterial compliance models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of MAP from Time-Averaged Pressure

The arterial pressure waveform $P(t)$ over one cardiac period $T = T_s + T_d$ (systolic + diastolic duration) is integrated to find the true mean.

### Step 1 — Define the true time average

$$ \overline{P} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} P(t) \, dt $$

### Step 2 — Decompose into systolic and diastolic integrals

$$ \overline{P} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_s} P(t) \, dt + \int_{T_s}^{T} P(t) \, dt \right] $$

### Step 3 — Approximate each integral

Assume the systolic interval is dominated by the upstroke from DBP → SBP, and the diastolic interval is dominated by exponential decay from SBP → DBP. Empirically, the systolic integral averages to roughly the **midpoint of SBP and DBP over the systolic duration**, while the diastolic integral averages to the **midpoint over the diastolic duration**.

### Step 4 — Substitute empirical 1/3 weighting

When $T_d \approx 2 T_s$ (normal sinus rhythm at 75 bpm, where $T \approx 0.8$ s, $T_s \approx 0.3$ s, $T_d \approx 0.5$ s), the time weighting simplifies to a 2:1 ratio of diastolic to systolic:

$$ \overline{P} \approx \frac{T_s \cdot \left(\frac{\text{SBP} + \text{DBP}}{2}\right) + T_d \cdot \left(\frac{\text{SBP} + \text{DBP}}{2}\right)}{T_s + T_d} $$

This is the **simple arithmetic mean**, but it over-weights systole. Empirically correcting for the longer diastolic period yields:

$$ \text{MAP} \approx \frac{\text{SBP} + 2 \cdot \text{DBP}}{3} $$

### Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate DBP (the KTU-favoured form)

$$ \text{DBP} = \text{MAP} - \frac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP}) $$

Or, more directly:

$$ \text{DBP} = \frac{3\,\text{MAP} - \text{SBP}}{2} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** For BP = 120/80 mmHg → MAP = 80 + (1/3)(40) = 80 + 13.33 = **93.33 mmHg**. This matches the cardiac output × SVR intuition for a healthy adult at rest.

## 3.2 Derivation of Diastolic Pressure Decay (Windkessel RC Model)

Treating the systemic arterial system as a single compliance $C$ in parallel with a peripheral resistance $R$, the diastolic pressure decay follows a first-order RC circuit response.

### Step 1 — Model the arterial tree

The aorta is a **compliance (capacitor)** $C$, the peripheral arterioles are a **resistor** $R$. During diastole, the aortic valve is closed, so the heart is disconnected — the circuit is a simple RC discharge.

### Step 2 — Write the governing ODE

Charge (volume) balance: flow out of compliance through resistance = rate of volume loss.

$$ -C \frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{P}{R} $$

### Step 3 — Rearrange

$$ \frac{dP}{dt} = -\frac{P}{R C} $$

### Step 4 — Solve the first-order linear ODE

Separating variables and integrating from $t=0$ (end-systole, $P = \text{SBP}$) to $t = T_d$ (end-diastole, $P = \text{DBP}$):

$$ \int_{\text{SBP}}^{\text{DBP}} \frac{dP}{P} = -\int_{0}^{T_d} \frac{dt}{R C} $$

$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{\text{DBP}}{\text{SBP}}\right) = -\frac{T_d}{R C} $$

### Step 5 — Exponentiate

$$ \text{DBP} = \text{SBP} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{T_d}{R C}\right) $$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

- A **larger compliance $C$** (more elastic aorta) → slower pressure decay → higher DBP.
- A **larger resistance $R$** (vasoconstriction) → slower decay → higher DBP.
- A **longer diastolic time $T_d$** (bradycardia) → lower DBP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Numerical:** For a healthy adult: $R \approx 1.0$ mmHg·s/mL (PRU), $C \approx 1.5$ mL/mmHg, $T_d \approx 0.5$ s. Thus $RC \approx 1.5$ s, and $\text{DBP} \approx \text{SBP} \cdot e^{-0.333} \approx 0.717 \cdot \text{SBP}$. So if SBP = 120, predicted DBP ≈ 86 mmHg. Real DBP (80) is slightly lower because of coronary runoff — the model approximates but does not include the coronary branch.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Diastolic Pressure

| Factor | Effect on DBP | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Peripheral vascular resistance | ↑ DBP | Vasoconstriction slows runoff |
| ↓ Arterial compliance (aging) | ↑ DBP (often) | Less Windkessel buffering |
| ↑ Heart rate (tachycardia) | ↑ DBP (slight) | Shorter $T_d$, less decay |
| ↓ Heart rate (bradycardia) | ↓ DBP | Longer decay time |
| Aortic regurgitation | ↓ DBP (wide PP) | Valve leaks during diastole |
| Hemorrhage / hypovolemia | ↓ DBP | Reduced stroke volume + reflex vasoconstriction (compensated) |
| Sympathetic activation | ↑ DBP | α-adrenergic vasoconstriction |
| Atherosclerosis | ↑ DBP | Loss of compliance + raised SVR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define diastolic blood pressure. Mention its normal range and significance in coronary perfusion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Diastolic blood pressure is the minimum arterial pressure recorded during the relaxation phase (ventricular diastole) of the cardiac cycle, when the ventricles fill with blood and the aortic valve is closed.
- **Normal range (1 mark):** **60 – 80 mmHg** in healthy adults; values ≥ 90 mmHg indicate diastolic hypertension.
- **Significance (1 mark):** Coronary arteries perfuse the myocardium predominantly during diastole. Adequate DBP (typically > 60 mmHg) is essential to maintain coronary blood flow and prevent myocardial ischemia.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**How is the diastolic endpoint identified in (a) auscultatory and (b) oscillometric methods of BP measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
